item.title: Australia weighing requests for assistance from countries attacked by Iran
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 8 (Reuters) - Australia's government said on Sunday it was considering requests to help protect countries attacked by Iran in ‚Äčthe widening Middle East conflict, but reiterated it would ‚ÄĆnot take part in any military operations in Iran.
"We've had many countries, which are non participants, (that) have been attacked by Iran through this. ‚ÄčYou would anticipate as a consequence that we have ‚Äčbeen asked for assistance, and we will work ‚Ā†through that carefully," Foreign Minister Penny Wong said in televised ‚Äčremarks.
Australia, a close U.S. ally, has said it will not deploy ‚Äčtroops to the Middle East if the conflict escalates.
Asked if Australia could help protect nations from Iranian drone and missile attacks, Wong said "correct".
"We will ‚Äčwork through that in accordance with the position that ‚ÄčI have outlined, which is we are not participating in offensive action ‚ÄĆagainst ‚Ā†Iran and we have made clear we will not participate in any ground troop deployment," Wong added.
Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said on Friday that three Australian defence personnel were on board ‚Äča U.S. ‚Äčsubmarine that sank ‚Ā†an Iranian warship with a torpedo in the Indian Ocean, but stressed that they did not ‚Äčtake part in the attack.
The conflict in the ‚ÄčMiddle ‚Ā†East has escalated this week, with U.S. and Israeli jets striking areas across Iran, and many Gulf cities - including in the ‚Ā†UAE ‚Äčand Qatar - coming under Iranian bombardment.
Australia ‚Äčis currently working with airlines to help thousands of Australians stranded in the ‚ÄčMiddle East.

item.title: Families of flight MH370 passengers urge Malaysia to extend search
item.description:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 8 (Reuters) - Families of those aboard Malaysia Airlines flight MH370 on Sunday urged the Malaysian government to extend a contract it signed with deep-sea exploration firm ‚ÄčOcean Infinity to continue a search for the aircraft that disappeared 12 ‚ÄĆyears ago.
The Boeing 777 was carrying 227 passengers and 12 crew when it vanished en route from Kuala Lumpur to Beijing on March 8, 2014, becoming one of the world‚Äôs enduring ‚Äčaviation mysteries.
Multiple search operations for the plane have been conducted in the ‚Äčsouthern Indian Ocean since then but all have proved fruitless.
Malaysia in ‚Ā†March last year agreed to allow Ocean Infinity to resume the hunt under a ‚Äúno ‚Äčfind, no fee‚ÄĚ principle, with the firm to be paid $70 million only if the ‚Äčwreckage was successfully located.
Malaysia‚Äôs Air Accident Investigation Bureau said on Sunday, however, that operations had not yielded any findings so far, after two search phases covering 28 days and around 7,571 square ‚Äčkilometres (2,923 square miles) of seabed.
Operations were periodically disrupted by weather and sea conditions, ‚Äčwith the second phase ending on January 23, the AAIB said.
‚ÄúThe government remains committed to keeping the ‚ÄĆfamilies ‚Ā†informed and will continue to provide updates as appropriate,‚ÄĚ it said.
Voice370, a group representing families of those onboard, said it was unlikely for Ocean Infinity to resume the search before its contract ends in June, due to the coming winter months ‚Äčin the southern hemisphere ‚Äčand deteriorating sea ‚Ā†conditions.
It urged the government to grant any request for Ocean Infinity to extend its agreement, as well as expand the ‚Äčsame terms to other interested exploration firms.
‚ÄúA simple addendum extending ‚Äčthe contract ‚Ā†period without altering the core terms of the agreement would allow the search to continue without delay,‚ÄĚ it said.
Ocean Infinity had conducted prior searches for the plane but failed ‚Ā†to ‚Äčfind substantive wreckage.
Malaysian investigators in a 2018 report drew ‚Äčno conclusion about what happened aboard the flight, but did not rule out the possibility that the ‚Äčaircraft had been deliberately taken off course.

item.title: Majority consensus reached on Iran's next supreme leader, Mehr news reports
item.description:
DUBAI, March 8 (Reuters) - A majority consensus over a ‚Äčsuccessor to Iran's slain ‚ÄĆSupreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei has more ‚Äčor less been ‚Äčreached, Assembly of Experts ‚Ā†member Ayatollah Mohammadmehdi Mirbaqeri ‚Äčsaid on Sunday, ‚Äčaccording to Mehr news agency.
He said, though, that "some obstacles" ‚Äčneed to be ‚Äčresolved regarding the process, according ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†the report.
Iranian media said the body tasked with appointing Iran's supreme ‚Äčleader ‚Äčhad ‚Ā†a minor disagreement over whether their ‚Äčfinal decision must ‚Äčfollow ‚Ā†an in-person meeting or instead be issued ‚Ā†without ‚Äčadhering to ‚Äčthis formality.

item.title: BHP Australia boss in running to lead Woodside, Bloomberg reports
item.description:
PERTH/MELBOURNE, March 8 (Reuters) - Global miner BHP‚Äôs (BHP.AX) , opens new tab Australia President Geraldine Slattery is among the ‚Äčcontenders for the top job at ‚ÄĆWoodside Energy (WDS.AX) , opens new tab , Bloomberg reported on Friday, citing people familiar with the situation.
Woodside is expected ‚Äčto name a replacement for ‚ÄčMeg O‚ÄôNeill soon, after she unexpectedly left ‚Ā†the company in December to lead ‚ÄčBP (BP.L) , opens new tab , a role she formally begins in April .
‚ÄúAs ‚Äčhas been disclosed, Woodside‚Äôs Board intends to announce a permanent CEO appointment in the first ‚Äčquarter of 2026. We do ‚Äčnot comment on market speculation,‚ÄĚ a Woodside spokeswoman ‚ÄĆsaid, when ‚Ā†asked whether Slattery had been formally interviewed for the role.
Acting CEO Liz Westcott and two other internal candidates are ‚Äčalso in ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†running for the CEO job, said MST Marquee analyst Saul ‚ÄčKavonic.
BHP declined to comment and ‚ÄčSlattery ‚Ā†did not respond to a request for comment at the time of writing.
Slattery ‚Ā†headed ‚ÄčBHP's petroleum division before ‚Äčit was acquired by Woodside.

item.title: Two Kuwait fire officers killed 'while performing duties,' Fire Force says
item.description:
CAIRO, March 8 (Reuters) - Kuwait's Fire Force ‚Äčsaid on ‚ÄĆSunday that two of its ‚Äčofficers were ‚Äčkilled "while performing duties," according ‚Ā†to a ‚Äčpost on ‚ÄčX.
It did not provide details on ‚Äčthe circumstances ‚Äčof their deaths, but ‚ÄĆKuwait ‚Ā†has been intercepting drones and missiles launched ‚Äčfrom ‚ÄčIran ‚Ā†following U.S. and Israeli ‚Äčattacks ‚Äčon ‚Ā†the Islamic Republic.

item.title: Trump praises Italy PM Meloni's willingness to help in U.S.-Israel war with Iran
item.description:
ROME, March 8 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump praised Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni ‚Äčand said she was willing to ‚ÄĆhelp the United States and Israel in their war with Iran, Italy‚Äôs Corriere della ‚ÄčSera reported on Sunday.
Speaking to ‚Äčthe newspaper in a telephone call ‚Ā†on Saturday, Trump described Meloni as ‚Äúa ‚Äčgreat leader‚ÄĚ and said Italy was ‚Äčdoing what it could to assist.
* "I love Italy, I think she is a great leader," ‚ÄčTrump was quoted as saying of ‚ÄčMeloni.
* "She always tries to help, she is an ‚ÄĆexcellent ‚Ā†leader and she is a friend of mine," Trump said, according to Corriere della Sera.
* Italy is planning to ‚Äčsend air-defence ‚Äčaid to ‚Ā†Gulf countries in the face of Iranian air strikes, ‚ÄčMeloni said on Thursday.
* An Italian ‚Äčnavy ‚Ā†vessel is preparing to sail to Cyprus, a navy spokesperson said on Friday, ‚Ā†as ‚Äčpart of a joint ‚ÄčEuropean mission to protect the island after it ‚Äčcame under Iranian fire.

item.title: Israeli military says it will pursue every successor of Iran's Khamenei
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - The Israeli ‚Äčmilitary warned it would ‚ÄĆcontinue pursuing every successor of Iran's slain Supreme Leader Ayatollah ‚ÄčAli Khamenei, who was ‚Äčkilled in U.S. and ‚Ā†Israeli strikes on Iran.
In ‚Äča post on ‚ÄčX in Farsi, the Israeli military also warned it would pursue every ‚Äčperson who seeks ‚Äčto appoint a successor for ‚ÄĆKhamenei, ‚Ā†referring to the clerical body charged with choosing the Islamic Republic's ‚Äčsupreme ‚Äčleader.
The ‚Ā†warning comes as the clerical body ‚Äčis set to ‚Äčmeet ‚Ā†to choose Iran's next supreme leader.

item.title: Explainer: Who might succeed in Iran's theocratic system of power?
item.description:
March 8 - Iranian clerics involved in choosing a successor to Ayatollah Ali Khamenei after his killing a week ago in U.S.-Israeli strikes say they are close to naming the Islamic Republic's new supreme leader.
Iran's revolutionary theocracy has never been in greater jeopardy , and with the clerical body tasked with naming a new leader ready for an announcement as soon as Sunday, it is hard to predict what might happen next.
Israel and the United States ‚Äčhave vowed no let up in their war, promising to kill whoever replaces Khamenei and even those involved in selecting the new leader - a group that may include the clerics who formally make the choice ‚ÄĆand the Revolutionary Guardsmen and political insiders who influence them.
The following explains how power is meant to operate in the Islamic Republic, how a new supreme leader can be chosen, some of the main candidates, and how the U.S. and Israeli attacks have changed the equation:
Iran's theocratic system dates to the 1979 revolution that ousted the Shah. Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, the leader of the revolution, introduced a new system of rule: vilayat-e faqih, or guardianship of the Islamic jurist.
The theory holds that until the return of the Shi'ite Muslim 12th Imam, who disappeared in the ninth century, power on earth ‚Äčshould be wielded by a venerable cleric.
It means whoever takes over as supreme leader, empowered by the constitution as the ultimate authority guiding the elected president and parliament, will have to be a senior cleric.
Under Khomeini, who died ‚Äčin 1989, and Khamenei, who has ruled since then, the supreme leader has had the last say in all matters of state. But any new leader will have to assert his authority at ‚Ā†a moment of enormous rupture - and may struggle to do so.
The constitution says a new leader must be chosen within three months. Until then President Masoud Pezeshkian, Guardians Council member Ayatollah Alireza Arafi and Judiciary chief Ayatollah Gholamhossein ‚ÄčMohseni-Ejei have taken charge as a temporary leadership council.
Choosing a new leader is meant to be the responsibility of the Assembly of Experts, a body of around 90 senior clerics who are elected every eight years, many of them very elderly.
With strikes continuing, they have been ‚Äčconducting their consultations online, Iranian officials have told Reuters, and appear close to naming a new leader.
Khamenei never publicly tapped a preferred successor and in practice the decision probably rests with the most senior figures in the Islamic Republic who have wielded power under Khamenei for many years. It is their preferred candidate who is most likely to be approved by the assembly.
Among the most important of those senior figures is Khamenei's veteran adviser Ali Larijani , often seen as Iran's foremost powerbroker. The Guards will also have a critical backroom voice in the process.
Khamenei's son, Mojtaba Khamenei , is widely seen as the most ‚Äčlikely candidate after surviving the first salvo of strikes, which killed his wife. Although Iran's ruling ideology frowns on the principle of hereditary succession, he has a powerful following within the Guards and his dead father's still-influential office.
The grandson of the revolutionary founder Ruhollah ‚ÄčKhomeini is another possible choice. Hassan Khomeini is closely associated with the reformist faction that has for decades tried to moderate the Islamic Republic's stance and may be seen as better able to assuage Western enmity and calm the fury of an embittered population.
Arafi and Mohseni-Ejei are less ‚ÄĆprominent possibilities who ‚Ā†would likely continue Khamenei's hardline stance. Mohseni-Ejei was responsible for stamping out internal protests following a disputed election outcome in 2009 when he was intelligence minister.
Assembly of Experts members Ahmad Alamolhoda and Mohsen Araki are also hardline senior clerics with a close involvement in Iranian politics who might be considered. Former president Hassan Rouhani is a senior cleric but he was distrusted by some of the most powerful hardliners who would have great sway over the choice.
The assembly could theoretically pick an even lesser known ayatollah as leader. But the ruling system has been so fractured by the strikes that it would be much harder to buttress the position of a newcomer.
The Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps had long been expected to play a central role behind the ‚Äčscenes in determining Khamenei's successor. Unlike the ordinary military, which ‚Äčcomes under the elected president, the guards answer only to ‚Ā†the supreme leader.
But its top echelons have been hollowed out by U.S. and Israeli strikes over recent years and it is far from clear how far it will still be able to influence the decision.
The most important Guards leader of recent times was Qassem Soleimani, head of the elite Quds Force unit that spearheaded Iran's regional strategy of exporting the revolution through affiliated Shi'ite militias ‚Äčin Arab countries. He was killed by a U.S. strike in 2020.
During the brief summer war last year Israeli strikes killed other top Guards commanders. And the latest strikes killed its ‚Äčlatest top commander Mohammed Pakpour, three ‚Ā†sources familiar with the matter said.
The Basij militia, a part-time paramilitary force under Guards control, is often used to quell protests inside Iran, giving the corps a formidable role in internal control.
Since the early 2000s, the Guards' economic power has also grown as its contracting company Khatam al-Anbiya won projects worth billions of dollars in Iran's oil and gas sector. Protecting that empire may contribute to the Guards' decision on backing a new leader.
Iranians elect a president and a parliament to four-year terms. The president appoints a government ‚Ā†that handles daily policy ‚Äčwithin parameters permitted by the supreme leader.
During the early years of the Islamic Republic the votes drew mass participation. But far fewer Iranians retain faith ‚Äčin electoral politics.
While President Pezeshkian, a reputed moderate, is on the three-member interim leadership committee, it is far from clear he has had any say over how events pan out and he was forced into an embarrassing climbdown on Saturday over the conduct of the war.
And while the Assembly of Experts is elected, all its ‚Äčcandidates - like those running in all Iranian national elections - must be vetted by the clerical Guardians Council, meaning only those already aligned with the authorities can take part.

item.title: Lebanese man flees hometown, months after repairing home damaged in last war
item.description:
HAZMIEH, Lebanon, March 8 (Reuters) - Just days ago, Hussain Khrais was proudly showing off his newly restored home in south ‚ÄčLebanon, fixed up after being badly damaged in 2024 clashes between Israel and Hezbollah. But a new war has since erupted ‚ÄĆand his home is in the line of fire again.
Khrais fled his hometown of Khiyam, about five km (three miles) from the border with Israel, as Israel pounded Lebanon with heavy airstrikes last week in retaliation for Iran-backed group Hezbollah's rocket and drone fire into Israel.
"Is the house I worked so hard to build, or the business I started, ‚Äčstill there? Or is it all gone?" Khrais told Reuters from a relative's home near the capital Beirut where he and his ‚Äčfamily are now staying.
"The feeling is very, very upsetting, because we still don't know if we'll go back ‚Ā†or not."
It wasn't Khrais' first time - or even his second. The 66-year-old has been displaced at least four times in ‚Äčthe last four decades by Israeli incursions and airstrikes, each time returning to a town in ruins and rebuilding patiently.
Last year, he spent months and ‚Äčaround $25,000 repairing the damage from the last war between Hezbollah and Israel, which ended 15 months ago. Hezbollah started firing at Israel after the United States and Israel launched airstrikes against Iran on February 28.
"It really bothers me to think this is the life I've lived," Khrais told Reuters. "Once again, displacement, return, rebuilding, restoration - then again displacement, return, ‚Äčrebuilding. What kind of life is that?"
With no support from the Lebanese state and little coming from Hezbollah's social welfare programme, most Lebanese whose ‚Äčhomes were damaged or destroyed in the 2024 war have used their own private funds to rebuild.
Reconstruction has placed a huge burden on affected Lebanese families, still struggling ‚ÄĆto access ‚Ā†their savings in commercial banks after a financial collapse in 2019.
Two weeks ago, Khrais had told Reuters he was scared that a new war would start. "I'm at an age where I can't start all over again. That's it," he said.
The new war has dealt Lebanese another blow. About 300,000 people have been displaced over the last week by Israel's strikes and by the Israeli military's evacuation orders, which encompass around 8% of ‚ÄčLebanese territory.
Khrais is staying with around ‚Äč20 other displaced relatives, some displaced ‚Ā†from Khiyam and others from Beirut's southern suburbs, which have been hit hard by Israeli strikes.
He is glued to the television, where news bulletins have reported on Israeli troops and tanks pushing deeper into his hometown.
"I've been ‚Äčin Beirut for four days now, and these four days feel like 400 years," Khrais said.
He misses ‚Äčhis house dearly.
"Maybe the ‚Ā†thing I'm most attached to, is when I open the door to my children's bedrooms and see the pictures of their children hanging on the walls," he said.
"That sight is worth the world's treasures - to see my grandchildren's pictures in Khiyam."
Khrais has no news on the state of his home. He said ‚Ā†he remains ‚Äčhopeful but that if it has been destroyed, he'll still do what he's always done.
"The ‚Äčbig shock would be if I came back and didn't find it. But my feeling says no, God willing, it will remain. And like I said, even if we don't find ‚Äčthe house, we'll go back and rebuild," he said.

item.title: Iran war threatens prolonged hit to global energy markets
item.description:
March 7 (Reuters) - The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran could leave consumers and businesses worldwide facing weeks or months of higher fuel prices even if the week-old conflict ends quickly, as suppliers grapple with damaged facilities, disrupted logistics, and elevated risks to shipping.
The outlook poses a global economic threat and a political vulnerability for ‚ÄčU.S. President Donald Trump leading into the midterm elections, with voters sensitive to energy bills and unfavorable to foreign entanglements .
"The market is shifting from pricing pure geopolitical risk to grappling with tangible operational disruption, as refinery shutdowns and export constraints ‚ÄĆbegin to impair crude processing and regional supply flows," JP Morgan analysts said in a research note on Friday.
The conflict has already led to the suspension of around a fifth of global crude and natural gas supply, as Tehran targets ships in the vital Strait of Hormuz between its shores and Oman, and attacks energy infrastructure across the region.
Global oil prices have surged more than 25% since the start of the war, driving up fuel prices for consumers worldwide.
A nearly complete shutdown of the Strait means the region's giant oil producers - Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Iraq and Kuwait - have had to suspend shipments of as much as 140 million barrels of oil - ‚Äčequal to about 1.4 days of global demand - to global refiners.
As a result, oil and gas storage at facilities in the Middle East Gulf are rapidly filling, forcing oil fields in Iraq and Kuwait to cut oil production , with the United Arab Emirates likely ‚Äčto cut next , analysts, traders and sources said.
"At some point soon, everyone will also shut in if vessels do not come," said a source with a state oil company in the region, who asked not ‚Ā†to be named.
Oilfields forced to shut in across the Middle East as a result of the shipping disruptions could take a while to return to normal, said Amir Zaman, head of the Americas commercial team at Rystad Energy.
"The conflict could be ended, but it could take days ‚Äčor weeks or months, depending on the types of fields, age of the field, the type of shut-in that they've had to do before you can get production back up to what it once was," he said.
Iranian forces, meanwhile, are targeting regional energy infrastructure - including refineries and terminals - ‚Äčforcing them to shut down too, with some of those operations badly damaged by attacks and in need of repairs.
Qatar declared force majeure on its huge volumes of gas exports on Wednesday after Iranian drone attacks and it may take at least a month to return to normal production levels, sources told Reuters. Qatar supplies 20% of global LNG.
Saudi Aramco‚Äôs mammoth Ras Tanura refinery and crude export terminal, meanwhile, has also closed due to attacks, with no details on damage.
The White House has justified the attack on Iran, saying the country posed an imminent threat to the United States , although it has not provided details. Trump has also said he was concerned about ‚ÄčIran's efforts to obtain a nuclear weapon.
A quick end to the war would soothe markets. But a return to pre-war supply and pricing could take weeks or months, depending on the extent of the damage to infrastructure and shipping.
"Considering physical damage due to Iranian ‚Äčstrikes, so far we have not seen anything that would be considered structural, although the risk remains as long as the war continues," said Joel Hancock, energy analyst, Natixis CIB.
The biggest question for energy supplies is how and when the Strait of Hormuz will become safe for shipping again. Trump has offered ‚ÄĆnaval escorts to ‚Ā†oil tankers and promised U.S. insurance support to vessels in the region.
But safety in the waterway may be elusive, as Iran has the capacity to sustain drone attacks on shipping for months , intelligence and military sources have said.
The conflict could also encourage countries to top up their strategic petroleum reserves in the weeks and months after the conflict ends, by exposing the dangers of thin inventories. That would increase demand for oil and support prices.
In the meantime, the disruption in energy shipments is reverberating through supply chains and economies in import-reliant Asia, which sources 60% of its crude oil from the Middle East.
In India, state-run Mangalore Refinery and Petrochemicals (MRPL.NS) , opens new tab declared force majeure on gasoline export cargoes, sources said this week, joining a growing number of refineries in the region unable to fulfill sales contracts due to lack of supply.
At least two refineries in China have ‚Äčcut runs. China, a big supplier to the region, has asked ‚Äčrefineries to suspend fuel exports. Thailand has also suspended fuel ‚Ā†exports, while Vietnam has suspended crude shipments.
Disruption has given Russia a boost. Prices for Russian crude cargoes have risen as the U.S. has given Indian refiners a 30-day waiver to buy Russian crude to substitute for lost Middle East supply. Washington had pressured India to cut Russian oil imports under the threat of tariffs.
In Japan, the No. 2 global LNG importer, baseload power futures for Tokyo for the fiscal year starting in April ‚Äčjumped more than a third this week on the EEX in anticipation of higher fuel prices. And in Seoul, drivers queued up at petrol stations in anticipation of rising pump prices.
For European consumers, the ‚Äčcrisis in gas supplies and the higher ‚Ā†prices are a double whammy. The region was hit the hardest by the disruption to gas supplies due to sanctions on Russian energy imports after Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022.
Europe turned to LNG imports to substitute for Russian pipeline gas. And Europe now needs to buy 180 more LNG cargoes than it did last year to fill gas storage to the levels needed before next winter.
The supply risks to the United States are fewer, as the country has grown in recent years into the world‚Äôs largest oil and gas producer. But U.S. crude and fuel prices rise in tandem with international crude ‚Ā†markets, so pump prices ‚Äčfor gasoline and diesel are affected even if domestic supply is plentiful.
U.S. average retail gasoline, for example, hit $3.32 a gallon nationally on Friday, up 34 cents over last ‚Äčweek, according to AAA. Diesel prices, meanwhile, hit $4.33 a gallon, up from $3.76 a gallon a week ago.
Higher prices at the pump mark a major risk for Trump and his fellow Republicans as they head into midterm elections in November.
"Gasoline prices are psychologically powerful," said Mark Malek, chief investment officer at Siebert Financial. "They are the inflation number that consumers ‚Äčsee every single day."

item.title: Slovakia PM Fico to meet EU's von der Leyen to push for restart of Druzhba oil flows
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - Slovak Prime Minister Robert Fico said on Sunday he ‚ÄĆwould meet with EU chief Ursula von der Leyen on Tuesday, as Slovakia and Hungary ramp up pressure on Ukraine to restart Russian oil flows, warning Slovakia could block a major EU ‚Äčloan to Kyiv.
Russian oil flows through the Druzhba pipeline to Hungary and Slovakia ‚Äčvia Ukraine have been suspended since late January, after Kyiv said a ‚Ā†Russian attack caused a fire that seriously damaged the line.
The outage has sparked one ‚Äčof the most heated disputes between the neighbours since Russia's 2022 full-scale invasion of ‚ÄčUkraine.
Ukraine says the pipeline cannot be repaired quickly .
Hungary and Slovakia, European Union members which still import Russian oil and maintain ties with Moscow, have accused Kyiv of deliberately delaying a restart for political ‚Äčreasons.
Hungary has vetoed new EU sanctions ‚Äčon Russia and a 90-billion-euro loan by the bloc for Ukraine. Fico reiterated his threat to ‚ÄĆblock the ‚Ā†loan in a Facebook video on Sunday.
He said he would meet von der Leyen in Paris and push for experts to visit the Druzhba pipeline.
"But the most important message will be that Slovakia is ready to take over the baton ‚Äčfrom Hungary, if necessary," ‚ÄčFico said in ‚Ā†reference to the loan.
"Blocking this huge military gift to Ukraine is a legitimate tool to achieve the restoration of oil ‚Äčsupplies."
The EU Commission said Friday it was looking into ways to ‚Äčrestore Druzhba ‚Ā†oil flows, including potential financial assistance.
Slovakia has already halted emergency power supplies to Ukraine amid the dispute.
The dispute escalates as Hungary heads into a tight election next month, with Prime ‚Ā†Minister Viktor ‚ÄčOrban, in power for 16 years, making the ‚Äčwar in Ukraine a central campaign theme.

item.title: Italy weighs fuel-tax cuts as firms warn of energy price surge from Middle East war
item.description:
ROME, March 8 (Reuters) - Italy is considering cutting fuel excise duties using higher-than-expected value-added tax revenues triggered by rising ‚Äčpump prices, the prime minister said, as businesses ‚ÄĆwarned the conflict in the Middle East could sharply raise energy costs.
Excise duties account for a large share of the pump ‚Äčprice in Italy and are levied per litre, ‚Äčnot as a percentage of the total price ‚Ā†like VAT.
($1 = ‚Äč0.8607 euros)

item.title: Tokyo told national oil reserve site to prepare for release amid Iran crisis, Japan lawmaker says
item.description:
TOKYO, March 8 (Reuters) - A senior Japanese parliament member said on Sunday that the government instructed a national oil reserve storage site to prepare for a possible release ‚Äčof crude, as the Iran crisis has cut supply from the Middle ‚ÄĆEast.
Japan relies on the Middle East for around 95% of its crude supplies, with roughly 70% shipped through the Strait of Hormuz, which has been effectively closed following U.S. and Israeli ‚Äčattacks on Iran.
Akira Nagatsuma, a member of the Centrist Reform Alliance opposition ‚Äčparty, told Reuters that an official from the Japan Organization for ‚Ā†Metals and Energy Security (JOGMEC) at the Shibushi national oil storage base said it ‚Äčhad received the directive from the Agency for Natural Resources and Energy (ANRE) on Friday.
Details ‚Äčsuch as the timing of the release remain unclear, Nagatsuma said.
It was not immediately clear if any other storage bases received the same instruction.
An official at ANRE, under the Ministry of ‚ÄčEconomy, Trade and Industry (METI), declined to comment. The Shibushi base was not immediately ‚Äčavailable for comment, while JOGMEC could not be reached outside office hours.
The Shibushi site in southern ‚ÄĆJapan ‚Ā†is one of the locations where the country's strategic oil stocks are stored.
Japan holds emergency oil reserves equivalent to 254 days of domestic consumption - among the world's largest - including government-owned stockpiles, private-sector inventories, and jointly held reserves with oil-producing countries.
Tokyo last ‚Äčdrew down oil from ‚Äčits reserves in ‚Ā†2022 as part of an International Energy Agency (IEA)-led coordinated release following Russia's invasion of Ukraine.
Industry Minister Ryosei Akazawa said early last ‚Äčweek that there were no specific plans to release oil reserves, ‚Äčbut the ‚Ā†ministry would continue to monitor the crude oil supply situation closely, working in coordination with the IEA.
Kyodo news agency reported on Friday that the Japanese government is considering using ‚Ā†part ‚Äčof its national oil reserves amid the ongoing Iran ‚Äčcrisis that has disrupted global energy supplies and may do so in coordination with other countries or ‚Äčeven on its own.

item.title: Iran attacks breach international law, Swiss Defence Minister says
item.description:
ZURICH/FRANKFURT, March 8 (Reuters) - The United States and Israel have broken international law with their attacks on Iran , Swiss Defence Minister Martin Pfister said in an ‚Äčinterview published on Sunday, the latest European leader to raise ‚ÄĆconcerns about the conflict.
Legal experts have said many countries will consider the attacks unjustified under the United Nations Charter, under which member countries must refrain from using force ‚Äčor the threat of force without U.N authorisation or unless acting ‚Äčin self-defence.
"The Federal Council is of the opinion that the ‚Ā†attack on Iran constitutes a violation of international law," Pfister told ‚ÄčSonntagsZeitung, referring to the Swiss cabinet.
"In our view it constitutes a violation ‚Äčon the prohibition of violence," he added, calling on all parties involved to halt the fighting to protect the civilian population.
Pfister said he was referring ‚Äčto all the countries not complying with the prohibition on violence, ‚Äčincluding the United States and Israel.
"The Americans and Israel have attacked Iran from the ‚ÄĆair. ‚Ā†In doing so, they, like Iran, violated international law," Pfister said.
The comments chime with those of German Vice Chancellor and Finance Minister Lars Klingbeil, who told the RND newspaper network that he had "serious doubts that this ‚Äčwar is legitimate under ‚Äčinternational law."
Klingbeil ‚Ā†also came out strongly against the idea of any potential German participation in the war: "I say clearly: this ‚Äčis not our war. We will not participate in ‚Äčthis war."
He ‚Ā†said there was a "great danger that we are sliding ever deeper into a world where there are no longer any rules. We do not want ‚Ā†to ‚Äčlive in a world where only the ‚Äčlaw of the strongest applies."
Spain has also denounced the U.S. and Israeli bombings of Iran as reckless ‚Äčand illegal.

item.title: Airstrikes, not occupation: Where Trump voters draw red lines on Iran
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - A week into a war with Iran that is already unpopular with much of the American public, President Donald Trump has offered various explanations for the bombing campaign, estimated the strikes could last weeks , cautioned there will likely be more U.S. casualties , and dismissed concerns about surging oil and gas prices .
While that has troubled many Americans, recent interviews with several who voted for Trump show they are largely standing by the ‚Äčpresident and his war ‚Äď at least for now. Even his most ardent supporters, however, warned that a large deployment of U.S. ground troops in Iran would alarm them.
In the days since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran, Reuters has spoken ‚ÄĆto eight Americans who voted for Trump in 2024, part of a group of 20 whom Reuters has interviewed monthly since February , to hear their thoughts on the rapidly escalating conflict.
All eight opposed the idea of the Trump administration sending substantial U.S. ground forces to Iran or getting involved in a protracted effort to install new leadership. But five said they fully supported the air and sea attacks as the only way to prevent Iran from stockpiling long-range and nuclear missiles. Three were less clear about why the administration started the conflict, saying they worried it was unduly damaging the U.S. economy and endangering U.S. citizens.
Their reactions to the war so far roughly reflect the results of a Reuters-Ipsos poll conducted last weekend ‚Äčthat surveyed 1,282 U.S. adults. Nearly two-thirds of respondents who voted for Trump in 2024 said they approved of the strikes, while 9% said they disapproved and 27% said they were not sure. Overall, just one in four respondents expressed support for the U.S. attack ‚Äčon Iran.
If energy prices keep surging and Trump's tactics against Iran start to alienate his own followers , the conflict could erode support for Republicans as the U.S. heads into all-important midterm elections in November that will determine ‚Ā†whether Congress stays in the party's control.
Although most of the eight voters Reuters interviewed reported that gasoline in their area had shot up between 20 and 50 cents per gallon, those who supported the strikes said they expected the higher prices to be short-lived.
Jon Webber, 45, a Walmart ‚Äčretail worker in Indiana, pointed to the struggle his parents had with volatile oil prices following the 1979 Iranian Revolution. ‚ÄúYeah, it's gonna suck for a little bit, but it'll go back,‚ÄĚ he said.
After watching U.S. presidents invoke the threat posed by Iran for most of his life, Webber said it ‚Äčfelt good to see Trump cripple the regime: ‚ÄúIt should have been done a long time ago and we wouldn't have had to deal with it for this long.‚ÄĚ
Near Houston, Texas, Loretta Torres, 38, said she trusted the president had acted judiciously. ‚ÄúTrump was trying to get ahead of the game and trying to be proactive with the threats,‚ÄĚ she said.
But Torres, a mother of three, also said she feared the war could spin ‚Äúout of control‚ÄĚ or inspire terrorist attacks on major metropolitan areas like hers. Like all the voters Reuters interviewed, she dreaded the prospect of the U.S. becoming enmeshed in the region for years if Trump sends in ground troops.
The voters ‚Äčwho supported the strikes were confident Trump had authorized them because they were necessary to thwart an imminent attack on the United States. Democrats and even prominent conservative commentators have expressed skepticism about this, citing the administration's varying explanations for the war.
Chad Hill, 50, a supervisor at a nuclear power ‚Äčplant near his home in northwestern Ohio, said he had been expecting some type of U.S. military action, despite U.S.-Iran negotiations that had been underway over Iran's nuclear program just days before the strikes: ‚ÄúUnfortunately, it seems like this was probably the only way because in the end they don't trust us and we don't trust ‚ÄĆthem.‚ÄĚ
Trump might need ‚Ā†to send a limited military detachment into the country to fully destroy Iran‚Äôs missile capabilities, Hill said, but any larger ground deployment would raise red flags for him. ‚ÄúNo nation-building, that doesn‚Äôt work,‚ÄĚ he said.
The idea of U.S. boots on Iranian ground also made Gerald Dunn, 67, uneasy. ‚ÄúOnly if they‚Äôre invited‚ÄĚ by a new Iranian government should Trump deploy ground troops, he said, and even then, ‚Äúthe scale should be limited.‚ÄĚ
Like Hill, Dunn, a martial arts instructor in New York‚Äôs Hudson Valley, applauded Trump for taking action where prior administrations had simply ‚Äúkicked the can down the road.‚ÄĚ
Near Savannah, Georgia, insurance firm employee Amanda Taylor, 52, said that while ‚Äúthere‚Äôs so much we don‚Äôt really know,‚ÄĚ she would back any military action that made the U.S. safer.
‚ÄúOur intelligence is usually right in these things so I'm going to hope and trust that [Trump acted] because of that, and not just because of his own gut feeling,‚ÄĚ ‚Äčshe said. At the same time, Taylor added, ‚Äúnobody wants a drawn-out, true ‚Äčwar ‚Äď I would hate to see that happen.‚ÄĚ
CONFUSION OVER RATIONALE
The shifting ‚Ā†reasons Trump administration officials gave for the strikes puzzled some voters.
On Monday, Herman Sims heard Secretary of State Marco Rubio say the U.S. learned Israel was planning to attack Iran and struck first to prevent retaliation ‚Äď but on Tuesday, he heard Trump claim responsibility for leading the charge based on the president's hunch that Iran would attack if the U.S. didn‚Äôt.
Sims, 66, a night operations manager for a trucking company in Dallas, Texas, said ‚Äčthe conflicting reports ‚Äúdidn‚Äôt make any sense,‚ÄĚ but added that he supported the strikes if they were indeed necessary to protect U.S. lives. Still, he was alarmed by the spiking gas prices and by a report that ‚Äčan ex-Marine ‚Äôs arm was broken while he ‚Ā†protested that the U.S. should not ‚Äúfight for Israel‚ÄĚ during a Senate hearing.
‚ÄúI agree 100%. We should not be fighting a war for someone else,‚ÄĚ Sims said.
In Madison, Wisconsin, college student Will Brown, 20, said he was frustrated by the administration's "wishy-washy" explanations for why the U.S. attacked when it did.
"Bombing them to the extent that we have is fine, but Trump's talked about boots on the ground and troops dying, and that I simply can't approve of," he said. Trump told the New York Post on Monday that he has not ruled out sending U.S. ground troops into Iran.
Though he was glad to see Iran's leader dead ‚Ā†and the country's nuclear ‚Äčcapacity reduced, Brown said he could "not fathom the amount of destruction and death" a ground invasion would bring.
Don Jernigan, 75, a retiree in Virginia Beach, said Trump had not ‚Äčjustified putting U.S. troops in harm‚Äôs way.
Assuming the Iranian threat was ‚Äúso imminent that our cities are in danger of being destroyed from a long distance, from the mainland of Iran, then perhaps we should be destroying people from a long distance, like the mainland of America,‚ÄĚ Jernigan said.
Though he couldn‚Äôt know what kind of threat Trump saw, Jernigan added that ‚Äčthe U.S. strikes had raised the odds of terrorist attacks against Americans.
‚ÄúIf we kill their brothers and their dads and their uncles over there, they're going to come over here and try to kill ours,‚ÄĚ he said.

item.title: Pope Leo urges end to bombing, calls for dialogue amid Iran, Middle East violence
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 8 (Reuters) - Pope Leo said on Sunday that ‚Äčdeeply troubling news continued ‚ÄĆto arrive from Iran and across the Middle East, urging ‚Äčan end to the ‚Äčviolence and renewed efforts to ‚Ā†open space for dialogue.
Speaking ‚Äčat the Angelus prayer ‚Äčin St. Peter‚Äôs Square, the pope said the conflict was fuelling ‚Äčfear and hatred and ‚Äčraised concerns that it could spread ‚ÄĆfurther, ‚Ā†dragging other countries, including "dear Lebanon".
"Let us raise our humble prayer to the Lord that ‚Äčthe ‚Äčroar ‚Ā†of bombs may cease, that weapons may ‚Äčfall silent, and that ‚Äčspace ‚Ā†may be opened for dialogue in which the voices ‚Ā†of ‚Äčpeoples can be ‚Äčheard", Pope Leo said.

item.title: From 1776 to 2026: Adam Smith's lessons for the global economy
item.description:
LONDON, March 8 (Reuters) - Tax the rich. Trash the tariffs. End monopolies.
Such are the rallying calls of many of today's most heated economic debates. They could also come straight from the pen of revered economist Adam Smith, hailed by some as the "father of capitalism" and others as an early progressive.
Smith knew nothing of Donald Trump ‚Äčor tech billionaires when he railed against trade protectionism and extreme affluence in "The Wealth of Nations", the best-read economics book in history, which celebrates its 250th birthday on Monday.
"It is the maxim of ‚ÄĆevery prudent master of a family never to attempt to make at home what it will cost him more to make than to buy," the Scot wrote in the seminal work, for example.
"Would it be a reasonable law to prohibit the importation of all foreign wines, merely to encourage the making of claret and burgundy in Scotland?" he adds.
The foundational text of classical economics was published on March 9, 1776, the same year as the U.S. Declaration of Independence, 13 years before the French Revolution, and amid the early convulsions of the Industrial Age.
Yet ‚Äčit's not hard to find parallels between the doctrines the book denounces - such as rival mercantilist empires seeking to minimise imports and maximise exports - and President Trump's trade tactics and "America First" credo today.
And while Smith is ‚Äčmost often cited for his free markets and free trade zeal, when it comes to distribution of wealth, he could almost be channelling Bernie Sanders or Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, leftist ‚Ā†U.S. lawmakers.
"It is not very unreasonable that the rich should contribute to the public expense, not only in proportion to their revenue, but something more than in that proportion," Smith writes in the 1,000-page-plus tome, which draws on everything from ‚Äčgrape-growing to pin factories.
"No society can surely be flourishing and happy, of which the far greater part of the members are poor and miserable," he says in one of his best-known quotes .
Many scholars of the book - full ‚Äčname "An Inquiry into the Nature and Causes of the Wealth of Nations" - say it remains uncannily relevant to the economic issues of our time, though debates continue to rage about what Smith was actually saying.
Advocates of free-market economics have long claimed him as their spiritual father, while some more recent readings even peg him as a moderate progressive - something akin to a left-leaning, modern-day European social democrat.
"You can find a 'Smith' to support anything you want to say," King's College London research associate Leo Steeds said of the Scottish Enlightenment thinker.
Smith also accepted ‚Äčthat there were certain circumstances when tariffs were called for, either because the terms of trade were unfair or for reasons of security - arguments increasingly heard in the United States, Europe and other trading blocs.
"Smith did understand those arguments," said ‚ÄčEamonn Butler, director of the free market policy think tank the Adam Smith Institute in London. "But he thought these things (tariffs) really should be as temporary as possible. He thought the more trade you have, the better everybody is."
One of the most ‚ÄĆfamous metaphors ‚Ā†from the "Wealth of Nations" is that of the "invisible hand", most often interpreted as meaning how free markets channel the self-interest of different participants to the best outcome for all.
"It is not from the benevolence of the butcher, the brewer, or the baker, that we expect our dinner, but from their regard to their own interest," Smith writes.
But other students of the work note the invisible hand metaphor was used just once in the book and must be taken alongside his wider arguments rather than being used to justify "laissez-faire" policies.
"This book ... is actually a critique of the way in which special interests, monopolists, powerful people, lobbies capture the state," said Pratap Bhanu Mehta, a leading Indian academic and public intellectual.
"He says: You fix that, ‚Äčthen free markets come."
U.S. economist Joseph Stiglitz, a professor at ‚ÄčColumbia University and Nobel Prize laureate, agreed.
"It was much ‚Ā†more of an enlightened self-interest looking at society more broadly," he said. "Modern economics is based on infinitely selfish people. And clearly, Adam Smith didn't believe that."
Indeed, Smith - who taught moral philosophy at the University of Glasgow - is explicit about his views on selfishness at the expense of others.
"All for ourselves, and nothing for other people, seems, in every age of the ‚Äčworld, to have been the vile maxim of the masters of mankind," he writes.
Events to mark the 250th anniversary of "The Wealth of Nations" are ‚Äčtaking place throughout the year in ‚Ā†Glasgow, Edinburgh, London and Smith's birthplace of Kirkcaldy on the Scottish coast.
In a sign of his lasting imprint on the popular imagination, the ghost of Smith made an appearance last year as a character in a satirical musical staged during the Edinburgh festival about the 2008 collapse of Royal Bank of Scotland.
There are, however, limits to how much Smith can be re-branded a progressive or anything else in today's terms.
While he criticised the rich and argued the accumulation of wealth by the few led to the ‚Ā†poverty of many, ‚ÄčMehta argued that Smith, like many of his contemporary thinkers, would have been comfortable with levels of inequality not acceptable today.
Still others - including Karl ‚ÄčMarx decades later - have criticised Smith's production-enhancing ideas on the division of labour into small tasks as being deployed in factories to leave workers with morale-sapping, mind-numbing jobs.
Nonetheless, economic historian Richard van den Berg, professor at Goldsmiths, University of London, said the many questions and differing interpretations surrounding the ‚Äčbook had clearly not diminished its appeal to subsequent generations.
"It is a tool," he said. "A tool for producing ideas."

item.title: Two Israeli soldiers killed in southern Lebanon, Israel military says
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - The Israeli military said on Sunday that two ‚Äčof its soldiers were ‚Äčkilled in southern Lebanon, marking ‚Ā†the first fatalities ‚Äčamong its troops ‚Äčsince Israel-Hezbollah hostilities resumed last week as Israeli intensified air ‚Äčstrikes on ‚ÄčLebanon.
The military did not immediately ‚ÄĆprovide ‚Ā†further details on the circumstances of the incident.
Clashes have continued ‚Äčalong ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†Israel-Lebanon frontier amid heightened tensions ‚Äčbetween Israel and ‚ÄčHezbollah ‚Ā†following the U.S.-Israeli military campaign on Iran .

item.title: Swiss voters set to reject plan to cut funding for state broadcaster
item.description:
ZURICH, March 8 (Reuters) - Swiss voters looked set on Sunday to reject a referendum to slash public broadcaster SRG‚Äôs funding, with early ‚Äčprojections showing 62% opposing a plan to cut the annual ‚Äčlicence fee, a move critics warned would weaken media ‚Ā†and fuel disinformation.
The campaign wanted to reduce the annual licence fee ‚Äčthat all Swiss households must pay from 335 Swiss francs ($431.87) to ‚Äč200 francs.
Supporters, mainly from right-wing groups including the Swiss People's Party (SVP), had argued that the charge ‚Äď the highest in the world ‚Äď was too expensive, and that ‚ÄčSRG, which runs 17 radio stations and seven TV channels ‚Äčin four languages had become too bloated.
They also said the SRG was not politically ‚ÄĆindependent, ‚Ā†and had a left-wing bias in its coverage.
Opponents had said the move reflected pressure on public media organisations from the political right, which has accused national broadcasters ‚Äčglobally of being ‚Äčpolitically biased ‚Ā†against them.
News, sports and cultural coverage would suffer, opponents said, while an SRG weakened by lower funding ‚Äčcould mean that disinformation would be easier to spread.
"A ‚Äčmajor ‚Ā†dismantling of Switzerland's media infrastructure has been prevented," said Laura Zimmermann, leader of the campaign against the cuts. "Our access to reliable information remains ‚Ā†protected."
"We remain ‚Äčfully committed to accompanying the public ‚Äčin their everyday lives with a diverse and high-quality programme," said Susanne Wille, SRG ‚Äčdirector general.
($1 = 0.7757 Swiss francs)

item.title: Greece sees deal to buy Italian-made frigates by April, minister says
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - Greece hopes to wrap up talks and sign a deal ‚Äčfor the purchase of Bergamini class ‚ÄĆfrigates from Italy by April, Defence Minister Nikos Dendias said in an interview ‚Äčreleased on Sunday.
High-ranking officials from ‚ÄčGreece and Italy last year signed memorandums for ‚Ā†naval cooperation, opening the way ‚Äčfor the acquisition of two FREMM frigates ‚Äčby Athens with an option for two more.
"We hope that negotiations with Italy will ‚Äčbe concluded and that we ‚Äčwill have signed (a deal) for the acquisition of ‚ÄĆ2+2 ‚Ā†frigates by April," Dendias told a Greek newspaper.
Dendias said Athens was working to ensure the frigates could also ‚Äčcarry ‚Äčthe new-generation ‚Ā†ELSA missile.
Greece plans to spend about 28 billion euros ‚Äčas part of a multi-year ‚Äčdefence ‚Ā†plan that includes the purchase from France and ‚Ā†new ‚Äčsubmarines as it tries ‚Äčto keep up with historic rival Turkey.

item.title: Colombians vote in legislative contest, presidential primaries
item.description:
BOGOTA, March 8 (Reuters) - Colombians were heading to the polls on Sunday to elect a new Congress and choose three of the presidential candidates who ‚Äčwill contest elections this May, a vote that will shape the next ‚ÄĆpresident's ability to push through legislation and fulfill their agenda.
Voters will choose from over 3,000 candidates to fill 102 Senate seats and 182 House seats, in an election analysts predict will ‚Äčbe divided among some two dozen parties, likely forcing the next president ‚Äčto form a coalition government.
Some 41.2 million eligible voters will also ‚Ā†be able to participate in consultations through which some right-wing, centrist and left-wing ‚Äčparties will select presidential candidates. Several leading candidates - including leftist Ivan Cepeda, right-winger Abelardo ‚ÄčDe La Espriella and centrist Sergio Fajardo - will not participate in Sunday's primaries.
"It is very important to come and exercise the right to vote. The most important thing is for Colombia to ‚Äčdecide its future and for the results to be respected," said Federico Rodriguez, ‚Äča 32-year-old business administrator, after voting in Bogota's north.
"It is a source of pride that we ‚ÄĆcan ‚Ā†go out and exercise our right to vote and to democracy, but I also have uncertainty about the results, about knowing what Colombia's future will be in the next four years," said university student Isabella Suarez, 21.
The polls are open from 8 ‚Äča.m. local time (1300 ‚ÄčGMT) until 4 ‚Ā†p.m. (2100 GMT).
President Gustavo Petro, whose term ends in August, has repeatedly questioned the software that will be used for the vote ‚Äčcount in the elections, alleging possible irregularities, while National Registrar ‚ÄčHernan Penagos ‚Ā†has guaranteed the transparency of the vote and said party observers can verify the software's results.
Some 246,000 members of the military forces and the national police have been placed ‚Ā†on ‚Äčhigh alert to prevent attacks by illegal armed ‚Äčgroups seeking to disrupt the election or pressure voters to cast their ballots for certain candidates, Defense Minister ‚ÄčPedro Sanchez said this week.

item.title: Zelenskiy says Ukraine has unique drone experience, discusses joint production with Dutch PM
item.description:
KYIV, March 8 (Reuters) - Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said he and Dutch Prime Minister Rob Jetten ‚Äčdiscussed joint arms production during his visit to Kyiv on ‚ÄĆSunday, and he stressed Ukraine's unique experience in defending against Iranian-made drones used by Russia.
"It is important that we are producing weapons together with ‚Äčthe Netherlands ‚Äď and we will certainly continue and expand this ‚Äčjoint work," Zelenskiy said on X, adding they had ‚Ā†discussed investments and possible production volumes in detail.
In recent months, Ukraine ‚Äčhas stepped up efforts to establish joint arms production with European ‚Äčcountries, opening several factories to manufacture drones.
As Iran launches drones at U.S. allies across the Gulf and beyond in response to intense U.S. and Israeli strikes, Ukraine ‚Äčis hoping the Middle East crisis will increase its leverage ‚Äčwith allies by showing them that the expertise it has built during four ‚ÄĆyears ‚Ā†of Russia's full-scale invasion could be invaluable to partners' own long-term security.
Zelenskiy said that Kyiv has unique experience in combating drones, which Iran actively uses in its attacks on Middle Eastern countries.
He said ‚Äčthat Ukraine is ready ‚Äčto sell ‚Ā†the interceptor drones that are not used by the Ukrainian military, and the first group of Ukrainian ‚Äčmilitary instructors will head to the Middle East ‚Äčon Monday.
"They ‚Ā†are coming with the means to help," Zelenskiy noted, adding that Ukraine would provide the expertise and experience in defending against "Shahed" model drones ‚Ā†and cruise ‚Äčmissiles.
Ukrainian manufacturers of cheap interceptor drones ‚Äčdesigned to knock out enemy unmanned aerial vehicles have said they have the capacity to export ‚Äčin large volumes.

item.title: Two killed, 12 injured after projectile fell on residential location in Saudi Arabia
item.description:
CAIRO, March 8 (Reuters) - Two people were ‚Äčkilled and 12 ‚ÄĆinjured after a projectile fell on ‚Äča residential ‚Äčlocation in Saudi Arabia's ‚Ā†Al-Kharj city ‚Äčon Sunday, the ‚ÄčSaudi Civil Defense said.
The two people killed ‚Äčwere of ‚ÄčIndian and Bangladeshi nationalities, ‚ÄĆit ‚Ā†added in a post on X.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards ‚Äčsaid ‚Äčearlier ‚Ā†on Sunday that they ‚Äčtargeted radar ‚Äčsystems ‚Ā†in locations including Saudi's Al-Kharj.

item.title: Israeli strike kills two Palestinians in a car in Gaza, medics say
item.description:
CAIRO, March 8 (Reuters) - An Israeli air strike killed at least two Palestinians in Gaza City on Sunday, local health officials ‚Äčsaid, the deadliest incident in Gaza since Israel and ‚ÄĆthe United States launched their war against Iran a week ago.
Medics said the two people were travelling in a car near Al-Azhar University ‚Äčin western Gaza City. Their identities were not immediately ‚Äčclear. Several other people in the area were ‚Ā†wounded in the strike, the health officials added.
Such attacks have ‚Äčdeclined since the start of the U.S.-Israeli campaign against Iran, ‚Äčalthough Israeli forces have killed several Palestinians over the past week.
The Israeli military did not immediately respond to a request for comment about ‚ÄčSunday's strike.
Israel and Hamas agreed to a U.S.-brokered Gaza ‚Äčceasefire deal that kicked off last October, but violence has continued on ‚ÄĆa ‚Ā†near-daily basis. Both sides have blamed the other for the violation of the truce agreement.
The Gaza health ministry said at least 640 Palestinians have been killed by Israeli fire ‚Äčsince October. Israel ‚Äčsays four ‚Ā†soldiers have been killed by militants in Gaza over the same period.
Gaza has been devastated ‚Äčby more than two years of an ‚ÄčIsraeli onslaught ‚Ā†that killed over 72,000 Palestinians, according to local health authorities, and left much of the enclave in ruins.
The war was ‚Ā†sparked ‚Äčby Hamas' October 7, 2023 attack ‚Äčon Israel, where the militants killed 1,200 people and took over 250 hostages, ‚Äčaccording to Israeli tallies.

item.title: Iran's hardline succession challenged by shrinking loyalist base
item.description:
DUBAI, March 8 (Reuters) - Iran's next leader faces a massive external assault and growing internal anger, and while previous leaders relied on a hardcore of diehard ideologues in the population, it is far from clear how far his ‚Äčsuccessor can do the same.
Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed by U.S. and Israeli strikes a week ago and his hardline son Mojtaba Khamenei is seen as a frontrunner ‚ÄĆto succeed him.
Yet whether loyalists remain as numerous or as committed as in earlier decades - and whether they would rally behind a figure like Mojtaba - is increasingly uncertain.
Reuters interviews with three Basij members, as well as ordinary Iranians, officials, insiders and political analysts, point to a much narrower support base than the Islamic Republic once enjoyed.
"The strategy in choosing a hardliner as the new leader would be to consolidate the base, but they're ending up with an ‚Äčincreasingly small circle of supporters," said Ali Ansari, a modern history professor at the University of St Andrews in the UK.
"And the longer this goes on, the more it will ‚Äčall fray at the edges," he said.
The Islamic Republic emerged from a 1979 revolution backed by millions of Iranians. But decades of rule marked by corruption, ‚Ā†repression and mismanagement have thinned that support, alienating many ordinary people.
Still, a core of loyalists remains ‚ÄĒ people who repeatedly show up at the ballot box to back the Islamic system and who ‚Äčturn out on the streets to crush opposition protests.
Highly organised and able to mobilise quickly, they still pose a major obstacle to any U.S. or Israeli hopes of effecting regime change.
"We have given many ‚Äčmartyrs. They have sacrificed themselves for our leader. Now we must show that the path of the leader Khamenei continues. We will solve any problems and support whoever is chosen as leader. We will even give our lives for him," said Mahdi Rastegari, 32, a religion teacher and member of the Basij, an official volunteer militia.
In the last presidential election, the most hardline candidate, Saeed Jalili, won around 9 million votes in the first round ‚Äčand 13 million in the second, according to official results. Over 61 million of Iran's more than 85 million people were eligible to vote that year.
However, the hardliners' apparent status as a small ‚Äčminority in Iran does not give much hope to all those who hope for change, and the continued bombardment has given rise to fears of chaos.
"The Guards and the system are still powerful. They have tens ‚ÄĆof thousands of ‚Ā†forces ready to fight to keep this regime in place. We, the people, have nothing," said Babak, 34, a businessman in Arak who asked to keep his family name secret.
With their leader killed on the first day of the war and cracks emerging within the country's hierarchy, the extent of continued hardline support for the Islamic Republic will now be tested like never before.
Men like the Basij member Rastegari represent a network of power extending from the supreme leader's now bombed-out office in central Tehran to every village and city neighbourhood that stands in the way of any internal ‚Äčopposition.
Every night since Khamenei's death, hardliners have held ‚Äčstate-backed mourning ceremonies for him despite the bombs ‚Ā†raining down across the country.
Among their number are true believers ready to die as martyrs for their fervent belief in rule by a divinely guided cleric, and those with more mercenary motives who have benefited from their status as public supporters of the system.
Another Basij member, Ali Mohammad Hosseini, goes ‚Äčfrom work at his father's grocery shop in the Shi'ite Muslim seminary city of Qom to spend his evenings manning checkpoints to deter ‚Äčany flashes of public dissent.
"The ‚Ā†most important issue is preserving the regime, which is what the Americans are targeting," the 29-year-old said, saying he would support whichever cleric replaced Khamenei as a "religious duty" that he was prepared to die for.
That degree of commitment is not universal, however. Another Basij member, who asked to give only his first name Hassan, and his location in the Shi'ite shrine city of Mashhad, said he had doubts the Islamic Republic ‚Ā†would survive.
"We ‚Äčneed to be realistic," he said, pointing to continued U.S. pressure and the ruinous aftermath of pulverising airstrikes if a ‚Äčhardliner like Mojtaba Khamenei is named as the new leader.
Members of the Basij and others who demonstrate loyalty to the system have for decades enjoyed privileges including preferential places at university, job offers and subsidised bank loans - but a collapsing economy ‚Äčcould end such perks.
"We do not even have airports any more. No ports. How are they going to rebuild this economy?" Hassan, 29, said.

item.title: Syria's Kurds caution Iran's Kurds against aligning with US against Tehran
item.description:
QAMISHLI, Syria March 8 (Reuters) - Kurdish residents of northeast ‚ÄčSyria warned Iran's Kurds against aligning with the U.S. to fight the Iranian government, citing their own experience in Syria in recent ‚ÄĆmonths as evidence their Iranian counterparts would be "abandoned."
Iranian Kurdish militias based in northern Iraq have consulted with the United States in recent days about whether and how to attack security forces in western Iran, as the United States and Israel pound Iran with air strikes , Reuters has reported .
But Syria's Kurds warned their Iranian counterparts against partnering with Washington.
"I hope that the Kurds of ‚ÄčIran will not ally themselves with America, because they will abandon them," said Saad Ali, a 45-year-old resident of the northeastern Syrian Kurdish ‚Äčtown of Qamishli.
"Tomorrow, if an agreement is made between them (the U.S.) and the Iranians, they will eliminate you. Do not ‚Ā†make our mistakes," he told Reuters.
Syrian Kurdish fighters aligned with the U.S. more than a decade ago to fight the Islamic State group, setting up ‚Äčtheir own semi-autonomous zone in the territory they had seized from the ultraconservative Islamist fighters.
But in January, Syria's new army under President Ahmed al-Sharaa captured most of ‚Äčthe Kurdish-held areas in a sweeping offensive. Syria's Kurds called on the U.S. to intervene on their behalf, and felt betrayed when Washington instead urged them to merge with Sharaa's forces.
'A NEGATIVE EXPERIENCE' WITH THE U.S.
It remains a bitter experience for Syria's Kurds and a lesson they say should be heeded by Iranian Kurds.
"In my opinion, the Kurds in Iran should ‚Äčmaintain a firm stance: they will not engage in any wars within Iranian territory without firm, signed guarantees from the United States regarding the future of ‚Äčthese Kurdish regions in Iran," said Amjad Kardo, a 26-year-old Syrian Kurd in Qamishli.
"We Kurds here in Syria, in particular, have had a negative experience with the Americans in ‚ÄĆSyria, and ‚Ā†their abandonment of Kurdish resistance movements."
An Iranian Kurdish source said Kurdish leaders did have concerns about being "betrayed" like the Kurdish groups in northern Syria.
The source said Iranian Kurdish leaders had requested guarantees from the U.S., without saying what they were.
U.S. President Donald Trump told Reuters on Thursday it would be "wonderful" if Kurdish forces crossed the border from northern Iraq into Iran, but declined to answer a question on whether the U.S. would offer them air support if they did so.
On ‚ÄčSaturday, he appeared to switch positions, ‚Äčtelling reporters he doesn't want Kurdish ‚Ā†fighters going into Iran.
Ahmed Barakat, head of the Kurdish Progressive Democratic Party in Syria, told Reuters that Iranian Kurdish forces should exercise "extreme caution".
Barakat said the decision was ultimately up to them, but he believed that "accepting the ‚Äčinvitation of the United States and being considered the spearhead in confronting or weakening the Iranian regime is ‚Äčnot, at present, in ‚Ā†the best interest of the Kurds of Iran."
Israel has been holding its own talks with Iranian Kurdish insurgent groups based in the semi-autonomous region of Iraqi Kurdistan for around a year, Reuters reported last week .
The Kurds are an ethnic group which was left stateless a century ago when the borders of the modern Middle East emerged from the ‚Ā†collapsing Ottoman ‚ÄčEmpire.
Mostly Sunni Muslims, they speak a language related to Farsi and are concentrated in a mountainous ‚Äčregion straddling the borders of Armenia, Iraq, Iran, Syria and Turkey.
In Iraq, they inhabit three northern provinces led by their own regional government. But in other countries - Iran, Turkey and now Syria - ‚Äčtheir dreams of an autonomous region or state have remained out of reach.

item.title: Brazil President Lula's annual medical checkup test results are normal
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 8 (Reuters) - Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, ‚Äčknown as Lula, underwent ‚ÄĆhis annual medical checkup on Saturday morning at Sao Paulo‚Äôs Sirio-Libanes ‚ÄčHospital and all test ‚Äčresults were normal, the hospital ‚Ā†said on Sunday.
Lula will receive ‚Äčroutine followup care with his ‚Äčmedical team, led by cardiologist Roberto Kalil Filho and physician Ana Helena ‚ÄčGermoglio, the hospital said ‚Äčin a statement, adding there are currently ‚ÄĆno ‚Ā†plans for additional exams.
Lula, 80, said in 2025 that he plans to seek reelection later ‚Äčthis year. ‚ÄčIn ‚Ā†2024, he underwent two operations to relieve and ‚Äčprevent bleeding in his ‚Äčskull. ‚Ā†He has been Brazil's president two times - from 2003 to ‚Ā†2010, ‚Äčand again since ‚ÄčJanuary 2023.

item.title: Russia says units intercept 170 Ukrainian drones over four hours
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - Russia's Defence Ministry said on Sunday ‚Äčits air defence units ‚ÄĆhad intercepted 170 drones over various parts ‚Äčof central and ‚Äčsouthern Russia over a ‚Ā†four-hour period, including two ‚Äčdrones headed for ‚ÄčMoscow.
No damage or casualties were reported.
The ministry figures, higher ‚Äčthan average, covered ‚Äčthe period of 2 p.m. ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†6 p.m. (1100-1500 GMT). The highest number - 73 - was recorded in Bryansk ‚Äčregion ‚Äčbordering ‚Ā†Ukraine.
Moscow Mayor Sergei Sobyanin, writing on ‚ÄčTelegram, also reported ‚Äčtwo ‚Ā†drones downed while heading for the capital, ‚Ā†with ‚Äčemergency crews ‚Äčexamining fragments at the point ‚Äčof impact.

item.title: US considers idea of special operation to seize Iran‚Äôs uranium, Bloomberg News reports
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - U.S. President ‚ÄčDonald Trump ‚ÄĆis weighing the ‚Äčoption of ‚Äčdeploying special forces ‚Ā†on ‚Äčthe ground ‚Äčto seize Iran‚Äôs near-bomb-grade ‚Äčuranium, ‚ÄčBloomberg News reported ‚ÄĆon ‚Ā†Sunday, citing three diplomatic officials ‚Äčbriefed ‚Äčon ‚Ā†the matter.
Reuters ‚Äčcould ‚Äčnot ‚Ā†immediately verify the ‚Ā†report.

item.title: German Greens lead Chancellor Merz's conservatives in state election
item.description:
BERLIN, March 8 (Reuters) - Germany's environmental Greens are set to win a state election in the southern region of Baden-Wuerttemberg, leaving them poised to continue a ‚Äčcoalition with Chancellor Friedrich Merz's conservatives, initial forecasts showed after polls ‚ÄĆclosed on Sunday.
The far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD) party, which has made strong gains in recent years, came third, confirming its position as Germany's main opposition party, even outside its heartland in ‚Äčthe former communist eastern states.
The first forecasts on public broadcaster ARD showed ‚Äčthe Greens in first place with 32% of the vote, followed ‚Ā†by Merz's Christian Democrats (CDU) on 29% and the AfD on 17.5%.
Merz's coalition partners ‚Äčin Berlin, the centre-left Social Democrats were on course for just 5.5% of ‚Äčthe vote, confirming the precipitous drop in support seen in recent years but just scraping past the minimum threshold to enter state parliament.
Baden-Wuerttemberg, home to Mercedes-Benz (MBGn.DE) , opens new tab , Porsche (P911_p.DE) , opens new tab , Bosch (ROBG.UL) and one of the ‚Äčtraditional strongholds of the German car industry, is one of Germany's most prosperous ‚Äčregions.
But its auto sector and wider economy have been hit hard by increasing competition from ‚ÄĆChina's surging ‚Ā†electric vehicles makers.
After a decade of Green-CDU coalition government in Baden-Wurttemberg, the moderate Green candidate for state premier, Cem Ozdemir is unlikely to make life difficult for Merz in the Bundesrat, the upper house of parliament that represents the states ‚Äčin Germany's federal system.
But ‚Äčthe result underlines ‚Ā†Merz's struggles to cut through with voters, as promised reforms have stalled and a sluggish economy comes back slowly from ‚Äčtwo years in recession.
Initially trailing in the race, the Greens ‚Äčovertook the ‚Ā†Christian Democrats as the ballot approached and the result is likely to fuel discontent among conservatives already alarmed at the record low approval levels for Merz's government in ‚Ā†Berlin.
The Baden-Wuerttemberg ‚Äčelection was the first of five state elections ‚Äčthis year, with voters in neighbouring Rhineland-Palatinate due to go to the polls on March 22, followed ‚Äčby Berlin, Saxony-Anhalt and Mecklenberg-Vorpommern in September.

item.title: Iraqi oil production collapses with Strait of Hormuz blocked by conflict, sources say
item.description:
BAGHDAD, March 8 (Reuters) - Iraqi oil ‚ÄĆproduction from its main southern oilfields has fallen by 70% to just 1.3 million barrels per day as the country is unable to export oil via the Strait of Hormuz due to ‚Äčthe Iran war, three industry sources said on Sunday.
Production from the fields ‚Äčstood at around 4.3 million bpd before the war.
"Crude storage has ‚Ā†reached maximum capacity and the remaining output after the major cut will be ‚Äčused to supply the country‚Äôs refineries," said an official with the state‚ÄĎrun Basra Oil ‚ÄčCompany (BOC), which manages production and export operations from the southern fields.
The Strait of Hormuz, a narrow, strategically vital waterway between the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman, is one of the ‚Äčworld's key oil transit chokepoints, carrying roughly a fifth of global oil and ‚Äčliquefied natural gas flows.
The OPEC member's exports also fell sharply to an average of around 800,000 ‚ÄĆbarrels ‚Ā†per day on Sunday, with only two tankers loading because vessels cannot move freely through the strait to Iraq's southern terminals, the source said.
With no new tankers able to reach Iraq‚Äôs southern terminals, exports from there are expected to halt completely ‚Äčat around 8 p.m. ‚Äčlocal time (1700 GMT), ‚Ā†said two oil officials with direct knowledge of terminal operations.
Iraq‚Äôs oil exports from the southern oilfields stood at 3.334 million ‚Äčbarrels per day in February, an oil ministry document showed.
A ‚Äčdrop in ‚Ā†Iraq's oil production and exports is set to strain the country‚Äôs already fragile finances, as the state relies on crude sales for nearly all public spending and more than ‚Ā†90% ‚Äčof its income.
"This is the most serious operational threat ‚ÄčIraq has faced in more than 20 years," a senior Iraqi oil ministry official said.

item.title: Iranian army says at least 104 killed in US attack on Iranian warship last week
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - The Iranian army said on ‚ÄčSunday that at ‚ÄĆleast 104 people were killed and 32 ‚Äčwere wounded ‚Äčin an attack by ‚Ā†the U.S. on ‚Äčan Iranian warship ‚Äčoff Sri Lanka's coast last week.
A U.S. submarine sank ‚Äčthe frigate Dena ‚Äčin the Indian Ocean ‚ÄĆabout ‚Ā†19 nautical miles off Sri Lanka's southern port city ‚Äčof Galle ‚Äčon ‚Ā†Wednesday, killing dozens of ‚Äčsailors and dramatically ‚Äčwidening ‚Ā†Washington's pursuit of the Iranian navy.

item.title: New York City police identify device outside Mamdani's home as explosive
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - New York City Police said on Sunday a device that was ignited and thrown during protests outside Mayor Zohran Mamdani‚Äôs home over the weekend was an explosive that ‚Äčcould have caused serious injury or death.
The device, a jar filled with nuts, ‚ÄĆbolts and screws and wrapped in black tape with a fuse, was thrown by a counterprotester on Saturday outside Gracie Mansion, but it extinguished itself before any explosion, according to a statement from New York ‚ÄčCity Police Commissioner Jessica Tisch. Two people were in custody, Tisch said.
The device was ‚Äčone of two that were thrown during the protests, which were led ‚Ā†by two opposing groups, according to police. The second device was still being examined, Tisch ‚Äčsaid.
Far-right activist Jake Lang led a protest on Saturday outside Gracie Mansion - where Mamdani lives ‚Äčwith his wife - against a purported Islamic ‚Äútakeover‚ÄĚ of New York City and against public prayer by Muslims. Tisch said at a press conference on Saturday that she did not believe Mamdani and his wife were home ‚Äčat the time.
In a statement on Sunday, Mamdani condemned Lang‚Äôs protest but said the violence ‚Äčthat followed it was more disturbing.
‚ÄúViolence at a protest is never acceptable,‚ÄĚ Mamdani said. ‚ÄúThe attempt to use ‚ÄĆan explosive ‚Ā†device and hurt others is not only criminal, it is reprehensible and the antithesis of who we are.‚ÄĚ
Lang‚Äôs protest, which consisted of about 20 people, was opposed by a far larger counterprotest of 125 people aimed at running ‚ÄúNazis‚ÄĚ out of New York, Tisch said at a ‚Äčpress conference on Saturday. ‚ÄčPolice said Emir ‚Ā†Balat, 18, was among the counterprotesters before he lit and threw the device.
The device rolled near police before it extinguished itself, Tisch said ‚Äčon Saturday. Balat ran after throwing it and eventually lit and dropped ‚Äča second ‚Ā†device in the street, according to Tisch.
Balat and another man, whom police on Sunday identified as Ibrahim Kayumi, were arrested at the scene, Tisch said. New York police are working with the ‚Ā†FBI and ‚Äčthe U.S. Department of Justice on the investigation.
Tisch said ‚Äčon Saturday there was no immediate indication the incident was related to the U.S. attack on Iran , but that authorities ‚Äčwere still investigating.

item.title: Iran names Khamenei's son, Mojtaba, as new supreme leader, state media report
item.description:
DUBAI, March 8 (Reuters) - Iran's Assembly ‚Äčof Experts has named ‚ÄĆMojtaba Khamenei to replace his father, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, as ‚Äčthe country's new ‚Äčsupreme leader, state media reported ‚Ā†on Sunday.
Mojtaba , a mid-ranking ‚Äčcleric with close ties ‚Äčto the powerful Revolutionary Guards, had long been viewed by elements ‚Äčof Iran's ruling ‚Äčestablishment as a potential successor to ‚ÄĆhis ‚Ā†father, who was killed after the United States and Israel launched strikes on ‚ÄčIran.
Although ‚ÄčIran's ‚Ā†ruling ideology frowns on the principle of hereditary ‚Äčsuccession, he has ‚Äča ‚Ā†powerful following within the Guards and his dead father's ‚Ā†still-influential ‚Äčoffice.

item.title: Iran defies Trump, elevates Khamenei's son Mojtaba as successor
item.description:
DUBAI, March 9 (Reuters) - Iran's clerical leadership chose confrontation over compromise in appointing Mojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father, Ali Khamenei, a move regional officials say is a direct rebuke to U.S. President Donald Trump, who had declared the son "unacceptable".
Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was ‚Äčkilled in a U.S.-Israeli strike at the start of the conflict, now in its second week .
The appointment of Mojtaba as his successor by the Assembly of Experts locks hardliners firmly ‚ÄĆin control in Tehran - a gamble that could reshape Iran's war with the U.S. and Israel and reverberate far beyond the Middle East.
"Having Mojtaba take over is the same playbook," said Alex Vatanka, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute.
"It's a big humiliation for the United States to carry out an operation of this scale, risk so much, and end up killing an 86-year-old man, only to have him replaced by his hardline son.‚ÄĚ
Under Iran's complex, theocratic system, the supreme ‚Äčleader is the ultimate authority, including over foreign policy and Iran‚Äôs nuclear programme, as well as guiding the elected president and parliament.
Analysts say the choice ‚Äčof Mojtaba, a deeply hardline cleric whose wife, mother and other family members were also killed in U.S.‚ÄďIsraeli strikes - sends an unequivocal message: Iran‚Äôs leadership ‚Ā†has rejected any prospect of compromise to preserve the system and sees no path forward except confrontation, revenge and endurance.
According to insiders, Mojtaba will face immense internal and external strain from a disaffected population and ‚Äčan escalating conflict, but is expected to move swiftly to consolidate power.
That will likely mean expanded authority for the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, harsher domestic controls and sweeping repression to crush dissent.
"The world will miss ‚Äčthe era of his father," a regional official close to Tehran told Reuters. "Mojtaba will have no choice but to show an iron fist... even if the war ends, there will be severe internal repression."
That stance comes after months of deepening domestic unrest - the bloodiest since the 1979 Islamic Revolution - that had already weakened the Islamic Republic before the war began.
Iran was grappling with a battered economy, soaring inflation, currency collapse and widening poverty, alongside tightening repression that had fuelled public anger and ‚Äčprotests - pressures now likely to intensify under wartime rule.
Difficult days lie ahead under Mojtaba, with far tighter internal controls, intensified pressure at home and an even more aggressive, hostile posture abroad, said ‚Äčanother Iranian insider familiar with the situation on the ground.
Paul Salem, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, said Mojtaba was not a figure positioned to strike a deal with the United States or pivot diplomatically.
‚ÄúNobody emerging now ‚ÄĆis going ‚Ā†to be able to compromise,‚ÄĚ Salem said. ‚ÄúThis is a hardline choice, made in a hardline moment.‚ÄĚ
In the eyes of Iran‚Äôs clerics, many of whom famously label America the "Great Satan", the assassination of Khamenei, the Islamic Republic's highest religious authority, has elevated him to "martyrdom".
Clerics have cast the slain leader as a heroic figure, likening him to Imam Hussein - the Shi‚Äôite symbol of sacrifice and resistance against oppression.
"Mojtaba is even worse and more hardline than his father," said Alan Eyre, former U.S. diplomat and Iran specialist, adding that he was the preferred candidate of the Guards. "He's going to have a lot of revenge to exact."
That calculus carries risks. Israel ‚Äčhas warned that any successor to Khamenei would also ‚Äčbe a target, while Trump has said ‚Ā†the war may only end once Iran‚Äôs military leadership and ruling elite are eliminated .
A powerful mid‚ÄĎranking cleric, Mojtaba, 56, has long opposed reformist groups advocating engagement with the West. His close ties to senior clerics and the IRGC - which dominates Iran‚Äôs security forces and its economy - give him leverage ‚Äčacross the state‚Äôs political and coercive security institutions.
He amassed influence under his father as a key figure within the security apparatus and the vast business ‚Äčempire it controls, operating for ‚Ā†years as Ali Khamenei‚Äôs gatekeeper and, in practice, a ‚Äúmini-supreme leader‚ÄĚ, analysts say.
His elevation comes as the U.S.-Israeli campaign against Iran intensifies, with joint strikes hitting fuel depots and other targets inside Iran, while Iranian missiles and drones have struck Gulf states, widening the conflict.
