"Short term oil prices, which will drop rapidly when the destruction of the Iran nuclear threat is over, ‚Äčis a very small price to pay for U.S.A., and World, Safety and Peace," Trump posted on Truth Social on Sunday night. "ONLY FOOLS WOULD THINK DIFFERENTLY!"
Oil jumped 25% , with ‚ÄĆBrent ‚Ā†on track for a record one-day gain, after Iran on Monday named Mojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father Ali Khamenei as supreme leader, while OPEC producers Kuwait and Iraq cut oil output during the weekend as the crucial Strait of Hormuz remained effectively shut.
Across Asia, which sources 60% of its oil from the Middle East, stock prices slid and the dollar rose, with officials looking to contain the impact ‚Äčof oil prices, including weighing the ‚Äčrelease of strategic reserves, as ‚Ā†worries mounted that disruption in energy supplies could be prolonged.
Iran on Monday named Mojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father Ali Khamenei as supreme leader, a move expected to draw the ire of Trump. Weekend attacks on Iranian ‚Äčoil storage facilities fueled fears of retaliatory strikes on energy facilities.
"Oil prices have now gathered all the ‚Äčingredients for a ‚Ā†perfect storm - Middle East Gulf producers cutting output, the prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz ... all compounded by a growing pessimism about a quick turnaround in the current situation," said Kpler senior oil analyst Muyu Xu.
Iraq cut oil production from its main southern oilfields by 70% to 1.3 million barrels per ‚Ā†day, three ‚Äčindustry sources said on Sunday, while Kuwait Petroleum Corp began cutting oil output on Saturday ‚Äčand declared force majeure.
No.2 LNG exporter Qatar has already halted exports of the superchilled fuel, and analysts predict that the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia will also have to ‚Äčcut output soon as they run out of oil storage due to the Strait of Hormuz closure.

item.title: Saudi Aramco offers crude in rare tenders as Iran conflict disrupts exports, traders say
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 9 (Reuters) - Saudi Aramco has offered ‚Äčmore than 2 ‚ÄĆmillion barrels of Saudi crude in ‚Äčrare tenders ‚Äčas the U.S.-Iran conflict ‚Ā†disrupted exports from ‚Äčthe Middle East, ‚Äčtrade sources said.
The state company offered 2 ‚Äčmillion barrels of ‚ÄčArab Heavy crude for ‚ÄĆMarch ‚Ā†loading in one of the tenders it issued last ‚Äčweek. ‚ÄčIt ‚Ā†also offered 650,000 barrels ‚Äčof Arab Light ‚Äčcrude ‚Ā†in a separate tender, the ‚Ā†people ‚Äčsaid.

item.title: Bahrain's Bapco announces force majeure on operations
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Bapco Energies declared ‚Äčforce majeure ‚ÄĆon its group operations ‚Äčon Monday ‚Äčfollowing a recent ‚Ā†attack on ‚Äčits refinery ‚Äčcomplex, the company said.
Bapco said ‚Äčthat all ‚Äčdomestic market needs ‚ÄĆremain ‚Ā†fully secured and supplies will continue ‚Äčwithout ‚Äčdisruption, ‚Ā†supported by proactive ‚Äčplans ‚Äčin ‚Ā†place.

item.title: Oil pares gains but still on track for record surge
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil prices came off earlier highs on Monday but were still up more than 15% at levels not seen since ‚Äčmid-2022 as some major producers cut supplies and fears of prolonged shipping disruptions gripped the market due to the expanding U.S.-Israeli war with Iran .
Brent crude futures were up $15.51, ‚ÄĆor 16.7%, at $108.20 per barrel at 0642 GMT - on track for the biggest-ever jump in a single day, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude futures were up $14.23, or 15.7%, at $105.13.
Disruptions in tanker movements and rising security risks have already slowed shipping activity, and left Asian buyers reliant on Middle Eastern crude especially vulnerable because the crisis is unfolding around the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly one-fifth of the world's oil supply passes.
WTI surged 31.4% ‚Äčto a session high of $119.48 a barrel earlier on Monday, while Brent rose as much as 29% to $119.50 a barrel. Before the surge on Monday, Brent had already climbed ‚Äč27% and WTI by 35.6% last week.
Prices pared gains after the Financial Times reported that the Group of Seven (G7) finance ministers and the International ‚Ā†Energy Agency will discuss on Monday a joint emergency oil reserves release , and Saudi Aramco offered prompt crude supply through a series of rare tenders .
""Unless oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz resume ‚Äčsoon and regional tensions ease, upward pressure on prices is likely to persist," said Vasu Menon, managing director for investment strategy at OCBC in Singapore.
Iraq and Kuwait have begun cutting oil output, adding ‚Äčto earlier liquefied natural gas reductions from Qatar, as the war blocked shipments from the Middle East. Analysts expect the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia will have to also cut output soon as they run out of oil storage.
Refinery disruptions continued due to escalating tensions in the region, with Bahrain's BAPCO announcing a force majeure following a recent attack on its refinery complex.
Fujairah Media Office said a fire broke out in the UAE's Fujairah oil ‚Äčindustry zone resulting from debris falling, with no injuries reported. Saudi Arabia's Defence Ministry said on X it intercepted a drone heading to the Shaybah oilfield.
Also boosting prices is the appointment of Mojtaba Khamenei ‚Äčto succeed his father Ali Khamenei as Iran's supreme leader, signalling that hardliners remain firmly in charge in Tehran a week into its conflict with the United States and Israel.
"With the appointment of the late leader's ‚ÄĆson as ‚Ā†Iran's new leader, U.S. President Donald Trump's goal of regime change in Iran has become more difficult," said Satoru Yoshida, a commodity analyst with Rakuten Securities.
"That view accelerated buying, as Iran is expected to continue its closure of the Strait of Hormuz and attacks on other oil-producing nations' facilities, as seen last week," he said, predicting WTI could rise to $120 and then $130 a barrel in a relatively short period.
The war could leave consumers and businesses worldwide facing weeks or months of higher fuel prices even if the week-old conflict ends quickly, as suppliers ‚Äčgrapple with damaged facilities, disrupted logistics and elevated ‚Äčrisks to shipping.
"The next flag will be ‚Ā†whether it eventually gets to a point where they have to start shutting in oil wells, which not only impacts output even further, it delays a response once the conflict eases as well. That would potentially sustain those prices for much longer," said Daniel Hynes, senior commodity strategist at ‚ÄčANZ.
Iraqi oil production from its main southern oilfields has fallen by 70% to just 1.3 million barrels per day as the country is unable ‚Äčto export oil via ‚Ā†the Strait of Hormuz due to the Iran war, three industry sources said on Sunday. Crude storage has reached maximum capacity, said an official with the state-run Basra Oil Company.
Kuwait Petroleum Corporation began cutting oil output on Saturday and declared force majeure on shipments, though it did not say how much production it would shut.
Israel's military has threatened to kill any replacement for the deceased Ali Khamenei, while Trump said the ‚Ā†war might only ‚Äčend once Iran's military and rulers had been wiped out.
Meanwhile, as oil prices surged, U.S. Senate Democratic Leader Chuck ‚ÄčSchumer called on Trump to release oil from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve.
"President Trump should release oil from the SPR now to stabilize markets, bring prices down, and stop the price shock that American families are already feeling thanks to his ‚Äčreckless war," Schumer said in a statement.

item.title: Players' union raises alarm over safety of Iran women's soccer team after Asian Cup exit
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 9 (Reuters) - Global players' union FIFPRO said on Monday there were serious concerns for the welfare of the ‚ÄčIranian women's soccer team, as they prepared to return home after being labelled "wartime traitors" for refusing to ‚ÄĆsing their national anthem before an Asian Cup match.
The Iranians' campaign in the Australian-hosted tournament started last weekend just as the U.S. and Israel launched air strikes on Iran, killing the Islamic Republic's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei.
The team was eliminated on Sunday after losing 2-0 to the Philippines. Fans waved the pre-1979 Iranian ‚Äčflag, booed the national anthem and tried to prevent the team coach from leaving afterwards, chanting "Save our girls!" ‚Äčamid concerns for their safety after their anthem silence.
More than 66,000 people have also signed a petition calling ‚Ā†on the Australian government to ensure the players, who are on the Gold Coast in Queensland, do not leave "while credible fears for ‚Äčtheir safety remain".
Beau Busch, FIFPRO president for Asia and Oceania, said the union had been unable to contact the players to discuss ‚Äčwhether they would like to seek asylum in Australia.
"The reality at the moment is that we're unable to get in touch with the players. That's incredibly concerning. That's not a new thing. That's really been since the repression really dialled up in February, January," Busch told reporters.
"So ‚Äčwe're really concerned about the players, but our responsibility right now is to do everything within our power to try and make ‚Äčsure that they're safe."
Busch said the organisation was working with FIFA, the Asian Football Confederation and the Australian government to ensure that "every bit ‚ÄĆof pressure ‚Ā†is applied" to protect the players and give them "agency around what happens next".
"It's a really challenging situation," he said.
"There may be players that want to return. There may be some players within the group that would like to seek asylum and would like to stay in Australia for longer."
The players' decision to stand in silence during Iran's anthem before their first ‚Äčmatch against South Korea was ‚Äčlabelled by a commentator ‚Ā†on Islamic Republic of Iran Broadcasting as the "pinnacle of dishonour".
"Traitors during wartime must be dealt with more severely," presenter Mohammad Reza Shahbazi said.
When they sang the anthem and saluted before their second ‚Äčmatch against Australia, it sparked fears among human rights campaigners the team had been coerced ‚Äčby government minders.
Iran ‚Ā†coach Marziyeh Jafari has said they were keen to return home. "We are very impatiently waiting to return," she said during a post-match press conference.
Most of the airspace in the Middle East remains closed as a result of the war.
When asked about whether Australia would grant the ‚Ā†players ‚Äčasylum, Matt Thistlethwaite, the assistant minister for foreign affairs and trade, said the ‚Äčgovernment could not "go into individual circumstances for privacy reasons".
"Anyone that seeks to come to Australia or applies for a visa obviously must meet the conditions, but ‚ÄčI can't go into the specifics of details," he told Sky News.

item.title: Synagogue in Belgian city of Liege damaged in explosion, media reports
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - A synagogue in ‚Äčthe Belgian city of Liege ‚ÄĆwas damaged by an explosion early on Monday, Belgian broadcasters VRT ‚Äčand RTBF reported, citing ‚Äčlocal police.
No injuries were reported. ‚Ā†The cause of the ‚Äčexplosion, which happened around 4 ‚Äča.m. local time (0300 GMT), was unknown, VRT said.
Liege's mayor Willy Demeyer called ‚Äčthe incident an "extremely violent ‚Äčact of antisemitism" in comments made to ‚ÄĆFrench ‚Ā†language public broadcaster RTBF.
The explosion blew out the windows of the synagogue, as well ‚Äčas those ‚Äčof ‚Ā†a building on the opposite side of ‚Äčthe road, the broadcaster ‚Äčsaid.
Police ‚Ā†cordoned off the street while a specialised counter-terrorism team investigated ‚Ā†the ‚Äčincident.
Local police were ‚Äčnot immediately available for comment.

item.title: Wall Street's immunity to Mideast oil shock will now be tested
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil prices surged 30% last week and swathes of global stocks sold off as war in the Middle East escalated, yet an air of complacency hung over Wall Street.
Investors have likely been betting that the turmoil will have a short-lived, mild economic impact, much like most crises in the recent past. Is this ‚Äčan efficient market at work, or wishful thinking? We'll soon find out.
West Texas Intermediate crude rose 35% last week, the biggest weekly rise since the U.S. oil benchmark futures ‚ÄĆwere launched in 1983, yet the S&P 500 (.SPX) , opens new tab fell only 2%. The Nasdaq (.IXIC) , opens new tab dipped just over 1%.
What gives?
Some investors may believe the U.S. is better positioned to handle an energy shock than many of its peers because it is a net energy exporter. A lingering belief that "U.S. exceptionalism" is alive and well could also be at play.
It's also notoriously difficult to price geopolitical risk, so many asset managers may simply be sitting on their hands and waiting for more clarity, particularly those reluctant ‚Äčto let go of their "fundamentally" bullish outlook on corporate America.
Whatever the root, there is a danger that this is misplaced optimism at best, or reckless complacency at worst.
Consider this. In the first ‚Äč41 trading days of 2026, the S&P 500's trading range, as measured by the difference between the highest and lowest daily closing prices, was just 2.7%, ‚Ā†according to The Kobbeissi Letter, a global markets newsletter. That's the narrowest range for that period on record, going back to 1928.
But this will now be tested. Oil is on track for one of its biggest ‚Äčdaily gains in decades , with both Brent and WTI soaring above $100 per barrel on Monday. Prices were up more than 25% at one point during Asian trading hours, though they have retreated slightly.
Other countries' stock markets have ‚Äčbeen rocked. Benchmark European, Asian and emerging market indices, which fell 5-7% last week, are putting up more big losses. Japan's Nikkei is down another 5% today, with Korea's benchmark index falling even further.
Granted, many of these countries are net energy importers, meaning they are more exposed to the historic spikes in oil and natural gas prices than the Unite States. Japan, for example, imports 90% of its energy, with 95% of its oil imports coming from the Middle East.
But can Wall ‚ÄčStreet remain an outlier?
From a U.S. macroeconomic perspective, the Middle East conflict couldn't be more ill-timed. Inflation was already running at 3%, well above the Federal Reserve's 2% target and creeping higher, and payrolls ‚Äčdata on Friday showed that the U.S. economy lost 92,000 jobs in February.
The whiff of stagflation is growing stronger, and Fed officials can only hold their noses for so long. Chair Jerome Powell, and his likely successor Kevin Warsh, ‚ÄĆmay be ‚Ā†damned by Wall Street ‚Äď or, more likely, bond investors ‚Äď if they risk letting inflation get out of control by taking a dovish stance moving forward. But they also risk angering markets if they take a hawkish, anti-growth position that prioritizes price stability.
Yet policy paralysis can't be good either.
Investors appear to be banking that history repeats itself. In recent decades, most bouts of global geopolitical turmoil have sparked a few weeks of mild volatility, then a quick recovery, leaving everyone wondering what all the fuss was about.
Research by Parag Thatte and Binky Chadha at Deutsche Bank shows that geopolitical shocks have had an average negative impact of around 6-8% on U.S. ‚Äčequities over the subsequent three-week period, with markets ‚Äčrecovering those losses within another three weeks.
Larry Adam, ‚Ā†chief investment officer at Raymond James, notes that since the 1990s, the S&P 500 has been higher on average one, three, six, and 12 months after geopolitical shocks.
JPMorgan analysts say the typical scenario around a major geopolitical event is a 5-6% drawdown in stocks, which is then clawed back within a few weeks.
"There is ‚Äča tendency among macro strategists to dismiss geopolitics and oversimplify the response: just buy the dip," they wrote on Friday, adding that this rule of ‚Äčthumb has been true approximately ‚Ā†80% of the time over the past 60 years.
"We think the current episode with the Iran invasion is indeed a buy-the-dip scenario," they added.
Yet the drawdowns appear to be getting shallower, and as of Friday, there was barely a dip to buy.
Are markets getting smarter and better able to look through headline noise now that computers are doing the trading? Maybe the playbook of past crises still applies. Or, has complacency set in, and it really is ‚Ā†different this ‚Äčtime?
The jury is still out, but Wall Street futures are down early on Monday. So a verdict may be imminent.
(The opinions expressed ‚Äčhere are those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the ‚Äčbiggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: South Korea to impose fuel price cap to shield economy from energy shock
item.description:
SEOUL, March 9 (Reuters) - South Korean President Lee Jae Myung said on Monday that authorities would cap ‚ÄĆdomestic fuel prices for the first time in nearly 30 years to contain a spike in prices after the conflict in the Middle East sent global crude prices sharply higher .
Speaking at an emergency meeting on the impact of the Middle East crisis, Lee said the ‚Äčgovernment would "swiftly introduce and boldly implement" a maximum price system on petroleum products "that have recently seen excessive price ‚Äčincreases".
The current crisis "is a significant burden on our economy, which is highly dependent on global ‚Ā†trade and energy imports from the Middle East," Lee said in opening remarks.
He added that South Korea will ‚Äčalso look for sources of energy beyond supplies shipped via the Strait of Hormuz.
Presidential policy adviser Kim Yong-beom said after ‚Äčthe meeting that the industry ministry would proceed quickly so that the price capping system could be implemented as soon as this week.
The maximum price could be changed every two weeks, he said, noting South Korea had sufficient oil reserves to meet 208 days ‚Äčof consumption.
The amount of crude oil shipped to South Korea affected by the Strait of Hormuz blockade ‚Äčis around 1.7 million barrels per day, Kim said.
South Korea could secure 20 million barrels of crude stock it jointly stores with ‚ÄĆoil-producing ‚Ā†countries if it exercises the right to buy, Kim said, as well as divert overseas production by the state-run Korea National Oil Corp for domestic use.
Kim said 14% of natural gas due to be imported this year is from the Middle East, with about 5 million tons of Qatari gas imports expected to be disrupted. Domestic supplies are unlikely ‚Äčto be hit, however, since ‚ÄčKorea Gas Corp and ‚Ā†other players could import from alternative sources, he said.
When asked if a supplementary budget might be drawn up in case the Middle East crisis is prolonged, Kim said: "If any ‚Äčadditional financial resources are needed... we have to seriously think about it."
Earlier on Monday, ‚ÄčPresident Lee said a ‚Ā†100 trillion won ($66.94 billion) market stabilisation programme should be expanded if needed, and called on the government and the central bank to prepare additional measures to respond to the volatility of the financial and foreign exchange markets.
The benchmark KOSPI (.KS11) , opens new tab closed 6% ‚Ā†lower on ‚ÄčMonday, after falling as much as 9% to trigger circuit breakers ‚Äčfor a second time this month. The won weakened nearly 1% to trade near a psychological barrier of 1,500 per dollar, while the benchmark ‚Äčbond yield hit more than two-year highs.
($1 = 1,493.9000 won)

item.title: NATO drills for war in Arctic with focus on civilians being ready
item.description:
OSLO, March 9 (Reuters) - NATO began its biennial drills in the Arctic on Monday, this ‚Äčtime placing more emphasis on the role of civilians in supporting the military, at ‚ÄĆa time of high tension over U.S. President Trump's push to take over Greenland from fellow NATO member Denmark.
The drills, named Cold Response, focus on defending the alliance in the European Arctic, where NATO-members Norway and Finland share a border with ‚ÄčRussia, and will run March 9-19 this time.
The exercise has now become part of Arctic Sentry , ‚Äčthe NATO mission to strengthen its presence in the polar region that was launched ‚Ā†to defuse tensions with Trump over Greenland.
Trump insists the U.S. needs Greenland to fend off threats from ‚ÄčRussian and Chinese interests in the Arctic, and that Denmark cannot ensure its security. The governments of ‚Äčboth Denmark and Greenland say the island is not for sale.
This year, some 25,000 troops from some 14 nations - including the U.S. and Denmark - will take part in Cold Response, mostly across northern Norway and Finland.
The U.S. is expected to ‚Äčhave some 4,000 troops present.
Ahead of the exercise, the U.S. military withdrew one squadron of F-35 fighter ‚Äčjets from the exercise. It declined to say whether the war in the Middle East played a role in ‚ÄĆthe decision.
"The ‚Ā†U.S. military is a globally deployed force and it is not abnormal for forces to be dynamically re-tasked or reallocated for a host of reasons," a spokesperson for the U.S. Marine Corps Forces Europe told Reuters.
Norway has declared 2026 the year of "total defence", which puts emphasis on boosting the ‚Äčreadiness of civilians, businesses ‚Äčand public institutions to ‚Ā†cope with war and other catastrophes, the latest move by a Nordic nation to boost civilian preparedness.
"We want our military to do its job of defending ‚Äčthe country. To do that, we are completely reliant on most aspects of ‚Äčsociety functioning ‚Ā†as normal," Major-General Lars Lervik, head of the Norwegian Army, told Reuters.
"This is also an opportunity to rehearse specifically where civilians can give direct support to the military effort, for instance with the health service treating a ‚Ā†higher ‚Äčnumber of injured soldiers, Norwegian or from allied forces, than usual."
On ‚ÄčThursday, the military will run a scenario testing the ability of hospitals in northern Norway to treat a high number of ‚Äčcasualties being transported from an imaginary frontline in Finland.

item.title: Tehran threatens to confiscate property of Iranians abroad who back attacks on Iran
item.description:
DUBAI, March 9 (Reuters) - Iranians living abroad could have property confiscated and face other legal penalties if ‚Äčthey express support for the United States and ‚ÄĆIsrael, the Iranian prosecutor general's office said on Monday.
Some members of the Iranian diaspora who want political change ‚Äčin Tehran took to the streets of ‚ÄčEuropean and American cities to celebrate the ‚Ā†killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei ‚Äčin the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran. Iran on Monday named ‚ÄčMojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father.
"A warning has been issued to those Iranians living abroad who in different ways ‚Äčsympathise, support or cooperate with the American-Zionist (Israeli) ‚Äčenemy," the prosecutor general's office was quoted as saying by ‚ÄĆstate ‚Ā†media.
"They will be met with the confiscation of all their properties and other legal penalties in accordance with the law."
Newly established channels on Telegram ‚Äčhave shared details ‚Äčof prominent ‚Ā†Iranians living abroad who have posted comments critical of Iran's clerical authorities ‚Äčand supportive of the U.S.-Israeli military campaign ‚Äčthat ‚Ā†began on February 28.
Up to 5 million Iranians live abroad, the majority of them in the United ‚Ā†States ‚Äčand Western Europe, according to ‚ÄčIranian government data. Iranian media put their numbers closer to ‚Äč10 million.

item.title: Guinea dissolves 40 political parties, opposition leader cries foul
item.description:
DAKAR, March 9 (Reuters) - Guinea's main opposition leader said "direct resistance" to the country's coup leader-turned-president was now the only way to bring about change ‚Äčafter the government consolidated its position by dissolving 40 political ‚ÄĆparties.
President Mamady Doumbouya, a former special forces commander, seized power in 2021 and won a seven-year term as president in December in a result contested by his opponents. Legislative elections ‚Äčare expected in May in the West African nation, which is ‚Äčrich in bauxite and iron ore.
Guinea's Ministry of Territorial Administration ‚Ā†and Decentralization said in a decree late on Friday that 40 parties' ‚Äčheadquarters and local offices would be closed and use of their logos, ‚Äčacronyms "and other distinctive signs" would be prohibited.
The decree said the parties had failed to meet legal obligations such as filing financial statements; several of the dissolved political parties have ‚Äčprotested, maintaining they had fulfilled all legal requirements.
In a video statement published ‚Äčon social media on Sunday, Guinea's main opposition leader Cellou Dalein Diallo said "war has been ‚ÄĆopenly ‚Ā†declared" on Doumbouya's challengers.
He called on Guineans to engage in "direct resistance", without specifying what exactly that should look like. And he said political change would not happen through dialogue or democratic processes.
"The head of the junta and ‚Äčhis malevolent clique want ‚Äčto rewrite the ‚Ā†country's history by erasing from the political landscape all forces likely to overshadow his nascent one-party state," he ‚Äčsaid.
A government spokesperson did not respond to a request for ‚Äčcomment.
Diallo is ‚Ā†in exile outside the country, as is former President Alpha Conde, who Doumbouya toppled and whose party was also dissolved on Friday.
Guinea has a history ‚Ā†of political ‚Äčviolence, including during the 2020 election which ‚ÄčDiallo said he had won. The December 2025 election was tightly controlled and unfolded without major ‚Äčsecurity incidents.

item.title: At least 14 dead after migrant boat crashes into coast guard vessel off southern Turkey
item.description:
ANKARA, March 9 (Reuters) - At least 14 people were killed when ‚Äčan inflatable boat carrying migrants ‚ÄĆcrashed into a Turkish coast guard vessel off Turkey's southern ‚Äčprovince of Antalya on ‚ÄčMonday, the coast guard said ‚Ā†in a statement.
The coast guard ‚Äčsaid the high-speed boat was ‚Äčdetected early on Monday off the coast of the Finike district ‚Äčof Antalya and that ‚Äčit tried to flee from the coast ‚ÄĆguard ‚Ā†despite repeated warnings to stop. It later collided with a coast guard vessel, the ‚Äčstatement ‚Äčadded.
It said ‚Ā†six migrants and one Turkish national were ‚Äčrescued, but that 14 ‚Äčwere ‚Ā†found dead and 15 others were caught after continuing ‚Ā†in ‚Äčtheir boat until ‚Äčthey reached land.

item.title: EU can no longer rely on 'rules-based' system against threats, von der Leyen says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 9 (Reuters) - The EU must be prepared to project its power more assertively as ‚Äčit can no longer rely on a "rules-based" ‚ÄĆsystem against threats and must determine if its institutions and systems help or hinder its credibility, ‚ÄčEuropean Commission President Ursula von der ‚ÄčLeyen said on Monday.
"We will always defend ‚Ā†and uphold the rules-based system that we ‚Äčhelped to build with our allies, but ‚Äčwe can no longer rely on it as the only way to defend our interests or assume its ‚Äčrules will shelter us from the ‚Äčcomplex threats that we face," von der Leyen said ‚ÄĆat ‚Ā†a conference for EU ambassadors.
"We urgently need to reflect on whether our doctrine, our institutions and our decision making ‚Äď all designed in ‚Äča postwar ‚Äčworld of ‚Ā†stability and multilateralism ‚Äď have kept pace with the speed of change ‚Äčaround us. Whether the system that ‚Äčwe ‚Ā†built ‚Äď with all of its well-intentioned attempts at consensus and compromise ‚Äď is more a help ‚Ā†or ‚Äča hindrance to our credibility ‚Äčas a geopolitical actor," she added.

item.title: Middle East conflict could spur palm oil demand from biodiesel sector
item.description:
MUMBAI/KUALA LUMPUR, March 9 (Reuters) - Rising crude oil prices and higher freight rates driven by the Middle East conflict could boost demand for palm oil ‚Äčfrom the biodiesel sector and for food use, as Asian buyers seek prompt ‚ÄĆshipments, industry officials told Reuters.
Indonesia and Malaysia's output rose to a record high in 2025, swelling stocks and weighing on prices. But the conflict has suddenly made palm oil attractive to the ‚Äčbiodiesel industry, pushing prices to their highest level in more than a year.
"Palm oil ‚Äčis now trading at a steep discount to gasoil, with the ‚Ā†current spread lucrative enough to boost demand from the biodiesel industry," said Anilkumar Bagani, ‚Äčresearch head of Mumbai-based vegetable oil broker Sunvin Group.
Oil prices surged more than 25% on Monday ‚Äčto their highest levels since mid-2022 as some major producers cut supplies and fears of prolonged shipping disruptions gripped the market.
Indonesia, the world's largest user of palm oil-based biodiesel, said it may revive plans ‚Äčto roll out a B50 grade of palm oil biodiesel mid-year to counter surging ‚Äčcrude oil prices.
In January, Jakarta shelved the plan to produce B50, an equal blend of palm ‚ÄĆoil biodiesel ‚Ā†and conventional diesel, citing technical and funding challenges and continued with the B40 mandate instead.
A long-term policy shift from countries such as Indonesia is likely only if palm oil trades at a consistent discount to gasoil over an extended period, said Bagani.
Used in ‚Äčeverything from cakes and ‚Äčfrying fats to ‚Ā†cosmetics and cleaning products, palm oil makes up more than half of global vegetable oil shipments and is especially popular among ‚Äčconsumers in emerging markets, led by India.
Southeast Asia is well positioned ‚Äčto consistently ‚Ā†supply palm oil to buyers in Asia, the Middle East, and Europe, said Carl Bek-Nielsen, vice chairman and chief executive director at United Plantations Berhad.
Palm oil supplies remain ample and ‚Ā†can ‚Äčbe shipped quickly to Asian buyers, but it has ‚Äčnow become more expensive than rival soyoil, which could limit gains in demand, said a New Delhi-based dealer ‚Äčwith a global trade house.

item.title: Indonesia may revive B50 biodiesel mix plan as oil prices soar
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 9 (Reuters) - Indonesia may revive a plan to launch a mandatory B50 grade of palm oil-based biodiesel in the middle of this year because of ‚Äčsurging crude oil prices due to the conflict in the ‚ÄĆMiddle East, deputy energy minister Yuliot Tanjung said.
No decision has been made yet by Indonesia, the world's largest palm oil producer, Yuliot added in comments sent to Reuters over the ‚Äčweekend.
In January, authorities scrapped a plan to launch B50 - a blend of ‚Äč50% palm oil-based biodiesel and 50% conventional diesel - this year ‚Ā†due to technical and funding concerns, instead sticking with a B40 blend.
In light ‚Äčof the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, however, the government is now looking at ‚Äčtwo scenarios, Yuliot said.
"B50 might be implemented in the second semester or even earlier...But for now the steering committee's decision for B40 until the end of 2026 still stands," ‚Äčhe said, adding that authorities were monitoring price movements in real time.
The steering ‚Äčcommittee consists of several ministries which determine biodiesel policy, led by chief economic minister, Airlangga Hartarto.
Indonesia's ‚ÄĆbiodiesel ‚Ā†mandate often affects global palm oil prices as increased domestic use reduces available exports.
Oil prices surged on Monday to above $100 per barrel . Palm oil prices also rallied on expectations that rising crude prices will boost demand for biodiesel feedstocks.
Yuliot ‚Äčdid not respond to ‚Äča request for ‚Ā†further comment on Monday. Energy minister Bahlil Lahadalia said the government may accelerate its biofuel programmes, which include B50 ‚Äčand a plan to require a certain blend of bioethanol ‚Äčwith gasoline, ‚Ā†according to local media reports.
Asked about potential implementation of B50 this year, Airlangga told Reuters that authorities were monitoring the impact of the conflict on biodiesel ‚Ā†policy.
Finance minister ‚ÄčPurbaya Yudhi Sadewa said Indonesia is prepared ‚Äčto raise the allocation for fuel subsidies to absorb the shock from rising global oil prices.

item.title: Norway police search for suspect after U.S. embassy blast
item.description:
OSLO, March 9 (Reuters) - Norwegian ‚Äčpolice are searching for ‚ÄĆa suspect seen on surveillance footage following an explosion ‚Äčduring the weekend ‚Äčat the U.S. embassy in ‚Ā†Oslo, investigators said ‚Äčon Monday.
The early Sunday morning ‚Äčblast damaged one of the entrances to the embassy, sending ‚Äčthick smoke into ‚Äčthe street but causing no injuries, ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†what police said may have been linked to the crisis in the Middle East.
"The ‚Äčinvestigation ‚Äčso ‚Ā†far has shown that this was an ‚Äčimprovised explosive device (IED)," ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†Oslo police said in a statement.
Norway's government has ‚Ā†promised ‚Äča thorough investigation ‚Äčof the incident.

item.title: King Charles' trip to US should be cancelled over Trump comments, Lib Dem leader says
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 (Reuters) - A planned visit by King Charles to the United States should be cancelled after President Donald Trump repeatedly insulted Britain, the leader of the country's ‚Äčthird-biggest party in parliament said on Monday.
Trump has repeatedly criticised Prime Minister Keir ‚ÄĆStarmer after London initially blocked the U.S. from using British bases to attack Iran , saying he was "not Winston Churchill" and that Starmer had helped to "ruin" the so-called Special Relationship between the two countries.
That followed ‚ÄčTrump's comments in January that European troops had stayed off the front lines ‚Äčin Afghanistan, drawing a rare rebuke from Starmer who described them ‚Ā†as " frankly appalling " and insulting to the families of the 457 British service personnel who ‚Äčdied in that war.
Ed Davey, the leader of the Liberal Democrats, said Starmer should avoid ‚Äčputting the king, Britain's ceremonial head of state, in the centre of a "highly political situation" with an April visit.
"A state visit from our king would be seen as yet another huge diplomatic coup ‚Äčfor President Trump, so it should not be given to someone who repeatedly insults ‚Äčand damages our country," Davey told LBC Radio, citing Trump's recent comments and his "reckless" war in Iran.
In ‚ÄĆBritain, ‚Ā†the government advises the monarch on his relations with foreign leaders. The Starmer government has not been slow to harness the soft power of the royal family to help smooth relations with Trump over the last year.
Charles hosted Trump for an unprecedented second state visit to ‚ÄčBritain last September, treating ‚Äčhim to a ‚Ā†carriage ride and state banquet at Windsor Castle.
Heir-to-the-throne Prince William is also due to visit the U.S. in the summer during the ‚Äčsoccer World Cup.
Davey said that while it was clear Trump ‚Äčliked the ‚Ā†royal family, he did not appear to treat Britain any better as a result, referring to his tariff policy.
Asked about Davey's remarks, senior minister Steve Reed said the king did a ‚Ā†remarkable ‚Äčjob of representing Britain abroad and it was not ‚Äčfor him or Davey to comment on the arrangements.
Buckingham Palace does not comment on official trips before they ‚Äčhave been confirmed.

item.title: Hezbollah reports Israeli raid in east Lebanon as war enters second week
item.description:
BEIRUT/JERUSALEM, March 9 (Reuters) - Hezbollah said on Monday its fighters clashed with Israeli troops in eastern Lebanon during an Israeli airborne raid overnight, ‚Äčin what would be the second such operation in the area in recent days, as the war between ‚ÄĆIsrael and the Iran-backed group entered a second week.
A spokesperson for the Israeli military had no immediate comment when asked about the Hezbollah statement.
A Hezbollah statement said its fighters "confronted the helicopters and the infiltrating forces with appropriate weapons".
Lebanon was pulled into the regional war when Hezbollah, founded by Iran's ‚ÄčRevolutionary Guards in 1982, opened fire to avenge the killing of Iran's former supreme leader, igniting a new Israeli ‚Äčoffensive against the group.
The Israeli military kept up its airstrikes in the Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs of ‚Ā†Beirut on Monday, sending plumes of smoke across the city. It reiterated warnings to its residents to leave, and announced ‚Äčit would act against a Hezbollah financial institution, Al-Qard Al-Hassan.
Nearly 400 people have been killed in Lebanon by Israeli strikes since ‚ÄčMarch 2, Lebanon's health ministry reported on Sunday, including at least 83 children and 42 women. The toll does not otherwise distinguish between combatants and civilians.
Israel's military announced on Sunday that two Israeli soldiers had been killed in southern Lebanon, the first casualties in the Israeli army since ‚Äčthe war began.
In its statement, Hezbollah said its fighters had observed 15 helicopters flying over eastern Lebanon just after midnight, ‚Äčdropping Israeli troops who were observed approaching Lebanese territory from an area on the Syrian side of the border.
Lebanon's eastern Bekaa Valley region ‚ÄĆis one ‚Ā†of the Shi'ite Muslim group's political and security strongholds.
The Israeli military carried out an airborne raid in the same area, near the village of Nabi Chit, overnight Friday-Saturday. Lebanon's health ministry said 41 people were killed in Israeli attacks in the Nabi Chit area on Saturday.
The Israeli military said that raid was an operation to seek the remains of Ron Arad, an Israeli ‚Äčair force navigator missing in ‚ÄčLebanon since 1986 but no ‚Ā†findings related to him were recovered.
Hundreds of thousands of people in Lebanon have fled their homes since the start of the war, pouring out of the predominantly Shi'ite Muslim southern Lebanon, ‚Äčand the southern suburbs known as Dahiyeh.
Israel has also struck outside those areas.
On Sunday, an ‚ÄčIsraeli drone strike hit ‚Ā†a hotel in Beirut's seafront Rouche district. The Israeli military said the strike killed five senior commanders of what it described as the Lebanon Corps of Iran's Revolutionary Guards Quds Force.
The Israeli military has sent more troops into southern Lebanon since the start of ‚Ā†the war, ‚Äčestablishing what it described as forward defensive positions to guard against Hezbollah attacks ‚Äčinto Israel.
Israel had kept soldiers at five positions in southern Lebanon since a war with Hezbollah in 2024.

item.title: Prosecutors expand Swiss ski bar fire probe to include mayor, document shows
item.description:
GENEVA, March 9 (Reuters) - Swiss prosecutors have extended their investigation into a ‚ÄĆfatal ski bar fire that killed 41 people, mostly teenagers, and added the mayor of the ski town of Crans-Montana to the list of suspects, a document showed.
Mayor Nicolas ‚ÄčFeraud is named as a defendant in one of the prosecutors' ‚Äčdocuments seen by Reuters summoning him for questioning on April ‚Ā†13. He did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Feraud, in ‚Äčhis 50s, has previously admitted that the municipality had missed multiple annual safety checks. "We ‚Äčare deeply sorry. We had no indication that the checks had not been done as requested," Feraud told reporters in January.
The Swiss prosecutor's office in the Valais canton confirmed ‚Äčto Reuters that new individuals had been placed under investigation in the ‚Äčcase involving suspected crimes including negligent homicide, without naming them.
The fire that tore through "Le Constellation" ‚ÄĆbar ‚Ā†on January 1 was one of the worst disasters in modern Swiss history and has tested relations with neighbouring Italy, which lost six of its nationals in the blaze.
Many young people remain hospitalised with burn injuries.
The tragedy has also sent ‚Äčchills through the lucrative ‚Äčtourism sector. ‚Ā†The affluent town of Crans-Montana is popular among French, Italian and American tourists and well known both for its ‚Äčsunny, south-facing ski slopes and its golf courses.
Prosecutors' inquiries initially ‚Äčfocused on ‚Ā†the French bar owners who remain under investigation. The probe was expanded in late January to also include a current and a former local official.
Bar owners ‚Ā†Jacques ‚Äčand Jessica Moretti have both voiced grief over ‚Äčthe fire and said they would cooperate with the probe.
If convicted, defendants could face a maximum ‚Äčpenalty of 4-1/2 years in prison.

item.title: Amid wave of kids‚Äô online safety laws, age-checking tech comes of age
item.description:
NEW YORK/STOCKHOLM/SYDNEY, March 9 (Reuters) - For years, tech companies successfully resisted pressure from child safety advocates to do more to keep kids off their services, claiming technical limitations would make any attempt to restrict access for teens impractical, overly broad or a security risk.
Now, a growing list of governments is concluding those hurdles are not insurmountable, and pushing ahead with aggressive new age-checking requirements for social networks, AI chatbots ‚Äčand porn purveyors alike.
Three months after Australia launched a landmark ban on teen social media accounts, regulators across Europe , in Brazil and in a handful of U.S. states are moving to emulate it . California Governor Gavin Newsom - seen as a likely Democratic candidate for president in 2028 - joined the ‚ÄĆcall , opens new tab last month, while Republican President Donald Trump is also reportedly ‚Äútaking an interest‚ÄĚ , opens new tab in age limits, according to his daughter-in-law.
Spurring them along are escalating concerns over online abuse and teen mental health , and a recent outcry over the spread of AI-generated child sexual images , as well as increased confidence in the capabilities of "age assurance" software that backers say can suss out a person's approximate age using facial analysis, parental approval, ID checks and other digital clues.
Recent advancements in artificial intelligence have boosted the effectiveness and slashed the cost of those age-gating tools, according to Reuters interviews with more than a dozen regulators, child safety advocates, independent researchers and vendors who perform the age checks for big tech companies, including TikTok, Facebook owner Meta (META.O) , opens new tab and OpenAI.
‚ÄúThe age-assurance market ‚Äčhas matured a lot in the last couple of years,‚ÄĚ said Ariel Fox Johnson, a senior adviser to San Francisco-based Common Sense Media, a children‚Äôs online advocacy group. She pointed to improving technology, as well as the establishment of trade groups, technical protocols and certification schemes standardizing evaluation ‚Äčof the various tools‚Äô effectiveness.
Social media companies now can often confidently guess a person‚Äôs age group using digital breadcrumbs like the year an account was established or the type of content it views, they said, while ‚Ā†a burgeoning industry of age assurance vendors like Yoti, k-ID and Persona offer additional layers of checks via automated tools like face scans and machine-based analysis of government IDs.
At the app-store level, too, Apple and Alphabet‚Äôs (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab Google have rolled out tools that allow parents to indicate their child‚Äôs age range to app developers.
‚ÄúThe tech ‚Äčdefinitely has gotten better, not just for age verification specifically but for overall identity verification,‚ÄĚ said Merritt Maxim, a vice president at Massachusetts-based research firm Forrester. ‚ÄúThat, in turn, has driven down the average cost of verification, so that where you were using it five years ago only for higher-value types of transactions, now you ‚Äčcan use it for pretty much anything without a significant financial impact.‚ÄĚ
Vendors generally charge well under $1 per check for basic machine-only age assurance tools, though for large volumes the price is often as low as single-digit cents, said industry executives. More costly traditional processes like human confirmation and triangulation of personal data that were standard a decade ago are still available at a premium, but are needed less frequently, the executives said.
Independent evaluations back up executives' descriptions of rapid progress. According to an ongoing study run by the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), face-scanning software from firms including Yoti ‚Äď which performs checks for TikTok and Meta‚Äôs (META.O) , opens new tab Facebook, Instagram and Threads ‚Äď were off in their age estimations by an average of ‚Äč4.1 years as of initial testing in 2014, while by 2024 that average had dropped to 3.1 years, and is currently 2.5 years.
UK-based Yoti said the performance of its latest face analysis model due out in April surpasses that of models it submitted for the NIST and Australian studies, with an ‚Äčaverage error of only 1.04 years for kids in regulators‚Äô target age range of 14 to 18. Persona, a San Francisco-based identity verification firm used by OpenAI and Reddit (RDDT.N) , opens new tab , touts a similar average error of 1.77 years for the 13-to-17-year-old age range.
A report commissioned by the Australian government likewise determined last year that photo-based age estimation products were ‚ÄĆbroadly accurate, although it ‚Ā†acknowledged that users within three years of the law‚Äôs age cutoff of 16 were in a ‚Äúgrey zone where system uncertainty is higher‚ÄĚ and recommended they be diverted to ‚Äúsupplementary assurance methods, such as ID-based verification or parental consent."
The systems also struggle more with certain skin types, with grainier imagery captured by older phones and when using privacy-protective ‚Äúon-device‚ÄĚ data processing, which entails performing a check entirely on the person's phone without sending their data out to a cloud server, executives said.
For instance, systems using on-device processing are less likely to catch attempts by enterprising youngsters to appear older than they are, said Rick Song, CEO of San Francisco-based Persona. Common tricks used by teens include donning masks, applying heavy makeup or fake facial hair, or scanning the plastic faces of action figures instead of their own, he said.
Still, said executives, facial age estimation can provide a digital version of the kind of screening performed daily at bars and liquor stores in the offline world.
"If you look ‚Äčyoung, you can be challenged, and you may have to provide your ‚ÄčID," said Robin Tombs, CEO of London-based Yoti.
He added that social media ‚Ā†services generally require fewer face scans and ID checks than porn or gambling sites because they already have reams of personal information on their users. This means they can lean more on an age assurance method called ‚Äúinference‚ÄĚ ‚ÄĒ involving analysis of online activities, connected financial information and other signals ‚ÄĒ to satisfy regulators‚Äô requirements.
The 10 social media companies included in Australia‚Äôs teen ban all declined Reuters requests for data on the effectiveness of their age assurance tools.
Australia‚Äôs internet regulator, the ‚ÄčeSafety commissioner, has said it will collect population data for two years to assess the ban's impact and publish first results later this year. Already, companies have locked 4.7 million suspected underage accounts since the law came ‚Äčinto effect in December, it said, although industry ‚Ā†participants have told Reuters that some of the accounts were likely underage Google accounts that were prevented from logging in to YouTube, regardless of whether they were active.
Meta said it took down about 550,000 Instagram, Facebook and Threads accounts suspected to be underage in the first weeks of the Australian ban. Snapchat (SNAP.N) , opens new tab said it took down about 415,000.
Regulators elsewhere are watching carefully. European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen is set to discuss age verification during an upcoming visit to Canberra, according to a European lawmaker briefed on her agenda. The United Kingdom, which requires age verification for porn websites and is considering tightening child safety rules for social media and AI chatbots as ‚Ā†well, is likewise swapping ‚Äčnotes with Australian counterparts.
Early results from the Australian experiment should be taken with a grain of salt, as companies affected by the ban generally were doing the bare minimum to comply with ‚Äčlegal requirements, said Iain Corby, the executive director of the Age Verification Providers Association, a trade association that represents about three dozen vendors including Yoti and Persona.
In some cases, he added, the social media companies asked AVPA member firms to turn off controls that make the age checks more robust.
‚ÄúThey are extremely worried this is going to be contagious and be a policy that is ‚Äčadopted around the world, so they are not really motivated for it to be a glowing success,‚ÄĚ he said.
‚ÄúThey are testing the regulator‚Äôs patience to see what they can get away with.‚ÄĚ

item.title: Republicans confront cooling support from young men over Trump policies
item.description:
MANCHESTER, New Hampshire, March 9 (Reuters) - In a college library lined with volumes on America's greatest presidents, six young men who voted for Donald Trump gathered to size up his second term. Their verdicts ranged from guarded approval to disappointment, reflecting growing tension in a demographic that helped propel Trump to victory in 2024.
While the students form a very small sample ‚Äčsize, their mixed reviews - including criticism of what several called overly harsh immigration enforcement and frustration over rising prices - mirror a broader shift in national polling that shows the Republican president is losing ground with young men.
Public opinion polling suggests that this ‚ÄĆsoftening of support, part of a broader unhappiness among Americans over Trump's policies, threatens Republicans' hopes of retaining their slim majorities in the Senate and House of Representatives.
Analysts who track the youth vote told Reuters many young men report feeling little tangible improvement in their economic prospects since Trump returned to office in January 2025.
Sitting with five classmates in the political library at Saint Anselm College in New Hampshire, 20-year-old sophomore Tyler Witzgall captured a sentiment common among his peers: The president, for all his accomplishments, has fallen short on issues shaping their daily lives, like high prices.
"I'm still going to graduate and be in an enormous amount of debt. I won‚Äôt be able to buy a home for a ‚Äčwhile," Witzgall said, grading Trump a C or C-plus, describing his disappointment in the president's efforts to tackle domestic economic issues.
None of the students said they regretted their vote, viewing the 2024 Democratic presidential nominee, then Vice President Kamala Harris, as a continuation of a ‚Äčpresidency they associated with unchecked immigration and spiraling costs.
Yet even solid Trump backers like Ian Pomfret, a 20-year-old sophomore, joined others in the student panel criticizing what he describes as overly aggressive tactics used by Immigration and ‚Ā†Customs Enforcement agents implementing Trump's crackdown.
"The ICE thing is a huge problem," Pomfret said, pointing to the two U.S. citizens killed by federal agents in Minneapolis. "I feel like there is a better way of going about it than raiding and killing and instant deportation."
Pomfret still broadly supports Trump, grading him a ‚ÄčB-plus.
Last month, some 33% of men aged 18-29 approved of Trump's performance in the White House, down from 43% in February 2025, according to Reuters/Ipsos polling. Trump garnered the support of 46% of young males in the 2024 presidential election, up 7 points from 2020, according to exit polling analyzed by ‚ÄčPew Research.
A party strategist who works on House races pointed to a CBS News survey in February showing that 43% of young voters view Republican positions as "mainstream", and said the party could still secure midterm wins if it captures at least 40% of the youth vote.
Turnout among young voters is historically low in midterm elections, with only a quarter casting a ballot in 2022, half the overall rate.
Still, young voters can tip close races, as in 2018 when a wave of mass shootings spurred youth turnout and helped flip more than a dozen congressional seats to the Democrats, according to John Della Volpe, a pollster who specializes in the Gen Z vote.
"All evidence in the last 15 ‚Äčor 16 months is that this cohort cannot be counted on at this stage to be reliable Republican voters," Della Volpe said. "Mostly because they haven't felt tangible improvements in their day-to-day lives."
Trump‚Äôs pledges in his 2024 campaign to curb inflation, boost economic growth, and toughen immigration ‚Äčenforcement helped attract young males to his MAGA movement. A string of attention-grabbing moves, like attending a sneaker event, sitting down with popular podcasters like Joe Rogan and Theo Von and posting content on TikTok, also enhanced his standing with younger men, political analysts said.
Last month, the Republican National Committee, the party's governing body, launched ‚ÄĆits own account on ‚Ā†TikTok, which is used by six in 10 Americans under 30, according to Pew Research.
White House spokesman Davis Ingle said no other president has done more for young men than Trump "who is working tirelessly to create jobs, cool inflation, increase housing affordability, and more."
John Brabender, a media consultant who helped the Trump campaign target young men in 2024, said without Trump on the ballot in November, Republican candidates will need to do a better job of talking about tax cuts and other policies that directly benefit someone in their 20s, rather than broad indicators like how the stock market is performing.
"I will be the first to say that collectively we've done a poor job of ... communicating directly to the hearts and minds of people at a much younger age," Brabender said.
Brabender's comments echo concerns being expressed by Republican lawmakers, party strategists and White House aides who say the president needs to give more priority to ‚Äčhis economic messaging and less to foreign policy. The economy is the top ‚Äčissue for voters, polls show.
The battle ‚Ā†for the attention of young voters can be seen in the race for an open Senate seat in New Hampshire, one of four competitive seats in the chamber that Democrats must defend in November.
U.S. Representative Chris Pappas, the Democratic frontrunner, launched a TikTok account in October and has been working to build a following, with several posts addressing the high cost of housing, a key concern for young voters.
"If you ask someone in their 20s about owning ‚Äča home, that seems like a far-off notion," Pappas said in an interview. "We've got to make that dream within reach of more people."
The Pappas campaign plans to host a series of events with online influencers to ‚Äčhelp engage with younger voters.
Former Senator Scott ‚Ā†Brown, who is seeking the Republican nomination, told Reuters that his own hobbies - including playing guitar in a rock band and coaching high school basketball - help him connect with younger voters.
"It‚Äôs not a stretch for me to walk in and start playing hoops with somebody or get up on stage and jam with some teenagers," said Brown, adding that two staffers, ages 17 and 26, were "social media whizzes" and helping build his presence online.
Brown will speak this month at the New Hampshire Institute of Politics at Saint Anselm, a hub for political discussion in the early presidential primary state.
Students pass through the institute each day for classes, walking hallways ‚Ā†decorated with photographs of ‚Äčpresidents who have visited and past rooms filled with more than half a century of political memorabilia, much of it from an era of more convivial bipartisanship.
Several of the students ‚Äčinterviewed by Reuters said they longed for a return to more civil discourse. While backing many of Trump's policies, they disliked his caustic style, in which he casts Democrats or other critics as the enemy. All said they were still likely to vote Republican in November.
Tyler Delaney, a 19-year-old freshman, said the divisiveness of Trump's approach will come back to bite him ‚Äčif the Democrats, as expected, take control of the House.
"It gets to a point where you need to have some bipartisanship, and I think that's gonna hit Trump hard after the midterms."

item.title: EU to sign defence partnerships with Australia, Iceland and Ghana, Kallas says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 9 (Reuters) - The EU will ‚Äčsign defence partnerships ‚ÄĆwith Australia, Iceland and Ghana in ‚Äčthe coming ‚Äčdays, EU foreign ‚Ā†policy chief Kaja ‚ÄčKallas said ‚Äčon Monday.
"There are many other interested countries ‚Äčknocking at ‚Äčour door," Kallas added ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†a speech in Brussels.
"A growing number of countries ‚Äčaround ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†globe are seeking to ‚Äčdiversify their ‚Äčpartnerships ‚Ā†to manage the heightened risk."

item.title: UK talking to partners about limiting economic impact of Iran crisis, says PM
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 (Reuters) - The British government is talking to international partners, and its central bank, to assess ‚Äčways to limit the economic damage from the escalating Iranian ‚ÄĆcrisis, warning that the longer it goes on, the worse it could get.
British borrowing costs have soared since the conflict erupted more than a week ago - by more than those of ‚Äčother European countries and the United States - as investors fear that ‚Äčsurging oil and gas prices will stoke already stubborn inflation ‚Ā†higher.
That also risks forcing the government to intervene again to cushion the economic ‚Äčblow, a potential huge challenge to an already unpopular government which has limited ‚Äčroom to increase spending.
Prime Minister Keir Starmer told an event in London on Monday that he wanted people to know that the government was working to deal with the ‚Äčfallout from the Iranian war.
"What we're doing is monitoring the risk, working with ‚Äčothers to mitigate the risk," he said.
"The chancellor is talking with the Bank of England ‚ÄĆevery ‚Ā†day to make sure that we're ahead of that, on energy prices for households."
He also said the government was talking to international partners about how to reduce the likely impact on people in Britain, and said Britain wanted ‚Äčto seek a way ‚Äčto de-escalate the ‚Ā†conflict.
Starmer said that he was acutely aware that people would be worried about their bills given the rising oil ‚Äčand gas price, but he noted that for consumers, an ‚Äčexisting energy ‚Ā†price cap was in place until June.
He also said the economy was more resilient than the last energy shock, when Russia launched its full scale invasion ‚Ā†of Ukraine.
"People ‚Äčwill sense, you will sense, the longer ‚Äčthis goes on, the more likely the potential for an impact on our economy," he said.

item.title: Saudi Aramco reducing output at two oilfields, two sources say
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Saudi oil giant Aramco has begun cutting ‚Äčoutput at two of ‚ÄĆits oilfields, two sources said on Monday, after ‚Äčthe vital Strait of ‚ÄčHormuz was choked by ‚Ā†the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran ‚Äčand subsequent attacks on ‚Äčthe waterway.
It was not immediately clear at which fields and ‚Äčby how much ‚Äčproduction was being curtailed. Aramco, which ‚ÄĆhas ‚Ā†been rerouting some of its crude cargoes to the Red Sea port ‚Äčof ‚ÄčYanbu, ‚Ā†did not immediately respond to an ‚Äčemailed request for ‚Äčcomment.
Several ‚Ā†of Saudi Arabia's neighbours, which have also been ‚Ā†subject ‚Äčto attacks, have ‚Äčalso cut production.

item.title: China makes biggest retail fuel price cap increase in four years amid Iran war
item.description:
BEIJING, March 9 (Reuters) - China raised regulated ceiling prices for retail gasoline and diesel in the sharpest ‚Äčincrease since March 2022 on Monday, following a surge in ‚ÄĆinternational oil prices due to the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
Gasoline and diesel retail price caps in the world's second-largest oil consumer will increase by 695 yuan ($100.46) and ‚Äč670 yuan ($96.84) per metric ton, respectively, from Tuesday, according to ‚Äča notice from the National Development and Reform Commission. ‚Ā†The previous adjustment was made on February 24.
International benchmark Brent crude ‚Äčfutures had climbed 27%, and West Texas Intermediate crude (WTI) futures were up ‚Äčby 35.6% last week.
China last week asked refiners to halt fuel exports and try to cancel shipments already committed as the Iran war curbed refinery output, several ‚Äčpeople with knowledge of the matter said on Thursday.
China's state planner ‚Äčreviews retail gasoline and diesel prices every 10 working days and applies uniform adjustments ‚ÄĆnationwide, ‚Ā†though benchmark prices vary by region.
The adjustment rate reflects changes in international crude oil prices while also taking into account average processing costs, taxes, distribution expenses and appropriate profit margins.
China allows retail gasoline and ‚Äčdiesel prices to ‚Äčfloat freely between ‚Ā†floors and ceilings. However, when international crude prices reach $130 per barrel, retail fuel prices are generally not ‚Äčraised or are raised only minimally. When crude prices ‚Äčfall to $40 ‚Ā†per barrel or below, retail fuel prices are calculated as if crude were priced at $40, with normal processing margins, according to the price ‚Ā†adjustment mechanism.
($1 = ‚Äč6.9185 Chinese yuan renminbi)

item.title: Lebanon parliament extends its own mandate for two years as war intensifies
item.description:
DUBAI, March 9 (Reuters) - Lebanon‚Äôs parliament extended its own mandate for two years on ‚ÄčMonday, a statement from the speaker's ‚ÄĆoffice said, pushing back elections that were meant to take place in May of ‚Äčthis year.
The office of parliamentary speaker ‚ÄčNabih Berri said 76 lawmakers of ‚Ā†the 128-seat body had voted in ‚Äčfavour of the extension. The vote ‚Äčcame a week after Lebanon was dragged into the regional war by militant group Hezbollah's ‚Äčlaunch of rockets and drones into ‚ÄčIsrael, which has responded with heavy bombardment across ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†country.
Lebanon last held parliamentary elections in 2022 and had been considering an extension of the current body's mandate before ‚Äčthe latest ‚Äčround of ‚Ā†fighting erupted between Hezbollah and Israel.
Lawmakers have taken similar measures ‚Äčin the past. The parliament ‚Äčelected ‚Ā†in 2009 voted to extend its own mandate until 2017 , citing security concerns linked ‚Ā†to ‚Äčthe war that was ‚Äčthen raging in neighbouring Syria.

item.title: Morning Bid: Oil's triple-digit trouble
item.description:
March 9 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
‚ÄėOil shock‚Äô and ‚Äėstagflation‚Äô are overused phrases in financial markets, often pulled out as a scare tactic when mapping out worst-case scenarios. But both are now very ‚Äčmuch in play with energy markets in turmoil as the Middle East war enters its second week with no end in sight.
Crude oil ‚ÄĆprices soared above $100 per barrel on Monday to their highest since just after the Ukraine invasion four years ago. If triple-digit oil prices are more than a short-term phenomenon, the U.S. and global economy could be in for some real pain - and central bankers may find themselves facing their worst nightmare: stagflation.
I‚Äôll get into that and more below.
But first, for more on the market impact ‚Äčof the eye-popping oil surge, listen to today's episode of Morning Bid .
The energy crisis set off by the war in Iran is escalating. U.S. ‚Äčfuel prices have surged well above $3 per gallon, and they‚Äôre set to rise further. President Donald Trump wrote on social media overnight ‚Ā†that rising oil prices were ‚Äúa very small price to pay‚ÄĚ for winning the war, though many voters may question that.
The worrying thing for central banks and investors ‚Äčis that this latest inflationary spur comes after Friday‚Äôs surprisingly weak U.S. employment report. The data may have been skewed by bad weather in February, but there were few ‚Äčpositive points and many signs that the labor market has stalled.
This raises the dark prospect of ‚Äėstagflation‚Äô - slow or no growth coupled with mounting price rises. How the Fed and other central banks react to that is very much an open question. Most likely, they will just stand pat for longer, which is apt to please almost no one.
The renewed inflation concerns have rattled global bond markets , ‚Äčintensifying last week's selloff and sending yields higher - especially in Britain, where two-year gilt yields are on track for their biggest one-day rise since 2022.
Meantime, stock markets tumbled around ‚Äčthe world , with Japan‚Äôs Nikkei losing over 5% and South Korea‚Äôs high-flying KOSPI shedding nearly 6%. That comes on top of losses last week of 5.5% and more than 10% for ‚ÄĆthose indexes, ‚Ā†respectively.
U.S. stock futures are down more than 1% before the bell and - much like last week - the greenback climbed as investors sought safety in the liquidity of dollar cash. Gold, usually the most reliable safe haven, once again failed to rise to the occasion , weighed by the stronger dollar and climbing Treasury yields.
Markets will now look to see if countries begin tapping their national oil reserves to stem the bleeding, with reports on Monday suggesting G7 finance ministers would discuss a joint release from emergency reserves.
Meantime, U.S. Senate ‚ÄčDemocratic Leader Chuck Schumer called on President Trump ‚Äčto release oil from the U.S. ‚Ā†Strategic Petroleum Reserve, a move the president has so far not supported.
How far or for how long any of that can alleviate the situation remains to be seen as the crisis itself deepens, with Gulf states continuing to cut output amid persistent threats ‚Äčto shipping through the vital Strait of Hormuz.
Meantime, Tehran appears to be doubling down as it appoints hardliner Mojtaba Khamenei, ‚Äčson of Ali Khamenei, ‚Ā†as Iran‚Äôs new supreme leader.
Chart of the day
The jump in fuel prices since the start of the U.S.-Israel war with Iran could prove a political headache for the Republican Party ahead of November's midterm elections.
Some of the steepest rises have been in parts of the Midwest and the South, including swing states that supported Donald Trump in the 2024 ‚Ā†presidential election.
Today's events ‚Äčto watch
* U.S. 3-month and 6-month bill auctions
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox ‚Äčevery weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of ‚ÄčReuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Hungary asked EU to suspend sanctions on Russian energy, PM says
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 9 (Reuters) - Hungary's Prime Minister Viktor ‚ÄčOrban said on ‚ÄĆMonday that he asked European Commission President ‚ÄčUrsula von ‚Äčder Leyen in a ‚Ā†letter to suspend ‚Äčall sanctions on ‚ÄčRussian energy, as that was needed to stop ‚Äča rise ‚Äčin crude oil prices in ‚ÄĆHungary.
Orban ‚Ā†also said in a video posted on his Facebook ‚Äčpage ‚Äčthat ‚Ā†he called a government meeting ‚Äčfor later ‚Äčin ‚Ā†the day to "prevent the price of ‚Ā†diesel ‚Äčand gasoline ‚Äčfrom rising to unbearable ‚Äčlevels."

item.title: France will send two warships to Red Sea, Macron says
item.description:
PAPHOS, Cyprus, March 9 (Reuters) - France will send ‚Äčtwo frigates as ‚ÄĆpart of the European Union's naval mission ‚ÄčAspides in ‚Äčthe Red Sea, French ‚Ā†President Emmanuel Macron ‚Äčsaid on Monday.
"We ‚Äčare in the process of setting up a ‚Äčpurely defensive, ‚Äčpurely escort mission, which must ‚ÄĆbe ‚Ā†prepared together with both European and non-European states," Macron ‚Äčsaid ‚Äčafter ‚Ā†meeting with Cypriot President Nikos ‚ÄčChristodoulides and ‚ÄčGreek ‚Ā†Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis in Cyprus.

item.title: Kuwait mourns two officials killed as Iran war rages
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Kuwait held a military funeral on Monday for two interior ministry officials ‚Äčit said were killed "while performing their ‚ÄĆduty".
The state - which hosts U.S. military installations and has come under Iranian drone and missile fire ‚Äčduring Tehran's war with Israel and ‚Äčthe U.S. - said the two men died ‚Ā†early on Sunday, but did not ‚Äčgo into further details.
Kuwait's army separately said ‚Äčareas near the international airport were hit on Sunday and its forces had intercepted missiles and ‚Äčdrones.
The Interior Ministry said on Sunday ‚Äčit mourned "the martyrs of duty, Lieutenant Colonel Staff Abdullah ‚ÄĆEmad ‚Ā†Al-Sharrah and Major Fahad Abdulaziz Al-Mejmed, from the General Administration of Land Border Security, who were martyred at dawn".
The funeral ‚Äčin Sulaibikhat ‚ÄčCemetery was ‚Ā†led by First Deputy Prime Minister and Interior Minister Sheikh ‚ÄčFahad Al-Yousef Al-Sabah, along with other ‚Äčsenior ‚Ā†figures.
Authorities there say five people have been killed since the start of the ‚Ā†Iran ‚Äčcrisis, including four military personnel ‚Äčand a young girl, and dozens have been wounded.

item.title: Germany's Merz rules out cooperation with far-right AfD after state election
item.description:
BERLIN, March 9 (Reuters) - German Chancellor Friedrich ‚ÄčMerz repeated on ‚ÄĆMonday his conservatives would not ‚Äčcooperate with ‚Äčthe far-right Alternative for ‚Ā†Germany party ‚Äčfollowing the close ‚Äčresult in the state election in ‚Äčthe southern ‚Äčregion of Baden Wuerttemberg.
He ‚ÄĆsaid ‚Ā†the election, in which the environmental Greens narrowly ‚Äčbeat ‚ÄčMerz's ‚Ā†conservative Christian Democrats, would ‚Äčnot affect ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†work of the coalition ‚Ā†government ‚Äčin Berlin.

item.title: Ukraine received 11 requests on countering Iran's Shahed drones, Zelenskiy says
item.description:
KYIV, March 9 (Reuters) - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on ‚ÄčMonday that Ukraine ‚ÄĆhad received 11 requests from countries neighboring Iran ‚Äčas well as ‚ÄčEuropean states and the ‚Ā†United States for ‚Äčdrone interceptors, electronic warfare ‚Äčsystems and training.
"We also reviewed in detail requests ‚Äčfrom the states ‚Äčfor security support from our ‚ÄĆside ‚Ā†in countering 'Shahed' drones and other similar challenges," Zelenskiy said on ‚Äčthe Telegram ‚Äčapp ‚Ā†after meeting his top military ‚Äčand government officials.
"Some ‚Äčrequests ‚Ā†have already been met with concrete decisions ‚Ā†and ‚Äčspecific support."

item.title: Turkey says second Iranian ballistic missile shot down by NATO defences in airspace
item.description:
ANKARA, March 9 (Reuters) - Turkey said on Monday that NATO ‚ÄĆair defences shot down a second ballistic missile that was fired from Iran and had entered Turkish airspace, warning it will take any necessary steps against threats.
This marks the second Iranian ballistic missile in the last week that has targeted the south of Turkey, ‚Äčwhich is a NATO member and Iran's neighbour. Ankara had warned Iran against attacking again on Saturday, but has ‚Äčnot suggested it wants to formally call on alliance members for further protection.
"We once ‚Ā†again emphasise that all necessary measures will be taken decisively and without hesitation against any threat directed at ‚Äčour country's territory and airspace," the Turkish Defence Ministry said, adding some ammunition parts had fallen in the ‚Äčsoutheastern province of Gaziantep but there had been no casualties.
"We also reiterate that it is in everyone's interest to heed Turkey's warnings in this regard," it said.
It was unclear where the missile was headed before it was intercepted by NATO defences stationed in ‚Äčthe eastern Mediterranean Sea.
U.S. air forces are stationed at the Incirlik base in southern Turkey, and there is ‚Äča NATO radar base in Malatya province to the northeast that provides vital protection for the alliance. Ankara said fragments ‚ÄĆfrom the ‚Ā†missile fell in empty fields in Gaziantep, which sits roughly between the two.
Burhanettin Duran, President Tayyip Erdogan's communications director, said Ankara was strongly reiterating its warning to all parties, namely Iran, to avoid steps that endanger regional stability and civilians.
Ankara says that Washington has not used Incirlik in its air assault, alongside Israel, on Iran, ‚Äčwhich triggered Tehran's missile and ‚Äčdrone attacks.
Iran did not ‚Ā†immediately comment on the incident, but it has said repeatedly that it is not at war with regional countries and is not explicitly targeting Turkey.
Turkey, which had sought to ‚Äčmediate U.S.-Iran talks before the air war that began last week, has previously ‚Äčsaid it had ‚Ā†no intention currently of invoking NATO's Article 4 that would call allies to consult if a member is threatened.
That could lead to invoking the alliance's Article 5, which would call NATO to defend its attacked ally.
Turkey has the trans-Atlantic alliances' second-largest ‚Ā†army ‚Äčand has become a leading global actor in the defence industry ‚Äčin recent years. However, it still lacks fulsome air defence of its own despite efforts to develop them. It has relied on NATO's ‚Äčair defences in both incidents in the last week.

item.title: Serbia suspends energy exports to shield market as crude prices surge on Iran war
item.description:
BELGRADE, March 9 (Reuters) - Serbia has suspended crude oil and fuel ‚ÄĆproduct exports to safeguard its market from shortages and price spikes, the energy minister said on Monday, as global crude prices jump above $119 a barrel amid supply ‚Äčuncertainty due to the war in Iran .
The suspension, effective until March 19, applies ‚Äčto the export of diesel, gasoline and crude oil by ‚Ā†all modes of transport, Energy Minister Dubravka Djedovic Handanovic said in ‚Äča statement.
Crude oil price on Monday reached its highest since mid-2022 of above $119 ‚Äča barrel, due to uncertainty over global supplies amid the war in Iran.
"The essence of this (temporary) ban is to protect the domestic market from shortages and price spikes ‚Äčdue to global disruptions in the world market,‚ÄĚ she said.
The government ‚Äčin neighboring Croatia on Monday introduced fuel price caps until March 23 to counter effects ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†surging crude oil prices.
Croatian Prime Minister Andrej Plenkovic said that without caps, the price of diesel could rise by 16 percentage points to 1.72 euros per litre. The government cap limited it at 1.55 euros/litre.
Croatia last July ‚Äčabolished fuel price ‚Äčcaps introduced in ‚Ā†2022 when the war in Ukraine threatened global energy supplies.
Serbia has kept a price cap on fuel since ‚ÄčFebruary 2022. The government controls the maximum retail prices ‚Äčfor Euro ‚Ā†diesel and Euro Premium BMB 95 gasoline to mitigate the impact of global price surges on the local economy.
Serbia's fuel exports in 2024, mainly to ‚Ā†neighboring countries, ‚Äčaccounted for around 3.6% of all exports, ‚Äčor $1.26 billion. In 2025, they sharply fell due to U.S. sanctions against the country's only ‚Äčoil refiner, Russian-owned NIS .

item.title: India not planning to release oil in coordination with IEA, source says
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - India is not ‚Äčplanning to ‚Äčrelease oil reserves ‚Ā†in coordination ‚Äčwith the ‚ÄčInternational Energy Agency and has ‚Äčno immediate ‚Äčplans to raise ‚ÄĆretail ‚Ā†prices for gasoline and diesel as ‚Äčof ‚Äčnow, ‚Ā†a government source ‚Äčtold ‚ÄčReuters ‚Ā†on Monday.
(This story has been refiled to correct the author's name in the byline)

item.title: Helsinki energy group plans large investment in small nuclear reactors
item.description:
HELSINKI, March 9 (Reuters) - The city of Helsinki's energy utility Helen plans to invest between 1 billion and 5 billion euros ($1.16 billion-$5.78 billion) in Small Modular Reactor ‚Äčcapacity in the coming years, the head of its newly-established nuclear subsidiary ‚ÄĆtold Reuters.
Helsinki is not alone in eyeing new nuclear generation, as countries ranging from the United States to South Korea , or companies such as Meta (META.O) , opens new tab turn to SMRs to cater for the rapid rise in ‚Äčpower demand from artificial intelligence and electrification.
SMRs are advanced reactors that can ‚Äčbe factory-assembled and transported for installation elsewhere. They have a power ‚Ā†generating capacity of up to 300 megawatts, about a third that of traditional reactors.
Finland, ‚Äčtoo, expects its power demand to increase. But it also needs more steady base ‚Äčload following a rapid rise in wind power production, which has rewarded Finns with some of Europe's lowest power prices on average, but also resulted in high price volatility during consumption peaks.
For ‚ÄčHelen, which shut down Finland's last coal-fired power plant last year , nuclear is a way ‚Äčto add capacity while further reducing CO2 emissions, Helen Nuclear Ltd's CEO Pekka Tolonen said.
"Nuclear power ‚ÄĆis ‚Ā†now the most promising and, among current options, the best way for us to achieve a future without combustion," he told Reuters. Helen is reviewing half a dozen potential Western SMR suppliers, he added.
"The value of the planned investment would be in ‚Äčthe low single billions, ‚Äčmeaning 1 to ‚Ā†5 billion euros," he said. The amount would be in the lower end if Helen went for nuclear heat production only, ‚Äčand in the upper end of the range if it opted ‚Äčfor combined ‚Ā†heat and power production.
Helen, which has to meet heat consumption peaks during cold winter days, believes power consumption in the capital region will double in a few years' time, ‚Ā†due ‚Äčto electrification, data centres and expansion of clean tech, ‚Äčincluding green hydrogen.
Last month, the company initiated a land use planning process in Helsinki and expects to pick ‚Äča site by late 2027, Tolonen said.
($1 = 0.8649 euros)

item.title: Exclusive: Trump's Gaza plan on hold as Iran war pauses disarmament talks
item.description:
JERUSALEM/CAIRO, March 9 (Reuters) - Talks to advance President Donald Trump's plan to end the Gaza war have been on hold since last week when the U.S. and ‚ÄčIsrael jointly attacked Iran, sparking a broader Middle East war , three sources with direct knowledge of the negotiations said.
The pause threatens to stall implementation of Trump's flagship Middle ‚ÄĆEast peace initiative, which he has cast as a major foreign policy objective. It comes less than a month after he secured billions of dollars in pledges for Gaza from Gulf Arab states - countries that are now facing Iranian attacks as the conflict widens.
Trump's Gaza plan has hinged in part on whether Hamas militants would lay down their arms in exchange for amnesty, a step intended to pave the way for reconstruction and further Israeli military withdrawals. ‚ÄčWhite House mediators have been backchanneling between Israel and Hamas on the disarmament question.
