Negotiations on this and other issues were paused when the Iran war began on February 28, ‚Äčthe three sources said, speaking on condition of anonymity to discuss sensitive talks. The pause in disarmament talks has not been previously reported.
Zaha Hassan, ‚Ā†of the Washington-based Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, said countries including the United Arab Emirates and Qatar which pledged funds for Trump's Board of Peace mission may be questioning if this is "really ‚Äčmoney well spent now that they are dodging rocket fire."
One of the sources, who has direct knowledge of work by Trump's Board of Peace mission, described ‚Äčthe pause as a brief, minor delay caused by flight disruptions preventing mediators and representatives from travelling around the region. Talks have frequently been held in Cairo.
Over the longer term, the Board of Peace believes the war could accelerate a resolution of the disarmament issue by removing Iranian influence, which has long supported Hamas financially, the source said.
Another source ‚ÄĒ a Palestinian official close to the mediation effort ‚ÄĒ said Hamas had been expected to hold talks ‚Äčwith Egyptian, Qatari and Turkish mediators on the day the war erupted, but the meeting was scrapped and no new date has been set.
A Hamas official confirmed that talks on Trump's Gaza ‚Äčplan had been frozen for now, but declined to elaborate. Israel's government did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The White House did not immediately provide comment.
Israel's military has eased off strikes in Gaza ‚ÄĆsince the ‚Ā†start of the war but, citing Hamas threats, has not ceased attacks, as Israeli jets carry out bombing campaigns in Iran and Lebanon. At least 16 Palestinians have been killed in Gaza since February 28, health officials say.
"The moment the war on Iran is over, (Israel) will come back at us with the same frequency, with the same violence," said Talal Hamouda, 46, who lives in Jabalia in the northern Gaza Strip, with his wife and five children.
Hamas, for its part, has continued to reassert its grip in areas under its control in Gaza since the war began. Sources close to ‚Äčthe group say Hamas fighters in recent days ‚Äčambushed several Israeli-backed militia members in Gaza's north ‚Ā†and south, killing at least two people.
Trump's plan for Gaza began with an October ceasefire that left Israel controlling more than half the territory, with Hamas controlling the rest. The initiative appeared to gain momentum in the month leading up to ‚Äčthe war, including the reopening of Gaza's border crossing with Egypt and new pledges for reconstruction.
Much of Washington's multinational coordination on Gaza policy ‚Äčhas been run out ‚Ā†of a U.S.-led military compound in southern Israel. Foreign diplomats posted there said momentum behind the plan appeared to stall as the Iran war escalated.
Three diplomats said the Civil Military Coordination Centre scaled back to minimal operations when the war started, and that there were concerns it could be targeted by Iranian missiles.
The diplomats said senior U.S. officials now appear focused on the Iran war, leaving Gaza with limited top-level attention. ‚Ā†Still, working-level ‚Äčdiscussions among countries have continued in the hope that the plan could move forward once the war ends.
Natan Sachs, a ‚Äčsenior fellow at the Washington-based Middle East Institute, said that "only sustained attention from the Trump administration can keep the plan on track ‚ÄĒ and the war with Iran has the potential to undermine exactly that."
"Without it, the divergent aims of the ‚Äčtwo warring parties could easily lead to very different outcomes, and potentially to a resumption of fighting."

item.title: Turkey's Halkbank, U.S. Justice Department in deferred prosecution agreement, judge says
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - Turkish state-run lender Halkbank (HALKB.IS) , opens new tab and the U.S. Justice Department have reached a deferred prosecution agreement, the judge overseeing the long-running U.S. criminal case against the bank said in a ‚Äčcourt filing on Monday, without providing further details of the deal.
In the case, prosecutors had accused ‚ÄĆof fraud, money laundering and conspiracy for allegedly helping Iran evade American economic sanctions.
Halkbank's case has long been a thorn in U.S.-Turkey relations, with Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan calling it an "unlawful, ugly" step.
The agreement was announced after the U.S. Supreme Court in October 2025 let ‚Äčstand a federal appeals court decision that let the prosecution proceed.
U.S. prosecutors originally charged Halkbank in ‚Äč2019, during President Donald Trump's first White House term.
Erdogan met with Trump in September, and expressed ‚Ā†hope the following month that the meeting would resolve various issues between the countries.
The Turkish president said Trump ‚Äčtold him at the White House and in a subsequent phone call that "the Halkbank problem is finished for us."
Halkbank was accused of using money servicers and front companies in Iran, Turkey and the United Arab Emirates in its sanctions-evasion scheme .
U.S. prosecutors said the bank secretly transferred $20 billion of restricted funds, converted oil revenue into gold and cash to benefit Iranian ‚Äčinterests and documented fake food shipments to justify transfers of oil proceeds.
Halkbank pleaded not guilty to bank fraud, money ‚Äčlaundering and conspiracy charges.
The case was an outgrowth of several related criminal cases, including against Turkish-Iranian gold trader Reza Zarrab and ‚ÄĆformer ‚Ā†Halkbank executive Mehmet Hakan Atilla.
Zarrab pleaded guilty to bank fraud, money laundering and conspiracy charges in 2017, but has not been sentenced.
He testified against Atilla, who was convicted in 2018 of bank fraud and conspiracy charges. Atilla returned to Turkey in 2019 after serving most of a 32-month prison term. He has denied wrongdoing.
Halkbank's case ‚Äčhas taken a circuitous path ‚Äčthrough the U.S. courts.
Berman originally ‚Ā†ruled in 2020 that Halkbank did not deserve immunity from prosecution.
The following year, the 2nd U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals in Manhattan upheld that ruling but on different grounds. ‚ÄčIt said Halkbank could be prosecuted under the federal Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act of ‚Äč1976, because its ‚Ā†alleged misconduct involved commercial activity not covered by that law.
In 2023, the U.S. Supreme Court temporarily voided the prosecution , despite agreeing that Congress' desire to shield foreign countries and their instrumentalities from civil liability did not cover criminal cases. Instead, ‚Ā†it ordered ‚Äčthe 2nd Circuit to more thoroughly review whether immunity under centuries-old ‚Äčcommon law shielded Halkbank.
The appeals court found no such shield in October 2024, prompting Halkbank's second Supreme Court appeal. The Trump administration argued that the common ‚Äčlaw does not shield foreign state-owned companies from criminal prosecution.

item.title: Israel military says it is unaware of clash with Hezbollah on Syria border
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 9 (Reuters) - The Israeli military on Monday said that it ‚Äčwas unaware of any clash with ‚ÄĆHezbollah fighters in eastern Lebanon, after the Iran-backed Lebanese group said it had fought Israeli soldiers ‚Äčapproaching from Syria.
Hezbollah said in a ‚Äčstatement that its fighters had observed 15 ‚Ā†helicopters flying over eastern Lebanon just ‚Äčafter midnight, dropping Israeli troops who were observed ‚Äčapproaching Lebanese territory from an area on the Syrian side of the border.
It said that its fighters "confronted ‚Äčthe helicopters and the infiltrating forces ‚Äčwith appropriate weapons". The Israeli military, however, said that ‚ÄĆit ‚Ā†was unaware of any such incident.
Reuters was unable to immediately verify Hezbollah's account.
The Israeli military carried out an airborne raid in ‚Äčthe same area, ‚Äčnear ‚Ā†the village of Nabi Chit, overnight Friday-Saturday in what it said ‚Äčwas a mission to seek the ‚Äčremains ‚Ā†of a soldier missing since 1986. Lebanon's Health Ministry said 41 people were killed ‚Ā†in the ‚ÄčIsraeli raid.

item.title: US to designate Sudanese Muslim Brotherhood a foreign terrorist organization, State Department says
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - The ‚ÄčTrump administration ‚ÄĆis designating ‚Äčthe ‚ÄčSudanese Muslim Brotherhood ‚Ā†as ‚Äča ‚Äčspecially designated global terrorist ‚Äčand intends ‚Äčto designate it ‚ÄĆas ‚Ā†a foreign terrorist ‚Äčorganization ‚Äčeffective ‚Ā†March 16, ‚Äčthe ‚ÄčU.S. ‚Ā†State Department ‚Ā†said ‚Äčon ‚ÄčMonday.

item.title: IEA called for joint release of emergency oil stocks at G7 online meeting, Japan says
item.description:
TOKYO, March 9 (Reuters) - The International Energy Agency called ‚Äčfor a coordinated ‚ÄĆrelease of emergency oil reserves during online meeting ‚Äčwith the ‚Äčfinance ministers from the Group ‚Ā†of Seven nations ‚Äčon Monday, Japanese ‚ÄčFinance Minister Satsuki Katayama told a briefing.
"IEA called for ‚Äčeach country to ‚Äčdo coordinated release of oil ‚ÄĆreserves," ‚Ā†Katayama said, as the ministers gathered online to discuss the ‚Äčimpact ‚Äčof ‚Ā†war in Iran on the ‚Äčmarkets which saw ‚Äčoil ‚Ā†prices surging to more than $119 a barrel ‚Ā†on ‚ÄčMonday.

item.title: Standard Lithium-Equinor JV inks lithium offtake deal with Trafigura
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Standard Lithium (SLI.V) , opens new tab said on Monday its joint venture with Norway's Equinor (EQNR.OL) , opens new tab ‚Äčhad signed its first binding offtake ‚ÄĆagreement to supply lithium carbonate from its s outhwest Arkansas project to commodities trader ‚ÄčTrafigura.
The joint venture, Smackover Lithium, will ‚Äčsupply Trafigura with 8,000 metric tons ‚Ā†per year of battery-grade lithium ‚Äčcarbonate over a 10-year period starting when ‚Äčcommercial production begins.
The agreement marks a key milestone for the southwest Arkansas project as the ‚Äčpartners seek to secure long-term ‚Äčcustomers and financing ahead of a final investment ‚ÄĆdecision ‚Ā†and construction.
Smackover Lithium is targeting about 22,500 metric tons of annual lithium carbonate production capacity in the project's first ‚Äčphase and ‚Äčplans ‚Ā†to secure offtake agreements for around 80% of that output.
The ‚ÄčTrafigura agreement covers more than ‚Äč40% ‚Ā†of that targeted volume.
Smackover Lithium expects to reach a final investment decision ‚Ā†on ‚Äčthe project in 2026, ‚Äčwith first production anticipated in 2028.

item.title: Oil shock to worsen should US-Israel seize Iran's Kharg Island, JP Morgan says
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 - Iran's oil exports would stall and output halve if the U.S. and Israel were to seize its port on Kharg Island, triggering further attacks from ‚ÄčTehran on regional oil infrastructure, JP Morgan said in a note.
Axios reported ‚ÄĆon March 7 that the U.S. administration had discussed seizing the island, which sits some 30 km off Iran's coast in the Gulf and processes 90% of its crude exports.
"A direct strike ‚Äčwould immediately halt the bulk of Iran‚Äôs crude exports, likely triggering severe ‚Äčretaliation in the Strait of Hormuz or against regional energy infrastructure," ‚Ā†JP Morgan said.
Iran , the third largest producer in the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting ‚ÄčCountries, pumps about 4.5% of global oil supplies, with output of about 3.3 million ‚Äčbarrels per day of crude, plus 1.3 million bpd of condensate and other liquids.
During the 1979 Iran hostage crisis, U.S. President Jimmy Carter imposed sanctions on Iran but refrained from ordering strikes ‚Äčon the island. His successor, Ronald Reagan, during the 1980s Iran-Iraq Tanker War, prioritised ‚Äčprotecting shipping and targeting Iranian vessels and missile batteries, leaving Kharg untouched.
"Although Iraqi forces struck some ‚ÄĆterminals ‚Ā†and tankers during the eight-year war, Kharg remained largely operational and damage was typically repaired quickly, demonstrating that disabling it would require sustained, large-scale attacks," JP Morgan said.
The island collects oil coming by pipeline from Iran‚Äôs largest producing fields, including Ahvaz, Marun ‚Äčand Gachsaran.
In the days ‚Äčleading up to ‚Ā†the U.S.-Israeli attack, Iran ramped up exports from Kharg to near record levels, loading over 3 million bpd over February 15-20, ‚Äčnearly triple its normal export pace of around 1.3 million to ‚Äč1.6 million ‚Ā†bpd, according to JP Morgan.
Storage capacity on Kharg is estimated at roughly 30 million barrels and, according to Kpler, approximately 18 million barrels of crude are currently stored on ‚Ā†the ‚Äčisland, equivalent to roughly 10-12 days of exports under ‚Äčnormal conditions.
Oil prices jumped to $119 per barrel on Monday as production cuts in the Middle East spread with Iraq , Kuwait , Saudi Arabia and ‚Äčthe United Arab Emirates all affected.

item.title: Shipper MSC introduces emergency fuel surcharges
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Shipping company MSC on Monday announced emergency fuel ‚Äčsurcharges on all cargo ‚ÄĆfrom the Mediterranean and Black Sea to the Indian sub-continent, ‚ÄčRed Sea and ‚ÄčEast Africa.
The move includes the ‚Ā†West Mediterranean, Adriatic, the ‚ÄčEast Mediterranean, Greece and Turkey ‚Äčand applies from March 16 until March 31, it said.
Details (per twenty-foot ‚Äčequivalent unit, or TEU):
Western ‚ÄčMediterranean and Adriatic
Eastern Mediterranean and Black Sea

item.title: Netherlands sends frigate to Mediterranean on protective mission
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 9 (Reuters) - The Dutch government on ‚ÄčMonday said it ‚ÄĆwould send a frigate to the Mediterranean ‚Äčat France's ‚Äčrequest to help protect ‚Ā†Cyprus and other ‚Äčallies and secure maritime ‚Äčtraffic imperilled by the escalating crisis in the ‚ÄčMiddle East.
The government ‚Äčsaid last week that ‚ÄĆFrance ‚Ā†had asked it to provide an air defence and ‚Äčcommand frigate ‚Äčto ‚Ā†support the Charles de ‚ÄčGaulle aircraft carrier ‚Äčthat ‚Ā†President Emmanuel Macron said would be ‚Ā†sent ‚Äčto the ‚ÄčMediterranean.

item.title: Exclusive: US nears deal to resume intelligence operations in Mali
item.description:
DAKAR, March 9 (Reuters) - The U.S. is nearing a deal with Mali that will allow Washington to resume flying aircraft and drones over the West African country's airspace to gather intelligence on jihadist groups linked to al Qaeda, according to one current ‚ÄčU.S. official and a former U.S. official.
Last month, Washington made the first move to implement the agreement by lifting sanctions targeting the defense minister and other senior officials who ‚ÄĆit said had ties with Russian mercenaries, a key request by Mali's government, the sources said. The U.S. hopes this will lead Mali to grant the U.S. permission to fly intelligence-gathering missions over the country's vast terrain where jihadists have been gaining ground, according to the current and former U.S. officials.
The Trump administration has sought to rebuild ties with Mali after they came under strain during the previous administration.
In Washington, the goal to resume intelligence gathering is partly driven by a ‚Äčdesire to find an American pilot who was kidnapped by armed men while working for Christian missionaries in neighboring Niger. The pilot is currently believed to be held in Mali by ‚Äčthe local al Qaeda affiliate, Jama'at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin (JNIM), the officials said.
The State Department declined to comment.
A Malian government spokesperson did not respond to a request ‚Ā†for comment.
But after the sanctions were lifted on February 27, Mali's government praised the move, describing it as a step that would help "improve relations between our two countries, while reiterating respect for national sovereignty."
Mali, ‚Äča gold-producing, landlocked nation in the Sahel region of Africa, is roughly twice the size of France. Like several of its neighbors in the Sahel, it is battling an increasingly violent insurgency led by JNIM.
JNIM ‚Äčhas pursued widespread kidnapping campaigns targeting foreign nationals to finance its operations in West Africa, according to U.S. crisis-monitoring group Armed Conflict Location & Event Data.
The top U.S. envoy to Africa, Nick Checker, visited Mali last month to meet with Malian Foreign Minister Abdoulaye Diop. The goal was to convey Washington's "desire to chart a new course in the bilateral relationship and move past policy missteps," according to the State Department, an apparent reference to Biden ‚Äčadministration policies.
Diop praised American efforts to relaunch the relationship with a new approach based on mutual respect for its sovereignty and non-interference.
The Trump administration has largely dropped efforts to promote elections in the Sahel ‚Äčregion, where military officials have overthrown elected but unpopular, Western-backed governments in recent years and turned to Russia for security support.
That has been a welcome move in Bamako and other Sahelian capitals. In the Sahel, perceived lecturing by ‚ÄĆWashington is often ‚Ā†viewed as paternalistic and even racist, according to Catherine Nzuki of the Center for Strategic and International Studies, a U.S.-based think tank, which published a note about the U.S. loss of a base in Niger.
The Biden administration‚Äôs efforts to promote democracy after the coups helped trigger a break with Niger in 2024, with Niamey ordering all U.S. troops to leave a newly built, sprawling drone base in Agadez after a meeting with Biden officials went poorly. The multimillion-dollar base was intended to underpin intelligence-gathering operations across the region.
But the Trump administration's effort to improve relations has also encountered setbacks. Mali, Niger and Burkina Faso in December took ‚Äčsteps to implement a reciprocal travel ban on Americans after ‚Äčthe three nations were added to the ‚Ā†White House's travel ban list.
In Mali, militants allied with al Qaeda and other groups have gained influence, spreading across the region despite a nearly decade-long intervention backed by France and the West, and later the efforts of Russian mercenaries. Over the past year, suspected jihadists have attacked gold mining assets ‚Äčand key highways , starving the capital of fuel for businesses and schools.
Reuters was unable to determine whether Mali was taking steps to give Washington permission ‚Äčto conduct missions in its ‚Ā†airspace or when it was expected to take action.
The former U.S. official said cooperation was likely to come eventually because it was in Mali's interest to allow the U.S. to resume intelligence-gathering operations. ‚ÄúThey will derive direct benefit from U.S. ISR activities where their national capabilities are insufficient,‚ÄĚ the former official said, referring to intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance.
"This overflight would build on likely ongoing cooperation with other USG entities,‚ÄĚ the former official added, referring to ‚Ā†U.S. government agencies.
The U.S. ‚Äčhas shared intelligence with Mali that helped lead to a strike on senior JNIM leadership last year.
It wasn‚Äôt immediately clear where ‚Äčthe U.S. might base aircraft if Mali proceeds with granting permission to resume overflight operations. Ivory Coast has previously been discussed as an option, as it shares its northern border with Mali. Ghana hosted U.S. contractors late last year when the Trump administration began surveillance ‚Äčflights over Nigeria, ahead of its Christmas Day bombing of what the U.S. said were Islamic State positions.

item.title: Trump says Australia has taken care of some Iranian women soccer players
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump on Monday said Australian Prime ‚ÄčMinister Anthony Albanese told him ‚ÄĆfive members of the Iranian national women's soccer team had been "taken ‚Äčcare of" amid fears ‚Äčthey could be punished.
Australia's SBS ‚Ā†News earlier said five players ‚Äčhad "broken free" after the team ‚Äčwas eliminated from the Asian Cup tournament and were now under the ‚Äčprotection of the federal police, ‚Äčseeking assistance from the government.
"Five have already ‚ÄĆbeen ‚Ā†taken care of, and the rest are on their way," Trump said in a ‚ÄčTruth Social ‚ÄčPost ‚Ā†after talking to Albanese. "Some, however, feel they must ‚Äčgo back because they ‚Äčare ‚Ā†worried about the safety of their families, including threats to ‚Ā†those ‚Äčfamily members if ‚Äčthey don't return."

item.title: Israel will target anyone promoting radical ideas against it, says UN envoy
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Israel's U.N. ambassador Danny Danon said on Monday that the new supreme leader of Iran , Mojtaba Khamenei, ‚Äčhas the same radical ideas as in the past ‚ÄĆand Israel will target anyone who promotes radical ideas against it.
"Changing the man at the top does not change the regime," Danon told reporters at the United ‚ÄčNations when asked about Iran's naming of Khamenei to replace ‚Äčhis father Ali Khamenei, who was killed on the ‚Ā†first day of the war the U.S. and Israel launched against Iran at ‚Äčthe end of last month.
"The new leader, unfortunately, is more of the ‚Äčsame ideology, the same radical ideas, and ... anyone who will promote those radical ideas against us, we will target them, we will find them," Danon said.
Danon said ‚Äčthe people of Iran should rise up to choose their next ‚Äčleader and added: "We will have to create the conditions for them, and that ‚ÄĆis ‚Ā†what we are doing now."
Asked about the threat to crucial energy traffic in the Strait of Hormuz and Iran's attacks against neighboring countries, Danon said Israel and the U.S. were hunting Iran's missile launchers and ‚Äčdegrading its capabilities.
"So ‚Äčit's going to ‚Ā†be harder for them to attack civilians in Dubai and to attack vessels in the Strait of ‚ÄčHormuz. It doesn't mean it will be 100% guaranteed, ‚Äčbut ‚Ā†it will be harder for them to do that.
"So I'm optimistic about that. Every day we see the numbers of attacks, going down," Danon ‚Ā†said.
The ‚Äčwar has effectively shut the Strait of ‚ÄčHormuz, where a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas is shipped along ‚ÄčIran's coast.

item.title: Anthropic sues to block Pentagon blacklisting over AI use restrictions
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - Anthropic on Monday filed a lawsuit to block ‚Äčthe Pentagon from placing it on a national security blacklist , escalating the artificial intelligence lab‚Äôs high-stakes battle with the U.S. military over usage ‚ÄĆrestrictions on its technology.
Anthropic said in its lawsuit that the designation was unlawful and violated its free speech and due process rights. The filing in federal court in California asked a judge to undo the designation and block federal agencies from enforcing it.
‚ÄúThese actions are unprecedented and unlawful. The Constitution does not allow the government to wield its enormous power to punish a ‚Äčcompany for its protected speech," Anthropic said.
The Pentagon on Thursday slapped a formal supply-chain risk designation on Anthropic, limiting use of a technology that a ‚Äčsource said was being used for military operations in Iran . Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth designated Anthropic after the startup refused to remove ‚Ā†guardrails against using its AI for autonomous weapons or domestic surveillance. The two sides had been in increasingly contentious talks over those limitations for months, Reuters first reported.
Anthropic ‚Äčofficials said the lawsuit doesn't preclude re-opening negotiations with the U.S. government and reaching a settlement. The company has said it does not want to be fighting with ‚Äčthe U.S. government. The Pentagon said it wouldn't comment on litigation. Last week, a Pentagon official said the two sides were no longer active talks.
The designation poses a big threat to Anthropic‚Äôs business with the government, and the outcome could shape how other AI companies negotiate restrictions on military use of their technology, though the company's CEO Dario Amodei clarified on Thursday that the ‚Äčdesignation had "a narrow scope" and businesses could still use its tools in projects unrelated to the Pentagon.
President Donald Trump has also directed the government to stop working ‚Äčwith Anthropic, whose financial backers include Alphabet's Google (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab and Amazon.com (AMZN.O) , opens new tab . Trump and Hegseth said there would be a six-month phase-out.
Reuters has reported that Anthropic's investors were racing to contain the damage ‚ÄĆcaused by ‚Ā†the fallout with the Pentagon.
Trump and Hegseth‚Äôs actions on February 27 came after months of talks with Anthropic over whether the company‚Äôs policies could constrain military action and shortly after Amodei met with Hegseth in hopes of reaching a deal.
The Pentagon said U.S. law, not a private company, would determine how to defend the country and insisted on having full flexibility in using AI for ‚Äúany lawful use,‚ÄĚ asserting that Anthropic‚Äôs restrictions could endanger American lives.
Anthropic said even the best AI models were not ‚Äčreliable enough for fully autonomous weapons ‚Äčand that using them for that ‚Ā†purpose would be dangerous. The company also drew a red line on domestic surveillance of Americans, calling that a violation of fundamental rights.
After Hegseth‚Äôs announcement, Anthropic said in a statement that the designation would be legally unsound and set a dangerous ‚Äčprecedent for companies that negotiate with the government. The company said it would not be swayed by ‚Äúintimidation or punishment,‚ÄĚ and ‚Äčon Thursday Amodei reiterated ‚Ā†that Anthropic would challenge the designation in court.
He also apologized for an internal memo published on Wednesday by tech news site The Information. In the memo, which was written last Friday, Amodei said Pentagon officials did not like the company in part because ‚Äúwe haven‚Äôt given dictator-style praise to Trump."
The Defense Department signed agreements worth up to $200 million ‚Ā†each with major ‚ÄčAI labs in the past year, including Anthropic, OpenAI and Google.
Microsoft-backed (MSFT.O) , opens new tab OpenAI announced a deal to ‚Äčuse its technology in the Defense Department network shortly after Hegseth moved to blacklist Anthropic. CEO Sam Altman said the Pentagon shared OpenAI's principles of ensuring human oversight of weapon systems and opposing ‚Äčmass U.S. surveillance.

item.title: Brazil's finance minister expected to step down next week, sources say
item.description:
BRASILIA, March 9 (Reuters) - Brazilian Finance Minister Fernando Haddad is expected to step down by the end of next week ‚Äčto run for governor of Sao Paulo state as pushed ‚ÄĆfor by President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, four sources told Reuters on Monday.
Haddad has not yet had a final conversation with Lula about running ‚Äčfor governor, one of the sources said, but the two ‚Äčhave been speaking frequently about the matter.
The move was ‚Ā†reported by newspaper O Globo earlier in the day. The finance ‚Äčministry declined to comment.
Lula's push for Haddad to run in Sao ‚ÄčPaulo reflects the president's need for a strong presence in a state that often votes heavily for the right-wing opposition. A weak performance for the leftist ‚Äčleader in Sao Paulo could undermine his presidential campaign, according ‚Äčto his party.
Haddad had not planned to run for office this year and previously ‚ÄĆvoiced ‚Ā†his intention to serve as one of the coordinators of Lula's re-election campaign .
Sources told Reuters, however, that expectations of a tough election for the president have put pressure on Haddad to become a candidate ‚Äčin Sao Paulo.
A Datafolha ‚Äčpoll released ‚Ā†over the weekend showed that a potential second-round runoff between Lula and Senator Flavio Bolsonaro, the eldest ‚Äčson of former President Jair Bolsonaro, would result in ‚Äča ‚Ā†technical tie.
Another Datafolha poll focused on Sao Paulo state showed Haddad would take 31% of the vote in a first round against 44% ‚Ā†for Governor ‚ÄčTarcisio de Freitas, backed by the ‚ÄčBolsonaro family, who defeated Haddad in 2022.

item.title: US indicts two alleged 'terrorists' accused of trying to bomb NYC protest
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - The U.S. ‚ÄčDepartment of ‚ÄĆJustice has indicted ‚Äčtwo ‚Äčpeople accused of trying ‚Ā†to ‚Äčbomb a ‚Äčprotest in New York City, ‚ÄčAttorney ‚ÄčGeneral Pam Bondi ‚ÄĆsaid ‚Ā†in a post on X ‚Äčon ‚ÄčMonday, ‚Ā†calling them "ISIS-inspired ‚Äčterrorists."

item.title: Ukraine ready for new peace talks 'at any moment', Zelenskiy says
item.description:
KYIV, March 9 (Reuters) - Ukraine is ready for ‚Äčnew U.S.-backed peace talks "at ‚ÄĆany moment", but its partners' attention is ‚Äčcurrently focused ‚Äčon the Iran conflict, President ‚Ā†Volodymyr Zelenskiy ‚Äčsaid on Monday, adding ‚Äčthat the U.S. had asked to postpone an ‚Äčupcoming meeting.
Writing on ‚ÄčX, Zelenskiy said Moscow was ‚ÄĆtrying ‚Ā†to manipulate the conflict in its favour and aiming ‚Äčto ‚Äčturn ‚Ā†Iran's strikes on its neighbours ‚Äčand U.S. bases ‚Äčinto "a ‚Ā†second front of Russia's war against ‚Ā†Ukraine".

item.title: Two right-wing politicians lead in Peru's presidential race, but most are undecided, poll says
item.description:
LIMA, March 9 (Reuters) - Two right-wing candidates - the daughter ‚ÄĆof a former jailed president and the former mayor of Lima - are out in front of other contenders for Peru's April 12 presidential election, according to the latest poll, though many voters are either undecided or do not plan ‚Äčon voting.
Keiko Fujimori and Rafael Lopez Aliaga are the only candidates out of a record ‚Äčroster of 36 presidential hopefuls polling in the double digits. Fujimori polled at ‚Ā†10.7% versus her rival's 10%, according to a Datum Internacional poll released Sunday night on ‚Äčlocal TV, though roughly two-fifths of those polled either declined to choose a candidate or do not ‚Äčplan on voting in the first place.
The scant support for the leaders suggests that a June 7 runoff is likely, which would be typical for the Andean nation, as no candidate has won in the first round since ‚Äčthe end of the last century.
The job of Peru's head of state has become a revolving ‚Äčdoor, with eight presidents since 2018. Four former leaders are currently behind bars. Its unpopular right-wing-controlled Congress removed its ‚ÄĆlatest ‚Ā†president last month after he held secret meetings with a Chinese businessman.
Over 38% of voters polled between February 27 and March 4 were undecided or had decided not to vote, Datum found. Many of those who do vote will not decide until a week before the election, CEO Urpi Torrado ‚Äčsaid.
Fujimori, daughter of late ‚Äčformer president Alberto Fujimori, ‚Ā†who was imprisoned for 16 years for crimes against humanity - including the use of death squads during his decade-long tenure - is on her fourth run ‚Äčfor the presidency.
She has been trading places in polling with Lopez Aliaga, ‚Äčan ultra-conservative ‚Ā†former mayor who has embraced his nickname "Porky" for his resemblance to a U.S. cartoon pig, as part of his campaign. A fortnight ago, another Datum poll showed Lopez Aliaga in the lead with 13.4% and ‚Ā†Fujimori at ‚Äč9.7%.
Other candidates polling around 5% include leftist Alfonso Lopez-Chau, ‚Äča former central bank director; millionaire businessman Cesar Acuna; Carlos Alvarez, a popular comedian and impersonator of former presidents; and retired ‚Äčgeneral and former intelligence chief Wolfgang Grozo.

item.title: Trump tells NY Post he is 'nowhere near' deciding to send troops to Iran to secure nuclear stockpile
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump said on Monday that he ‚Äčwas "nowhere near" deciding whether to send ‚ÄĆU.S. troops into Iran to secure the stockpile of highly enriched uranium there.
"We haven‚Äôt made any ‚Äčdecision on that. We‚Äôre nowhere near ‚Äčit,‚ÄĚ Trump told the New York Post ‚Ā†when asked about reported discussions between ‚ÄčIsrael and the United States on ‚Äčpossibly deploying special forces to Iran to seize and secure the material.
A longstanding dispute over Iran's ‚Äčnuclear program escalated into military conflict ten ‚Äčdays ago, with the U.S. and Israel launching strikes ‚ÄĆagainst ‚Ā†Iran and Iran responding with attacks across the Middle East.
Without providing evidence, Trump said last month that Tehran was ‚Äčbeginning to ‚Äčrebuild the ‚Ā†nuclear program that he claimed had been ‚Äúobliterated‚ÄĚ by U.S. strikes ‚Äčin June last year.
Iran denies ‚Äčseeking ‚Ā†a nuclear arsenal, saying its enrichment of uranium ‚Äď a process that produces fuel for ‚Ā†power ‚Äčplants and nuclear warheads ‚Äčdepending on its duration ‚Äď is strictly for civilian use.

item.title: Rubio could designate Afghanistan as sponsor of wrongful detention, source says
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. ‚ÄčSecretary of ‚ÄĆState Marco Rubio ‚Äčis ‚Äčconsidering whether to ‚Ā†designate ‚ÄčAfghanistan as ‚Äča state sponsor of ‚Äčwrongful ‚Äčdetention and could ‚ÄĆact ‚Ā†as soon as Monday, ‚Äčaccording ‚Äčto ‚Ā†a source ‚Äčfamiliar ‚Äčwith ‚Ā†the matter.

item.title: Islamist militants kill 15 in coordinated raids across northeast Nigeria
item.description:
MAIDUGURI, Nigeria, March 9 (Reuters) - Islamist militants killed at least 12 soldiers and three civilians in coordinated overnight attacks in northeast Nigeria, military ‚Äčsources and residents said on Monday, as attacks on armed forces intensify.
A ‚ÄĆ17-year Islamist insurgency in the region has killed thousands of people and displaced 2 million, according to aid groups, despite major military campaigns.
The assaults in Kukawa, Dalwa and Goniri came days after a similar ‚Äčattack on an army post in Ngoshe and showed the militant groups' ‚Äčability to hit multiple fronts at once.
Boko Haram and Islamic State West ‚Ā†Africa Province (ISWAP) fighters swept into Kukawa district in Borno state before dawn on Monday, ‚Äčpushing towards the nearby military camp in a three-hour battle, soldiers involved told Reuters.
Troops ‚Äčlater retook the camp but not before the commanding officer and five other soldiers were killed, a military source said. Karta Maina Ma'aji Lawan, the lawmaker representing Kukawa, confirmed the attack and the officer's ‚Äčdeath.
In Dalwa, militants killed two soldiers and three residents before torching more than 250 ‚Äčhomes, said resident and traditional leader Shetima Isa.
In neighbouring Yobe state, insurgents overran the Goniri military ‚ÄĆbase, killing ‚Ā†four soldiers and setting vehicles and buildings ablaze, another soldier said.
Military spokesperson Sani Uba said troops had repelled several coordinated attacks on military positions in the northeast, and that all locations remained firmly under control.
"Regrettably, the engagements claimed the lives of some brave ‚Äčand gallant soldiers, including ‚Äčan outstanding officer ‚Ā†in Kukawa, who paid the ultimate price," Uba said, without providing further casualty details.
Ground forces, backed by air support, were conducting follow-up ‚Äčoperations in the affected areas, while cordon-and-search missions continued in nearby ‚Äčvillages where ‚Ā†wounded militants were reported to be hiding, he said.
Nigeria's military has ramped up raids on insurgent hideouts this year as part of a renewed offensive, but ISWAP and Boko Haram continue ‚Ā†to ‚Äčexploit the tough terrain, porous borders and a thin ‚Äčstate presence across the northeast's arid belt.
The latest attacks came just hours after military intelligence had warned of ‚Äča likely ISWAP offensive.

item.title: China's foreign minister calls for ceasefire in Gulf region
item.description:
BEIJING, March 9 (Reuters) - Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi on Monday ‚Äčcalled for a ceasefire in ‚ÄĆthe Gulf region as soon as possible, stressing that the ‚Äčsovereignty, security and territorial ‚Äčintegrity of Gulf countries should be ‚Ā†fully respected, according to ‚Äčstatements from his ministry.
Wang made ‚Äčthe remarks in a phone call with his Kuwaiti counterpart Jarrah Jaber ‚ÄčAl-Ahmad Al-Sabah on Monday ‚Äčas Iran answered a U.S.-Israeli bombing ‚ÄĆcampaign ‚Ā†with strikes around the Gulf region.
In a separate call, Wang told Bahraini Foreign Minister ‚ÄčAbdullatif bin ‚ÄčRashid ‚Ā†Al Zayani that China was deeply concerned about ‚Äčthe rapidly escalating situation ‚Äčand ‚Ā†would play a constructive role in maintaining peace and ‚Ā†stability ‚Äčin the region.

item.title: Exclusive: Trump reviews options to curb energy prices as Iran strikes roil markets
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump is expected to review as early as Monday a set of options to tame oil prices, ‚Äčwhich have spiked to more than $100 a barrel due to the Iran war, according to two people familiar ‚ÄĆwith the matter.
The effort reflects White House worries that the surge in oil prices will hurt U.S. businesses and consumers ahead of the November midterm elections when Trump's fellow Republicans are hoping to retain control of Congress.
U.S. officials in Washington have been discussing with counterparts from the Group of Seven major ‚Äčeconomies a possible joint release of crude oil from strategic reserves as one of several measures currently under discussion, the ‚Äčsources said.
Other options include restricting U.S. exports, intervening in oil futures markets, waiving some federal taxes and ‚Ā†lifting requirements under a U.S. law called the Jones Act that domestic fuel move only on U.S.-flagged ships, among others, the ‚Äčsources said, speaking on condition of anonymity.
Analysts have said that U.S. policy options will have little sway over global oil markets as ‚Äčlong as the fighting blocks Middle East oil exports accounting for a fifth of global supply through the Strait of Hormuz.
"The White House is in constant coordination with the relevant agencies on this important issue, as it is a top priority to the president. President Trump and his entire energy ‚Äčteam have had a strong game plan to keep the energy markets stable well before Operation Epic Fury began, and they will ‚Äčcontinue to review all credible options," White House spokesperson Taylor Rogers said in a statement, using the Trump administration's name for the U.S.-Israel military ‚ÄĆoperations targeting ‚Ā†Iran.
Global crude oil prices have hit levels not seen since mid-2022, briefly touching $119 a barrel, with gasoline and other fuel costs surging as a result since the U.S. and Israeli strikes began on February 28.
The White House last week asked federal agencies to assemble proposals that could help ease pressure on crude and gasoline prices, Reuters previously reported. The deliberations involve top White House officials, including White ‚ÄčHouse Chief of Staff Susie Wiles ‚Äčand top adviser Stephen Miller, ‚Ā†the sources said.
Analysts and industry officials have said the White House has few meaningful tools to quickly curb rising oil prices unless authorities can restore the flow of tankers through the Strait ‚Äčof Hormuz, the narrow waterway between Iran and Oman that carries roughly a fifth of the ‚Äčworld's oil supply.
"The ‚Ā†problem is options range from marginal through symbolic to deeply unwise," said one of the sources, who is engaged with the White House on the effort.
The turmoil in energy markets comes at a sensitive moment for the president, who has sought to keep fuel prices low ‚Ā†as a ‚Äčcornerstone of his economic message to voters. A prolonged spike in oil and ‚Äčgasoline costs could ripple through the broader economy, raising transportation and consumer prices.
A White House plan to provide naval escorts and backstop insurance for tankers traveling the Strait ‚Äčof Hormuz so far has failed to significantly boost shipping traffic through the vital waterway.

item.title: Back to the 1970s? Investors brace for a return of stagflation
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 (Reuters) - Investors are now seriously considering the possibility that war in the Middle East could create a stagflationary shock, just as it did 50 years ago, when disruption to global ‚Äčenergy supplies sent inflation surging, and battered growth
‚ÄúThe risk of a 1970s scenario is rising,‚ÄĚ said Kaspar Hense, portfolio manager at RBC BlueBay Asset Management.
‚ÄúIf there is another extended war, ‚ÄĆwith oil prices going up significantly further, then the safe-haven status of government bonds are at risk, and with that, all assets.‚ÄĚ
Here's how stagflation is a risk and could play out in markets in five charts.
The epicentre of stagflation fears is the surge in oil prices, and the biggest question for world markets now is how long will those prices remain elevated.
Brent crude vaulted above $100 a barrel on Monday and was on track for its biggest daily jump since the 2020 COVID ‚Äčcrisis .
It is up 70% since the start of the year, while European wholesale gas prices are at their highest level in over three years .
That's bad news for inflation.
"A useful rule of thumb is ‚Äčthat a 5% rise in oil prices adds around 0.1 percentage points to developed market inflation," Capital Economics said.
And high oil prices also could dampen economic growth.
The ‚Ā†International Monetary Fund estimates for every persistent 10% rise in oil prices, a 0.1‚Äď0.2 percent fall in global economic output follows.
Oil price surges contributed to U.S. recessions in 1973, 1980, 1990 and 2008.
This ‚Äčputs central banks in a bind, as rate hikes to restrain inflation could further undermine economic growth.
Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee told the Wall Street Journal on Friday a "stagflationary environment that's as uncomfortable as any" could be looming.
Markets now ‚Äčsee at least one European Central Bank hike this year, compared to a 40% chance of a cut before the war.
They also see a chance of a rate hike this year from the Bank of England, having earlier priced at least two cuts.
‚ÄúIt seems only retreating oil prices could reverse rate hike fears, even with dovish minds at the ECB also stressing downside growth risks,‚ÄĚ said Commerzbank rates strategist Rainer Guntermann.
That has battered global bond markets as investors ditch fixed-income assets, ‚Äčwhere inflation erodes future returns.
Short-dated bonds are the most sensitive. In Britain, two-year gilt yields have shot up by nearly 50 basis points in the last week, their worst sell-off since the budget crisis of 2022, in ‚Äčlight of the UK's sticky inflation and flat-lining growth .
German and Australian two-year yields have risen more than 30 bps in that time, while U.S. two-year yields are up a comparatively modest 13 bps. ,
That's got investors looking at inflation-linked ‚ÄĆdebt, where both ‚Ā†the principal and the interest are linked to the rate of inflation.
This has pushed British five-year breakeven inflation rates - the difference between inflation-linked and nominal yields - up 28 basis points since the end of February. On Monday, they hit almost 3.5%, their highest since last April .
Meanwhile, five-year inflation-linked Treasury yields have risen by 4.2 bps in the last week, compared with a 15-bp rise in nominal yields .
Those wondering whether the market and economic hit will cause U.S. President Donald Trump to change course have to remember the stagflationary hit from the war will likely hurt the U.S. less than Europe or Asia.
"The U.S., with the Americas, ‚Äčis self-sufficient in many (of the) commodities being choked off ‚Äčdirectly or indirectly via (Strait of) Hormuz," said Rabobank ‚Ā†senior global strategist Michael Every in a note.
As well as oil he flags fertiliser and helium, important for semiconductor manufacturing.
Not surprisingly, U.S. markets have held up better in relative terms. The S&P 500 dropped 2% last week compared to a 5.5% fall in Europe and 6.3% drop for MSCI's Asia Pacific ex-Japan index. (.SPX) , opens new tab , (.STOXX) , opens new tab , (.MIAPJ0000PUS) , opens new tab
U.S. bonds also ‚Äčoutperformed German last week. ,
However, the U.S. is by no means immune to stagflation and was looking a bit vulnerable even before the energy price spike. ‚ÄčThe economy unexpectedly shed jobs in ‚Ā†February, and data this week is expected to show a tick higher in U.S. inflation.
Investors don't like stagflation because it hurts stocks, bonds - that aren't inflation linked - and even potentially gold, given it does not have a yield.
The precious metal dropped 2% last week, and was down again on Monday, though analysts said this was in part selling to make up for losses elsewhere.
The only safe haven that has really held up since the ‚Ā†war begun has ‚Äčbeen the dollar, which has gained on nearly every other developed market currency.
"The U.S. is a major oil producer and ‚Äčcan withstand an oil shock - though there will be political fallout," said Kit Juckes, head of FX strategy at Societe Generale.
"The same simply isn‚Äôt true of Europe, and the UK in particular."

item.title: Swiss parliament approves compensation for Crans-Montana bar fire victims
item.description:
GENEVA, March 9 (Reuters) - The Swiss lower house of parliament on Monday approved plans to make a ‚Äčone-off payment of 50,000 Swiss francs ($64,000) ‚ÄĆto survivors and bereaved families of a New Year bar fire at a ski resort that killed 41 ‚Äčpeople and injured 115.
The so-called solidarity contribution, ‚Äčwhich aims to provide swift financial support ‚Ā†to victims of the blaze at the ‚ÄčLe Constellation bar in the town of Crans-Montana, ‚Äčhad already been voted through by the upper house of parliament last week.
The payment is meant to apply ‚Äčto each individual who lost their life ‚Äčin the fire and everyone who was hospitalised. Most ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†those who died were teenagers and many of the victims were foreigners, including several from France and Italy.
According to witnesses and prosecutors, the ‚Äčfire appeared ‚Äčto have ‚Ā†been started by the use of sparkling candles that ignited foam ‚Äčsoundproofing on the bar's basement ceiling.
Earlier, a document ‚Äčshowed ‚Ā†that Swiss prosecutors have widened their investigation into the fire, adding the mayor of Crans-Montana ‚Ā†and ‚Äčfour other current and former ‚Äčlocal officials to the list of suspects.
($1 = 0.7785 Swiss francs)

item.title: Venture Global pledges on-time LNG deliveries amid Middle East conflict
item.description:
LONDON/HOUSTON, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. liquefied natural gas developer Venture ‚ÄĆGlobal LNG (VG.N) , opens new tab has told customers of its Plaquemines export plant under development in Louisiana that it will start delivering contracted cargoes on schedule and at the already-agreed-upon prices, according to a letter seen by Reuters.
The Virginia-based company wrote on Friday to buyers of Plaquemines Phase 1 - ‚Äčwhich include British major Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab and Poland‚Äôs Orlen (PKN.WA) , opens new tab - and said it remains committed to beginning long‚ÄĎterm deliveries from ‚ÄčOctober 31 despite a surge in global gas prices because of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
‚ÄúAs global ‚Ā†energy markets react to the critical developments surrounding Iran and the Middle East, we wanted to assure you that, ‚Äčas of today, Phase 1 remains on schedule,‚ÄĚ the letter said.
Companies including BP (BP.L) , opens new tab , Shell, Unipec, Edison (EDNn.MI) , opens new tab , Galp (GALP.LS) , opens new tab , Repsol (REP.MC) , opens new tab and Orlen took ‚ÄčVenture Global to arbitration in one of the biggest disputes in the history of the LNG industry. The companies accused Venture Global of withholding contracted cargoes during commissioning of its nearby Calcasieu Pass plant to take advantage of higher prices on the spot market during the ‚Äč2021‚Äď2023 energy crisis.
While Venture Global - which blamed technical issues for the delays in formal commissioning at Calcasieu - prevailed over Shell, ‚ÄčBP is seeking at least $3.7 billion after winning its case, a Venture Global filing showed. Orlen‚Äôs case remains pending.
‚ÄúTo be clear, fluctuating market ‚ÄĆconditions have ‚Ā†no impact on our previously communicated schedule,‚ÄĚ a Venture Global spokesperson said. ‚ÄúAs we reaffirmed on our earnings call last week, nearly 70% of the 2026 cargoes at Plaquemines are already contracted for and we reaffirm that Phase One remains on target to declare COD in Q4 2026.‚ÄĚ
The U.S.-Israeli war on Iran and Tehran's attacks on Gulf neighbors have disrupted oil and ‚Äčnatural gas exports from the ‚ÄčMiddle East and forced production ‚Ā†stoppages, including in Qatar, which stopped operations at its LNG facilities, affecting some of the world's largest plants and a source that supplies about 20% of global LNG.
Benchmark gas prices ‚Äčhave jumped, with Dutch Title Transfer Facility futures trading at a three-year high of ‚Äčaround $21 per million ‚Ā†British thermal units, and the Japan-Korea Marker near a two-year high around $16.
Venture Global is the second-largest U.S. LNG exporter. Plaquemines is its second export terminal and it shipped 2 million metric tons of LNG last month from Plaquemines, according to LSEG data. ‚Ā†The plant ‚Äčis sending out early commission cargoes even though it is still under ‚Äčdevelopment.
The company told customers it will begin developing its annual delivery program for the first contract year in early May, followed by the second contract ‚Äčyear in July.

item.title: Oil and gas industry urge EU to pause methane emissions law
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil and gas companies urged the European Union on Monday to pause its methane emissions law, warning it could disrupt Europe's fuel ‚Äčimports when tougher provisions kick in next year.
The call from industry ‚ÄĆcomes after the U.S. government demanded the EU exempt U.S. oil and gas from the rules. Brussels has since offered companies more flexible compliance options but declined to roll back the policy, a central ‚Äčpillar of its climate strategy.
An industry-backed study by Wood Mackenzie, published on ‚ÄčMonday, said up to 43% of EU gas imports and 87% ‚Ā†of EU crude oil imports could struggle to meet the EU rules ‚Äčfrom 2027, exposing importers to heavy fines and risking supply gaps if producers are ‚Äčdeterred from shipping fuel to Europe.
IOGP and fuel manufacturing group FuelsEurope called on Brussels to pause and amend the law.
"The EU cannot afford a self-made regulatory supply shock, even more so ‚Äčin the current geopolitical context," said Francois-Regis Mouton de Lostalot, managing director of ‚ÄčIOGP Europe, whose members include ExxonMobil, Chevron, BP and TotalEnergies.
Oil and gas prices have surged ‚ÄĆthis ‚Ā†month in response to the U.S. and Israeli war on Iran , adding short-term pressure on governments already navigating Europe's phase-out of Russian energy imports. Most EU oil and gas now comes from Norway and the U.S.
The EU methane law requires that, from January ‚Äč2027, imported gas ‚Äčmust comply with ‚Ā†monitoring and verification rules equivalent to Europe's, or meet a voluntary industry standard known as "Oil and Gas Methane Partnership 2.0 ‚Äčlevel 5". The aforementioned companies are all part of the ‚ÄčOGMP initiative.
Climate ‚Ā†groups disputed the study's findings. The Environmental Defense Fund said its own analysis indicated that, following methane-reduction commitments by many oil and gas majors, the global volume of ‚Ā†OGMP ‚Äč2.0 level 5-compliant gas in 2027 would be ‚Äčmore than double EU demand, including supplies from Norway and the U.S.
A European Commission spokesperson did not ‚Äčimmediately respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Much of Iran's near-bomb-grade uranium likely to be in Isfahan, IAEA's Grossi says
item.description:
PARIS, March 9 (Reuters) - Almost half of ‚ÄĆIran's uranium enriched to up to 60% purity, a short step from weapons-grade, was stored in a tunnel complex at Isfahan and is probably still there, U.N. nuclear watchdog chief Rafael Grossi said on Monday.
The tunnel complex is the only ‚Äčtarget that appears not to have been badly damaged in attacks last June by Israel ‚Äčand the U.S. on Iran's nuclear facilities.
Diplomats have long said Isfahan has been ‚Ā†used to store 60% uranium, which the International Atomic Energy Agency confirmed in a report to member ‚Äčstates last month, without saying how much was there.
The IAEA estimates ‚Äčthat when Israel launched its first attacks in June, Iran had 440.9 kg of 60% uranium. If enriched further, that would provide the explosive needed for 10 nuclear weapons, according to an IAEA yardstick.
"What we believe is that Isfahan had ‚Äčuntil our last inspection a bit more than 200 kg, maybe a little bit more than that, ‚Äčof 60% uranium," IAEA chief Rafael Grossi told reporters in Paris.
He said the stock was "mainly" at Isfahan, and some held elsewhere ‚ÄĆmay ‚Ā†have been destroyed.
"The widespread assumption is that the material is still there. So we haven't seen - and not only us, I think in general all those observing the facility through satellite imagery and other means to see what's going on there - movement indicating that the material could have been transferred," Grossi ‚Äčsaid.
Iran has not informed the ‚ÄčIAEA of the ‚Ā†status or whereabouts of its highly enriched uranium since the June attacks, nor has it let IAEA inspectors return to its bombed facilities.
Iran's nuclear programme is ‚Äčone reason Israel and the U.S. have given for their current attacks on ‚ÄčIran , arguing ‚Ā†that it was getting too close to being able to produce a bomb, despite Trump saying in June that U.S. strikes had obliterated the programme. The IAEA has said it has no credible indication of a coordinated nuclear ‚Ā†weapons ‚Äčprogramme.
All three Iranian uranium-enrichment plants known to have been operating - two at ‚ÄčNatanz and one at Fordow - were destroyed or badly damaged in June.
"There is an amount (of 60% uranium) in Natanz also, which ‚Äčwe believe is still there," Grossi said.

item.title: Airline shares battered, airfares surge as Iran war pushes oil above $100
item.description:
HONG KONG/FRANKFURT, March 9 (Reuters) - Airline stocks were hammered on ‚ÄčMonday, while airfares soared as the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran sent oil prices surging, sparking fears of a deep travel slump and the potential for the widespread grounding ‚ÄĆof planes.
Oil prices jumped 15% to above $105 a barrel, hitting levels not seen since 2022 as some major producers cut supplies and fears of prolonged shipping disruptions gripped the market. At one point, Brent crude futures jumped as much as 29%.
Some jet fuel prices have doubled since the start of the conflict, piling pressure on carriers already navigating tight airspace as pilots reroute to avoid the Middle East conflict and thousands of stranded passengers try to leave the ‚Äčregion.
"Absent near-term relief, airlines around the world could be forced to ground thousands of aircraft while some of the industry's financially weakest carriers could halt operations," Deutsche analysts said ‚Äčin a note to clients.
In Asia, airline shares tumbled, with the worst-hit, including Korean Air Lines (003490.KS) , opens new tab , which slid 8.6%, Air ‚Ā†New Zealand (AIR.NZ) , opens new tab down 7.8% and Hong Kong's Cathay Pacific (0293.HK) , opens new tab which dropped 5%.
In Europe, Air France KLM (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab , British Airways-owner IAG (ICAG.L) , opens new tab , Wizz Air (WIZZ.L) , opens new tab and Lufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab fell between 2.5% and 6% in morning trade.
Major U.S. ‚Äčairlines shares were down about 1% to 5% in afternoon trading with JetBlue Airways (JBLU.O) , opens new tab down 5.35% followed by American Airlines (AAL.O) , opens new tab down 3.44%.
Underscoring that pain on the consumer side were jumps in ticket prices. Direct ‚Äčflights from Seoul to London on March 11 with Korean Air Lines, for example, leapt to $4,359, from $564 seven days earlier, according to Google Flights data. Flights from Los Angeles to Lima on LATAM Airlines (LTM.SN) , opens new tab rose to $2,125 compared to $499 in the same period.
"The issue for the airlines now is that travel demand may be curtailed as costs become prohibitive for leisure travellers and as some companies start to limit business travel due to the uncertain outlook," said ‚ÄčLorraine Tan, director of equity research, Asia at Morningstar.
The impact of high airfares could limit travel demand for much of 2026, Tan added.
Fuel is the second-largest expense for air carriers after labour, typically ‚Äčaccounting for a fifth to a quarter of operating expenses. Some major Asian and European airlines have oil hedging in place, but U.S. airlines largely stopped the practice over the last two decades.
"We assume the airlines are ‚ÄĆable to recapture ‚Ā†a portion of the spike in fuel prices, but it's hard to envision margin expansion this year barring a rapid decline in energy prices," said Tom Fitzgerald, TD Cowen vice president of equity research on six major U.S. airlines and Air Canada.
A one-way trip to Quebec City from Newark on March 11 aboard Air Canada nearly tripled to $1,499 compared to a week ago, according to data from Google Flights.
High prices could have severe implications for the industry.
"If crude is rising 20%, jet fuel is rising several times more as it is even more scarce, adding significant cost ‚Äčto operations together with crew resources, which are ‚Äčstretched due to longer flying times when ‚Ā†airspace is closed," said Subhas Menon, head of the Association of Asia Pacific Airlines.
The Deutsche analysts noted that a sharp spike in jet fuel costs in 2005 in the aftermath of hurricanes Katrina and Rita resulted in widespread and significant damage to the industry, including major airlines Delta and Northwest ‚Äčfiling for Chapter 11 bankruptcy that year.
Since February 28, when the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran started, through March 9, more than 40,000 flights to ‚Äčand from the Middle ‚Ā†East have been cancelled, according to data from Cirium.
With airspace severely constrained, airlines have been forced to reroute flights, carry extra fuel or make additional refuelling stops to guard against sudden diversions or longer flight paths through safer corridors.
Combined, Emirates, Qatar Airways and Etihad normally fly about one-third of the passengers from Europe to Asia and more than half of all passengers from Europe to Australia, New Zealand and nearby Pacific ‚Ā†Islands, according to ‚ÄčCirium.
Flights to Iraq, Syria, Lebanon and Jordan by Turkish Airlines (THYAO.IS) , opens new tab , AJet, Pegasus (PGSUS.IS) , opens new tab and SunExpress have been cancelled until March ‚Äč13, Turkish Transport Minister Abdulkadir Uraloglu said on Sunday.

item.title: Exclusive: Canadian oil tycoon backs South Bow pipeline to boost US exports
item.description:
CALGARY, March 9 (Reuters) - The Canadian tycoon who heads one of North America's fastest growing oil companies told Reuters he supports ‚Äča proposal to potentially revive parts of the former Keystone XL pipeline and wants to see Canada use the project as ‚ÄĆleverage in upcoming trade negotiations with the United States.
Still, Adam Waterous, CEO of Strathcona Resources (SCR.TO) , opens new tab , said he would prefer a new West Coast pipeline to increase access to Asian markets.
The comments by Waterous, one of Canada's most aggressive oil industry dealmakers, are the first public statements by a Canadian oil shipper in favour of a new pipeline proposal led by Canadian company ‚ÄčSouth Bow (SOBO.TO) , opens new tab that could increase the country's crude exports to the U.S. by more than 12%.
They are an early indication that the project ‚ÄĒ which would require both ‚Äča green light from U.S. President Donald Trump and the construction of additional links to U.S. refining hubs ‚ÄĒ has at ‚Ā†least some level of commercial support from Canadian oil sands producers at a time when the U.S.-Israel war on Iran has disrupted international supply and sent global ‚Äčcrude prices soaring.
The plan involves a different route through the U.S. than the previous Keystone XL pipeline, which was canceled by former U.S. President Joe Biden ‚Äčin 2021 after years of Indigenous and environmental opposition, but would use some of that project's already built pipe in Alberta.
South Bow launched a competitive bidding process last week offering 450,000 barrels per day of contracted pipeline capacity from Hardisty, Alberta to multiple U.S. delivery points including Cushing, Oklahoma, and destinations on the U.S. Gulf Coast, to gauge potential commercial use.
Waterous ‚Äčdeclined to say if Strathcona will commit barrels to the South Bow project, citing ongoing commercial negotiations. But he pointed to his company's stated goal to expand ‚Äčits own oil production from 125,000 barrels per day in 2026 to up to 300,000 bpd by 2035.
"I've been a very early advocate of this project," Waterous said.
"And we ‚ÄĆare looking ‚Ā†for egress now," he said, referring to oil shippers' ability to transport their product to market.
But Waterous said the South Bow proposal is not the Canadian oil industry's first choice for bolstering crude exports. Currently more than 90% of Canada's oil exports go to the United States, and Waterous said Canadian oil shippers have long wanted greater market diversification.
Canadian oil companies would prefer another pipeline to the Pacific, to supplement the existing Trans Mountain pipeline which runs from Alberta to the British Columbia coast ‚Äčand offers an outlet to Asian ‚Äčmarkets, Waterous said.
Four other large Canadian ‚Ā†oil sands producers did not respond to a Reuters request for comment on whether they support the South Bow proposal.
The Alberta government has said it will submit an application for a new crude oil pipeline to the west ‚Äčcoast for fast-track approval by the federal government, but no private company has said it will build the project.
The ‚Äčmain benefit to ‚Ā†the South Bow proposal, Waterous said, is that it can likely be built faster and more cheaply than a new West Coast pipeline, while also carrying potential political benefits for Canada.
He said Canada should try to leverage Trump's previously stated comments that he would like to see the Keystone XL project built.
Prime Minister Mark Carney has also raised with ‚Ā†Trump the prospect of ‚Äčreviving the Keystone XL oil pipeline from Alberta to the United States as part of ‚Äčhis efforts to ease trade tensions between the two countries.
"The utility of a pipeline to the south is that it is a tool to achieve a tariff agreement to protect principally Canada's steel, ‚Äčauto and aluminum sectors, while also allowing the Canadian oil sector to grow," Waterous said.

item.title: Peru's pipeline disruption tests industrial resilience; businesses blanch at remote work order
item.description:
LIMA, March 9 (Reuters) - Peru's business groups are clashing with the new interim government over sweeping emergency measures imposed after a rupture in the country's ‚Äčmain natural‚ÄĎgas pipeline triggered the worst energy crisis in two decades, an early test of President Jose Balcazar‚Äôs ‚ÄĆinterim mandate weeks before national elections.
The breakdown of Transportadora de Gas del Peru's pipeline, the backbone of the system that supplies nearly half of Peru‚Äôs electricity and most of its LPG, has forced widespread gas rationing, pushing up energy prices and exposing long‚ÄĎstanding vulnerabilities in the Andean nation's energy ‚Äčsystem.
Balcazar, appointed on February 18 after Congress ousted his predecessor, now faces a high‚ÄĎstakes leadership test. More than ‚Äča thousand firms have struggled with halted gas production, and business associations have called for the rollback ‚Ā†of emergency directives.
Authorities say gas flows may not resume until mid‚ÄĎMarch.
The outage could stoke inflation, which has remained among the ‚Äčlowest in the world despite years of political turmoil.
"It can hit inflation, but in a heterogeneous way depending on how energy ‚Äčaffects each activity,‚ÄĚ said former Economy Minister Alonso Segura.
Inflation reached 0.69% in February and could exceed the 0.81% recorded in March last year, Segura said.
Natural gas is used to produce 70% of the LPG consumed domestically. Some 2.1 million households rely on piped gas and about one million vehicles ‚Äčrun on natural gas or LPG.
Peru is the world‚Äôs third-largest copper producer.
Former Energy and Mines Minister Carlos Herrera said the rupture ‚Äčunderscored Peru‚Äôs vulnerability after an alternative pipeline project was canceled in 2017 when the Brazilian construction company Odebrecht was unable to secure financing ‚ÄĆwhile being ‚Ā†investigated in a case involving bribes paid to secure public works contracts in Peru.
"This crisis highlights the need to diversify the country‚Äôs energy matrix," Herrera said. "The serious problem we have is that we haven‚Äôt made exploratory efforts for natural gas over time either."
Industry groups said more than 1,000 companies have been affected, with some halting production. Felipe James, head of the National Society of Industry (SNI), ‚Äčsaid emergency rules allowing firms ‚Äčto switch temporarily to alternative ‚Ā†fuels will be costly and not all companies are equipped to make the change.
"Not all can switch... they are not prepared," James said.
On Friday, the government ordered public employees in Lima to ‚Äčwork remotely for a week and encouraged private firms to do the same. Schools and ‚Äčuniversities will shift ‚Ā†to virtual classes during the contingency.
Business associations criticized the measures, saying they amount to a partial shutdown of the economy.
"Stopping the country can never be the solution. We demand this decision be reversed," said COMEX, the foreign trade guild.
Peru's mining and energy chamber said it ‚Ā†was working "intensely" ‚Äčto mitigate the crisis and seeking to import LPG in "record time."
Spokespeople for ‚Äčthe presidency and the Ministry of Energy and Mines did not respond to a request for comment.
The TGP gas pipeline began operating in 2004 and also ‚Äčenables the export of natural gas to Japan, South Korea and China.

item.title: Two men charged with terrorism after homemade bomb thrown at anti-Islam protesters in New York
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - Two men accused of igniting homemade bombs at a chaotic anti-Islam protest outside the New York City mayor's mansion told police they were inspired by Islamic State, the Islamist militant group, and were charged on Monday with ‚Äčfederal terrorism-related crimes, according to court records.
Emir Balat, 18, and Ibrahim Kayumi, 19, are accused of traveling from Pennsylvania with ‚ÄĆdangerous improvised explosive devices to Manhattan on Saturday, where a small group of far-right activists had planned the anti-Muslim rally outside Gracie Mansion against Zohran Mamdani, New York City's first Muslim mayor.
Balat was captured in photographs and video lighting a small IED and throwing it towards the anti-Muslim protesters before lighting a second device ‚Äčhanded to him by Kayumi and dropping it, according to the criminal complaint. Police were able to intercept the smoking devices, ‚Äčduct-taped jars containing screws and bolts and the explosive substance TATP, and no one was hurt.
After police arrested ‚Ā†them, they said they were inspired by Islamic State, and Balat told police that he wanted to carry out an attack "even bigger" than ‚Äčthe Boston Marathon bombing in 2013 that killed three people, according to the criminal complaint filed in the U.S. district court in Manhattan on ‚ÄčMonday.
Mehdi Essmidi, who represents Balat, did not immediately reply to a request for comment. Kayumi's lawyer Michael Arthus declined to comment.
Speaking alongside police outside Gracie Mansion on Monday, Mamdani condemned the violence. He and his wife were not home at the time, because they had known about the "Stop the Muslim takeover" rally organized by ‚Äčfar-right activist Jake Lang more than a week in advance. More than 100 counter-protesters gathered nearby, the vast majority peaceful, saying they were there ‚Äčto "run Nazis out" of New York.
"New York City will never tolerate violence whether from protests or counter-protests," Mamdani told reporters. He said while he thought the ‚ÄĆfar-right anti-Muslim ‚Ā†rally was "vile", he defended the "sacred right" of protest, even for objectionable speech.
Police said it was the first time an improvised bomb was deployed in New York City in nearly a decade. In 2017, Akayed Ullah, who also cited Islamic State as inspiration, ignited a homemade bomb strapped to his body in a passage near the Times Square subway station, wounding only himself.
Balat and Kayumi are each charged with five federal crimes, ‚Äčaccording to a complaint filed by ‚Äčthe U.S. attorney's office in Manhattan, ‚Ā†including use of a weapon of mass destruction, attempting to provide material support to a designated foreign terrorist organization, and three charges related to the illegal possession or transportation of explosives.
During their separate police interviews, ‚ÄčBalat asked for a piece of paper and wrote on it he had pledged allegiance to Islamic ‚ÄčState, while Kayumi ‚Ā†told police he watched Islamic State videos on his phone, according to the criminal complaint.
A third man, Ian McGinnis, was arrested and appeared in a New York state court on Sunday accused of squirting pepper spray or a similar chemical irritant into the faces of two of the counter-protesters opposing ‚Ā†the anti-Islam ‚Äčrally, according to the Manhattan district attorney's office and police. McGinnis faces six charges, including ‚Äčthird-degree assault.
Police in New York have been on alert since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran, but did not say there was a sign of Saturday's attack being linked ‚Äčto the war.

item.title: Trump says war against Iran is 'very complete,' CBS News reports
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump ‚Äčthinks the war against Iran "is ‚ÄĆvery complete" and that Washington was "very far ahead" of his initial four ‚Äčto five week estimated ‚Äčtime frame, according to a CBS News ‚Ā†reporter on X, who cited an ‚Äčinterview with him.
"I think the ‚Äčwar is very complete, pretty much. They have no navy, no communications, they‚Äôve ‚Äčgot no Air Force," Trump ‚Äčwas quoted as saying by CBS News' ‚ÄĆWhite ‚Ā†House correspondent Weijia Jiang.
Trump also said the U.S. is "very far" ahead of his initial 4-5 ‚Äčweek estimated ‚Äčtime frame ‚Ā†for the war.
On Iran's new Supreme leader Mojtaba ‚ÄčKhamenei, Trump told CBS ‚ÄčNews: "I ‚Ā†have no message for him." Trump said he has someone in ‚Ā†mind ‚Äčto replace Khamenei, ‚Äčbut did not elaborate.

item.title: First Brands row hints at banks‚Äô shadow exposure
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Cockroach troubles tend to extend further than they first appear. JPMorgan boss Jamie Dimon likened high-profile credit ‚Äčblowups to the sneaky insects, implying that deeper problems lurk. And now, six months after heavily indebted auto-parts retailer ‚ÄĆFirst Brands toppled, regional lender Western Alliance (WAL.N) , opens new tab has disclosed , opens new tab a $126 million hit from the mess. It never lent directly to First Brands, but instead to a fund run by Wall Street financiers at Jefferies (JEF.N) , opens new tab . It‚Äôs just one of many smaller banks piling into the murky world of shadow finance.
First Brands‚Äô surprise bankruptcy last ‚Äčyear sent a chill through credit markets. Federal prosecutors alleged , opens new tab that it pledged assets multiple times over to secure billions of ‚Äčborrowings, largely from non‚ÄĎbank lenders. It was not an isolated case, with the government also indicting , opens new tab the ‚Ā†founder of sub-prime auto lender Tricolor on similar claims. Jefferies, meanwhile, also lent to now-teetering real-estate lender Market Financial Solutions.
Western Alliance claims a ‚Äčdeliberate breach of contract, saying that Jefferies has ceased to pay $126 million outstanding on loans to special-purpose vehicles that held First Brands‚Äô ‚Äčaccounts receivable. Jefferies said , opens new tab on Monday that these are non-recourse loans, with no guarantee of payment from the SPVs‚Äô parents, and that the suit lacks merit.
Whatever the case, shares of Western Alliance and Jefferies have tanked 11% and 14%, respectively, since Thursday. Morgan Stanley analysts downgraded their recommendation on Jefferies‚Äô shares, citing ‚Äčthe litigation.
Western Alliance is a relative pipsqueak, at roughly $8 billion in market value. Yet many similarly sized institutions have been swept up ‚Äčin the flood of lending to lightly regulated firms that the Federal Reserve labels ‚Äúnon-depository financial institutions.‚ÄĚ Lending to these shadow banks has climbed to $1.9 trillion, ‚ÄĆup 180% ‚Ā†from $680 billion in 2021, according to Fed data. This growth tracks the vertiginous rise of private credit and asset-backed lending , which have increasingly displaced traditional banks since the 2008 financial crisis. Providing leverage is a way to get a cut of the action back.
The question is how much risk this poses. Banks‚Äô equity is a loss-absorbing buffer, taking the hit if loans go awry en masse. Yet 40 ‚ÄčU.S. lenders with a combined $4.7 ‚Äčtrillion in assets now have ‚Ā†lent the equivalent of more than 100% of their total equity capital to shadow banks, up from 29 banks holding $1.1 trillion two years ago, according to data from KBRA Financial Intelligence. Nearly all borrowers ‚Äčare current on their payments, and much of the activity relates to the mortgage market, which ‚Äčenjoys effective government ‚Ā†support. Yet the territory in which cockroaches can breed is growing.
Follow Stephen Gandel on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: Iran war boosts oil price, but oil major shares are stuck on the sidelines
item.description:
LONDON/HOUSTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil prices on Monday climbed to the highest level since 2022, but shares of major producers like Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab ‚Äčand Exxon Mobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab have so far seen limited gains since the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran broke out late last month, ‚ÄĆsuggesting that traders think the pain in the market may be short term.
While crude oil futures have risen more than 40% since Israel and the U.S. launched joint airstrikes on February 28, the iShares Global Energy ETF - which tracks global energy companies - is up around 2%. That suggests that any immediate gains from increased prices will be temporary, ‚Äčor offset by reduced production.
"The market is anticipating a swift end to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and a subsequent ‚Äčcollapse in oil prices back to normalized levels," said James West, head of energy and power research at Melius ‚Ā†Research. "The rally in oil prices is primarily contained to near-term spot prices rather than longer-dated crude oil futures."
Brent crude futures and U.S. West Texas Intermediate ‚Äčcrude futures rose by around 30% to start the week, as the war on Iran has disrupted the passage of traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, ‚Äčoil and natural gas exports from the Middle East and has forced production stoppages.
At the same time, Brent's front-month contract hit an all-time high at around $36 per barrel over its 6-month delivery contract, indicating near-term supply tightness.
The rally conjured oil futures' 2008 all-time highs at around $147 per barrel, when tension between the West and Iran over its nuclear program, ‚Äča weak U.S. dollar and inflationary factors pushed prices higher. Continued growth in China and other emerging economies was also expected to offset the ‚Äčimpact of any fall in consumption in developed countries.
However, just months after the all-time high, the 2008 financial crisis and concerns around an overbought market sunk oil to ‚ÄĆbelow $40 per ‚Ā†barrel, at the time the lowest since 2004.
"Go back to 2008," said David Hewitt, senior consultant at Hewitt Energy Perspectives. "Oil stocks followed crude's rally until about $100 then almost completely disconnected as it went to $147."
"The market was right then - $147 per barrel rapidly became $30," he added. "It's a bit of the same now."
Shell shares rose as much as 4.9% on Monday from their close on Feb. 27, while Chevron (CVX.N) , opens new tab gained 2.6% and Exxon gained 0.9% over the same period. ‚ÄčBP (BP.L) , opens new tab gained 7.8%.
Deferred oil contracts are ‚Äčnot as high as the ‚Ā†spot price, signaling that investors do not expect the supply disruption to last, said Simon Lack, portfolio manager of the Catalyst Energy Infrastructure Fund.
U.S. shale producers, which have seen a bigger boost in share prices than the global ‚Äčoil majors, may be benefitting because U.S. energy supply and infrastructure are safe and insulated from the risk, ‚Äčprompting investors to place ‚Ā†a higher value on those companies, he added.
Diamondback (FANG.O) , opens new tab , the largest independent producer in the Permian basin, has seen its shares gain as much as 7% on Monday from their close on February 27.
Simon Wong, a portfolio manager at Gabelli Funds, said share prices of Diamondback, APA Corp (APA.O) , opens new tab and Occidental (OXY.N) , opens new tab had risen less ‚Ā†year-to-date compared with ‚ÄčExxon and Chevron and are now beginning to catch up.
He also believes that the ‚Äčhigher crude prices are temporary and will likely moderate when the disruptions are over.

item.title: Surging oil drives worries for US stock investors
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - A stunning surge in oil prices above $100 a barrel is rattling U.S. stock investors, as they brace for an even sharper rise in energy prices that could stagger the economy and further damage equities.
The 50% jump in U.S. crude to its highest ‚Äčlevel in more than three years is among the most significant financial consequences of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, as investors continue to calibrate economic and market fallout following the ‚ÄĆattacks launched just over a week ago.
"It's a shock," said Michael Reynolds, vice president of investment strategy at Glenmede. "It's out of left field, and people and investors are having to figure out what it means in real time as it's unfolding."
Higher oil and gas prices stand to drive up costs for energy-intensive companies, erode the discretionary spending budgets of consumers and lead to worries about inflation that could stop the Federal Reserve from lowering interest rates.
Reynolds and other investors were scrambling to ‚Äčgame out scenarios should oil reach heights that were not on the horizon just days earlier.
U.S. and Brent crude on Monday both broke above $100 a barrel, which investors have pointed to as a ‚Äčlevel that could create more stock turbulence. The commodities at one point on Monday neared $120. U.S. crude settled at $67.02 on February 27, the last session before the ‚Ā†U.S.-Israeli strikes.
Meanwhile, stock volatility has spiked. The Cboe Volatility Index (.VIX) , opens new tab on Monday topped 30 for the first time in nearly a year, up from below 20 in late February.
While the declines for U.S. stocks have been moderate ‚Äčin comparison to other global regions, the benchmark S&P 500 (.SPX) , opens new tab was last down nearly 4% from its late January all-time closing high, after paring steeper declines earlier in Monday's session.
Strategists at Yardeni Research said last Tuesday they ‚Äčexpected a 10% stock market correction.
