"Thankfully, one of our older vendors helped us," he added, because the ‚Äčchain offers immediate ‚Ā†payment, rather than credit terms of a week or month. "We're also seeing a spike in the price of the sunflower oil that we use for cooking."
Few restaurants stockpile LPG cylinders, because of safety concerns, relying instead on frequent replacement, said Veerendra Kamat, secretary ‚Ā†of the ‚ÄčBengaluru Hotels Association.
"It's a very grave situation. Most of the (gas) companies have ‚Äčstopped supplying," said Ananth Narayan of the NRAI's Bengaluru branch, adding that even those restaurants that do store gas could run out within a week or ‚Äčtwo.

item.title: Middle East countries cut daily oil output, Bloomberg News reports
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia has lowered oil ‚Äčoutput by between ‚ÄĆ2 million and 2.5 million ‚Äčbarrels a day, ‚Äčand the United ‚Ā†Arab Emirates has ‚Äčcut its output ‚Äčby 500,00-800,000 barrels a day, Bloomberg News ‚Äčreported on ‚ÄčTuesday.
Kuwait has also cut output ‚ÄĆby ‚Ā†half a million barrels a day, and Iraq ‚Äčby ‚Äčabout ‚Ā†2.9 million, the report ‚Äčadded, citing ‚Äčpeople ‚Ā†with knowledge of the matter.
Reuters could ‚Ā†not ‚Äčimmediately ‚Äčverify the report.

item.title: India invokes force majeure, gives priority to gas sales to key sectors
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 10 - India has invoked emergency measures to divert gas supplies from non-priority ‚Äčsectors to key users after the ‚ÄĆdisruption of liquefied natural gas shipments through the Strait of Hormuz, a government notification said.
India meets ‚Äčhalf of its 195 million standard ‚Äčcubic metres per day (mmscmd) of gas consumption ‚Ā†through imports. The country was getting ‚Äčabout 60 mmscmd gas from the Middle East ‚Äčprior to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and force majeure by Qatar .
Depending on the availability of ‚Äčgas, India will be supplying the ‚Äčfuel in proportion to the average use over the ‚ÄĆlast ‚Ā†six months by priority sectors, the notification, issued late on Monday, said.

item.title: German start-up plans 30-megawatt AI data centre in boost to sovereign control
item.description:
FRANKFURT, March 10 (Reuters) - German start-up Polarise plans to build a new 30-megawatt artificial intelligence ‚Äčdata centre that would double Germany's domestically-run computing capacity as European nations push ‚ÄĆto gain more control over critical tech infrastructure.
The facility, set to come online in the Bavarian town of Amberg in mid-2027, could eventually expand to 120 MW, the company told Reuters.
AI data centres in ‚ÄčGermany boasted total capacity of 530 MW at the end of last year, according ‚Äčto figures from German lobby group Bitkom. Much of that, however, was ‚Ā†operated by non-German providers.
European countries are pushing for more sovereign control over data and ‚Äčthe infrastructure required for AI due to global tensions - from tariffs to armed conflict - as well ‚Äčas sharply diverging rules around online content.
The plans for what would be one of Germany's largest data centres have not been previously reported. Large technology companies like Google (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab and Amazon's (AMZN.O) , opens new tab ‚ÄčAWS typically run data centres of around 100 MW or more.
Polarise, which operates 13 ‚Äčdata centres in Germany and abroad, declined to give details on the size of the required investment.
"The final ‚ÄĆinvestment ‚Ā†volume depends heavily on how many customers install their own servers or rent computing power," said Marc Gazivoda, Polarise's marketing director, adding that it did not receive state subsidies and investments could vary.
A source close to the company said the first stage of the project would ‚Äčcost in the "triple-digit million ‚Äčeuro range", which ‚Ā†includes the main infrastructure but not the chips themselves. The final cost would depend on the number of chips required and their specific ‚Äčtypes, the source said.
A 12-MW data centre opened by Polarise in ‚ÄčMunich last ‚Ā†month cost 1 billion euros ($1.16 billion), according to an estimate by Deutsche Telekom. That facility already doubled Germany's existing locally-operated capacity.
With oil prices shooting above $100 per barrel, the cost of energy for ‚Ā†power-hungry ‚Äčdata centres is increasingly in focus.
Polarise said its partner ‚ÄčWV Energie will install a wind and solar power plant for the centre as well as batteries for temporary ‚Äčstorage of electricity.
($1 = 0.8640 euros)

item.title: Horse racing-Leading British horses line up for stellar first day as Cheltenham Festival begins
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - British trainers Dan Skelton and Nicky Henderson will take on the might of the Irish at jump racing's showcase event with the likes of The New Lion ‚Äčand Lulamba vying for a share of the five-million-pound ($6.70 million) prize pot as the Cheltenham Festival opens on Tuesday.
Attracting some 200,000 racegoers across four ‚ÄĆdays, the festival is worth an estimated 274 million pounds to the local economy boosting local restaurants, hotels and pubs.
It is also horse racing's biggest betting week of the year with bookmaker William Hill predicting 450 million pounds will be wagered across Britain. Guinness, reduced in price by 30 pence, will be drunk by the gallon.
But for those at the heart of the sport, it is all about the horses ‚Äčand whether this year's crop of jumps stars can rival the Irish.
It is almost two decades since the golden years of British jump racing when Somerset-based Paul ‚ÄčNicholls and his crop of horses like dual Gold Cup-winner Kauto Star and 18-times consecutive hurdle winner Big Buck's outclassed the rest, before ‚Ā†the pendulum swung in favour of the Irish.
Since then, Willie Mullins' sizeable team has continued to dominate the festival, racking up 113 winners to snatch the leading trainer title ‚Äč12 times. Backed by big-spending owners like former Barclays executive Rich Ricci and businessman J.P. McManus, this year's squad looks no less dangerous.
Mullins' pick for jump racing's most prestigious chase is ‚ÄčGaelic Warrior who looks set to go off favourite in Friday's feature, the Gold Cup, a race that has so far evaded owner Susannah Ricci. Before that, Majborough is favourite for the Queen Mother Champion Chase on Wednesday.
In with a strong chance of ending Irish dominance is a fresh crop of talented trainers that includes Skelton and Ben Pauling who will take on Mullins in the 625,000-pound Gold Cup ‚Äčwith the fancied Grey Dawning and the Harry Redknapp-owned King George VI Chase winner The Jukebox Man.
Battle lines will first be drawn in Tuesday's opening race, the Grade 1 ‚ÄčSupreme Novices' Hurdle, when Mullins' Mighty Park takes on British hope and three-time winning 2/1 favourite Old Park Star, trained by Henderson.
Henderson has made no secret of the fact a win on Tuesday would ‚ÄĆbe a ‚Ā†big boost for Seven Barrows, setting a strong tone for the week, after Constitution Hill and Sir Gino were sidelined. He also sends out highly fancied Lulamba in the Arkle Novices' Chase and Jango Baie in the Gold Cup.
Were Britain to also clinch Tuesday's feature race, the 450,000-pound Champion Hurdle in which Mullins' prolific mare Lossiemouth will battle with Skelton's The New Lion, nerves may start jangling in the Irish camp.
Skelton sends his strongest ever team to Cheltenham, lining up some 30-35 horses. But while he's bullish about some big British winners, ‚Äčhe described his chances of snatching the ‚Äčtop trainer prize at the festival as "a ‚Ā†possibility... not a probability."
"I've thought about it because I'm a competitive bugger but the reality is Willie will be leading trainer," he told the Racing Post on Sunday. "With a bit of luck, we might be pitching behind him along with a few others."
Ireland has won ‚ÄčCheltenham's Prestbury Cup title, awarded to the country fielding the most winners, outright in nine of the last 10 years.
Cheltenham's opening ‚Äčday also marks an important ‚Ā†one for British racing, which is in the spotlight for governance issues at its regulator and recent moves by gaming companies to reduce sponsorship in light of higher taxes .
Cheltenham is also looking to recover from lower ticket sales in recent years, something CEO Guy Lavender is trying to address by lowering the cost of Guinness to 2022 rates, reducing restrictions on drinking areas and reintroducing ‚Ā†Ladies Day ‚Äčon Wednesday.
"There is no doubt that everyone operating in the leisure sector is having to work harder to ‚Äčmake their offerings stand out to the customer," said Lavender.
"However, British racing continues to provide an exceptional day out and we saw total attendance across all 60 racecourses top 5 million last year for the first time ‚Äčsince 2019."
Lavender added he was "very positive" about ticket sales with growth from last year. "We are definitely moving in the right direction," he said.
($1 = 0.7460 pounds)

item.title: Russia is the only winner of Middle East war, EU's Costa says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 10 (Reuters) - Russia has so far been the only winner from the war in the ‚ÄčMiddle East as energy prices soar and ‚ÄĆattention for its war against Ukraine has faded, EU Council President Antonio Costa said on Tuesday.
"So ‚Äčfar, there is only one winner ‚Äčin this war ‚Äď Russia," Costa said in ‚Ā†a speech to EU ambassadors in Brussels.
"It ‚Äčgains new resources to finance its war ‚Äčagainst Ukraine as energy prices rise. It profits from the diversion of military capabilities that could otherwise have ‚Äčbeen sent to support Ukraine. And ‚Äčit benefits from reduced attention to the Ukrainian front ‚ÄĆas ‚Ā†the conflict in the Middle East takes centre stage."
Costa stressed the need for the EU to protect the international rules-based order, which ‚Äčhe said ‚Äčwas now ‚Ā†being challenged by the United States, and for all parties in ‚Äčthe Middle East to return to ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†negotiating table.
"Freedom and human rights cannot be achieved through bombs. Only international law upholds them," ‚Ā†he ‚Äčsaid.
"We must avoid further escalation. ‚ÄčSuch a path threatens the Middle East, Europe, and ‚Äčbeyond."

item.title: How many people have been killed in the US-Israel war on Iran?
item.description:
March 10 - Scores of people have been killed across the Middle East since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran on February 28, with Gulf states that host U.S. military bases and personnel ‚Äčas well as Lebanon quickly drawn into the conflict.
Here are the death tolls from the war ‚ÄĆas reported by countries as of March 9, more than a week after it began. Reuters has not independently verified these deaths.

item.title: Israel ahead of schedule on war goals in Iran, ambassador to France says
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - Israel is ahead of schedule in achieving its war goals in Iran, its ambassador to France ‚Äčsaid on Tuesday, adding that the operation aims ‚ÄĆto weaken Iran‚Äôs authorities to curb attacks beyond its borders and allow its people to shape their own future.
"When we were asked at ‚Äčthe start of this war about its duration, ‚Äčwe said it would last a few weeks. That ‚Ā†hasn‚Äôt changed," Joshua Zarka told BFM TV. "We are ahead ‚Äčof schedule to achieve our war objectives."
Zarka, formerly Israel‚Äôs lead ‚Äčdiplomat on Iran, said the goals extend beyond ending Iran‚Äôs nuclear programme. They include weakening the government to the point that its population ‚Äčcan "take its fate into its own hands," and ensuring ‚ÄčTehran can no longer mount attacks beyond its borders.
Asked about the appointment ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†Mojtaba Khamenei as the new Supreme Leader, Zarka said that if he follows his father Ali Khamenei's positions, he "would also be on a list of those who should be eliminated."
Israel ‚Äčhas simultaneously ‚Äčlaunched major operations ‚Ā†against Iran‚ÄĎbacked Hezbollah after it struck Israeli territory. The Lebanese government has said it wants ‚Äčdirect talks with Israel to halt the ‚Äčfighting, but ‚Ā†Zarka argued that Beirut is not disarming Hezbollah.
"At this stage, I‚Äôm not aware of any decision to enter negotiations to ‚Ā†end ‚Äčthis war," he said. "What would end ‚Äčit is the disarmament of Hezbollah ‚ÄĒ and that is a choice for the ‚ÄčLebanese government."

item.title: Over 10,000 Chinese citizens returned from Middle East amid war
item.description: BEIJING, March 10 (Reuters) - Chinese authorities and airlines have ‚Äčassisted over 10,000 Chinese citizens in returning from ‚ÄĆthe Middle East, the foreign ministry said on Tuesday, after the war in the region forced airspace closures and grounded ‚Äčcommercial flights.

item.title: Bangladesh secures diesel supplies amid major energy disruptions, sources say
item.description:
DHAKA, March 10 (Reuters) - Bangladesh has begun receiving diesel from suppliers including China and India, with officials ‚Äčsaying the country now has enough fuel to cover about one month of demand, with arrangements under ‚ÄĆway for another month, after the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran disrupted shipments, including to the massive garments industry.
The South Asian nation of 175 million people, which relies on imports for roughly 95% of its energy needs, has imposed fuel rationing for vehicles, restrictions on diesel sales and closed universities as the war ‚Äčon Iran causes severe disruption to Middle East oil exports.
Some garment makers in Bangladesh, the world‚Äôs second‚ÄĎlargest clothing exporter ‚Äčafter China, have already complained they do not have enough diesel to run their back-up generators when ‚Ā†public utilities cut power supplies.
Mahmud Hasan Khan, president of the Bangladesh Garment Manufacturers and Exporters Association, said power cuts had ‚Äčdoubled to up to five hours a day since the war began on February 28.
"When power goes out, we have to ‚Äčrun our standby generators on diesel, but we can‚Äôt buy enough diesel anymore, and it‚Äôs causing problems for us," he said.
State-run Bangladesh Petroleum Corporation (BPC) is receiving around 60,000 metric tons of diesel from three traders, with a further 90,000 metric tons scheduled to arrive later this month, two energy ‚Äčofficials said, requesting anonymity because they were not authorised to speak to the media.
A 27,000-metric-ton diesel cargo from PetroChina arrived ‚Äčat Chittagong port on Monday, while a cargo of 28,000 metric tons from Vitol is waiting at the port‚Äôs outer anchorage, a BPC official ‚ÄĆsaid.
In addition, ‚Ā†around 5,000 metric tons of diesel is being supplied through a cross-border pipeline from India‚Äôs Numaligarh Refinery. Officials said talks are ongoing to secure some 30,000 metric tons from Indian Oil (IOC.NS) , opens new tab .
"We have supplies for one month, and another month is being arranged,‚ÄĚ a BPC official said.
The BPC did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Bangladesh normally needs about 380,000 metric tons of diesel ‚Äčeach month, but officials said ‚Äčthat since rationing began, one ‚Ā†estimate put current monthly requirements at roughly 270,000 metric tons.
Officials said securing refined fuel cargoes had been relatively easy because most of the traders did not rely on the disrupted Gulf shipping ‚Äčroutes used for crude oil shipments.
However, prices have increased as a result of the intense market ‚Äčvolatility triggered by ‚Ā†the Middle East crisis, they added.
Bangladesh also imports around 1.4 million metric tons of crude oil a year for its refineries under long-term contracts with Saudi Aramco and Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (ADNOC).
However, supplies from Saudi Aramco and ADNOC could be affected because their ‚Ā†crude shipments ‚Äčto Bangladesh must pass through the Strait of Hormuz. One Aramco cargo ‚Äčof about 100,000 tons has already been delayed in the Gulf due to the ongoing crisis, officials said.
Severe gas shortages have already forced Bangladesh to halt operations ‚Äčat four of its five state‚ÄĎrun fertiliser factories, redirecting available gas to power plants.

item.title: Greenpeace activists storm stage at France's nuclear summit, confront Macron
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - Two Greenpeace activists broke onto the stage at the start of a global nuclear summit in ‚ÄčFrance on Tuesday, interrupting President Emmanuel Macron and U.N. ‚ÄĆnuclear watchdog chief Rafael Grossi as they were greeting heads of state.
The protesters, dressed sharply in black suits and ties, held banners bearing the Greenpeace logo ‚Äčand reading "Nuclear Power = Energy Insecurity" and "Nuclear power fuels Russia‚Äôs war".
One ‚Äčof them shouted at Macron, "Why are we still buying ‚Ā†uranium from Russia?" to which the president replied, "We produce nuclear power ‚Äčourselves."
France has its own uranium enrichment capacity, but also imports enriched ‚Äčuranium for its power plants, including from Russia, according to the latest customs data published by the French government.
Russia‚Äôs state nuclear company Rosatom accounted for about ‚Äč44% of the global uranium enrichment capacity in 2025, according to ‚Äčthe World Nuclear Association, and European nuclear power producers have struggled to wean ‚ÄĆthemselves ‚Ā†off these supplies four years after Russia invaded Ukraine.
Around 15 Greenpeace activists blocked arriving convoys outside the venue in Boulogne-Billancourt on the outskirts of Paris on Tuesday, the environmental campaigning group said in ‚Äča statement.
France is ‚Äčhosting the ‚Ā†second world nuclear energy summit on Tuesday, where world leaders will meet to discuss and promote nuclear ‚Äčpower.
"For Greenpeace France, the holding of such a ‚Äčsummit is ‚Ā†an anachronism, an event completely out of touch with reality and with the lessons to be learned from the tragic situations of the ‚Ā†Russian ‚Äčaggression in Ukraine, the strikes on ‚ÄčIran, and the impacts of the worsening climate disruption," the group said.

item.title: 'My friends are still in jail': Cubans take to the streets, but fear crossing line
item.description:
SAN ANTONIO DE LOS BA√ĎOS, Cuba, March 10 (Reuters) - Without warning, the sleepy Cuban farm town of San Antonio de los Ba√Īos burst into protest one morning nearly five years ago, triggering a series of copycat demonstrations across the country that shook the Communist government unlike any event in decades.
Frustrated by chronic shortages of food and medicine and ‚Äčlengthy power outages as the coronavirus pandemic lingered, protesters marched in the streets chanting ‚ÄúPatria y Vida,‚ÄĚ or ‚ÄúHomeland and Life,‚ÄĚ the title of a popular song that inverts the Communist slogan "Patria o Muerte," or "Homeland or Death."
The phrase became a rallying cry for a movement that ‚ÄĆwas quickly crushed by state security forces.
Now, with Cuba under a U.S.-imposed oil blockade and sanctions that have thrown the country into a deeper crisis, Reuters visited the same neighborhoods where police and protesters clashed most fiercely on July 11, 2021: San Antonio de los Ba√Īos and the marginalized Havana neighborhood of La G√ľinera.
In interviews with 12 people on the record, and several others who declined to be identified, Reuters found that people remember the brutality of the last crackdown, a testament to the enduring power of Cuba‚Äôs security apparatus. Some, however, were eager to place blame on Washington and the punishing U.S. economic sanctions against Cuba.
While Cubans have expressed frustration over lengthy power blackouts, banging on kitchen pots in a plea to turn the ‚Äčlights back on, for now, at least, a repeat of July 11 seems unlikely. Dissident leaders have largely been jailed or exiled since then, and the government has shut off the internet at signs of protests, as it did that day. Fear, though, is also a factor.
‚ÄúI assure ‚Äčyou, people won't protest in the streets because they're afraid,‚ÄĚ said Brian Jimenez, 26, a baker from San Antonio de los Ba√Īos, who said he was beaten by police that day and detained for several days afterward.
Police showed restraint ‚Ā†at first, he said, but then came the feared "black berets," a brigade of the Interior Ministry, and "they roughed up the people," setting an example that was still reverberating.
"A lot of my friends are still in jail," Jimenez said.
Reuters could not verify his account of police beatings of detainees, though other demonstrators and human ‚Äčrights groups reported similar accounts of abuse.
The Cuban government did not reply to a request for comment.
Cubans expressed a range of opinions, from sympathy toward the Cuban government in the face of U.S. antagonism to openly criticizing the Communist government for failing to produce prosperity. Others called for ‚Äčdialogue and understanding.
But all agreed there was little prospect of another massive popular uprising any time soon, citing the government's violent repression of 2021, resulting in long prison terms for many protesters, while also observing that demonstrations failed to produce any meaningful change.
Though only 40 km (25 miles) outside Havana, San Antonio de los Ba√Īos, population 50,000, is a world apart, typical of many towns across the eyebrow-shaped island. Tractors and carts drawn by scraggly horses, their ribs showing through weather-beaten hides, are almost as common as cars on the streets, where vendors sell locally grown potatoes, onions and cucumbers.
At the town square where many Cubans gathered in front of the church that day, university student Robert Perez said the government has retaliated against ‚ÄčJuly 11 protesters and their families, effectively causing self-censorship.
"When you protest, you can't find work. They take action against you or your family," said Perez, 27, a sociology student at Artemisa University. "In Cuba, everyone knows that one way to make a living is to open a small business and sell goods. Those who ‚Äčrose up on July 11th can't do that."
Rights groups, the European Union and the United States estimate Cuba has jailed between 1,000 and 1,500 people since those widespread protests, many of whom continue to serve extended sentences.
The Cuban government did not respond to a query on how many prisoners it currently holds on convictions related to July 11 ‚ÄĆprotests.
Cuban authorities say those ‚Ā†jailed were found guilty of crimes including public disorder, resisting arrest, robbery and vandalism. Cuba blames the United States for funding the unrest and encouraging its spread, part of a broader plot to overthrow the island's government.
Frustration over blackouts sometimes spills into the streets. Reuters witnessed several demonstrations in poor neighborhoods of Havana on Saturday night, as people banged on pots. In one instance, the lights came back on mid-protest. The protest promptly ended.
"We're tired of so many days of blackouts. Three days in a row of the same," said a man who identified himself as Edward Rafael, 18.
On Monday, about 20 students sat on the steps of the University of Havana in protest, airing grievances over blackouts and internet disruptions that have made remote learning almost impossible, as classes have been suspended during the crisis.
Long before July 11, the United States imposed the most comprehensive and longest‚ÄĎrunning unilateral sanctions regime in the world on Cuba, largely with the intent to pressure and destabilize ‚Äčthe Communist government in the name of promoting democracy and human rights.
The ‚ÄčUnited States has further tightened the screws this year, capturing Venezuelan President ‚Ā†Nicolas Maduro, Cuba's most important patron. It then imposed a near-total oil blockade on Cuba and joined Israel in attacking another longtime Washington adversary, Iran. Trump has said his government is negotiating with Cuban leaders to reach some kind of deal, though Cuba has yet to acknowledge official talks.
While tempers may flare should the shortages grow more acute, for now some Cubans are turning their ire toward Washington, not Havana.
Nieves Fuentes Osoria, an 82-year-old woman from San Antonio de los Ba√Īos, ‚Äčcalled the U.S. embargo of Cuba a "genocide." She said Cuba would show the same type of fierce resistance as it did in the Bay of Pigs invasion of 1961, when the armed forces defeated an invasion of ‚Äčexiles financed and trained by the United States.
"When ‚Ā†we're in danger, this is what we do," she said, holding up two fists. "Because I saw it on different occasions. When they tried to come through the Bay of Pigs, we stuck together."
Others took a neutral political stand but still concluded the protests went for naught.
"What did they accomplish? Nothing," said Deisy Garcia, 73. "They made a fuss, stirred up all this, and life goes on as usual."
Back in Havana, the streets of La G√ľinera bustled under the midday heat, despite the fuel blockade and lengthy blackouts. Pedestrians and electric motorbikes navigated the potholed streets, while barbers clipped hair with rechargeable cordless razors and food kiosks sold whatever produce was available. ‚Ā†On this day, tomatoes ‚Äčwere abundant at several points of sale.
Yuniel Romero, 37, doubted there would be another uprising, as he cut hair from the front porch of a high-street business.
"The people are not going to ‚Äčget involved because in real life nobody wants to be imprisoned again. The people have no way to defend themselves," Romero said.
Ariel Anillo, 52, sold sacks of charcoal, suddenly in demand due to a lack of cooking gas, beneath a poster of Fidel Castro.
He said most Cubans were persevering - "the lights go out for three days and nobody dies" - but the repression was also a factor.
"You know ‚Äčthat if you go to a protest they can put you in prison for 15 to 20 years," Anillo said. "Then the situation is worse, and your family suffers."

item.title: Suspected Hamas member detained in Cyprus over weapons procurement
item.description:
BERLIN, March 10 (Reuters) - Cypriot authorities have detained a suspected member of Palestinian militant group Hamas ‚Äčwanted in Germany for procuring weapons ‚ÄĆand ammunition for attacks on Israeli or Jewish facilities, German federal prosecutors said on Tuesday.
The Lebanese-born ‚Äčsuspect, identified only as Kamel M. ‚Äčin line with German privacy rules, ‚Ā†was detained at Cyprus' Larnaca airport on ‚ÄčMarch 6, arriving from Lebanon, they added ‚Äčin a statement.
The suspect is wanted in relation to the transport of 300 rounds of live ammunition, ‚Äčaccording to prosecutors. It wasn't clear ‚Äčfrom the statement where the rounds had come from, ‚ÄĆor ‚Ā†where they were thought to be heading.
"The operation served as preparation for deadly Hamas attacks on Israeli or Jewish institutions in ‚ÄčGermany and ‚ÄčEurope," they ‚Ā†said.
Police also searched the suspect's apartment in Berlin.
Once Kamel M. is ‚Äčextradited to Germany, a judge ‚Äčwill ‚Ā†decide on pre-trial detention, the statement said.
Attacks against Jews and Jewish targets have risen worldwide ‚Ā†since ‚ÄčIsrael's war against Hamas in ‚ÄčGaza, triggered by the militant group's 2023 attacks on ‚ÄčIsrael.

item.title: Sri Lanka says second Iranian ship nine nautical miles off its coast
item.description: NEW DELHI, March 10 (Reuters) - A second ‚ÄĆIranian ship is ‚Äčcurrently ‚Äčnine nautical miles ‚Ā†from ‚ÄčSri Lanka's ‚Äčcoast, a spokesperson for ‚ÄčSri ‚ÄčLanka's cabinet said ‚ÄĆon ‚Ā†Tuesday, adding that it ‚Äčwould ‚Äčeventually ‚Ā†be moved.

item.title: Kremlin says Moscow mobile internet outages are done for sake of security
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 10 (Reuters) - The Kremlin on Tuesday said that recent instances of ‚Äčmobile internet being turned off in ‚ÄĆMoscow and other major cities were aimed at ensuring security and were in accordance ‚Äčwith the law.
In a call with ‚Äčreporters, Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said: "All ‚Ā†disconnections and communication restrictions are carried ‚Äčout in strict compliance with current legislation."
"This ‚Äčis all related to the need to ensure security," he added.
Peskov said that the impact ‚Äčof the move on businesses needed ‚Äčadditional analysis, and that solutions to their problems would ‚ÄĆbe ‚Ā†proposed.
On a call with reporters on Monday after Russian President Vladimir Putin spoke to U.S. President Donald Trump, Kremlin ‚Äčforeign policy ‚Äčaide Yury ‚Ā†Ushakov's connection cut out at least three times, Reuters reporters ‚Äčon the call said.
In recent ‚Äčweeks, ‚Ā†the Kremlin has moved to block messenger apps Telegram and WhatsApp, promoting the ‚Ā†state-backed ‚Äčservice MAX, which critics ‚Äčsay is not secure.

item.title: Exxon looks to ditch¬†New Jersey incorporation for Texas homecoming
item.description:
HOUSTON/BOSTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Exxon Mobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab is seeking to abandon its corporate registration in New Jersey and reincorporate ‚ÄĆin Texas where its headquarters is located, the U.S. oil major said on Tuesday, in a move that could help bolster its defenses against activist shareholders and climate advocates.
Exxon announced the proposal in a proxy filing. If the move is approved by ‚Äčshareholders, Exxon would become the latest of several high-profile companies including SpaceX, Tesla (TSLA.O) , opens new tab and Coinbase (COIN.O) , opens new tab to register in ‚Äčthe business-friendly Lone Star State.
Last year, a new Texas law enhanced legal protections for ‚Ā†businesses through several mechanisms, including reducing the threat of shareholder litigation by allowing companies to set stock ownership ‚Äčthresholds for lawsuits.
"The Board believes Texas legislators, judges, and juries who might make decisions that impact Exxon Mobil are ‚Äčgenerally more familiar with our business and operations," the company said in the filing.
The top U.S. oil producer, physically headquartered in Spring, Texas, has long been the target of environment-related lawsuits.
New Jersey officials sued Exxon, Chevron (CVX.N) , opens new tab and other fossil-fuel companies in 2022, ‚Äčalleging that they contributed to climate change, forcing the state to spend billions of dollars to clean ‚Äčup after major natural disasters such as Superstorm Sandy and Hurricane Ida. The suit was dismissed last year.
Incorporating in the same ‚ÄĆstate ‚Ā†as a company's headquarters can help executives get the attention of state politicians whose help they might need on taxes or other policy questions, said Jill Fisch, a law professor at the University of Pennsylvania.
‚ÄúOne way you might show your loyalty and get the legislators to pay attention to you is to incorporate in your ‚Äčhome state,‚ÄĚ she said.
Exxon's ‚Äčroots in New Jersey ‚Ā†trace back to its formation after the breakup of Standard Oil in the early 1900s, though its headquarters has been in Texas since 1989.
In September, Exxon took a novel ‚Äčstep to help fend off activist shareholder resolutions during annual meetings, introducing a program ‚Äčto allow ‚Ā†retail investors to automatically cast their ballots in step with the board's recommendations.
Nearly 40% of the company's shares are held by individuals but just a quarter of them vote during proxy season, though they mostly support the board, ‚Ā†the company ‚Äčsaid at the time.
Texas has sought to reinforce its reputation as ‚Äča safe haven for business and has been a major beneficiary as some companies have departed states such as Delaware, a top destination ‚Äčfor incorporation.

item.title: India gains favour with global investors on returns, ESG momentum
item.description:
MUMBAI, March 10 (Reuters) - India is maturing as a market in which foreign investors who take into account ‚Äčenvironmental, social and governance (ESG) factors are confident in dedicating more ‚ÄĆof their portfolio to, according to fund managers at the Indian Venture and Alternate Capital Association conclave in Mumbai, who cite the economy's scale, improving return outlook and advancing ‚ÄčESG standards as key differentiators.
India's market regulator SEBI has been ‚Äčexpanding the scope of ESG-related regulations for over a decade, ‚Ā†making detailed Business and Sustainability Report disclosures mandatory for the ‚Äčcountry's top 1000 listed companies since 2023.
"India is clearly earning its space ‚Äčin terms of allocations from global investors," said Ralph Keitel, head of Fund Investments at Zurich-based responsAbility Investment, speaking to Reuters at the conclave.
"Fifteen to 20 years ‚Äčago, India was a great space, but returns took longer," Keitel ‚Äčsaid. Now he sees a stronger near-term case as well.
Investors also said the ‚ÄĆcountry's ‚Ā†appeal rests on its relative resilience at a time when global allocations are being reassessed across China, the United States and other emerging markets.
India's economy is projected to grow 6.5% in both fiscal years 2024-25 and 2025-26, ‚Äčaccording to the ‚ÄčInternational Monetary Fund.
"Besides ‚Ā†risk mitigation, ESG impact ‚ÄĒ having (a) good impact on communities adds value," Neha Grover, South Asia Lead for ‚ÄčPrivate Equity at IFC told Reuters. Grover added that India has become ‚Äčone ‚Ā†of the most consistent emerging markets for profitable exits, supported by a strengthening initial public offering (IPO) pipeline.
ResponsAbility Investment's Keitel also pointed to climate‚ÄĎrelated opportunities as ‚Ā†a ‚Äčcore area of interest, citing sectors such ‚Äčas mobility and solar panels. As incomes rise, environmental pressures tend to intensify, he added.

item.title: Russian strike on Ukraine's Sloviansk kills four, local official says
item.description:
KYIV, March 10 (Reuters) - A Russian ‚Äčstrike on the ‚ÄĆeastern Ukrainian frontline city ‚Äčof ‚ÄčSloviansk killed four people ‚Ā†and ‚Äčinjured 16 others, ‚Äčlocal governor Vadym Filashkin said ‚Äčon Tuesday.
Filashkin ‚Äčsaid Russia had ‚ÄĆdropped ‚Ā†three guided bombs on the city, ‚Äčand ‚Äčthat ‚Ā†a 14-year-old girl ‚Äčwas among ‚Äčthose ‚Ā†wounded.

item.title: China ready to work with countries on inclusive nuclear governance, Vice Premier says
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - China stands ready ‚Äčto work ‚ÄĆwith countries on a more ‚Äčinclusive nuclear ‚Äčgovernance and development, ‚Ā†Chinese Vice ‚ÄčPremier Zhang Guoqing ‚Äčsaid on Tuesday.
"We should open an ‚Äčinternational ‚Äčdivision of labour for ‚ÄĆan ‚Ā†inclusive workforce across nuclear projects," Zhang Guoqing ‚Äčsaid ‚Äčat ‚Ā†the world nuclear ‚Äčenergy summit ‚Äčin ‚Ā†Paris.

item.title: Court bars Kosovo president from announcing snap election date
item.description:
PRISTINA, March 10 (Reuters) - Kosovo's highest court told the president late on Monday not to announce the date for a snap election before March 31, ‚Äča move that further prolongs a political crisis that has ‚ÄĆengulfed the tiny Balkan nation.
Last Friday, President Vjosa Osmani dissolved the parliament and called a snap election - without setting a date - after lawmakers failed to pick a ‚Äčnew head of state within the constitutional deadline that expired at ‚Äčmidnight on Thursday.
Her move extended a political deadlock in ‚Ā†Kosovo, which is Europe's youngest nation and has aspirations to join the ‚ÄčEuropean Union. Kosovo had no functioning government for most of last year ‚Äčas the fractured parliament failed to elect a speaker for months.
In a verdict published late on Monday, the Constitutional Court temporarily barred the president from setting an ‚Äčelection date and also froze the parliament's efforts to elect a ‚Äčnew head of state. The court decision stays in effect until March 31.
Prime Minister ‚ÄĆAlbin ‚Ā†Kurti had asked the Constitutional Court to rule on whether Osmani had acted in line with the constitution. Kurti is a former ally of Osmani but decided not to back her for a second term in ‚Äčthe ceremonial role ‚Äčof president.
Kurti's government ‚Ā†welcomed the court verdict but added: "For three weeks we will be in a state of waiting, while ‚Äča number of agreements and decisions of importance for ‚Äčcitizens continue ‚Ā†to remain without approval by the parliament."
The parliamentary election, if it goes ahead, would be the third in just over a year.
Choosing a president - ‚Ā†though the ‚Äčrole brings no significant political powers - has ‚Äčalways proven politically challenging because two-thirds of lawmakers must attend the voting session in parliament, ‚Äčmaking it easy to disrupt.

item.title: US designation of Afghanistan 'regrettable,' Afghan Taliban says
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - The Afghan Taliban on Tuesday ‚Äčcalled the U.S decision ‚ÄĆto designate Afghanistan a "state sponsor of ‚Äčwrongful detention" regrettable, ‚Äčand said it wanted ‚Ā†to resolve the ‚Äčmatter through dialogue ‚Äčafter the U.S. demanded the release of U.S. ‚Äčcitizens held.
U.S. Secretary ‚Äčof State Marco Rubio said on ‚ÄĆMonday ‚Ā†he was designating the Taliban government a "state sponsor of ‚Äčwrongful ‚Äčdetention," ‚Ā†and urged Kabul to free ‚Äčall detained ‚ÄčU.S. ‚Ā†citizens, including Mahmood Habibi and Dennis Coyle.

item.title: Morning Bid: Oil's combustible calm
item.description:
March 10 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
The fog of war remains thick. Crude oil prices have become wildly volatile this week, with intraday swings exceeding $30 per barrel - some of the biggest one-day moves ‚Äčon record.
After soaring as high as $120 early Monday - the highest in four years - oil prices then slumped back below $100 later in the day after ‚ÄĆPresident Trump once again teased the prospect of a short-lived conflict with Iran, saying the war was ‚Äúvery complete‚ÄĚ.
I‚Äôll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why inflation isn't the only risk keeping central bankers up at night as the conflict in Iran continues.
And listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast where I discuss how headline‚ÄĎdriven oil swings are ‚Äčrewiring stocks, bonds and rate expectations.
Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.
Trump‚Äôs apparent ‚Äčabout-face on Monday not only calmed oil prices but also the complex of world stocks and bonds that had shivered after oil's ‚Ā†triple-digit price spike. Wall Street stocks closed up on Monday, while on Tuesday South Korea‚Äôs KOSPI index regained nearly 6% and Japan‚Äôs Nikkei climbed nearly 3%.
Meantime, U.S. ‚ÄčTreasury yields tumbled and the dollar took a breather on Tuesday, steadying against major currencies, helping gold edge up in turn. U.S. stock futures were up ahead of the bell, having ‚Äčremained remarkably calm amid yesterday's tumult.
Many will say this is the TACO (‚ÄúTrump always chickens out‚ÄĚ) trade par excellence, but there are few signs that Trump‚Äôs optimistic turn is playing out on the ground, with Iran‚Äôs Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps still asserting that no oil will be exported as long as U.S.-Israeli attacks continued.
Responding to that over social media, Trump threatened further retaliation against Iran if ‚Äčit continued to disrupt oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz. So, for now, the tit-for-tat looks set to continue.
Through all of this, oil prices held above $90 per ‚Äčbarrel - a level that would have seemed frightening only last month. And pass-through to fuel costs is already being felt in the U.S., where a comfortable majority of Americans now believe prices ‚ÄĆwill worsen ‚Ā†over the next year.
In the background, G7 finance ministers on Monday mulled a possible joint release of their oil reserves to calm the horses, though they stopped short of doing so now , with a G7 official telling Reuters that the decision was ‚Äújust about timing‚ÄĚ.
Elsewhere, China reported a spectacular surge in its trade surplus for the first two months of the year, with exports up more than 20% year-on-year. While that certainly supports its new growth target of just under 5% and comes despite falling bilateral trade with the U.S., ‚Äčit also predates this month‚Äôs spike in ‚Äčoil prices.
Later in the day, after ‚Ā†the bell, Oracle is set to report earnings, with traders likely to be on the lookout for signs of a payoff from spiralling capex as it ploughs billions into AI data center expansion.
Chart of the day
China roared into 2026 with exports far outstripping ‚Äčforecasts, fuelled by red-hot electronics demand, putting the economy on track to top last year's record $1.2 trillion trade surplus, despite ‚Äčthe persistent drop in ‚Ā†bilateral trade with the United States.
Although the data preceded this month's energy and shipping shock from the war in Iran, outbound shipments from the world's second-largest economy grew 21.8% in U.S. dollar terms in the January-February period - up sharply from the 6.6% increase recorded in December and blowing past the median forecast of 7.1% in a Reuters poll.
Today's events to ‚Ā†watch
* U.S. ‚ÄčFebruary existing home sales (10:00 AM EST)
* U.S. 3-year note auction
* U.S. corporate earnings: Oracle, Domino's Pizza
Want to ‚Äčreceive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the ‚Äčauthor. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: US ready to work with other countries to develop civil nuclear power, official says
item.description: PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - The United States ‚Äčis ready to ‚ÄĆwork with other countries ‚Äčto develop ‚Äčcivil nuclear power to ‚Ā†meet the ‚Äčglobe's growing energy ‚Äčdemand needs, including from data centres, ‚ÄčThomas DiNanno, ‚Äčthe U.S. under secretary ‚ÄĆfor ‚Ā†arms control and international security, said on ‚ÄčTuesday ‚Äčat ‚Ā†a nuclear energy ‚Äčconference in ‚ÄčParis.

item.title: Al Qaeda-linked group killed at least 12 truck drivers in Mali, HRW says
item.description: DAKAR, March 10 (Reuters) - Islamist militants from an al Qaeda-linked group killed 10 long-haul truck drivers and two ‚Äčteenage apprentices who were travelling through Mali's western ‚ÄĆKayes region in late January, Human Rights Watch (HRW) said on Tuesday.

item.title: Qatar wants to bolster security partnership with US after Iran's strikes
item.description:
DOHA, March 10 (Reuters) - Qatar wants to strengthen its defence partnership with the United States in ‚Äčthe wake of Iranian air strikes on Qatari territory, ‚ÄĆthe foreign ministry spokesperson said on Tuesday, even as it sees the existing deal as an important deterrent.
Iran launched missile and drone attacks ‚Äčon its oil-producing Gulf neighbours after U.S.-Israeli strikes that ‚Äčkilled its top leaders. The conflict disrupted output in the ‚Ā†region and sent oil prices surging.
Qatar hosts the biggest U.S. ‚Äčbase in the Middle East, Al Udeid Air Base, which ‚Äčwas attacked by Iran during the war that erupted on February 28.
The spokesperson, Majed Al Ansari, told a media briefing on Tuesday the security partnership ‚Äčwith the United States was not in question, but added ‚Äčthat its deals with the U.S. and European countries need to be strengthened.
"The ‚ÄĆpartnerships ‚Ā†are the main stop-gap and deterrent against any attack on our country," he added.
"When things go out of control and spiral, the result is deterrents do not work... But that doesn't ‚Äčmean you shouldn't ‚Äčhave deterrents. ‚Ā†You should strengthen these deterrents and that is exactly what are we trying to do right," ‚ÄčAl Ansari said.
U.S. President Donald Trump pledged in ‚ÄčOctober ‚Ā†to treat any armed attack on Qatar as a threat to the United States' own security.
Iran has vowed to retaliate ‚Ā†against U.S. ‚Äčtargets in the region in case ‚Äčof any attack.

item.title: Tuesday will be most intense day of strikes on Iran, Hegseth says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - U.S. Defense ‚ÄčSecretary Pete ‚ÄĆHegseth said Tuesday would ‚Äčbe ‚Äčthe most intense ‚Ā†day of ‚Äčstrikes against ‚ÄčIran in the campaign so ‚Äčfar.
Speaking ‚Äčwith reporters, Hegseth said ‚ÄĆTuesday ‚Ā†would bring the most fighter ‚Äčjets ‚Äčand ‚Ā†bombers against ‚ÄčIran.

item.title: Human Rights Watch says that Israel has been illegally using white phosphorus in Lebanon
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 10 (Reuters) - The Israeli military illegally used white phosphorus munitions over homes in the southern Lebanese town Yohmor on ‚ÄčMarch 3, posing a threat to civilians, Human Rights Watch said in a ‚ÄĆreport on Monday.
"The incendiary effects of white phosphorus can cause death or cruel injuries that result in lifelong suffering," Ramzi Kaiss, Lebanon researcher at Human Rights Watch, said in the statement.
The rights group said it ‚Äčhad verified eight images showing white phosphorus used over a residential part of Yohmor, ‚Äčand civil defense workers responding to fires in the area.
Reuters could not ‚Ā†independently verify the group's findings.
The Israeli military told Reuters it was unaware and could not ‚Äčconfirm the use of shells that contain white phosphorus in Lebanon. It added that it ‚Äčhad not reviewed the same videos as HRW and could not comment on the claims.
Lebanese authorities have not commented.
The Israeli military told residents of Yohmor and 50 other villages and towns to evacuate in a ‚Äčstatement early on March 3.
Reuters previously reported on Lebanese farmers testing their soil to determine whether ‚Äčthey could resume planting in the aftermath of Israel's reported use of white phosphorus in 2023.
As of July 2024, the Lebanese ‚ÄĆNational ‚Ā†Council for Scientific Research said there had been 175 Israeli attacks on south Lebanon using white phosphorus from October 2023, many of them sparking fires that have affected over 600 hectares (1,480 acres) of farmland.
White phosphorus munitions can legally be used on battlefields to make smoke screens, ‚Äčgenerate illumination, mark targets ‚Äčor burn bunkers and ‚Ā†buildings.
HRW said under international humanitarian law the use of airburst white phosphorus over populated areas is unlawful.
White phosphorus is considered an incendiary weapon ‚Äčunder Protocol III of the Convention on the Prohibition of Use ‚Äčof Certain ‚Ā†Conventional Weapons. The protocol prohibits using incendiary weapons against military targets located among civilians, although Israel has not signed it and is not bound by it.
Nearly 400 people have been killed and hundreds ‚Ā†of thousands ‚Äčhave been forced to leave their homes as Israeli ‚Äčstrikes have pounded Lebanon for over a week.
HRW previously accused Israel of using white phosphorus during Israel's 2023 operations against ‚ÄčLebanon, a charge Israel's military denied at the time.
(This story has been refiled to correct the spelling of 'phosphorus' in the headline, and to add the missing word 'of' in paragraph 8)

item.title: Iran not more formidable than thought, top U.S. general says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The top U.S. general said on Tuesday that while Iran was fighting, ‚Äčit was not more formidable than Washington had thought, as ‚ÄĆthe United States geared up for the most intense day of strikes against Iran in the war so far.
General Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint ‚ÄčChiefs of Staff, told reporters that the United States was ‚Äčcarrying out strikes against Iranian mine-laying vessels and the ‚Ā†Pentagon would look at a range of options if it was ‚Äčtasked with escorting ships through the Strait of Hormuz.
The war has effectively ‚Äčshut the Strait of Hormuz, a chokepoint for global oil and liquefied natural gas transport, leaving tankers unable to sail for more than a week and ‚Äčforcing producers to halt pumping as storage fills.
"I think they're fighting, ‚Äčand I respect that, but I don't think they're more formidable than what we ‚ÄĆthought," ‚Ā†Caine said.
U.S. President Donald Trump on Monday threatened to escalate the war with Iran if it blocked oil shipments from the Middle East, even as he predicted a quick end to the conflict.
‚ÄčDuring the press ‚Äčconference at the ‚Ā†Pentagon, U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said the United States would carry out the most intense day ‚Äčof strikes against Iran on Tuesday.
Hegseth reiterated that ‚Äčthis would ‚Ā†not be an endless war and said Trump would decide when the U.S. campaign would end.
The United States has carried out strikes ‚Ā†against more ‚Äčthan 5,000 targets in the first ‚Äč10 days of the campaign, including against more than 50 naval ships, Caine said.

item.title: Shots fired at US consulate in Toronto, no injuries reported
item.description:
TORONTO/WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Shots were ‚Äčfired at the U.S. consulate in Toronto on Tuesday, ‚ÄĆpolice said, adding that evidence was found of a discharged firearm and that no injuries were ‚Äčreported.
Toronto police said on X they responded to ‚Äčreports of shots at 5:29 a.m. (0929 GMT).
Representatives for ‚Ā†the U.S. consulate in Toronto and ‚Äčthe U.S. Department of State did not ‚Äčimmediately respond to requests for comment.
In another post, police said they had no information on a potential ‚Äčsuspect and were on scene investigating the ‚Äčincident. They did not provide details of any motive.
Separately, ‚ÄĆon ‚Ā†Sunday, an improvised device exploded in Norway at the U.S. embassy in Oslo , and police were still searching for a suspect, with ‚Äča possible ‚Äčlink to ‚Ā†the Iran war among the lines of inquiry.
In New York ‚ÄčCity, two men have been charged with ‚Äčterrorism ‚Ā†after throwing a homemade bomb at anti-Islam protesters over the weekend.

item.title: Britain working with allies to support shipping through Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - Britain is working with allies on a range ‚Äčof options to support commercial ‚ÄĆshipping through the Strait of Hormuz in the face of Iranian ‚Äčthreats, Prime Minister Keir ‚ÄčStarmer's spokesperson said on Tuesday ‚Ā†after calls with the leaders ‚Äčof Italy and Germany.
After speaking ‚Äčto Germany's Friedrich Merz and Italy's Giorgia Meloni late on Monday, Downing ‚ÄčStreet said in a readout ‚Äčthat they agreed on the "vital importance of ‚ÄĆfreedom ‚Ā†of navigation" through the Strait and "agreed to work closely together in the coming days ‚Äčin the ‚Äčface ‚Ā†of Iranian threats."
"We're working with our allies on ‚Äča range of options ‚Äčto ‚Ā†support commercial shipping through the Strait as the threat picture ‚Ā†develops," ‚Äčthe spokesperson told ‚Äčreporters when asked about the readout.

item.title: Russia's deportations of Ukrainian children amount to crimes against humanity, UN inquiry finds
item.description:
GENEVA, March 10 (Reuters) - A U.N. investigation found on Tuesday that the deportation and transfer of ‚ÄčUkrainian children by Russian authorities since Moscow's full-scale invasion of ‚ÄĆthe country in 2022 amounted to crimes against humanity.
"In this report, the Commission concluded that crimes against humanity and war crimes ‚Äčby Russian authorities have targeted children, who are ‚Äčamong the most vulnerable victims. These crimes have ‚Ā†irreversible consequences on their lives and their future," according ‚Äčto the report to be presented to the U.N. Human ‚ÄčRights Council in Geneva on Thursday.
The report by the Independent International Commission of Inquiry on Ukraine studied the cases of 1,205 children ‚Äčfrom five oblasts, or regions, in Ukraine and said ‚Äčthat 80% of them have yet to return to Ukraine.
Ukrainian authorities say ‚ÄĆRussia ‚Ā†has illegally deported or forcibly displaced more than 19,500 children to Russia and Belarus in violation of the Geneva Conventions.
U.S.-funded research last year showed Russia expanded its forced ‚Äčre-education programmes of ‚Äčdeported children.
"The ‚Ā†deportations and transfers have originated from various locations across a wide geographic area in ‚ÄčRussian-occupied areas in Ukraine, following a well-established ‚Äčpattern ‚Ā†of conduct, indicating that these acts have been widespread and systematic," the U.N. report said.
Russia denies it is taking ‚Ā†children against ‚Äčtheir will and says it ‚Äčhas been evacuating people voluntarily to remove them from the war zone.

item.title: Pope Leo fires San Diego bishop accused of stealing $250,000
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 10 (Reuters) - Pope Leo has accepted the resignation of a Catholic bishop in ‚ÄčSan Diego who was arrested by ‚ÄĆlocal authorities on suspicion of stealing $250,000 from his congregation, the Vatican announced on Tuesday.
Bishop Emanuel Hana Shaleta had ‚Äčled the small Chaldean Catholic community ‚Äčin the California city since 2017. He ‚Ā†pleaded not guilty to 16 counts ‚Äčof embezzlement and money laundering at a hearing ‚Äčon Monday, according to local media reports.
San Diego Deputy District Attorney Joel Madero said the alleged crimes ‚Äčtook place in 2024 and were reported ‚Äčby a church employee who noticed the missing funds, ‚ÄĆaccording ‚Ā†to KGTV, a local ABC affiliate.
Hana Shaleta was arrested on March 5 at San Diego's international airport while attempting to leave ‚Äčthe U.S., according ‚Äčto ‚Ā†a sheriff's office statement.
The bishop's lawyer said at Monday's hearing that ‚ÄčHana Shaleta was taking a planned ‚Äčtrip ‚Ā†to Germany.
Chaldean Catholics recognise the authority of the pope but worship according to an eastern ‚Ā†Christian ‚Äčliturgical rite. There are about ‚Äč71,000 Chaldean Catholics in San Diego, according to Vatican ‚Äčstatistics.

item.title: Ex-Syrian intelligence officer appears in UK court charged with crimes against humanity
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - A former member of Syria's Air Force Intelligence attended a British court hearing via videolink on Tuesday charged with crimes against humanity and torture relating to the ‚Äčsuppression of pro-democracy demonstrations in Damascus in 2011.
Salem Michel Al-Salem, 58, who now ‚ÄĆlives in Britain, appeared virtually at the hearing at London's Westminster Magistrates' Court from his home. He was wearing a breathing apparatus mask and the court was told he suffered from degenerative motor neurone disease.
Al-Salem is charged ‚Äčwith three counts of murder as a crime against humanity relating to deaths in ‚ÄčApril and July 2011 "as part of a widespread or systematic attack against a ‚Ā†civilian population with knowledge of the attack".
He is also accused of three charges of torture ‚Äčrelating to incidents in 2011 and 2012, and one of conduct ancillary to murder as a crime ‚Äčagainst humanity. He did not speak during the hearing and there was no indication as to how he would plead.
His lawyer Sean Caulfield told the court that Al-Salem was too unwell to confirm his name.
The seven charges were ‚Äčbrought under a British law that allows the prosecution of serious international crimes committed abroad. ‚ÄčThe Crown Prosecution Service said it was the first time it had brought charges of murder as crimes against ‚ÄĆhumanity.
In ‚Ā†2005, Afghan warlord Faryadi Zardad was convicted by a British court of torture that had taken place in Afghanistan.
Al-Salem, who has sought indefinite leave to remain in Britain, was a colonel in the Syrian Air Force Intelligence department with oversight of the Information Branch in the district of ‚ÄčJobar, to the east of ‚Äčcentral Damascus, British ‚Ā†prosecutors say.
He is accused of leading a group tasked with quelling the demonstrations, which mostly occurred during Friday afternoon prayers. Prosecutors say he gave ‚Äčhis men orders to open fire on protesters, which led to the ‚Äčdeaths of ‚Ā†some individuals.
Prosecutors say he was also present at, or took part in, the torture of men at the Information Branch building.
Al-Salem was first arrested in central England in December 2021. His lawyer had sought ‚Ā†an order ‚Äčto withhold his name, arguing it could pose a ‚Äčrisk to his safety. England's Chief Magistrate Paul Goldspring rejected the application but ordered that his address not be made ‚Äčpublic.
He will next appear on Friday at London's Old Bailey court.

item.title: Iran shock creates problematic fiscal loop
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - It‚Äôs never a convenient time for war-induced spikes in oil and gas prices, as Russia‚Äôs invasion of Ukraine in 2022 can attest. But today‚Äôs flare up in the Middle East hits Western economies at a ‚Äčmore fragile moment. Labour markets are looser, corporate margins thinner, and interest rates much higher than they were four years ago. Whether ‚ÄĆgovernments shield households and firms from costlier energy or leave them exposed, the result is likely the same: borrowing costs will climb.
Bond markets are already tense. Even after Donald Trump on Tuesday predicted rapid de escalation with Iran , 10 year yields in Germany, France, the United Kingdom and Japan remain well above their pre crisis levels. Unlike the United States, all are large ‚Äčnet importers of energy and particularly natural gas, which plays a key role in setting electricity prices.
Yet, if disruption at the Strait of Hormuz ‚Äčpersists, governments may be forced to brave hostile debt markets and once again fund energy price caps, subsidies and tax ‚Ā†cuts. South Korea has already moved in that direction, and UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer signalled on Monday that Britain may follow. Bruegel‚Äôs data on Europe‚Äôs last ‚Äčenergy shock shows how expensive such measures can be: between September 2021 and January 2023, they cost European countries -- including the UK -- 3.7% of GDP. This would ‚Äčsit atop the 3.4% deficit that official projections , opens new tab had already pinned on the European Union for this year.
But inaction would lead to a similar place. The post-pandemic experience showed that companies react to energy shocks by coordinating price hikes to protect their margins , opens new tab , which is why even ‚Äúcore‚ÄĚ inflation in the U.S., the eurozone and Britain ‚Äď which excludes the price of fuel and food ‚Äď peaked ‚Äčat range of between 5.7% and 7.1% in 2023. In theory, central banks should look through one off energy shocks; in practice, this so-called pass through ‚Äčeffect forces them to respond. Markets have already shifted from expecting year end rate cuts at the Bank of England and the European Central Bank to pricing in a hike ‚ÄĆfrom the ‚Ā†former and no change from the latter, according to derivative prices collected by LSEG.
The inflation hit should still be smaller this time. European natural gas trades around 50 euros per megawatt hour, far below the 300-euro peak of 2022. Deutsche Bank‚Äôs Sanjay Raja estimates that even a generous UK support package would total only 14 billion pounds, or 0.4% of GDP, compared with 90 billion pounds previously. Still, that extra fiscal hit comes at a time when government tax revenues are already ‚Äčstrained by low growth. The danger is ‚Äčthat more, potentially open-ended financial commitments ‚Ā†push up bond yields, making the fiscal math even trickier.
Central banks could help break the feedback loop with a clear pledge to backstop sovereign bond markets, as the ECB did during the pandemic. At the margin, energy subsidies might help ease ‚Äčfears of a price spiral. But the current level of inflation, which is at or above target in the ‚ÄčEU and UK, means ‚Ā†ratesetters probably won‚Äôt act unless bond markets are under extreme duress. If the Iran war continues, government coffers could become a pain point.
Follow Jon Sindreu on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Critical Metals approves $30 million for Greenland rare earth project development
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Critical Metals (CRML.O) , opens new tab said on Tuesday its board approved a $30 ‚Äčmillion acceleration program for the ‚ÄĆdevelopment of the Tanbreez heavy rare earth project in Greenland.
The push ‚Äčcomes as the United ‚ÄčStates ramps up efforts to build ‚Ā†domestic rare earth and ‚Äčcritical minerals supply chains amid concerns ‚Äčover reliance on China for these materials used in electronics, defense and ‚Äčclean energy technologies.
The program for ‚Äčone of the world's largest known rare ‚ÄĆearth ‚Ā†deposits will advance drilling, infrastructure, engineering design and metallurgical programs.
It expects first ore production in ‚Äčfourth ‚Äčquarter of ‚Ā†2028 or first quarter of 2029, with concentrate ‚Äčexport beginning by third ‚Äčquarter ‚Ā†of 2029.
Last year, Reuters reported Trump administration officials had discussed the ‚Ā†possibility ‚Äčof taking a ‚Äčstake in the company.

item.title: WHO warns of health risks from 'black rain' in Iran
item.description:
GENEVA, March 10 (Reuters) - The World Health Organization warned on Tuesday that the "black rain" falling in Iran ‚Äčafter strikes on oil facilities could cause respiratory ‚ÄĆproblems, and it backed Iran‚Äôs advisory urging people to remain indoors.
The U.N. health agency, which has an office in ‚ÄčIran and works with authorities on health emergencies, ‚Äčsaid it has received multiple reports of oil-laden ‚Ā†rain this week. Tehran was choked in black ‚Äčsmoke on Monday after an oil refinery was hit, ‚Äčin an escalation in strikes on Iran's domestic energy supplies as part of the U.S.-Israeli campaign.
"The black rain and the acidic rain ‚Äčcoming with it is indeed a danger for ‚Äčthe population, respiratory mainly," WHO spokesperson Christian Lindmeier told a press ‚ÄĆbriefing ‚Ā†in Geneva, adding that Iran had advised people to stay indoors.
Asked whether the WHO backed that advice, he said: "Given what is at risk right now, the ‚Äčoil storage facilities, ‚Äčthe refineries ‚Ā†that have been struck, triggering fires, bringing serious air quality concerns, that is ‚Äčdefinitely a good idea."
One video sent to ‚ÄčReuters by ‚Ā†a WHO staff member showed what they said was a cleaner mopping up black liquid at its office ‚Ā†entrance ‚Äčin Tehran on March 8. ‚ÄčReuters was not able to independently verify the footage.

item.title: Decision to turn back on nuclear was a strategic mistake, EU's Von der Leyen says
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - The decision to reduce the share of nuclear energy in the electricity production mix in the European Union ‚Äčwas a strategic mistake given that the bloc is ‚ÄĆneither an oil or gas producer, EU Commission President Ursula Von der Leyen said on Tuesday.
"This reduction in the share of nuclear was ‚Äča choice, I believe that it was a strategic ‚Äčmistake for Europe to turn its back on a ‚Ā†reliable, affordable source of low-emissions power," she said in ‚Äča speech at an event on nuclear energy held in Paris.
The ‚ÄčEU relied on nuclear for one third of its electricity in 1990 and now the share is only close to 15%, she said.
The European ‚ÄčUnion aims to stimulate the development of small nuclear reactors, ‚Äčto make Europe less dependent on imported fossil fuels, she added.
"We see ‚ÄĆa ‚Ā†global revival of nuclear energy. And Europe wants to be part of it," von der Leyen said as she announced a 200 million euros ($233 million) guarantee by the EU to stimulate ‚Äčinvestments in the ‚Äčdevelopment of ‚Ā†small modular reactors.
The money would come from the EU's Emissions Trading System, she said.
"We want this ‚Äčnew technology to be operational by the early ‚Äč2030s," ‚Ā†she said.
Von der Leyen said soaring energy prices caused by the war in the Middle east were a "stark reminder" of Europe's ‚Ā†vulnerability as ‚Äčan importer of fossil fuels and ‚Äčunderlined the importance of increasing power production from renewable sources and nuclear reactors.
($1 = ‚Äč0.8584 euros)

item.title: Spanish researchers develop low-cost artificial cornea from fish scales
item.description:
MADRID, March 10 (Reuters) - Researchers at Spain's University of Granada have developed an artificial cornea made from the scales of several fish ‚Äčspecies commonly found in markets, which could become a ‚ÄĆlower-cost alternative to donor transplants for severe eye diseases.
The cornea - the eye's transparent front layer - is difficult to repair when badly damaged because it ‚Äčhas no blood vessels and limited regenerative capacity. Severe ‚Äčcorneal disease is often treated with donor transplants, which ‚Ā†can be constrained by organ availability and waiting lists.
Scientists from ‚Äčthe university's Tissue Engineering Group and the ibs.GRANADA biomedical research institute ‚Äčsaid in a press release they had created corneal implants with fish scales that were highly biocompatible, transparent and resistant.
"Due to its origin, this product ‚Äčis very accessible, easy to obtain and inexpensive, and could ‚Äčhelp boost the local fishing industry," said Ingrid Garzon, professor of histology ‚ÄĆat ‚Ā†the southern Spanish university, who co-authored the research.
While testing suggested they could be useful for corneal repair and regeneration, the technology remains far from possible clinical use in humans pending human trials.
The ‚Äčteam said the ‚Äčmaterial produced ‚Ā†good functional results in laboratory studies and in animals that received the implants.
"Although the standard transplant ‚Äčusually offers good results, it's necessary to develop ‚Äčnew effective ‚Ā†regeneration methods that do not depend on organ donation, which is subject to waiting lists," Miguel Alaminos, also a histology professor at ‚Ā†the ‚Äčsame university, said in the release.
A ‚Äč2016 peer-reviewed paper by researchers at Antwerp University found that fish scale-derived artificial corneas ‚Äčwere biocompatible with humans.

item.title: Argentina's Milei is pitching to Wall Street as Middle East spooks investors
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 10 (Reuters) - President Javier Milei aims on Tuesday to persuade investors that Argentina's economic turnaround can stay on track even as war ‚Äčin Iran pushes oil prices higher, strengthens the dollar and rattles emerging markets.
Milei is due to speak to investors and ‚ÄĆexecutives inside JPMorgan's new headquarters in Midtown Manhattan as part of "Argentina Week," a New York roadshow to convince financiers that the South American country's stabilization effort remains an attractive investment story despite the global backdrop.
"We're talking about making connections, about creating the right environment for these conversations to be productive," said Manuel Adorni, chief of Milei's ‚Äčcabinet and his spokesperson, addressing investors at a reception on Monday night in the Argentine consulate in New York. "We want to ‚Äčbuild trust, we want to lay the foundations so that we can establish a long-term relationship."
U.S. government backing has ‚Ā†become a key part of that pitch. President Donald Trump 's administration publicly supported Milei ahead of Argentina's October 2025 midterm elections and expanded financial ‚Äčcooperation with Buenos Aires. A liquidity facility championed by the United States helped avoid a run on the peso ahead of that vote.
The two countries ‚Äčdeepened economic ties in February, when the United States and Argentina signed a reciprocal trade and investment agreement designed to facilitate U.S. investment, including in the critical minerals sector.
"They're simply reiterating the investment opportunities in Argentina and sending a message of macroeconomic and political stability," Armando Armenta, a senior economist and strategist at AllianceBernstein, said of ‚Äčthe roadshow. "It's good that they're doing this because Argentina has been out of the spotlight for a long time."
Argentina's government says aggressive spending cuts, ‚Äčderegulation and fiscal tightening are beginning to restore macroeconomic stability after years of deficits, currency crises and galloping inflation.
Investors have cautiously welcomed the adjustment and recent reforms, ‚ÄĆincluding a labor ‚Ā†overhaul approved by Congress that marked a major legislative victory for Milei.
Still, Argentina must rebuild foreign-exchange reserves, attract longer-term investment and regain reliable access to international capital markets after years of default and capital controls -some of which remain in place.
Officials hope Argentina Week, which will also host Economy Minister Luis Caputo, central bank governor Santiago Bausili and deregulation minister Federico Sturzenegger, will help demonstrate that reforms are translating into investment opportunities, particularly in ‚Äčsectors such as energy, mining, agriculture ‚Äčand technology.
The closer alignment with the ‚Ā†United States marks a shift after years in which China expanded its economic footprint across South America. Yet China remains one of the country's largest trading partners and a major creditor.
Global conditions may complicate the message.
Oil ‚Äčprices are up nearly 30% this month to about $90 per barrel as the United States and Israel ‚Äčattack Iran, while a ‚Ā†flight to safety has strengthened the dollar and drawn some investors away from emerging markets. The dollar (.DXY) , opens new tab has gained more than 4% against developed currencies since late January, while the EM currency index (.MIEM00000CUS) , opens new tab has surrendered its gains for the year.
Last week, the Argentine local stock benchmark (.MERV) , opens new tab touched its lowest level since ‚Ā†October, while ‚Äčthe dollar bond yield spread to U.S. Treasuries widened in line with the global ‚Äčbenchmark (.JPMEGD) , opens new tab .
For Milei, the challenge is convincing investors that Argentina's reforms deserve attention even amid a flight to safety.

item.title: China's leaders mourn the death of 109-year-old Communist Party elder
item.description:
BEIJING, March 10 - Chinese leaders on Tuesday paid their respects to Song Ping, the longest-lived member of the most powerful decision-making body in the Communist Party, who died last week in Beijing at the age of 109.
President Xi ‚ÄčJinping, Premier Li Qiang and other members of the party's current Politburo Standing Committee attended a ceremony ‚ÄĆthat closed the chapter on a political career that spanned five generations of leaders of the People's Republic.
They stood in silent tribute then bowed three times in front of his remains, which were draped in the red flag of the Communist Party, before he ‚Äčwas cremated. The event was shown on state broadcaster CCTV.
"The life of Comrade Song Ping was a ‚Äčlife of revolution, of struggle and of glory, a life spent in the pursuit ‚Ā†of truth and progress and in dedication to the communist cause," read an official account of his life released ‚Äčby state-run Xinhua news agency on Tuesday.
Born in 1917 into a family of farmers in the eastern province of ‚ÄčShandong, Song became a revolutionary youth when he joined the Communist Party in 1937.
He briefly served as a personal secretary of Zhou Enlai, who became communist China's first premier after 1949.
Song, as an economic planner and the party's personnel chief, was a prominent member ‚Äčof China's second generation of leadership, an era when reform-minded Deng Xiaoping remade China after the 27-year rule ‚Äčof Mao Zedong ended in 1976 with Mao's death.
He rose to the apex of power in China's political system - the Standing Committee ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†the Politburo, comprised of only a handful of people - in June 1989, following the bloody crackdown on pro-democracy demonstrations in and around Tiananmen Square in Beijing.
Song was elevated by Deng to the top leadership in 1989, as Jiang Zemin emerged as the head of China's third generation of leaders - a move partly made in recognition of Song's commitment to Deng's ‚Äčreform programme.
In the 1970s, ‚Äčwhile serving as the party ‚Ā†chief of Gansu, a province in the country's barren northwest that housed the young republic's space and atomic programmes, Song sided with Deng in the tumultuous years following ‚ÄčMao's death, as party elites debated whether to continue on the path set by ‚ÄčMao or to ‚Ā†follow Deng's call to modernise, reform and open up.
Song was also widely credited with bringing Hu Jintao, China's leader before Xi, to the attention of Deng.
"Hu Jintao sent a wreath to express his condolences on the passing of Comrade Song ‚Ā†Ping," ‚ÄčXinhua reported on Tuesday.
Song retired from the Politburo in 1992, aged 75.
Popular ‚Äčand respected within the party for his seniority, Song remained politically active after his retirement. In 2022, already aged 105, he was seen on ‚Äčstage at a Communist Party meeting flanked by other retired senior officials.

item.title: Germany's Merz sees no plan for bringing Iran war to swift end
item.description:
BERLIN, March 10 (Reuters) - German Chancellor Friedrich Merz underlined growing concern in Europe at the U.S. and Israeli war against Iran on Tuesday ‚Äčsaying a "dangerous escalation" was underway with "clearly no joint plan" for bringing ‚ÄĆit to an end.
Merz's comments, which echoed remarks he made at the end of last week, came after U.S. President Donald Trump said the war could end "soon" ‚Äčbut also said the U.S. could escalate its attacks if Iran ‚Äčsought to block tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz.
Merz ‚Ā†said that Germany shared "many of the goals" of the U.S. and Israeli ‚Äčoperation, which killed Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamanei, but he said questions ‚Äčwere arising with each day that passed, amid signs of a dangerous escalation.
"We are particularly concerned that there is clearly no joint plan for bringing this war to ‚Äča swift and convincing end," Merz said at a joint press conference ‚Äčin Berlin with Czech Prime Minister Andrej Babis.
"We have no interest in a war ‚ÄĆwithout ‚Ā†end. We have no interest in the dissolution of Iran's territorial integrity, statehood or economic viability," he added.
The world needed "a stable, viable Iran as part of a regional peace and security order in which neither Israel nor other ‚Äčpartners are threatened," ‚Äčhe said.
The remarks ‚Ā†reflect the concern in Europe at the economic damage inflicted by any prolonged closure of the Strait of ‚ÄčHormuz, through which a fifth of the world's oil ‚Äčpasses, as ‚Ā†well as by fears of the disorder that would follow a breakdown in Iran's state institutions.
"A scenario such as we have seen in Libya, Iraq ‚Ā†or other ‚Äčcountries in the region would also harm ‚Äčus all," he said. "This affects our security, our energy supply and possibly also the situation ‚Äčsurrounding migration."

item.title: Brazil's Haddad says Durigan likely to replace him as finance minister
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 10 (Reuters) - Brazil's Finance ‚ÄĆMinister Fernando Haddad confirmed on Tuesday that he will step down next week ‚Äčand said that ‚Äčhis deputy, Dario Durigan, is ‚Ā†likely to succeed him.
A ‚Äčformal announcement on who will take over ‚Äčthe ministry, however, is up to President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, Haddad told reporters ‚Äčin Brasilia.
"Dario has a ‚Äčvery good relationship with the president, ‚ÄĆone ‚Ā†of great trust... He is a great public administrator," said Haddad, who had ‚Äčpreviously announced ‚Äčhe would leave ‚Ā†his role ahead of Brazil's general elections ‚Äčin October.
Haddad is ‚Äčexpected ‚Ā†to run for governor of Sao Paulo state, four sources ‚Ā†told ‚ÄčReuters earlier this ‚Äčweek.

item.title: Netherlands moves Iran embassy staff to Azerbaijan
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 10 (Reuters) - The Netherlands will ‚Äčtemporarily move its ‚ÄĆembassy staff in Iran to ‚ÄčAzerbaijan, Foreign ‚ÄčMinister Tom Berendsen said ‚Ā†on Tuesday, ‚Äčciting safety concerns ‚Äčover the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
"Due to ‚Äčincreasing risks ‚Äčto the safety of ‚ÄĆour ‚Ā†staff, it has been decided to temporarily relocate ‚Äčthe ‚Äčactivities ‚Ā†of the Dutch embassy ‚Äčin Iran ‚Äčto ‚Ā†Baku, Azerbaijan," Berendsen wrote ‚Ā†on ‚ÄčX.

item.title: Private credit alarm bells echo 2007 subprime warnings
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 10 (Reuters) - Every financial market crisis is different, but they do rhyme, and parallels are beginning to emerge between the tremors now rippling through private credit and those in U.S. subprime housing that led to the 2007-09 Global Financial Crisis.
This isn't to say a repeat of that historic crash is in the cards. ‚ÄčBut there is a growing risk that the mounting stress in private credit - scarce or nonexistent liquidity, opaque pricing, and spiking redemptions - could spill over into the public ‚ÄĆsecurities markets.
BlackRock (BLK.N) , opens new tab , the world's biggest asset manager with some $14 trillion under management, said on Friday it had limited withdrawals from a flagship debt fund after a surge in redemption requests. A few days earlier, alternative asset manager Blackstone (BX.N) , opens new tab said it had raised the redemption cap on its BCRED private credit fund to meet record withdrawal requests.
The alarm bells at these two behemoths come after a similar event at smaller alternative asset manager Blue Owl (OWL.N) , opens new tab last month, and the bankruptcies of U.S. auto-parts supplier First Brands and car dealership Tricolor late last year ‚Äčthat prompted JPMorgan Chase (JPM.N) , opens new tab CEO Jamie Dimon to warn: "When you see one cockroach, there are probably more."
Investors with a sense of history, or who were around in the 2000s, may think this ‚Äčall sounds a bit familiar. In 2007, BNP Paribas, Bear Stearns and HSBC blocked redemptions from U.S. subprime funds or warned that they were in trouble ‚Äď ‚Ā†a seemingly small risk that metastasized into a global financial meltdown.
Of course, the GFC didn't fully explode until September 2008 when U.S. authorities allowed Lehman Brothers to go under. But the crisis had been building ‚Äčup steadily for at least 18 months, with tremors at those subprime funds offering investors early warnings that trouble was brewing.
The rationale for not letting investors access their own money today is likely similar to ‚Äčthe justifications in 2007: assets have probably fallen significantly in value and thus would need to be sold at a heavy loss; the asset manager may fear triggering a fire sale in other assets to raise the cash being demanded; or the fund may be struggling to unload illiquid assets. Or it may be a bit of all three.
Either way, as was the case with subprime mortgages and related derivatives in 2007, it is hard to know what private credit ‚Äčassets today are truly worth because the market is so opaque and illiquid. When price discovery evaporates, the more bearish assumptions often win out.
Another similarity with subprime in 2007 is that private credit and private ‚Äčmarkets more broadly are not believed to pose a systemic financial stability risk. As we all know, that turned out to be wishful thinking back then.
Is it different this time?
Probably, if we're looking at sheer ‚ÄĆsize. The mortgage-backed ‚Ā†securities market, the root of the GFC, was worth around $7.2 trillion in 2007, or 5% of the total value of global securities at the time, according to Investec. The private credit market today is worth around $2 trillion, less than 1% of all global securities.
On the other hand, like subprime in 2007, private credit today is loosely regulated, certainly relative to traditional bank lenders, meaning its true reach isn't easily ascertained.
What's more, ordinary mom-and-pop investors are getting more involved. Retail investors represented 16.6% of holdings in private credit funds at the end of 2024, up from 5.5% in 2020, according to Investec.
Meanwhile, private credit default rates are ‚Äčrising, hitting a record 9.2% in 2025, credit rating ‚Äčagency Fitch Ratings said last week. That's ‚Ā†up from the previous record of 8.1% in 2024.
