Perhaps ominously, these defaults didn't include any software companies, which have become major private borrowers. The software sector has been hammered this year by fears of artificial intelligence-related disruption, which has slammed shares in private credit giants Blackstone, KKR (KKR.N) , opens new tab and Apollo (APO.N) , opens new tab down 30-45% in recent months.
The broader risks ‚Äčfrom private credit seem skewed to the downside. The U.S. economy is at a delicate juncture, facing a shaky labor market and the fallout from ‚Äčthe war in the Middle East , ‚Ā†including wild volatility in oil markets and the specter of modern-day "stagflation."
True, the consensus view is that the economy's underlying fundamentals are solid and that private credit isn't large enough or well-integrated enough to torpedo GDP growth or broader asset markets. As strategists at Barclays note, private credit has problems, but they aren't big enough to push the U.S. into recession.
Of course, this is exactly how subprime was viewed in 2007.
To paraphrase Warren Buffett, when ‚Ā†the liquidity tide ‚Äčgoes out, you see who's been swimming naked. Recent events in the private credit market suggest more funds may soon ‚Äčbe exposed.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to ‚Äčhear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Exclusive: Exxon CEO says non-essential employees in Middle East have been evacuated
item.description:
HOUSTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Exxon Mobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab has evacuated ‚Äčnon-essential employees from its operations in the ‚ÄĆMiddle East, CEO Darren Woods said in an interview on Tuesday, as the U.S.-Israel war ‚Äčon Iran continues.
Some operations have been ‚Äčscaled back to manage inventory levels as ‚Ā†traffic through the Strait of Hormuz ‚Äčhas been challenged, he said. Exxon is a ‚Äčminority partner in oil projects in the UAE, Qatar and Saudi Arabia.
"Our first and highest priority ‚Äčis making sure our people remain safe, ‚Äčand we evacuated folks who weren't critical or essential ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†the operations that we were providing support for," Woods said.
The Strait of Hormuz, an important waterway that handles one-fifth of ‚Äčthe world's oil ‚Äčsupply, ‚Ā†has been strained as Iran has threatened to attack tankers that ‚Äčnavigate through it. U.S. President Donald ‚ÄčTrump ‚Ā†on Monday threatened to escalate the war with Iran if it blocked oil shipments from ‚Ā†the ‚ÄčMiddle East, even as ‚Äčhe predicted a quick end to the conflict.

item.title: Denmark's Vestas receives UK order for 92 wind turbines
item.description:
COPENHAGEN, March 10 (Reuters) - Denmark's Vestas (VWS.CO) , opens new tab said on Tuesday it has received a ‚Äčfirm order for German utility ‚ÄĆRWE's 1.38 gigawatt (GW) Vanguard East offshore wind project in Britain.
The order supports ‚ÄčBritain's advance towards its 2030 ‚Äčclean power targets and the wider ‚Ā†energy transition across Europe, Vestas ‚Äčsaid in a statement.

item.title: Zambia seeking global investors to help triple copper output by 2031
item.description:
LUSAKA, March 10 (Reuters) - Zambia is courting global investors, including from the ‚ÄĆUnited States, as it aims to more than triple its copper output to 3 million metric tons by 2031, its mines minister said on Tuesday.
Africa's second-largest copper producer after Democratic Republic ‚Äčof Congo is seen as one of the countries Washington is keen ‚Äčto partner with as the U.S. escalates efforts to loosen China's ‚Ā†grip on materials crucial to advanced manufacturing.
Zambia produced 890,346 tons of copper ‚Äčlast year, missing an annual target of 1 million tons. The red metal is ‚Äča key material in electric vehicles and renewable energy infrastructure vital for the transition to a low-carbon economy.
Zambian Mines Minister Paul Kabuswe said investment talks extend to various countries as part ‚Äčof a broader strategy to meet the 2031 target.
"The U.S. is part of ‚Äčit," he said, without providing details of specific discussions.
Negotiations between Lusaka and Washington are also underway ‚ÄĆfor ‚Ā†a more than $1 billion health aid deal that health advocates have warned links the money to mining access and has data-sharing risks.
"There is nothing linked to anything," Kabuswe told Reuters when asked whether ‚ÄčU.S. health funding ‚Äčwill be linked ‚Ā†to mining collaboration, adding: "Zambia is very mindful when seated on the table to do negotiations over any matter."
Kabuswe emphasized the ‚Äčimportance of fair deals, adding that "investors must also take their share". "It ‚Äčhas ‚Ā†to be a win-win situation for Zambia and for the investors," he said.
In addition to copper, Zambia is also rich in cobalt, nickel, manganese, graphite, lithium and rare-earth elements.
Mining ‚Ā†firms ‚Äčoperating in Zambia include China's JCHX Mining (603979.SS) , opens new tab , Canada's ‚ÄčBarrick Gold (ABX.TO) , opens new tab and First Quantum Minerals (FM.TO) , opens new tab , India's Vedanta Resources (VDAN.NS) , opens new tab , United Arab Emirates' International Resources Holding and KoBold ‚ÄčMetals, which is backed by U.S. investors.

item.title: Reducing Europe's nuclear energy sector was 'strategic mistake', EU chief says
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - Reducing Europe's nuclear energy sector was a "strategic mistake", European Commission chief Ursula von der Leyen said on Tuesday, as governments grapple ‚ÄĆwith an energy crunch from the Iran war .
Europe produced around a third of electricity from nuclear power in 1990 but that has fallen to 15%, she told an event in Paris, leaving it reliant on oil and gas imports whose prices have surged in recent days.
Being "completely dependent on expensive and volatile imports" of fossil fuels ‚Äčputs Europe at a disadvantage to other regions, von der Leyen said in a speech.
"This reduction in the share of nuclear ‚Äčwas a choice. I believe that it was a strategic mistake for Europe to turn its back ‚Ā†on a reliable, affordable source of low-emissions power."
Von der Leyen's native Germany took a political decision under then-Chancellor Angela Merkel to phase out nuclear power ‚Äčplants owing to public opposition and safety concerns after the 2011 Fukushima disaster. Von der Leyen was a minister in Merkel's government when that decision ‚Äčwas made.
Germany's Environment Minister Carsten Schneider on Tuesday criticised von der Leyen's "backward-looking strategy" on nuclear power.
"Cleaner, safer electricity from wind and sun is cheaper, has long been driving the energy transition, and produces no radioactive waste," Schneider said in a statement.
The EU has rapidly expanded renewable energy, but gas power plants still form an ‚Äčimportant part of its power mix, and fossil fuels still dominate energy consumption in sectors such as transport and heating.
The continued heavy reliance on imported oil ‚Äčand gas exposed European countries to soaring energy prices in 2022, when Russia cut gas deliveries after the invasion of Ukraine .
The EU budget does not directly ‚ÄĆfund nuclear ‚Ā†energy projects because they are not unanimously supported by its 27 member governments.
In a sign of the EU's increasing acceptance of the technology, von der Leyen said the executive Commission would offer a 200-million-euro guarantee for private investments in innovative nuclear technologies.
She said the money would come from the EU's carbon market.
Some EU countries which previously opposed nuclear, such as Denmark and the Netherlands, have recently softened their stance, as they ‚Äčhunt for ways to secure large ‚Äčamounts of stable, low-carbon electricity ‚Ā†for heavy industry.
Others, including Austria and Luxembourg, remain opposed.
France, Europe's biggest nuclear energy producer, argues that stable, low-carbon power from nuclear plants is key to industrial competitiveness.
French President Emmanuel Macron said the EU - where nuclear power producers still imported ‚Äč15% of their uranium from Russia in 2024 - needed to move towards other suppliers.
"We need to ‚Äčcooperate internationally to make ‚Ā†progress on this issue, to diversify our supply sources," he told the Paris event, adding that France planned to increase its own enrichment capacity.
France imported 39% of its enriched uranium from Russia in 2025, customs data showed.
Macron also proposed standardising reactor designs across Europe. That could benefit France's state-owned ‚Ā†nuclear giant ‚ÄčEDF, which has struggled to win recent tenders for new projects.
In 2024 South ‚ÄčKorea's KHNP won a tender worth at least $18 billion to build a new nuclear power plant in the Czech Republic, a decision which losing bidder EDF sought to block in the ‚Äčcourts.
($1 = 0.8584 euros)

item.title: Western nations bolster military presence in Eastern Mediterranean during Iran conflict
item.description:
ATHENS, March 10 - Western nations have bolstered their military presence in the eastern Mediterranean during the conflict in Iran , focusing on the security of Cyprus after an Iranian-made drone hit a British military ‚Äčbase on the island on March 2.
Here is a snapshot of military assets ‚ÄĆthat have been sent to the region in addition to those that had been there long-term before the conflict began on February 28.
USS Gerald R. Ford, the biggest U.S. aircraft carrier, docked at the ‚ÄčSouda base on the Greek island of Crete two weeks ago, before sailing ‚Äčfurther east with escort ships carrying missiles.
Britain dispatched Wildcat helicopters on March ‚Ā†6, equipped with counter-drone technology. As speculation mounted in February on U.S.-led strikes on Iran, ‚ÄčBritain stationed additional F-35B jets at its base in Cyprus, joining the Typhoon FGR4 aircraft ‚Äčalready there. Britain has said it will also send its destroyer, the HMS Dragon, to Cyprus.
France sent its flagship aircraft carrier Charles de Gaulle to the East Mediterranean last week, deploying a dozen naval vessels around ‚Äčit. It was off the Greek island of Crete on Tuesday and was heading ‚Äčto Cyprus. Another French frigate was docked in Souda base on Tuesday.
German navy frigate FGS Nordrhein-Westfalen arrived in ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†Cyprus region on March 8.
Greece has sent its new Belharra frigate, Kimon, and MEKO 200 frigate called Psara, equipped with the Centauros anti-drone system, to protect the island's airspace. It also sent 4 F-16 Viper fighter jets to be stationed in western Cyprus and deployed a ‚ÄčPatriot anti-aircraft missile system ‚Äčon the southeastern ‚Ā†Greek island of Karpathos to protect eastern Crete.
Italy deployed the Martinengo frigate to the region as part of a coordinated mission with EU ‚Äčpartners. It was docked in Souda base on Tuesday and was ‚Äčexpected to sail ‚Ā†to Cyprus in the coming days.
Spain sent its most advanced warship, the √Ālvaro de Baz√°n-class frigate Cristobal Colon, to the eastern Mediterranean. It was docked in Souda Bay on Tuesday.
The Netherlands ‚Ā†is preparing ‚Äčto send the air defence frigate HNLMS Evertsen to ‚Äčthe region.
Turkey has sent six F-16 aircraft and air-defence systems to northern Cyprus, a breakaway Turkish Cypriot state recognized ‚Äčonly by Ankara.

item.title: Governments plan repatriations as Middle East flights stop
item.description:
March 10 - Commercial flights in parts of the Middle East have been grounded due to the escalating conflict following U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran , stranding foreign nationals and prompting governments to plan repatriations.
Below is what governments have said about repatriation plans:
Austria's foreign ministry said it had helped 117 vulnerable citizens leave the UAE and Israel via neighbouring states and had planned a first charter flight from Muscat on March 4 for 170 people, while warning that land departures would be ‚Äčundertaken at travellers' own risk.
Three flights by GullivAir, Bulgaria Air and the State Aviation Operator planned to return Bulgarian citizens from Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Oman on March 4 and March 5, including a 326‚ÄĎseat GullivAir ‚ÄĆflight from Dubai, a Bulgaria Air Boeing 737 rotation via Oman, and a 90‚ÄĎseat government aircraft from Abu Dhabi.
The Czech government has arranged 10 repatriation flights to return more than 1,500 stranded citizens, the Foreign Ministry said on March 9.
Estonia's foreign ministry on March 4 said it had organised a 180-seat flight from Muscat the following day, available to its citizens in Oman and the United Arab Emirates.
The European Commission has said it is coordinating repatriation flights under the EU Civil Protection Mechanism as more member states request help.
The EU has supported so far more than 42 flights, bringing ‚Äčover 4,100 Europeans safely back to Belgium, Bulgaria, Czechia, Italy, Cyprus, Latvia, Lithuania, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, Austria, Portugal, Romania, Slovakia, and Sweden, a statement from the European Commission said on March 9.
Finland planned a single repatriation flight with about ‚Äč160 seats on March 8 for the roughly 3,000 Finnish citizens stranded in the United Arab Emirates, the foreign ministry said.
Finnair said on March 6 that it was preparing to operate ‚Ā†flights from Muscat, Oman to Helsinki to bring home customers who have been stranded in Dubai. The first flight is scheduled for March 10, with more flights planned later in the week.
The French foreign minister said several repatriation flights for French nationals, around ‚Äč400,000 of whom are in the region, were planned from March 4.
France said it had deployed consular teams at Israel's borders with Egypt and Jordan to facilitate land exits so people can fly onward, and has put in place a similar mechanism in the ‚ÄčUAE at borders with Oman and Saudi Arabia, where airspace has remained open.
Two flights with about 250 people were scheduled for March 5 and March 6 from the Gulf, after the first landed March 5 in Frankfurt, said German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul.
Greek foreign ministry said it has repatriated hundreds of citizens from Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Jerusalem, Oman and the UAE during last week.
87 people were repatriated on March 4 from Amman, Jordan, with another flight carrying 88 people planned for March 5, Hungary's foreign ministry said in a statement.
Another flight was scheduled for March 6 from Sharm-el Sheikh, Egypt. ‚ÄčHungary rented a Flydubai plane for March 5 and March 6 to repatriate Hungarians from Dubai. Hungary had two flights each on March 7 and March 8 leaving from Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
Italy's foreign ministry said about 25,000 Italians have returned from the Middle ‚ÄčEast on commercial flights facilitated by the ministry.
It has expanded consular staffing in Oman and the UAE and is coordinating returns across multiple countries, with large groups moving from the UAE and others travelling by land through Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain and Saudi Arabia. Italians in Oman, Israel, ‚ÄĆthe Maldives and ‚Ā†Thailand are also getting help.
The Dutch government on Friday said it was preparing to use its military to repatriate its citizens from countries they cannot leave on their own. No details on specific countries and timing were given.
The first group of Polish citizens evacuated by military airlift from the Middle East arrived in Poland on March 6, the Operational Command of the Polish Armed Forces said.
A charter TAP flight carrying 139 Portuguese citizens and eight foreigners landed in Lisbon on March 6, with Portugal's foreign ministry planning further repatriations.
Romania's foreign ministry said on March 4 airline FlyDubai has scheduled two flights to Bucharest on March 4 and March 5. It had received more than 3,000 repatriation requests and had around 16,000 nationals registered in the region.
An Air Serbia flight from Sharm el-Sheikh landed ‚Äčin Belgrade early on March 4, carrying 67 passengers, all ‚Äčevacuated from Israel.
The government will deploy an air force A330 ‚Ā†multi-role aircraft to support the assisted departure of Singaporeans from Riyadh on March 10, with a second flight from Saudi Arabia planned for March 12, the foreign affairs ministry said on Facebook.
Slovakia's government has carried out 15 repatriation flights since evacuations started, transporting 641 people, most of whom were Slovak nationals, the Foreign Ministry said on March 9. It is planning more flights.
Slovenia organised four ‚Äčbuses on March 3, escorted by police, to take Slovenian citizens and families with children from Dubai to Muscat airport in Oman, the prime minister's office said in a statement.
The first ‚Äčflight for Slovenia was organised on ‚Ā†March 3, with two more flights scheduled for March 4.
Spain has begun evacuating its citizens from the Middle East on March 3, with more than 175 Spaniards arriving on a flight from Abu Dhabi and further flights expected from the UAE via Istanbul, according to Foreign Minister Jose Manuel Albares.
Spain was also reinforcing its embassies in the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Oman and Bahrain to provide support and facilitate further repatriations, Albares said.
Sweden chartered a plane to repatriate on Saturday 180 stranded Swedes identified as vulnerable.
Thailand plans to evacuate its nationals from Iran by land ‚Ā†to Turkey on ‚ÄčMarch 7 and March 10, while others stranded in Iraq, Qatar, Bahrain, the UAE and Jordan are returning or will return once airspace reopens.
The UAE civil aviation authority plans to begin operating "special flights" across the country's airports to help some of the tens of thousands of passengers stranded in the region leave, state news agency WAM reported.
British chartered flights left Oman on March 5 after technical delays on March 4, prioritising vulnerable UK nationals wanting to ‚Äčleave the region, the British Foreign Office said, after some 130,000 citizens registered their presence in the region.
($1 = 0.8615 euros)

item.title: Iran's intelligence ministry arrests foreign agents, state media reports
item.description:
DUBAI, March 10 (Reuters) - Iran's intelligence ministry ‚Äčhas arrested a ‚ÄĆforeign national it said was conducting ‚Äčespionage on ‚Äčbehalf of the ‚Ā†United States and ‚ÄčIsrael, and ‚Äčacting as a proxy for two Gulf ‚Äčcountries, state ‚Äčmedia cited the ministry ‚ÄĆas ‚Ā†saying on Tuesday.
The ministry also said it had ‚Äčarrested ‚Äč30 ‚Ā†spies, internal mercenaries, and ‚Äčoperational agents ‚Äčof ‚Ā†Israel and the U.S. over ‚Ā†the ‚Äčpast few ‚Äčdays.

item.title: EU leaders to demand carbon market reform by July, draft shows
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 10 (Reuters) - European Union governments are set to ask the European Commission to ‚Äčpropose reforms to the bloc's carbon ‚ÄĆmarket by July, draft conclusions for a summit of EU leaders next week showed.
At ‚Äčtheir summit on 19 March, EU ‚Äčleaders will ask Brussels "to present a ‚Ā†review of the emissions trading system (ETS) ‚Äčat the latest by July 2026, to ‚Äčboth reduce the volatility of the carbon price and mitigate its impact on electricity prices," ‚Äčaccording to the draft conclusions, seen ‚Äčby Reuters.
The draft conclusions said the reform should preserve ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†central role of the ETS in the EU's energy transition. That's despite calls from some governments, including Slovakia and ‚Äčthe Czech ‚ÄčRepublic, for ‚Ā†the EU to suspend or weaken the system, as a ‚Äčway to cut energy bills.
The ‚ÄčEuropean ‚Ā†Commission has said it plans to propose the ETS reform in the third ‚Ā†quarter ‚Äčof the year, but ‚Äčhas not given a specific date.

item.title: Spain's Repsol may bring new investors into US renewables unit
item.description:
MADRID, March 10 (Reuters) - Spanish energy group Repsol (REP.MC) , opens new tab is considering welcoming new investors in its renewables business in ‚Äčthe United States as it reviews its approach ‚ÄĆto renewable power generation, CEO Josu Jon Imaz said on Tuesday.
Since 2018, Repsol has built up a portfolio of wind, ‚Äčsolar and hydroelectric projects as part of its ‚Äčbroader strategy of transitioning from a traditional oil ‚Ā†and gas company to a multi-energy player. It has ‚Äčalso been investing in the production of green hydrogen and ‚Äčlow-carbon fuels.
The United States, alongside Spain, is a key market for its low‚ÄĎcarbon division. In the U.S., the company has about ‚Äč2.8 gigawatts (GW) of capacity operating or under construction, plus ‚Äča development pipeline of more than 15 GW.
"We are evaluating the ‚ÄĆpotential ‚Ā†incorporation of additional investors at the platform level in the U.S.," Imaz told analysts and investors at an event presenting the company's strategic update .
"The key message for this ‚Äčdivision is clear - ‚Äčthis business ‚Ā†will be self-financed over the next three years," Imaz said.
"This represents a critical inflection ‚Äčpoint as the business transitions from cash ‚Äčconsumer to ‚Ā†cash generator, moving from a build-out phase to disciplined growth," he said.
Repsol, which also cut its renewable power generation target, ‚Ā†will ‚Äčfocus on green energy projects ‚Äčoffering the best returns while limiting exposure thanks to asset sales and ‚Äčpartnerships, he said.

item.title: Exclusive: Hezbollah returns to guerrilla roots, awaits Israeli invasion
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 10 (Reuters) - Lebanon's Hezbollah is applying lessons from its last war with Israel as it braces ‚Äčfor a possible full-scale Israeli invasion and protracted conflict, returning to its roots in guerrilla warfare in south Lebanon, four Lebanese sources said.
Operating in ‚ÄĆsmall units, fighters from the Iran-backed group are avoiding the use of communication devices that could be at risk of Israeli tapping, and are rationing the use of key anti-tank rockets as they engage Israeli troops, said the sources, who are familiar with Hezbollah military activities.
Some 15 months since Israel pounded Hezbollah in their last war, the group sparked a new Israeli offensive last week by ‚Äčopening fire to avenge the killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei at the start of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
Heavily criticised in Lebanon for dragging ‚Äčthe country into a war that has displaced 700,000 people, Hezbollah has described its actions as "existential defence", framing it as a response to Israeli ‚Ā†attacks that have continued since a 2024 ceasefire.
While Israel plans for the likely continuation of its Lebanon offensive after the Iran war, the four sources said Hezbollah's calculations ‚Äčare based on Iran's clerical leadership surviving the war, leading to a regional ceasefire of which it would be part.
The sources who ‚Äčare familiar with Hezbollah thinking declined to be named due to the sensitivity of the matter. The details of how Hezbollah is operating in the field have not previously been reported.
Hezbollah's media office didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.
Hezbollah, a Shi'ite Muslim group founded by Iran's Revolutionary Guards in 1982, was the only Lebanese group to keep its weapons at the end of ‚Äčthe 1975-90 civil war to fight Israeli troops who occupied the south until withdrawing in 2000.
Hezbollah's role in driving them out has underpinned its popularity among many Shi'ites, ‚Äčthough its decision to enter the Iran war has drawn criticism from within the Shi'ite community.
This war has come at a critical juncture for Hezbollah.
Greatly weakened during the 2024 war, Hezbollah has ‚ÄĆfaced pressure ‚Ā†from the Lebanese state to disarm. The Beirut government last week banned Hezbollah's military activities.
Adding to the pressures on Hezbollah since the 2024 war, its Syrian ally President Bashar al-Assad was toppled in December of that year, severing the main supply route from Iran.
The sources said much of Hezbollah's fighting on the ground had been focused so far near the town of Khiyam, near the intersection of Lebanon's border with Israel and Syria.
This is one area where Hezbollah believes any Israeli land invasion could begin.
Reuters reported last week ‚Äčthat Hezbollah's elite Radwan fighters, who withdrew from ‚Äčthe south following the 2024 ceasefire, ‚Ā†had returned to the area.
ISRAELI SOURCE: HEZBOLLAH STABILIZING RANKS DESPITE BLOWS
An Israeli security source said there was no sign that Hezbollah was looking to de-escalate - quite the opposite. While Israel had eliminated a few of Hezbollah's very senior commanders, it seemed that the ‚Äčgroup was managing to stabilize its ranks and make and execute decisions.
Two of the Lebanese sources said four deputies had ‚Äčbeen appointed for every ‚Ā†Hezbollah commander, to ensure continued operations.
The Israeli military says it has struck hundreds of Hezbollah targets since March 2, launching airstrikes in the south, Beirut's Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs, and the eastern Bekaa Valley.
The Israeli military has also sent more soldiers into south Lebanon, where some of its troops had remained since 2024, establishing what it has called forward defensive positions ‚Ā†to guard ‚Äčagainst the risk of Hezbollah attacks on northern Israel.
Two Israeli soldiers have been killed in Lebanon.
Hezbollah has ‚Äčlaunched daily drone and rocket attacks at Israel.
In 2024, not only did Israel booby-trap hundreds of pagers used by Hezbollah, but it also penetrated the group's private phone network, according to Lebanese officials familiar with ‚ÄčHezbollah's post-war investigation into breaches.
The sources said Hezbollah was avoiding any devices that could be susceptible to eavesdropping.

item.title: Israeli strikes hit near Beirut as envoy says disarming Hezbollah could end war
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 10 (Reuters) - Israel's military pounded the Lebanese capital's southern suburbs with air strikes on Tuesday and its troops ‚Äčpushed deeper into the country's south, as an Israeli envoy said the key to ending the war ‚ÄĆwas disarming Lebanese militant group Hezbollah.
Lebanon was pulled deep into the war in the Middle East last week, when Iran-backed Hezbollah opened fire on Israel to avenge the killing of Iran's supreme leader.
Israel has since launched air strikes across Lebanon's south, east and Beirut's suburbs, killing nearly 500 people including more ‚Äčthan 80 children, according to Lebanon's health ministry.
Strikes on Beirut's southern suburbs on Tuesday afternoon sent thick columns of ‚Äčsmoke over the city. Two hours before they began, an Israeli military spokesperson ordered residents to ‚Ā†leave immediately, specifying three new districts that should be evacuated.
A member of the municipal council for the area told Reuters ‚Äčfamilies there were fleeing, adding to the nearly 700,000 that Lebanese authorities say have already been displaced by the war.
Lebanon's Minister of ‚ÄčSocial Affairs Haneen Sayed said on Tuesday that the state was bracing itself for higher displacement figures than in 2024, when the last war between Israel and Hezbollah pushed more than a million people out of their homes.
"So we expect that the needs, the numbers of displacement, will ‚Äčbe higher than in 2024. Now on the other side in terms of resources, there's far less resources this year ‚Äčgiven the global situation, the regional war that's happening," she said.
Sayed spoke to Reuters at Beirut's airport, ‚ÄĆwhere ‚Ā†the European Union was delivering 45 tons of emergency supplies including medical kits and blankets.
"Our traditional partners and friends in the Gulf are of course under stress themselves. So we're appealing to the international community to be with us at this moment to help stabilize the situation in terms of humanitarian needs," Sayed said.
Israeli troops made advances on Tuesday in additional towns in ‚Äčsoutheastern Lebanon, including with armoured columns, ‚ÄčLebanese security sources told ‚Ā†Reuters.
Lebanese President Joseph Aoun on Monday had signaled his openness to enter direct negotiations with Israel to end the war.
But Israel's ambassador to France Joshua Zarka said on Tuesday that words ‚Äčwere not enough.
"At this stage, I‚Äôm not aware of any decision to enter negotiations to end ‚Äčthis war," Zarka ‚Ā†said. "What would end it is the disarmament of Hezbollah ‚ÄĒ and that is a choice for the Lebanese government," he said.
Zarka said Lebanon's government was "making very good statements, but to these comments they need to add actions."
Lebanon's government last year vowed to establish a ‚Ā†state monopoly ‚Äčon arms and confiscated part of Hezbollah's arsenal in the country's south, without ‚Äčobjections from the group.
But Hezbollah has refused to disarm in full, and Lebanese authorities were fearful that taking its arms by force could ignite a civil ‚Äčconflict.

item.title: British warship HMS Dragon departs for Eastern Mediterranean
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - British warship HMS Dragon departed for the Eastern Mediterranean on Tuesday, more than a week after Britain's air base in ‚ÄčCyprus was attacked by a drone in the aftermath of ‚ÄĆthe United States and Israel launching attacks against Iran.
Britain's government has been criticised for not being able to send a warship to the region sooner, ‚Äčwith military vessels from allies Greece and France arriving within days ‚Äčof Iran launching retaliatory attacks across the Gulf.
On March ‚Ā†1 RAF Akrotiri in Cyprus was hit by an Iranian-made drone, ‚Äčthought to have been launched from Lebanon or Iraq.
The decision to ‚Äčdeploy the destroyer was made a week ago but it has taken days to prepare it for the journey, adding to scrutiny of Britain's military readiness .
The government ‚Äčhas defended its response, saying it had pre-deployed other assets to ‚Äčthe region including radar systems, air defence, and F-35 jets.
HMS Dragon is a Type ‚ÄĆ45 ‚Ā†air-defence destroyer equipped with the Sea Viper missile system and advanced radar designed to track and neutralise airborne threats, according to the Royal Navy's website.
The government has also cited its close cooperation with ‚Äčthe United States in ‚Äčpreparation and ‚Ā†in response to the conflict, despite criticism from Trump over Britain's support.
Trump has lambasted Prime Minister Keir ‚ÄčStarmer for limiting Britain's military support for the ‚Äčstrikes on ‚Ā†Iran, plunging the relationship between the historically close military allies into crisis.
Starmer has allowed the U.S. to use British bases for defensive action ‚Ā†but ‚Äčhas said that he would not approve ‚ÄčUK involvement in offensive actions unless he was sure they were legal and were ‚Äčpart of a clear plan.

item.title: US State Department OKs potential sale of rocket systems to Sweden
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The ‚ÄčU.S. State ‚ÄĆDepartment said on ‚ÄčTuesday ‚Äčthat it had ‚Ā†approved ‚Äčthe potential ‚Äčsale of M142 High ‚ÄčMobility ‚ÄčArtillery Rocket Systems ‚ÄĆand ‚Ā†related equipment to Sweden.
The ‚Äčprincipal ‚Äčcontractor ‚Ā†will be ‚ÄčLockheed ‚ÄčMartin (LMT.N) , opens new tab .

item.title: Russia told Trump it isn't sharing US military asset info with Iran, says Witkoff
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Russia has denied sharing ‚Äčintelligence with Iran on U.S. military ‚ÄĆassets in the Middle East, U.S. special envoy Steve Witkoff said on Tuesday in a CNBC ‚Äčinterview.
Witkoff said the denial came during ‚Äča phone call that U.S. President Donald ‚Ā†Trump had with Russian President Vladimir ‚ÄčPutin on Monday.
The Washington Post reported on ‚ÄčFriday that Russia was providing Iran with targeting information that included locations of U.S. warships and aircraft ‚Äčin the Middle East.
"Yesterday on the call ‚Äčwith the president, the Russians said that they have ‚ÄĆnot ‚Ā†been sharing," Witkoff said when asked if Washington thought Russia had shared with Tehran intelligence about the location of U.S. military ‚Äčassets.
"We can ‚Äčtake them ‚Ā†at their word. But they did say that. And yesterday ‚Äčmorning, independently, Jared (Kushner) and I had ‚Äča ‚Ā†call with (Kremlin foreign policy aide Yuri) Ushakov who reiterated the same," said Witkoff.
He added: "That's a ‚Ā†better ‚Äčquestion for the intel ‚Äčpeople, but let's hope that they're not sharing."

item.title: Ukrainian missile strike on Russia's Bryansk causes casualties, local governor says
item.description: MOSCOW, March 10 (Reuters) - Ukraine ‚Äčhas ‚ÄĆlaunched a ‚Äčmissile ‚Äčstrike on the ‚Ā†city ‚Äčof ‚ÄčBryansk in south-western ‚ÄčRussia, ‚Äčcausing casualties and ‚ÄĆinjuries, ‚Ā†regional governor Alexander ‚ÄčBogomaz ‚Äčsaid ‚Ā†on ‚ÄčTuesday.

item.title: Trump administration estimates cost of Iran war's first two days at $5.6 bln, source says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump's administration said it used $5.6 billion in munitions during the ‚Äčfirst two days of strikes against ‚ÄĆIran in a report provided to U.S. congressional committees, a source familiar with the information said on Tuesday.
Members ‚Äčof Congress, who may soon have ‚Äčto approve additional funding for the war, ‚Ā†have expressed concern that the conflict will deplete ‚ÄčU.S. military stocks at a time when the ‚Äčdefense industry was already struggling to keep up with demand.
Trump met executives from seven defense contractors on Friday as ‚Äčthe Pentagon worked to replenish supplies.
The administration ‚Äčhas not provided a public assessment of the cost of ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†conflict it launched on Feb. 28 along with ally Israel.
Members of Congress have clamored for more information, including public testimony from officials about ‚Äčissues including ‚Äčhow the ‚Ā†conflict might affect the U.S. military's readiness to defend the country.
Several ‚Äčcongressional aides have said they expect ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†White House to soon submit a request to Congress for additional funding for the war. Some ‚Ā†officials ‚Äčhave said the request could ‚Äčbe for $50 billion, but others have said that estimate seems ‚Äčlow.

item.title: Zelenskiy says Turkey ready to host next trilateral peace talks
item.description:
KYIV, March 10 (Reuters) - Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy ‚Äčsaid Turkey was ‚ÄĆready to host the next ‚Äčround of trilateral ‚Äčpeace talks between ‚Ā†Kyiv, Moscow and ‚ÄčWashington, after speaking ‚Äčto President Tayyip Erdogan on Tuesday.
"The President ‚Äčnoted that ‚ÄčT√ľrkiye is ready to host ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†next round of talks in a trilateral format. ‚ÄčWe ‚Äčappreciate ‚Ā†this initiative and hope ‚Äčit can ‚Äčproduce ‚Ā†results," he wrote on Facebook.

item.title: Trump envoy Witkoff says he will probably travel to Israel next week-CNBC interview
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - U.S. ‚Äčspecial envoy ‚ÄĆSteve Witkoff, a ‚Äčtop adviser ‚Äčto President Donald ‚Ā†Trump, ‚Äčsaid in ‚Äča CNBC interview on ‚ÄčTuesday ‚Äčthat he probably ‚ÄĆwill ‚Ā†travel to Israel next week ‚Äčto ‚Äčcoordinate ‚Ā†on Iran ‚Äčwar ‚Äčplans.

item.title: Number of Iranian missiles and drones fired at Gulf countries
item.description:
March 10 - Here are statistics released by Gulf ‚Äčdefence ministries and militaries on drones ‚ÄĆand missiles that Iran has fired at their territories since the start of the U.S.-Israeli ‚Äčair war against Iran , as ‚Äčof March 9:
Ballistic missiles:
Drones:
Cruise missiles:
8 detected and destroyed
QATAR
Ballistic missiles:
Drones:
Cruise ‚Äčmissiles:

BAHRAIN
Missiles:

KUWAIT
Ballistic missiles:
Drones:
Kuwait reported missile and drone attacks from March 2-5 ‚Äčbut gave no specific data. No ‚Äčdata ‚Ā†was available for Saudi Arabia and Oman.

item.title: Israel not seeking endless war with Iran, foreign minister says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 10 (Reuters) - Israel is not seeking an endless war with Iran and will coordinate ‚ÄĆwith the United States on when to end the fighting, Foreign Minister Gideon Saar said on Tuesday, declining to publicly state a timeline for when the conflict could end.
The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran, now in its ‚Äč11th day, has engulfed the Middle East, with Iranian strikes hitting neighbouring states, including ‚Äčthe United Arab Emirates, and Israel fighting Hezbollah in Lebanon as well as ‚Ā†striking Iran.
"We will continue until the minute that we and our partners think that is ‚Äčappropriate to stop," Saar told journalists in Jerusalem alongside his German counterpart.
"We are not looking for ‚Äčan endless war," he said.
German Chancellor Friedrich Merz said earlier in Berlin that there was growing concern in Europe over the war and there appeared to be no plan to bring it to an end.
"We want ‚Äčto remove, for the long-term, existential threats from Iran to Israel," Saar said in response to ‚Äča Reuters question on what victory would look like to the government.
He described Iran's newly appointed supreme leader ‚ÄĆMojtaba ‚Ā†Khamanei - son of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei who was killed by the Israeli military on the first day of the war - as an extremist.
Israel has said it aims to eliminate Iran's clerical rule by destroying its nuclear and ballistic missile programs and creating conditions for Iranians to overthrow their ‚Äčrulers.
Saar said there was ‚Äčan opportunity to ‚Ā†create conditions for Iranians to "regain their freedom," while acknowledging that it may not happen during the war and could come afterward.
"We must not miss ‚Äčthis opportunity with partial results."
German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul, the first senior ‚Äčforeign official ‚Ā†to publicly visit Israel since the war began, said he was confident Israel and Washington were open to a diplomatic solution that could lead to an end to the war.
But any such solution ‚Ā†would need ‚Äčto include agreements with Iran on its nuclear and ‚Äčmissile programs, as well as its support for regional militias, terms that Wadephul said Tehran had made clear that it ‚Äčwas not currently prepared to accept.

item.title: Spain's LNG imports rise almost 20% from last year, with US as main supplier
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Liquefied natural gas from the United States made up 33.8% of Spain's ‚Äčtotal gas imports in February, as ‚ÄĆthe country's gas imports rose almost 20% from the same month last year, data ‚Äčshowed on Tuesday.
The U.S. has become Europe's ‚Äčdominant supplier of LNG as the region ‚Ā†pushed to phase out energy imports ‚Äčfrom Russia in the wake of ‚Äčits invasion of Ukraine.
In February, Spain imported the equivalent of 31,422 gigawatt-hours (GWh) of gas, sharply up ‚Äčfrom the 26,318 GWh it imported ‚Äča year earlier, according to data from Spanish gas ‚ÄĆgrid ‚Ā†operator Enagas.
Spain imported 10,612 GWh of gas from the United States, while Algeria, traditionally Spain's largest gas supplier, which ‚Äčmostly pumps ‚Äčgas directly ‚Ā†to Spain through pipelines, was the second biggest supplier with ‚Äč9,151 GWh of gas, about ‚Äč29% ‚Ā†of the total imported.
Natural gas imports from Russia accounted for 14.6% of supply in ‚Ā†February, ‚Äčdeclining roughly 52% in ‚Äčabsolute terms from a year ago.

item.title: Higher gasoline prices, volatile stocks, could hit both low- and high-income US consumers as war persists
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Rising gasoline prices and a wobbly stock market are increasing the risk that the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran could hit hard among consumers across the economic spectrum in the United States, undercutting a key prop of economic growth that had been expected to surge this year on the basis of beefed-up income tax refunds, ‚Äčrelatively low unemployment, and rising asset values.
Heading into the year, analysts felt there was reason both "spurs" of the U.S.' so-called K-shaped economy could sustain or even grow spending in coming months, with wealth effects ‚ÄĆleading better-off families to consume more and windfall tax refunds from new exemptions on overtime and tip income helping hourly and some service industry workers.
Instead, both sides could be under different sorts of pressure. Average national gasoline prices topped $3.50 a gallon as of Tuesday morning according to American Automobile Association data, a 17% jump from the roughly $3 average from before the beginning of the conflict. Prices are now above $3 in every state except Kansas, where the average was $2.96, and with oil markets still volatile given shipping disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz analysts see $4 gas as ‚Äča possibility if the conflict persists.
Stocks have come off their highs and the uncertainty about what's next, a factor in household spending based on rising net worth, was on display when President Donald Trump on Monday hinted at ‚Äča possible quick end to the conflict and sent stocks higher, only to temper his language overnight and keep open the chance of an extended conflict with all of the attendant ‚Ā†risks to global supply chains, commodity markets, and corporate earnings.
Major U.S. stock indices were little changed when trading opened on Tuesday morning.
For lower-income households, rising gasoline costs could pull money from other categories of spending, spreading the pain across businesses that ‚Äčcould in turn begin to lower job and investment plans in a suddenly uncertain environment.
"The higher the price and the longer it goes the more you shift from higher prices benefitting some companies who boost oil production and get more revenue...to really ‚Äčpinching consumers and being a drag on the economy. There is some point of prices being at a certain level for a certain amount of time that it flips from being a plus to GDP to a drag on GDP and increasing the likelihood of a downturn," said Luke Tilley, chief economist at Wilmington Trust, with oil in the $85 to $100 per barrel range for several months likely to "materially increase the risk of recession because the labor market is already in such a challenging state."
Benchmark Brent crude topped $116 a barrel on Monday before falling below $90 ‚Äča barrel, then rising again on Tuesday morning. Far from an offramp, U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said Tuesday would see the most intense strikes yet on Iran, even as a top general talked of targeting Iran's capacity to lay mines ‚Äčin the Strait of Hormuz, a possible step towards reopening shipping through a chokepoint where the movement of oil from the Middle East has all but stopped.
The sudden shift in risks to the U.S. and global economy has posed a challenge to central bank policymakers, particularly ‚ÄĆin the U.S., ‚Ā†where officials saw an essentially strong economy facing a balanced set of risks between inflation that was lodged about a percentage point above their 2% target but expected to fall, and an unemployment rate seemingly stable in a range around 4.3% with no clear consensus that it was about to spike higher.
That view has now been hit from both sides: with the economy already having shed jobs unexpectedly in February, there is now the added risk that uncertainty stemming from the conflict could cause businesses to become more cautious about hiring, while inflation could rise more broadly if higher oil prices drive up costs for other things, like shipping rates or home heating bills.
Even though higher fuel prices may have only a temporary influence on inflation, ‚Äčor could even ease broader price pressures on net if ‚Äčconsumers shift spending away from other areas and overall ‚Ā†growth slows, the current context poses other risks.
In analysis conducted after oil costs jumped in 2022 following Russia's invasion of Ukraine, Kansas City Fed researchers concluded that increases in high-profile consumer prices , opens new tab like those for gasoline can have an outsized effect on household inflation expectations if those are already elevated by a previous inflation shock - a dynamic Fed policymakers continue to worry about and ‚Äčhave cited as a reason to keep monetary policy restrictive in order to hold expectations in check.
Investors still expect the Fed to cut interest rates this year, but the ‚Äčtiming shifted back after the ‚Ā†start of U.S. military action, with a potential standoff developing among policymakers between inflation and growth concerns.
The Fed meets next week and is expected to keep its policy rate steady in the current 3.5% to 3.75% range.
Vincent Reinhart, chief economist at BNY Investments and a former top Fed staffer, said it was far too early for the central bank to draw any conclusions about the likely economic fallout from the conflict. Even concerns about the impact on growth have to be tempered by the fact that the U.S.' role ‚Ā†as a net ‚Äčenergy producer means that while higher global prices may mean higher prices at the pump for U.S. consumers, it also means higher income, and ‚Äčpotentially more jobs and investment, for U.S.-based energy firms.
But the longer prices remain elevated the greater the risks, with per-barrel costs in the upper $90 range for a month or longer the sort of material shock that could start to undercut consumption and growth.
"You have to have prices meaningfully higher than what ‚Äčpeople are used to," for an oil shock to change the course of the U.S. economy, Reinhart said. "It's got to be big enough. We're not at the big enough stage."

item.title: Brent oil prices to remain above $95/bbl over next 2 months on Mideast conflict, EIA says
item.description:
HOUSTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Brent oil prices are set to remain ‚Äčabove $95 a barrel over the ‚ÄĆnext two months as conflict in the Middle East rages ‚Äčon, the Energy Information ‚ÄčAdministration said on Tuesday in ‚Ā†its monthly short term ‚Äčenergy outlook report.
Brent crude futures ‚Äčwill then fall below $80 a barrel in the third quarter of 2026, ‚Äčbefore slipping to around $70 ‚Äčby the end of the year.
The ‚ÄĆeffective ‚Ā†closure of the critical chokepoint, the Strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of ‚Äčglobal oil ‚Äčflows ‚Ā†every day, will cause Mideast oil output ‚Äčto fall further in ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†coming weeks.
Those production shut-ins will gradually ease as transit ‚Ā†resumes, ‚Äčthe EIA added.

item.title: Elected in an economic upswing, Chile's Kast takes office as global turmoil rattles markets
item.description:
SANTIAGO, March 10 (Reuters) - Elected on promises of economic growth, deregulation and public spending cuts, local markets rallied after Chile elected far-right Jose Antonio Kast as president in December, but the economic tailwinds have turned turbulent as the Iran war has sent global markets into a tailspin.
Kast, who ‚Äčtakes power on Wednesday, is now in charge of managing the turmoil. A spokesperson for Kast's economic team said that no economic ‚ÄĆcontingencies were planned at the moment, but did not provide further details of how recent developments would impact their economic agenda.
"(Kast) was elected on a promise of being an 'emergency government' that was going to fix things that mattered to Chileans quickly," said Kenneth Bunker, a political analyst and academic at University of San Sebastian, adding that one of Kast's main promises ‚Äčwas economic growth.
"Kast's priorities will probably remain stable, but his ability to carry them out will probably be affected by the exchange rate, inflation ‚Äčand economic growth," Bunker said, adding that if his government can't achieve the growth it was expecting, several of its ‚Ā†plans could be delayed.
Chile, the world's largest copper producer and second-largest lithium producer, is sensitive to fluctuations in international markets.
Copper, Chile's main export and the country's ‚Äčmain source of revenue, climbed from less than $10,000 per ton last June to a peak of $13,618 at the end of January and every cent increase represents ‚Äčan additional $27 million to $35 million for the Chilean treasury, according to the outgoing government.
Before the Iran war, experts estimated Chile could receive up to $4 billion in additional revenue from the soaring prices of the red metal but the price has been volatile in recent weeks, dropping up to 8% from recent highs. As of Tuesday, the price has risen back up ‚Äčto $13,098.
Chile is also one of the largest oil importers in Latin America due to its lack of domestic production, increasing the impact of rising oil prices ‚Äčwhich hit nearly $120 a barrel since the start of the war.
"The Iran war has increased inflation risks considerably," Oxford Economics said in a report surveying emerging markets published on Monday.
The ‚ÄĆreport added ‚Ā†Central and Eastern Europe, Chile and India would be the hardest hit in its oil shock simulation, which showed Q2 inflation estimates rising between 0.4ppts to 1.7ppts.
"(Chile) is very sensitive to external shocks, very sensitive, whether they be geopolitical, military, or fluctuations in global markets," said Marcela Vera, an economist at the University of Santiago.
"The economy has very few financial protection barriers and it has a lot of free trade agreements, so its model is based on a primary export system."
After ‚Äčmonths of setting new highs, Chile's ‚ÄčIPSA stock market continued to rise ‚Ā†following the election and peaked in late January, with about a 65% surge from a year earlier.
Chile's peso, boosted by strong copper prices, had been gaining steam since July and reached its strongest point in years in early February.
But since ‚Äčthose recent highs, the stock market has dropped over 10% and the currency has depreciated about 5% as spiking ‚Äčoil prices and global ‚Ā†economic uncertainty rock the nation.
Vera noted that Chile has a fuel stabilization fund, MEPCO, that operates on three-week cycles to help smooth out price spikes.
"If the war continues for months, then we'll have a chronic effect on our economy," Vera said. "It won't just be the price of oil going up, but the cost of logistics and ‚Ā†the price ‚Äčof the dollar."
A JPMorgan report released Friday said that while MEPCO dampens the impacts of spiking ‚Äčoil prices, it "does not eliminate pass-through effects" and raised its inflation forecast by 20 bp to 3.6% for December with "risks tilted to the upside."

item.title: Brazil's Bolsonaro seeks court approval for prison visit by Trump adviser
item.description: BRASILIA, March 10 (Reuters) - Lawyers ‚Äčfor former ‚ÄĆBrazilian President Jair ‚ÄčBolsonaro have ‚Äčasked the country's ‚Ā†Supreme ‚ÄčCourt to ‚Äčallow Darren Beattie, who ‚Äčwas ‚Äčrecently tapped by ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†Trump administration for a senior advisory ‚Äčrole ‚Äčoverseeing ‚Ā†Brazil , to ‚Äčvisit ‚Äčhim ‚Ā†in prison, a ‚Ā†document ‚Äčshowed ‚Äčon Tuesday.

item.title: US urges Israel to stop attacks on Iran energy sites, Axios reports
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The ‚ÄčUnited States has asked Israel ‚ÄĆto halt strikes on Iran's energy infrastructure, Axios reported on Tuesday, ‚Äčciting three sources familiar ‚Äčwith the matter.
Washington sent the message ‚Ā†at a senior political ‚Äčlevel and to IDF Chief of ‚ÄčStaff Eyal Zamir, Axios reported, citing an Israeli official.
The Trump administration cited ‚Äčthree reasons, including a goal ‚Äčto cooperate with Iran's oil sector after ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†war, according to the report.
Other reasons included a fear that such strikes would harm the ‚ÄčIranian ‚Äčpublic and ‚Ā†trigger massive Iranian retaliatory attacks on energy infrastructure ‚Äčacross Gulf states, Axios ‚Äčreported.
The ‚Ā†White House, U.S. State Department and Israeli Embassy in Washington ‚Ā†did ‚Äčnot immediately respond ‚Äčto requests for comments.

item.title: Ukraine sends drone experts to three countries in Middle East, Zelenskiy says
item.description:
KYIV, March 10 (Reuters) - Ukraine has sent air defence teams to Qatar, the United Arab Emirates and ‚ÄĆSaudi Arabia to help them in their fight against Iran's aerial attacks, President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Tuesday.
Gulf states have expended large quantities of precious air-defence missiles to counter Iran‚Äôs attack drones and have sought Ukraine's expertise in downing ‚Äčthem. Kyiv downs Russian drones every night using an array of weaponry including cheaper, ‚Äčsmaller drones or jamming equipment.
"Regarding the situation in the Middle East, we have ‚Ā†sent our teams: three professional, equipped teams," Zelenskiy told reporters. He did not give details ‚Äčon the teams but said they should all be in place this week.
His communications adviser also said ‚ÄčUkrainian specialists had already been deployed to a U.S. military base in Jordan, without providing details.
Zelenskiy said the presence of Ukrainian pilots was key to the effectiveness of interceptor drone operations.
"Even those countries that were quietly buying ‚Äčinterceptors have, I think, realised that without our military, our operators, our software... without all of ‚Äčthat, the interceptors simply don't work."
Since the U.S. and Israel began airstrikes on Iran on February 28, ‚ÄĆUkraine has ‚Ā†sought to leverage its long experience of countering Russia's Iranian-designed Shahed attack drones.
Kyiv says Russia - which now manufactures them itself - has launched more than 57,000 Shahed-type drones at Ukraine so far.
Reuters reported last week that the U.S. and Qatar were looking to buy interceptor drones from Ukraine as a ‚Äčmuch cheaper alternative to missiles ‚Äčfor downing Shaheds.
Asked ‚Ā†what Kyiv would get in return for the deployment of the specialist teams, Zelenskiy said Ukraine was seeking air defence missiles above all else.
Kyiv has ‚Äčcomplained for months of critical shortages of those munitions, particularly for the ‚ÄčU.S.-made Patriot ‚Ā†systems which are the Ukraine's only effective tool to intercept ballistic missiles.
The Iran war has delayed a fresh round of peace talks brokered by Washington between Russia and Ukraine.
Zelenskiy told reporters on Tuesday the ‚Ā†next ‚Äčtrilateral meeting had been postponed to next week, adding that ‚Äčhe wanted more swaps of prisoners of war and discussion of a possible meeting between himself, U.S. President Donald Trump ‚Äčand Russia's Vladimir Putin.

item.title: Exclusive: As many as 150 US troops wounded so far in Iran war, sources say
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - As many as 150 U.S. troops have been wounded in the 10-day-old ‚Äčwar with Iran, two people familiar with the matter told ‚ÄĆReuters on Tuesday.
The casualty figure has not been previously reported. Prior to Reuters' publication of the figure, the Pentagon had only disclosed eight U.S. personnel seriously injured.
In a statement after ‚ÄčReuters published its report, the Pentagon estimated the figure to be approximately ‚Äč140 wounded and said the vast majority of them were ‚Ā†minor.
"Since the start of Operation Epic Fury, approximately 140 U.S. service members ‚Äčhave been wounded over 10 days of sustained attacks," said chief Pentagon ‚Äčspokesperson Sean Parnell.
He said 108 of the wounded service members had already returned to duty.
Parnell said the eight seriously wounded service members were receiving the highest level of medical ‚Äčcare.
Reuters could not determine the types of injuries and whether they include traumatic brain ‚Äčinjuries, which are common after exposure to blasts.
Iran has launched retaliatory strikes against U.S. military bases ‚ÄĆsince ‚Ā†the start of the conflict on Feb. 28. It has also struck diplomatic missions in Arab Gulf states as well as hotels and airports and damaged oil infrastructure.
The Pentagon says the number of Iranian strikes has fallen sharply ‚Äčsince the start of ‚Äčthe war, as ‚Ā†the U.S. military bombs Iran's weapons inventories and targets Iran's more limited number of missile launchers.
Asked if ‚ÄčIran was a stronger adversary than he expected when the ‚ÄčU.S. military ‚Ā†drew up its war plans, General Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, told reporters earlier on Tuesday that the fight was not harder than ‚Ā†expected.
"I think ‚Äčthey're fighting, and I respect that, but ‚ÄčI don't think they are more formidable than what we thought," Caine told a Pentagon briefing.

item.title: US Senate confirms National Security Agency director, ending long vacancy
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The U.S. Senate confirmed General Joshua Rudd on Tuesday to lead the National Security Agency and the ‚Äčnation's Cyber Command, filling a position that had been vacant ‚ÄĆsince President Donald Trump abruptly fired his predecessor in a national security purge.
The Senate voted 71-29 to confirm Rudd, making him a four-star general.
Trump nominated Rudd, then ‚Äča lieutenant general and No. 2 at the military's ‚ÄčIndo-Pacific Command, for the so-called dual-hat position in December.
The NSA ‚Ā†monitors, collects and processes information and data for global intelligence ‚Äčand counterintelligence purposes. Cyber Command conducts offensive and defensive cyber operations, ‚Äčan increasingly important part of military campaigns including the current Iran war .
The agency was shaken up in April, when Trump fired General Timothy Haugh along with Wendy ‚ÄčNoble, his deputy.
No reason was given for the firings, which took ‚Äčplace after the Republican president had an Oval Office meeting with Laura Loomer, ‚ÄĆa ‚Ā†far-right political activist known as a conspiracy theorist, who said on X that she had given Trump a list of officials she considered disloyal to him.
In a statement, Republican U.S. Senator Tom Cotton ‚Äčof Arkansas, chairman ‚Äčof the Senate ‚Ā†Intelligence Committee, praised the confirmation.
"General Rudd is a war hero with a lifetime of service to ‚Äčour nation. He is the right choice to lead ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†protection of our nation from cyberattacks by Iran , Russia, and China," said Cotton, who criticized Democrats for not acting more quickly.
Senator Ron Wyden of ‚Ā†Oregon, a ‚Äčsenior member of the intelligence panel, ‚Äčsaid in a letter to Trump , opens new tab that he did not feel Rudd was qualified and ‚Äčblocked efforts to fast-track his confirmation.

item.title: U.S. Navy has not yet escorted ships through Strait of Hormuz, White House says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The U.S. military has not yet escorted any commercial ships through the Strait of ‚ÄčHormuz, the White House said on Tuesday, just after U.S. Secretary of ‚ÄĆEnergy Chris Wright deleted a post on X in which had said the U.S. Navy successfully escorted an oil tanker through the key water way.
The U.S.-Israel war against Iran has already effectively halted ‚Äčshipments through the Strait along Iran's coast, where a fifth of global ‚Äčoil and liquefied natural gas normally passes, and Middle East oil ‚Ā†producers have run out of storage and stopped pumping.
U.S. President Donald Trump said ‚Äčon March 3 that the U.S. would provide protection through the Strait for oil ‚Äčtankers. The Pentagon on Tuesday renewed threats to hit Iran harder unless shipments can flow through and said it was striking Iranian mine-laying vessels and mine storage facilities.
Wright then posted on X ‚Äčthat the U.S. Navy had escorted an oil tanker through the Strait of ‚ÄčHormuz "to ensure oil remains flowing to global markets."
Soon after, he deleted the post for reasons that ‚ÄĆwere ‚Ā†unclear.
The United States has not yet escorted any oil tankers or vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters when asked about the issue at a press conference later on Tuesday.
Commenting on Wright's remarks, a ‚Äčspokesperson for Iran's Revolutionary ‚ÄčGuards denied an ‚Ā†oil ship had been escorted.
"Any movement of the U.S. fleet and its allies will be stopped by our missiles and ‚Äčdrones," Ali Mohammad Naini said in comments carried by Iranian ‚Äčstate media.
The ‚Ā†top U.S. general earlier on Tuesday said the U.S. military has started looking at ways to potentially escort ships through the Strait of Hormuz, should it be ordered to ‚Ā†do ‚Äčso.
"We're looking at a range of options there," General ‚ÄčDan Caine told reporters at the Pentagon.

item.title: White House to Americans: Rise in energy prices over Iran is temporary
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The American public will see oil and gas ‚Äčprices drop rapidly, White House press secretary ‚ÄĆKaroline Leavitt said on Tuesday, once the objectives of the joint Israeli-U.S. air war against Iran are fully achieved.
"Rest ‚Äčassured, to the American people, the recent ‚Äčincrease in oil and gas prices is temporary, and ‚Ā†this operation will result in lower gas ‚Äčprices in the long term," Leavitt told reporters.
Oil ‚Äčprices on Monday surged to more than $119 a barrel - their highest since June 2022 - as supply cuts by Saudi Arabia ‚Äčand other producers stoked fears of major ‚Äčdisruptions to global supplies.
Leavitt added that Trump and his energy team ‚ÄĆwere ‚Ā†closely watching the markets, speaking with industry leaders, and that the U.S. military was drawing up additional options in line with the president's directive ‚Äčto keep the ‚ÄčStrait of ‚Ā†Hormuz open.
The deliberations reflect White House worries that the surge in ‚Äčoil prices - following more than a week ‚Äčof ‚Ā†U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran - will hurt U.S. businesses and consumers ahead of the November midterm ‚Ā†elections, ‚Äčwhen Trump's fellow Republicans hope ‚Äčto retain control of Congress.

item.title: Nigeria prioritises local gasoline supply, suspends import licences
item.description:
LAGOS, March 10 (Reuters) - Nigeria has suspended the issuance of gasoline import licences for a second straight month as regulators begin enforcing provisions of ‚Äčthe Petroleum Industry Act (PIA) that only allow imports when the African ‚ÄĆcountry's domestic supply falls short.
Data from the Nigerian Midstream and Downstream Petroleum Regulatory Authority (NMDPRA) show no import licences were issued in February, while the Crude Oil Refineries Association of ‚ÄčNigeria (CORAN) confirmed that none has been issued so far in March, signalling ‚Äča shift towards prioritising local output.
This shift highlights a stronger ‚Ā†intent by Nigerian authorities to protect domestic refining and marks a win ‚Äčfor the Dangote Refinery, which last year sued the regulator and the state ‚Äčoil company to force a halt to imports.
Under the PIA, the regulator may grant import permits only when domestic production is not enough to meet national demand.
The previous regulator, who quit ‚Äčlast year, argued that issuing licences was necessary to maintain competition and ‚Äčprevent market dominance.
Fuel pump prices have surged by more than 54% since the U.S. and ‚ÄĆIsrael ‚Ā†began strikes on Iran last week, pushing global oil markets higher. NMDPRA spokesperson George Ene‚ÄĎIta blamed the sharp rise in prices on escalating conflict in the Middle East.
Nigeria's average daily petrol consumption fell to 56.9 million litres per day ‚Äčin February 2026, ‚Äčdown from 60.2 ‚Ā†million litres in January.
In February, the Dangote Refinery supplied 36.5 million litres of petrol and 8 million litres of ‚Äčdiesel to the local market. The regulator deemed these volumes ‚Äčwere sufficient, ‚Ā†leading to its decision to withhold import licences.
Eche Idoko, spokesperson for the Crude Oil Refiners Association of Nigeria (CORAN), which has long urged the government to stop ‚Ā†issuing import ‚Äčlicences that undermine local refiners' margins, welcomed the ‚Äčregulator's stance.
"For us, anything that protects local production is a good move. The challenge now is ‚Äčto sustain the momentum," Idoko said.

item.title: Rising gas prices from Iran war imperil Trump's Republican majority in Congress
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Minutes into Donald Trump 's State of the Union address laying out his argument for why voters should keep Republicans in power in November's midterm elections, the president boasted that falling gasoline prices had ‚Äčsolved a "disaster" left behind by his predecessor.
Two weeks later, gas prices have risen by nearly 60 cents a gallon, part of a burgeoning energy crisis that began when ‚ÄĆTrump and Israel launched air strikes against Iran on February 28 and set off a regional conflict that has expanded well beyond Iran's borders.
The pain at the pump is quickly turning into a political headache for Trump and his fellow Republicans, whose narrow congressional majority looks increasingly fragile ahead of the midterms.
Crude oil prices on Tuesday were easing off Monday's dramatic gains, and it remains to be seen where gasoline prices will head in the nearly nine months ‚Äčleading up to the November 3 election. But even before the Iran war, U.S. voters were already angry about the high cost of living and frustrated that Trump has not done ‚Äčmore to address it, Reuters/Ipsos polls show .
"You can't hide gas prices," said Jacob Perry, a Republican strategist. "You can lie about all this other stuff and claim ‚Ā†everything is fake news. But there's a giant sign on every street corner saying how bad things are. Literally every commute to work is a reminder."
Democrats have promised to put ‚Äčaffordability center stage in their campaigns. The party needs to flip only three Republican-held House of Representatives seats to win a majority, though they face a tougher path in the Senate.
"Trump promised a Golden ‚ÄčAge in America," House Democratic leader Hakeem Jeffries said on X. "Meanwhile, Republicans are crashing the economy, gas prices are out of control and the extremists are spending billions dropping bombs in the Middle East."
Some House Republicans, who are huddling in Florida this week at a policy retreat to discuss their legislative agenda, acknowledged that surging gas prices are a worry to voters, but cited Trump's assertion that the increase would be short-lived and said the administration's efforts ‚Äčto increase domestic energy production would lessen the sting.
"Every day that people pull into the gas station and pay what they're paying for fuel, I mean, it hurts, and we know it hurts," ‚Äčsaid Representative Austin Scott, of Georgia. "We're going to do what we can to fix it. They understand it's a short-term issue."
The average gas price nationwide on Tuesday was $3.54 a gallon, up about 19% ‚ÄĆsince the ‚Ā†war began, according to AAA.
In a sign of the White House‚Äôs concern, Trump planned to review several options aimed at curbing oil prices, Reuters reported on Monday, including a possible release of crude oil from strategic reserves, restricting U.S. exports and waiving some federal taxes. Trump said on Monday evening that the U.S. would waive some oil-related sanctions, though he did not offer details.
Trump plans to discuss the economy when he travels to Ohio and Kentucky on Wednesday, the White House said.
Higher gas prices may be particularly damaging for a president who won the White House in 2024 largely on the promise that ‚Äčhe would curb inflation, lower energy rates and ‚Äčbring down the cost of living.
Representative Warren Davidson, ‚Ā†an Ohio Republican, said the rise in gas prices is a "concern" for the midterms.
"The economy is always going to be one of the things on the ballot," he said. "The other things the president's done on the economy have been tremendous, so we'll see how it turns out."
Trump's own response to the spiking prices seems unlikely to assuage Americans' anxiety. In an interview with Reuters last week, Trump said he had no ‚Äčconcern about gas prices and ‚Ā†predicted they would drop once the Iran conflict ends, adding, ‚ÄúIf they rise, they rise.‚ÄĚ
He repeated his contention on social media over the weekend, saying that ‚Äúshort term oil prices‚ÄĚ were a ‚Äúvery small price to pay‚ÄĚ for global security.
Trump‚Äôs shifting rationales for the Iran war, however, have made it more difficult for him to argue that the disruption to the U.S. economy is worthwhile. Administration officials have not presented evidence that Iran posed an ‚Ā†immediate threat, and ‚ÄčTrump himself had insisted that U.S. strikes last year ‚Äúobliterated‚ÄĚ the country‚Äôs nuclear weapons program.
"I don‚Äôt think that people care that ‚Äčmuch about Iran," said John Feehery, a Republican strategist. "I think they care about their gas prices. Any president that spends the majority of his time talking about international issues and not talking about dinner table issues, they run a risk of ‚Äčalienating voters.
"This better be pretty quick, that‚Äôs all I can say," he added of the war.

item.title: US power use to beat record highs in 2026 and 2027 as AI use surges, EIA says
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 10 (Reuters) - U.S. ‚ÄĆpower consumption, which hit its second straight record high in 2025, will rise further in 2026 and 2027, the Energy Information Administration (EIA) said in its Short-Term Energy Outlook (STEO) on Tuesday.
The EIA projected power demand ‚Äčwill rise from a record 4,195 billion kilowatt-hours (kWh) in 2025 to 4,260 billion ‚ÄčkWh in 2026 and 4,388 billion kWh in 2027.
Demand is surging due ‚Ā†in large part to data centers dedicated to artificial intelligence and cryptocurrency, and as ‚Äčhomes and businesses use more electricity and less fossil fuel for heat and transportation.
The EIA ‚Äčforecast power sales in 2026 will rise to 1,535 billion kWh for residential consumers, 1,525 billion kWh for commercial customers and 1,056 billion kWh for industrial customers.
The forecasts compare with all-time highs of 1,517 ‚Äčbillion kWh for residential consumers in 2025, 1,486 billion kWh in 2025 for commercial ‚Äčcustomers and 1,064 billion kWh for industrial customers in 2000.
As renewable output rises, the EIA said the ‚ÄĆshare ‚Ā†of power generation from natural gas would hold at 40% in 2026, the same as 2025, before sliding to 39% in 2027. Coal's share will slide from 17% in 2025 to 16% in 2026 and 15% in 2027.
The percentage of renewable generation will rise ‚Äčfrom around 24% in ‚Äč2025 to 25% ‚Ā†in 2026 and 27% in 2027, while nuclear power's share will rise from 18% in 2025 to 19% in 2026 before easing ‚Äčback to 18% in 2027, according to the outlook.
The EIA projected ‚Äčgas sales ‚Ā†in 2026 would slide to 13.0 billion cubic feet per day (bcfd) for residential consumers, 9.6 bcfd for commercial customers and 23.3 bcfd for industrial customers, but rise to 36.2 bcfd for ‚Ā†power generation.
Those ‚Äčfigures compare with all-time highs of 14.3 bcfd ‚Äčin 1996 for residential consumers, 9.9 bcfd in 2025 for commercial customers, 23.8 bcfd in 1973 for industrial customers, ‚Äčand 36.8 bcfd in 2024 for power generation.

item.title: Russian oil prices soar though tanker costs eat into gains
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - The price of Russian Urals oil, shunned by many buyers ‚ÄĆamid Ukraine-related sanctions, has surged on the war in Iran, though costs are also rising, traders said on Tuesday.
With tankers from top exporter Saudi Arabia effectively blocked by Iranian ‚Äčthreats to Gulf shipping, attention has returned to Russia, the world's ‚Äčsecond-largest seller.
Urals crude from Russian ports was being offered at around $76 ‚Ā†per barrel on Monday on a free on board (FOB) basis, Reuters data ‚Äčshowed, up from $45 just two weeks ago.
The price of a Urals cargo loading ‚Äčfrom the Baltic Sea port of Primorsk has jumped to around $54 million this week from about $35 million in February, according to Reuters calculations.
Russian sellers faced near negative margins on exports earlier this ‚Äčyear, traders said.
Despite G7 and other efforts to cap Russian seaborne oil ‚Äčprices, Urals for delivery to Indian ports is now selling at a premium to benchmark Brent ‚ÄĆcrude ‚Ā†for the first time ever.
The United States last week granted Indian refiners a temporary waiver to resume imports of Russian crude already aboard tankers.
Yet traders said Russian sellers also face sharply higher costs. They said several vessels were fixed ‚Äčat around $22-23 million from ‚ÄčRussia's Baltic ‚Ā†Sea ports to India amid a scarcity of the vessels.
That's nearly double rates seen in early February and up about $5-8 ‚Äčmillion from late last week.
From Russia's Black Sea port ‚Äčof Novorossiysk, ‚Ā†which resumed loadings on Friday after recent drone damage, freight costs also rose above $20 million to India, traders said.
Brent oil surged to more than $119 a barrel on ‚Ā†Monday ‚Äčto its highest since mid-2022 amid supply cuts by ‚ÄčSaudi Arabia and other Gulf producers.
Prices pared gains after U.S. President Donald Trump predicted the war in the ‚ÄčMiddle East could end soon .

item.title: US Navy tells shipping industry Hormuz escorts not possible for now
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - The U.S. Navy has refused near-daily requests from the shipping industry for military escorts through the Strait of Hormuz since ‚Äčthe start of the war on Iran, saying the risk of attacks is too high for now, according to sources familiar with the matter.
The ‚ÄĆNavy's assessments spell continued disruption to Middle East oil exports and reflect a divergence from President Donald Trump‚Äôs statements that the U.S. is prepared to provide naval escorts whenever needed to restart regular shipments along the key waterway.
Shipping along the narrow strait has all but halted since the start of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran more than a week ago, preventing exports of around a fifth of the world‚Äôs oil supply ‚Äčand sending global oil prices surging to highs not seen since 2022.
A senior official with Iran's Revolutionary Guards has said the strait is closed and Iran will ‚Äčfire on any ship trying to pass, Iranian media reported last week . Several ships have already been hit.
The U.S. Navy has held regular ‚Ā†briefings with shipping and oil industry counterparts and has said during those briefings it is unable to provide escorts for the time being, three shipping industry sources familiar with ‚Äčthe matter said.
The sources, who declined to be identified due to the sensitivity of the matter, said the shipping industry has been making requests almost daily during the calls for naval ‚Äčescorts through the strait.
One of the sources said the Navy‚Äôs assessment during Tuesday‚Äôs briefing had not changed and that escorts would only be possible once the risk of attack was reduced.
The Pentagon did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Trump has said repeatedly in recent days that the United States is prepared to escort tankers through the Strait of Hormuz when necessary.
"When the time comes, ‚Äčthe U.S. Navy and its partners will escort tankers through the strait, if needed. I hope it's not going to be needed, but if it's needed, we'll escort them ‚Äčright through," he said on Monday during a press conference at his Mar-a-Lago resort in Florida.
The U.S. military has started looking at options to potentially escort ships through the strait, should it be ‚ÄĆordered to do ‚Ā†so, General Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, said on Tuesday.
"We're looking at a range of options there," Caine told reporters at the Pentagon.
A U.S. official told Reuters the U.S. military has not yet escorted any commercial ships through the strait. Earlier in the day, U.S. Secretary of Energy Chris Wright deleted a post on X in which he said the Navy had successfully escorted one through.
While there have been some voyages through the waterway in recent days , the majority of shipping traffic remains on hold with hundreds of ships ‚Äčanchored.
Saudi Arabia's Aramco, the ‚Äčworld's top oil exporter, said on ‚Ā†Tuesday there would be "catastrophic consequences" for the world's oil markets if the war on Iran continues to disrupt shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.
Maritime security specialists and analysts said securing the strait will be difficult, even if the effort involves an international coalition, because of ‚ÄčIran‚Äôs ability to deploy mines or cheaply made attack drones.
"Neither France, the United States, an international coalition or anybody is in a ‚Äčposition to secure the ‚Ā†Strait of Hormuz," said Adel Bakawan, director of the European Institute for Studies on the Middle East and North Africa.
Last week, Iran used a remote-controlled boat laden with explosives to damage a crude oil tanker anchored in Iraqi waters, according to initial assessments from two Iraqi port security sources.
A maritime security source said securing the strait could require the U.S. to take control of Iran's ‚Ā†vast coast.
"There ‚Äčare not enough naval vessels to do that and the risks remain high even with an escort. One ‚Äčor two vessels can be overwhelmed by a swarm (of fast boats or drones)," the source said.
The Pentagon renewed threats on Tuesday to hit Iran harder unless shipments can flow through and said it was striking Iranian mine-laying ‚Äčvessels and mine-storage facilities.

item.title: Zelenskiy says Ukraine's military hit key Russian missile plant in Bryansk region
item.description:
KYIV, March 10 (Reuters) - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said Ukraine's military struck a key ‚ÄĆplant in Russia's Bryansk region bordering Ukraine on Tuesday that produces components for missiles .
Ukraine's military said British Storm Shadow missiles were deployed against the Kremniy bfactory ‚Äčand posted a video which it said showed explosions ‚Äčand a fire at the plant. It said the facility ‚Ā†produced critical missile components.
"Our fighters struck one of the important ‚ÄčRussian military factories in Bryansk," Zelenskiy said in his nightly video ‚Äčaddress, referring to a report from top Ukrainian commander Oleksandr Syrskyi.
"The plant produced electronics and components for Russian missiles. The very missiles that strike our ‚Äčcities, our villages and civilians," he said.
The governor of Bryansk region, ‚ÄčAlexander Bogomaz, said on Telegram that six civilians were killed and 37 injured ‚ÄĆand ‚Ā†posted a video showing him at the scene.
Bogomaz made no mention of the plant in what he described as a "terrorist missile attack". The injured were being treated in hospital.
The Ukrainian military's general ‚Äčstaff, posting on ‚ÄčTelegram, described the ‚Ā†plant as "a critically important link in the chain of production of Russian high precision weapons" producing ‚Äčsemiconductor devices and integrated micro chips.
"The target was hit ‚Äčand ‚Ā†significant damage to production facilities was recorded," the General Staff said. "The extent of the damage is being clarified."
The video posted on Telegram shows ‚Ā†aerial ‚Äčimages of a series of explosions and ‚Äčlarge fires over a large area near woods.

item.title: Israeli president tells Bild: War with Iran needs 'end result', not exact timetable
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Israel's President Isaac Herzog on Tuesday did not offer a timetable on when the war ‚Äčwith Iran could end, telling Germany's Bild newspaper: "We ‚ÄĆneed to take a deep breath and get to the end result."
Herzog said the U.S. and Israeli attacks on ‚ÄčIran were changing the whole configuration of the ‚ÄčMiddle East. He defended strikes on Iranian ‚Ā†oil sites as a way of taking away ‚Äčmoney from Tehran's "war machine".
The interview was published as the ‚ÄčU.S. and Israel pounded Iran with what the Pentagon and Iranians on the ground said were the most intense airstrikes of ‚Äčthe war, despite global markets betting that President ‚ÄčDonald Trump will seek to end the conflict soon.
Israel's foreign minister, ‚ÄĆGideon ‚Ā†Saar, had earlier said his country was not planning for an endless war and was consulting with Washington about when to stop it.
