Pair that with a war roiling the petro-states of the Gulf, home to some 10 million ‚ÄčIndian expatriates who account for 38% of the country's inward remittances, and it's easy to see why the ‚ÄĆU.S.-Israel war against Iran has put the world's fifth-largest economy on edge.
Opposition politicians jeered Subrahmanyam Jaishankar, India's foreign minister, on Monday during his speech in parliament on the conflict after crude shot up to $119 a barrel and the Indian rupee hit a fresh low of 92.35 against the U.S. dollar. ‚ÄčIt was already the worst-performing major Asian currency in 2025.
There will be limited relief from Washington's green light for Indian ‚Äčcompanies including Reliance Industries (RELI.NS) , opens new tab to buy otherwise-sanctioned Russian oil. Juicy discounts on that supply narrowed long ‚Ā†ago, and now there will be more competition from other buyers.
And in a situation of very limited supply, India's stockpile can ‚Äčonly meet its needs for 25 days, per a Reuters report citing refining sources. India's demand for liquefied natural gas is a ‚Äčproblem too. It imports 80% of its needs from the Middle East. New Delhi on Tuesday curbed supply to industries, a day after extending waiting periods for cooking gas.
India has multiple levers it can pull to shield consumers from any price shock. New Delhi can ask state-backed fuel retailers like ‚ÄčBharat Petroleum (BPCL.NS) , opens new tab and Indian Oil (IOC.NS) , opens new tab to absorb the increased cost. At a pinch, the government could cut excise duties, albeit at ‚Äčthe cost of a wider budget gap. Inflation in India is also low: retail prices grew 2.75% year-on-year in January.
Protecting the rupee, however, is ‚ÄĆharder. Bigger ‚Ā†fiscal deficits in national accounts will hurt. India's central bank intervened on Monday to stem the currency's slide. Though the price of oil receded to $92 per barrel after U.S. President Donald Trump claimed the war would be over "very soon", it remains volatile. If it held at $100 per barrel for three months, India‚Äôs current account deficit could rise to 2% of GDP from the baseline assumption of ‚Äč1.6%, according to Gaura Sengupta, ‚Äčan economist at IDFC First ‚Ā†Bank. That would be close to the 2.3% level clocked in 2008-09, soon after the global financial crisis.
India's currency is already suffering from weak net foreign direct investment and capital outflows, in part ‚Äčbecause of worries about the threat new artificial intelligence tools pose to India's services exports. ‚ÄčA prolonged war ‚Ā†in the Middle East will really grease the rupee's problems.
Follow Shritama Bose on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: What happens if OpenAI or Anthropic fail?
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters Breakingviews) - The artificial intelligence boom would make for a nasty bust. Alphabet (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab , Amazon.com (AMZN.O) , opens new tab , Meta Platforms (META.O) , opens new tab , and Microsoft (MSFT.O) , opens new tab plan to spend
The scale is staggering. Big Tech‚Äôs spending commitments are roughly equivalent to 2% of U.S. GDP last year. Morgan Stanley foresees $2.9 trillion of global data-centre investment between 2025 and 2028, of ‚ÄĆwhich it expects roughly $900 billion will come from private credit and asset-backed lending. The boom now stretches far beyond Silicon Valley into lenders, utilities and infrastructure investors.
OpenAI's ambitions underscore the sheer scale of the financial mobilization. CEO Sam Altman has floated infrastructure build-out deals worth $1.4 trillion, requiring 30 gigawatts of power, enough to supply 20 million U.S. households. Headline figures probably overstate the immediate outlay, given the company‚Äôs complex web of overlapping deals and multi-year partnerships. Still, this is a massive amount of capital due to flow into cloud providers like ‚ÄčOracle (ORCL.N) , opens new tab , Microsoft, Amazon, and CoreWeave (CRWV.O) , opens new tab , as well as chip and gear-makers including Nvidia (NVDA.O) , opens new tab and Broadcom (AVGO.O) , opens new tab .
Funding all of this will be an increasingly difficult challenge. HSBC estimates that OpenAI may need $207 billion in additional financing by 2030. ‚ÄčTrue, the company just announced a $110 billion funding round . But such sums increasingly bear asterisks.
Take Amazon. It pledged $50 billion of the round, but this begins with an initial payment ‚Ā†of about $15 billion, with further outlays tied to Altman successfully leading his company onto public markets or surpassing a smarter-than-human technological milestone known as ‚Äúartificial general intelligence", whichever comes first. SoftBank, expected to add another $30 billion, is itself under pressure: ‚ÄčS&P Global recently assigned , opens new tab the Japanese group a negative credit outlook as it explores a bridge loan of up to $40 billion, according to Bloomberg.
Both OpenAI and Anthropic are considering initial public offerings as soon as this year,‚ÄĮas the Financial Times and Reuters reported, ‚Äčso the hope may be that public investors can soon substitute for private ones. True, growth is extraordinary: OpenAI topped
Public investors would also need to have strong stomachs for red ink. HSBC analysts in late November estimated
To nonetheless achieve a mooted $1 trillion IPO valuation, OpenAI would need to maintain extremely rapid growth. Nvidia and Alphabet trade on average at 5.6 times expected sales in 2030, according to Visible Alpha. Applying a standard 30% IPO discount to ‚ÄĆthat multiple, OpenAI ‚Ā†would need to generate at least $250 billion in annual revenue by 2030. CNBC reports , opens new tab that internal targets are even higher, at $280 billion. But to succeed, OpenAI would have to build a business the size of today's Microsoft in just four years.
Big Tech, along with the broader AI supply chain, ultimately depends on the success of OpenAI and Anthropic. Microsoft said last month that 45% of its $625 billion in demand backlog is tied to OpenAI, while Altman has inked a $300 billion deal with Oracle. Together, that‚Äôs about two-thirds of the $800 billion HSBC estimates OpenAI could spend on chips and data centers, with the rest spread across Nvidia, AMD (AMD.O) , opens new tab , Amazon and CoreWeave.
While the scale is smaller, Anthropic is also set , opens new tab to spend many billions ‚Äčmore. Yet a recent battle with the Pentagon adds fresh ‚Äčuncertainty, beyond whether it can even fund its ‚Ā†plans. After CEO Dario Amodei resisted allowing Claude to be used for mass surveillance or autonomous weapons, the Trump administration labelled the company a supply-chain risk, with defense chief Pete Hegseth claiming that no government contractor can work with it. Anthropic has sued to overturn these moves, saying that they violate
Granted, in a worst-case scenario, creditors and investors tend to assume that a buyer would ‚Äčemerge for a weakened OpenAI or ‚Ā†Anthropic. Microsoft, Amazon or another strategic bidder would benefit greatly from their technology, talent and user base.
But such a rescue would likely come with a sharp valuation reset and a rethink of how much computing power the industry truly needs. And there‚Äôs another, more general problem. Wall Street is beginning to question the sustainability of the AI spending boom itself. Credit-default swaps on Oracle have climbed to their highest levels since 2008. Shares of Microsoft and Amazon slid after both companies reiterated plans to ‚Ā†increase capital spending.
Should ‚ÄčAI labs or Big Tech's generative AI monetization fall short of expectations, the capital flows required to build capacity could quickly dry up. ‚ÄčIf data-centre expansion slows, it would plausibly reduce sales of Nvidia chips, leave major investments in power infrastructure underutilized, and lenders uncertain. In that sense, the failure of a single lab would not be a simple corporate stumble. It would drag down a historically massive boom.
Follow Karen Kwok on LinkedIn , opens new tab ‚Äčand X , opens new tab .

item.title: Japan's weak spot on energy in focus as Middle East crisis worsens -REI chair
item.description:
TOKYO, March 11 (Reuters) - Japan should do more to reduce its reliance on imported fossil fuels, the chair of the Renewable Energy Institute said, after the ‚Äčescalating Middle East conflict halted tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, ‚ÄĆunderlining Japan's vulnerability to geopolitical risks.
Japan relies on the region for about 95% of its crude oil and 11% of its liquefied natural gas imports, with roughly 70% and 6% respectively ‚Äčshipped through the Strait. The channel's closure has triggered sharp spikes in fuel prices, ‚Äčhitting energy‚ÄĎimporting economies, such as Japan, the hardest.
Tomas Kaberger, the chair of ‚Ā†the Tokyo-based REI's executive board and a longtime observer of Japan's energy system, ‚Äčsaid the country needed to reduce vulnerability by cutting dependence on fossil fuels. "When ‚Äčfossil fuel imports are cut, the power plants stop and your cars stop running," he told Reuters in an interview last week.
The Iran crisis, he suggested, was a stark reminder ‚Äčthat fossil‚ÄĎfuel dependence carried geopolitical risks and was an economic liability.
This month marks ‚Äč15 years since the Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster, which fundamentally reshaped Japan's perception of energy ‚ÄĆsecurity. ‚Ā†But Kaberger cautioned that nuclear power was not a solution.
"Large power plants are easily hit nowadays with modern weapons and drones. It's proven that the Russians have managed to destroy a lot of the large power plants in Ukraine," he ‚Äčsaid.
By contrast, decentralised ‚Äčrenewable systems ‚ÄĒ solar, ‚Ā†wind and battery storage ‚ÄĒ offer stronger resilience because no single strike can take down national supply.
Kaberger also urged Japan to ‚Äčrecognise its strength in renewables. "In the 20th century Japan ‚Äčwas resource‚ÄĎpoor ‚Ā†because the dominant energy sources were oil, coal, gas and uranium. In the 21st century with solar, wind and battery technologies becoming the world's cheapest forms of power ‚Ā†generation, ‚ÄčJapan is resource‚ÄĎrich," he said.
With Middle Eastern tensions ‚Äčrising and Fukushima's lessons still vivid, Kaberger says Japan has a historic opportunity to accelerate renewables ‚Äčto secure energy independence and national resilience.

item.title: Activist shareholder Follow This vows to fight BP on climate resolution
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - Climate shareholder activist group Follow This said it would fight BP's (BP.L) , opens new tab decision not to include ‚Äčits climate resolution in its annual general ‚ÄĆshareholder meeting invitation.
($1 = 0.8588 euros)

item.title: Cargo ship hit by projectile in Strait of Hormuz, crew evacuates
item.description: DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - A ‚Äčcargo vessel ‚ÄĆwas hit by an ‚Äčunknown ‚Äčprojectile in the ‚Ā†Strait ‚Äčof Hormuz, ‚Äčresulting in a fire onboard ‚Äčand ‚Äčprompting the crew ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†evacuate and request assistance, ‚Äčaccording ‚Äčto ‚Ā†the United Kingdom ‚ÄčMaritime ‚ÄčTrade ‚Ā†Operations on Wednesday.

item.title: Sri Lanka court orders 84 Iranian sailors's bodies be handed to Iran embassy, local media says
item.description:
COLOMBO, March 11 (Reuters) - A Sri Lankan court has ordered that the bodies of 84 sailors killed in an attack on an Iranian warship off the ‚Äčisland nation's coast last week be handed over to the embassy ‚ÄĆof Iran, local media reported on Wednesday.
The warship, IRIS Dena, was hit by a torpedo from a U.S. submarine while it was returning from a naval exercise organised by India, amid ‚Äčthe U.S.-Israeli war on Iran that has wreaked havoc on global markets, sent ‚Äčoil prices soaring, and disrupted trade and travel.
The court order was ‚Ā†issued on Wednesday following a request from the Galle Harbour Police in the ‚Äčsouthern port city of Galle, the media reports said.
The bodies are currently at the ‚Äčmorgue in Galle's National Hospital.
Sri Lanka has also granted 30-day entry visas to 208 crew members from a second Iranian vessel who were taken in by the South Asian country after the vessel ‚Äčexperienced engine problems in the same region, Deputy Defence Minister Aruna Jayasekera told ‚ÄčReuters.
The Sri Lankan foreign ministry is in touch with the Iranian embassy in Colombo about the ‚ÄĆcrew ‚Ā†and the mission is in turn consulting Tehran, Jayasekara said.
"We are expecting a response from Tehran over the next few days," he said.
A spokesperson for Sri Lanka's cabinet said on Tuesday that the second vessel, IRIS Booshehr, was nine nautical miles from ‚Äčthe coast and ‚Äčwould eventually be ‚Ā†moved.
Sri Lankan President Anura Kumara Dissanayake last week said the ship would be moved to the Trincomalee harbour on the east ‚Äčcoast.
Reuters reported that Washington was pressing Colombo to not repatriate the ‚Äčsurvivors from ‚Ā†the two Iranian vessels.
Thirty-two people survived the attack on IRIS Dena.
Both Washington and Tehran are key trade partners for Sri Lanka. The U.S. accounts for about 40% of ‚Ā†its apparel ‚Äčexports and Iran is one of its main ‚Äčtea buyers.
A third Iranian warship , the IRIS Lavan with 183 crew members, is docked in the southern Indian ‚Äčport of Kochi.

item.title: Russian drone strike in Ukraine's Kharkiv kills two, officials say
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - A Russian ‚ÄĆdrone hit a civilian business in Ukraine's second-largest city of Kharkiv, ‚Äčkilling two people and injuring ‚Äčfive, local officials said.
"Unfortunately, there ‚Ā†is preliminary information about ‚Äčtwo people killed," Mayor Ihor Terekhov ‚Äčsaid on the Telegram messaging app.
Kharkiv Regional Governor Oleh Syniehubov said that ‚Äčaccording to preliminary information ‚Äčall of the wounded were in serious ‚ÄĆcondition ‚Ā†and were receiving necessary assistance.
He added that the attack caused a fire at the business.
Kharkiv, ‚Äčlocated ‚Äč30 km (18 ‚Ā†miles) from the Russian border, withstood early ‚Äčadvances by Russian forces ‚Äčafter ‚Ā†their full-scale invasion in February 2022 and has since been ‚Ā†a ‚Äčfrequent target of Russian ‚Äčair attacks.

item.title: Trump says white South Africans are persecuted; some are returning to a better life
item.description:
JOHANNESBURG, March 11 (Reuters) - Andrew Veitch left South Africa after being held up at gunpoint in his car. But now he feels there are greater threats in the United States, he said, citing mass shootings in public ‚Äčplaces as well as violence by U.S. immigration officers.
"People are being shot in broad daylight. American citizens are being shot and killed," said the 53-year-old, who moved to California in ‚ÄĆ2003. "I don't want to live in a place like this."
President Donald Trump's officials have said Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers were justified in firing the shots that killed two U.S. citizens in January, although video evidence has contradicted their accounts.
Veitch plans to return to South Africa this year, one of thousands of white South Africans coming back, despite Trump's statements that the white minority is being persecuted by the country's Black majority government.
Pretoria says there is no evidence of discrimination or persecution against whites. Many have left since the ‚Äčend of white minority rule in 1994, some citing crime and difficulty getting jobs, but many are also returning.
Veitch is among 12,000 people who have checked their citizenship status in an online portal ‚Äčlaunched by the government in November after the overturning of a 1995 law that stripped citizenship from some South Africans who left.
They represent a fraction of South Africans ‚Ā†abroad. The latest official statistics on returnees, from 2022, show that almost 15,000 white South Africans returned that year.
Home Affairs Minister Leon Schreiber said 1,000 people had reclaimed their ‚Äčcitizenship, a number he expected to grow significantly as the programme takes off.
"There is definitely a sense of optimism for South Africans abroad," said Schreiber, part of the white-led Democratic Alliance party that has ruled in coalition with ‚Äčthe African National Congress since 2024. He is a returnee himself, having spent time in the U.S. and Germany before coming home in 2019.
Two recruitment agencies that help expats relocate said the number of inquiries had jumped, and Reuters spoke to 10 South Africans who had either returned or were planning to, seven of them from across Europe and three from the United States.
Their reasons, echoed in a 25,000-strong "Return to South Africa" Facebook group some belong to, included being closer to family, lower living costs and ‚Äčpolitical turmoil abroad.
The Trump administration is ramping up its new refugee programme for white South Africans, focusing on Afrikaners, the descendants of Dutch settlers. About 3,500 South Africans have entered the United States as refugees since the programme ‚Äčstarted in May 2025.
Applicants interviewed by Reuters complained of being victims of racially motivated crime and employment equity laws that favour non-white candidates in order to redress decades of white minority rule.
Other Afrikaners, like Naomi Saphire, take a different view.
She had been ‚ÄĆsettled in ‚Ā†the United States for two decades when she came back for a holiday and realized how much she missed home.
Last year, she left North Carolina for a seaside town in South Africa's Western Cape, where she said her three children spend more time outdoors, health insurance is affordable and she prefers the schools.
"My heart is just full of gratefulness to be here," the 46-year-old said from her home in Plettenberg Bay. "The U.S. has been really good to me (but) I just felt like I was depriving my kids of this life."
Saphire said she knows many people who are returning home.
Crime and joblessness are major issues in South Africa, but ‚Äčthe unemployment rate is 35% for Black people compared ‚Äčwith 8% for whites, according to the latest ‚Ā†figures from the national statistics agency Stats SA.
Police statistics released last year showed that even farm murders, which Trump has focused on, killed more Black people than whites. Reuters has found that photos and videos Trump has presented on the matter were taken out of context or misrepresented.
Still, Stats SA estimated a net outward flow of half a million ‚Äčwhites since 2001, including 95,000 from 2021 to 2026. There is no regular data on returnees, but a Stats SA analysis showed that 28,000 South Africans ‚Äčreturned in 2022, 52.9% - or some ‚Ā†14,800 - of whom were white.
Anton van Heerden, CEO of employment agency DNA Employer of Record, said inquiries from white South Africans seeking to return had jumped 70% in the past six months. Angel Jones, CEO of Johannesburg-based recruitment firm HomecomingEx, reported a roughly 30% rise in inquiries since 2024.
A boom in remote working since the COVID-19 pandemic has also helped; three of the returnees interviewed by Reuters kept their jobs abroad.
Many South African professionals have extensive private security at home ‚Ā†which minimizes ‚Äčcrime risks, Van Heerden said.
"If you can afford to live in a safe environment, you can have a much better life than I ‚Äčthink in most places in the northern hemisphere," he said.
Several returnees also said they felt life in South Africa had improved since they left. Power cuts, which used to be daily, for example, have largely stopped.
Thirty-eight-year-old engineer Eugene Jansen, who returned from the Netherlands in December ‚Äčwith his wife and two children, said the returnees he knows feel things are getting better.
"The opinion is that the country is improving," he said.

item.title: Australia's competition regulator to heighten fuel market scrutiny amid Mideast war
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - Australia's competition regulator ‚Äčwill seek explanations from fuel retailers ‚ÄĆon pricing during the current Middle East crisis, the regulator said on Wednesday, owing to ‚Äčconsumer concerns about price spikes and ‚Äčdistribution issues in regional and rural ‚Ā†parts of the country.
Global oil prices ‚Äčhad surged to their highest since 2022 earlier ‚Äčin the week as Saudi Arabia and other OPEC members cut supplies amid the U.S.-Israeli war ‚Äčon Iran.
The Australian Competition and Consumer ‚ÄčCommission said it will also commence weekly market updates ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†provide increased transparency to consumers and enhanced scrutiny of retailers‚Äô behaviour.
The regulator, which wrote to petrol retailers last week seeking ‚Äčinformation about ‚Äčrecent ‚Ā†price increases, will take action against them if consumers are misled ‚Äčon rapid increase in prices ‚Äčor ‚Ā†for breaches of the competition provisions, Commissioner Anna Brakey said.
The regulator is also prioritising ‚Ā†measures ‚Äčto assist with diesel ‚Äčdistribution issues in regional and rural areas.

item.title: Australia expects Iran war to intensify, shuts some Middle East embassies
item.description: SYDNEY, March 11 (Reuters) - Australia has closed its embassies in Abu Dhabi and Tel Aviv as well ‚Äčas its consulate in Dubai, the ‚ÄĆforeign minister said on Wednesday, amid fears the conflict in the Middle East could intensify.

item.title: Iran war shatters Trump's case for fossil fuels
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - The economic risks of the world‚Äôs heavy dependence on oil and gas have been laid bare twice in four years - first with Russia‚Äôs invasion of Ukraine and now with the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran - undermining U.S. President Donald Trump‚Äôs flagship push to double down on fossil fuels.
The dramatic supply disruptions and price spikes unleashed by the latest Middle East conflict are ‚Äčlikely to harden the resolve of many governments to accelerate the drive for greater energy self‚ÄĎsufficiency ‚Äď and that will include clean energy.
The ongoing Middle East conflict has seen the closure ‚ÄĆof the vital Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20% of global oil and gas shipments flow, sending shockwaves through energy markets.
While Brent crude prices have dropped from $119 per barrel on Monday - their highest level since June 2022 - to around $90, the global benchmark is still more than 24% higher since the start of the war.
In any case, the physical shortages that are rapidly developing are heaping pressure on economies worldwide, reviving fears of higher inflation, weaker industrial output and slower growth globally. The pain has been particularly acute in Asia, ‚Äčwhich relies on the Middle East for nearly 60% of its crude oil imports.
The latest crisis is a reminder that fossil fuels, like renewables, can be unreliable and volatile ‚Äď and also that securing ‚Äčdomestic sources of energy is critical.
This, in turn, is altering the debate about the global energy system‚Äôs two supposedly competing objectives: tackling climate change and ensuring access ‚Ā†to cheap, reliable energy.
It turns out the latter may end up helping with the former.
THE ERA OF ENERGY SHOCKS
In the debate about whether to prioritize cutting fossil fuels or keeping energy cheap, the pendulum swung decisively ‚Äčtoward the energy transition after the signing of the 2015 United Nations‚ÄĎbacked Paris climate agreement . This triggered a wave of investment in renewable power and a sharp pullback in spending on oil, gas and coal ‚Äď the main sources ‚Äčof greenhouse gas emissions.
However, Russia‚Äôs full‚ÄĎscale invasion of Ukraine in 2022 delivered a brutal wake‚ÄĎup call.
Europe‚Äôs heavy reliance on imported Russian gas was exposed as a significant vulnerability when supplies collapsed, sending prices soaring and compounding global inflation just as economies were emerging from the COVID‚ÄĎ19 pandemic.
That extreme volatility forced governments from Europe to Asia into rapid action . Some fast‚ÄĎtracked solar and wind projects to insulate their economies from future shocks. But many also ramped up domestic production of gas and coal, and others - notably China and India - ‚Äčembraced an unapologetic ‚Äúall-of-the-above‚ÄĚ approach.
The crisis highlighted a stubborn reality: fossil fuels remain deeply embedded in the global economy. Despite record growth in renewables last year, oil and gas still account for almost 60% of global energy demand, ‚Äčaccording to the Energy Institute, underscoring how far the world remains from any rapid exit - and how exposed it remains to future shocks.
NO NET ZERO?
The Russia-Ukraine war is not the only force that has reshaped energy geopolitics over the ‚ÄĆpast decade. Another ‚Ā†tectonic shift is the rise of the U.S. as the world‚Äôs top oil and gas producer, powered by the shale revolution.
Since taking office more than a year ago, Trump has championed U.S. ‚Äúenergy dominance,‚ÄĚ seeking to unleash fossil fuel development while rolling back support for renewable energy.
The Trump administration, alongside a growing chorus of politicians globally, has attacked net‚ÄĎzero ambitions, which refer to the point where greenhouse gas emissions are balanced by the amount removed from the atmosphere.
U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright slammed net-zero as a ‚Äúdestructive illusion‚ÄĚ during an International Energy Agency (IEA) meeting last month, arguing that the shift to renewables imposes unnecessary costs on consumers already strained by inflation.
The price tag attached to building new renewable ‚Äčpower systems is certainly daunting.
Global investment in renewables would ‚Äčneed to climb to about $1.4 trillion per year ‚Ā†by 2030, from roughly $624 billion in 2024, to meet emissions‚ÄĎreduction targets, according to the International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA). The IEA estimates that spending on electricity grids alone must rise by around 50% by 2030 from today‚Äôs roughly $400 billion a year.
Even if these enormous sums were met, the notion that the world can quickly switch from fossil fuels to ‚Äčrenewables is clearly unrealistic.
But when viewing the situation through the lens of energy security, it makes a lot of sense for governments to look beyond ‚Äčfossil fuels. Indeed, it appears ‚Ā†negligent not to.
Consider that in the past week, a single country has been able to upend the global energy system by blocking one waterway that is a mere 39 kilometers (24 miles) wide at its narrowest point. If that were to persist, it could bring significant segments of the global economy to a veritable standstill.
Governments will take notice, especially the most vulnerable economies, and will likely seek to develop more domestic sources of energy, whether they be fossil ‚Ā†fuels or renewables.
The ‚Äčpush for energy security may thus end up doing what climate policy alone could not: pushing countries to move faster toward a ‚Äčmore diversified and resilient energy system.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Ron Bousso , a columnist for Reuters.)

And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters ‚Äčapp , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Mideast conflict may hasten Europe‚Äôs gas divorce from Russia
item.description:
SOFIA, March 11 (Reuters) - The natural gas price shock Europe is experiencing from the Iran war could accelerate the region‚Äôs decoupling from Russian energy, while the continent is pushed further into the arms of the U.S.
The Iranian attack that forced QatarEnergy, the world's second-largest liquefied natural gas exporter , to halt production last week sent European benchmark gas prices surging ‚Äčby nearly 50%. This underscored how exposed the continent remains to geopolitical shocks beyond its borders and the necessity of prioritizing energy security above all else.
Qatar supplied only about 4% of European Union gas ‚ÄĆimports in 2025, according to the European Council , opens new tab , but with these volumes now unavailable, the marginal gas molecule will increasingly come from the U.S., the world's top gas producer and LNG exporter.
This, in turn, could give the U.S. even more leverage to push Europe to accelerate its full decoupling from Russian gas. That is something Western leaders have sought to achieve through sanctions since Moscow's invasion of Ukraine four years ago.
Any temporary easing of U.S. sanctions on Russian oil during the war shouldn‚Äôt alter this trajectory for gas. And Russian President Vladimir Putin‚Äôs threat last week to halt ‚ÄčRussia‚Äôs remaining gas exports to Europe will provide further impetus for reducing this dependency.
Russian gas still accounts for roughly 10% of EU imports. The EU has mandated that all Russian gas imports must end by September 2027, but ‚Äčlegal ambiguities and loopholes could prolong dependence on Russian gas beyond 2028.
RUSSIA'S LINGERING GRIP ON EASTERN EUROPE
While the U.S. now supplies the majority of Europe‚Äôs LNG, Russia‚Äôs state-controlled ‚Ā†giant Gazprom (GAZP.MM) , opens new tab remains the dominant supplier of the super-chilled fuel in Central and Eastern Europe and Southeastern Europe. These regions are also the destination for almost all of the Russian pipeline gas flowing into Europe, with the remainder ‚Äčentering EU markets via the TurkStream pipeline system linking Russia and Turkey.
Unlike Northern Europe, which has rapidly diversified its energy operations in recent years through new LNG terminals and pipeline interconnections, Central and Southern countries remain structurally exposed. Limited storage, fragmented ‚Äčtransmission tariffs and poorly integrated markets have kept Russian pipeline gas commercially more attractive.
This is why, at the Transatlantic Gas Security Summit , opens new tab held in Washington in late February, just days before the conflict began, the Americans had a clear message: accelerate the flow of U.S. LNG into Europe's most vulnerable markets.
NEW ENERGY FRONTIER
To achieve this, senior U.S. officials and European energy ministers are focused on a new flagship project: the Vertical Gas Corridor linking Greek LNG terminals with Bulgaria, Romania, Moldova and Ukraine.
The Vertical Gas Corridor could fundamentally reorient Central and Southern ‚ÄčEuropean gas trade flows toward Atlantic supply chains, locking in long-term LNG imports and making the Balkans the next frontier market for exporters.
As part of this effort, major U.S. exporters including Cheniere (LNG.N) , opens new tab and Venture Global (VG.N) , opens new tab have agreed to supply around 8 billion ‚Äčcubic metres (bcm) per year under 20‚ÄĎyear contracts with traders and governments in Central and Eastern Europe. That is equivalent to roughly 10% of U.S. LNG exports to the EU in 2025.
Those buyers have in turn signed non-binding import deals with European intermediaries to support ‚ÄĆthe flow of ‚Ā†LNG through these regions.
European leaders‚Äô willingness to lock themselves into long-term gas contracts after spending over a decade speaking about the need to reduce fossil fuel consumption highlights Europe‚Äôs new reality: energy security concerns outweigh decarbonisation needs.
OBSTACLES TO A U.S. LNG SURGE
The success of the Vertical Gas Corridor is far from guaranteed. Cross-border tariffs between Central and Southern European countries remain prohibitively high. However, regional operators are proposing a new ‚Äúsuper-bundled‚ÄĚ agreement that would allow suppliers to reserve the entire corridor in a single contract.
Infrastructure bottlenecks also persist. LNG terminals lack sufficient storage, meaning underground facilities in Bulgaria and Ukraine would need to be expanded to enable large-scale flows.
Additionally, without EU financial support to cover the extra costs needed for transmission system ‚Äčoperators to upgrade the grid, Russian gas delivered via existing ‚Äčpipelines will remain cheaper, undermining diversification efforts.
Finally, for ‚Ā†the Vertical Gas Corridor to function at scale, LNG must flow north from Turkey. The country hosts substantial underutilised regasification capacity, totalling 58 bcm annually, based on my calculations using data from Argus , opens new tab , enough to increase U.S. LNG supply to Europe by an estimated 70%. Around half of that capacity could potentially be used to ship volumes along the Corridor ‚Äčto Ukraine and Central Europe.
In practice, however, Russian gas still occupies a significant share of Turkish transit capacity through TurkStream. And in an increasingly fractured geopolitical world, Turkey ‚Äčmay be unwilling to align ‚Ā†with the interests of Europe and the U.S., especially if the financial incentives still make the alternative more attractive.
The U.S. has framed its LNG exports as essential to Europe‚Äôs economic security, industrial growth, and even its emerging infrastructure demand ‚Äď and the conflict in Iran has shown that, under current circumstances, that is probably correct.
Some may question whether deepening this dependence is prudent, especially given America‚Äôs decision to pursue the war in Iran despite knowing the potential disruption it could cause to the global energy ‚Ā†system.
But Europe has ‚Äčfew other options, and reducing Moscow‚Äôs influence over those EU economies most exposed to political and economic pressure may be worth the risk.
(The ‚Äčviews expressed here are those of Martin Vladimirov , Director of the Geoeconomics Program of the Center for the Study of Democracy (CSD))

item.title: The dangerous politics of US oil-driven redistribution
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - U.S. stock market indexes have weathered this month's oil shock well so far because investors expect only a glancing overall GDP hit from higher energy costs. But that resilience masks a potentially toxic shift of income from households to "Big Energy" - and packs big risks in an election year.
With uncertainty still rife about the extent and length of the Iran war , sharp ‚Äčand volatile spikes in energy prices have left oil and natural gas costs looking structurally higher over the coming year. Worries about food prices are creeping in too, as fertilizer ‚ÄĆshipments are also being snagged in the Middle East Gulf region.
As inflation is spurred once again, the chances of U.S. interest rate cuts this year have been reduced or pushed back.
None of that sounds like good news for the U.S. economy or consumers overall, at least not until you make calculations about America's new-found position as a net exporter of energy.
For the producers and exporters of all that domestic U.S. oil and natural gas, this is a ‚Äčbig windfall. And unlike their competitors in the Middle East or elsewhere, there's no security threat or blockage in getting their product to market.
Carlyle strategist Jason Thomas points out ‚Äčthat the U.S. ran an annualized energy trade surplus of nearly $100 billion in the last 12 months and the surge in oil and gas ‚Ā†prices will only increase that substantially.
"Higher gasoline prices still could be conceived of as a 'tax' on U.S. consumers, but that's offset today by an increase in domestic revenues that didn't ‚Äčexist when the energy price shock of 2007-08 shaved over 1% from U.S. GDP," he wrote, pointing to the period in which oil and gas prices roughly trebled in 18 months.
But ‚Äčwith the U.S. now a net exporter of petroleum products for the first time since the 1950s, assumptions of a 2007‚Äď08‚ÄĎstyle GDP hit - one that could halve U.S. growth on a comparable oil price surge today - now look wide of the mark.
GLANCING BLOW
Apollo Global (APO.N) , opens new tab Chief Economist Torsten Slok's "ready reckoner" - a quick rule‚ÄĎof‚ÄĎthumb guide built from Federal Reserve models - shows that even if crude oil stays above $100 a barrel through 2027, the shock's impact fades over time and leaves only minor aggregate impacts on the ‚Äčmacro economy.
While the net impact on headline inflation could be as high as 0.7 percentage point, the effect on real GDP, unemployment and core inflation is just 0.1 point, he ‚Äčshowed.
"The U.S. is a net oil exporter and energy efficiency has improved significantly over the years - meaning the economy burns less oil per unit of GDP compared to historical levels (and) helps dampen the overall negative impact ‚ÄĆof price spikes."
Not ‚Ā†everyone thinks it would be such plain sailing if the shock persists.
Morgan Stanley estimates that if a 25% oil price jump driven by a supply shock lasts four quarters, real GDP would be about 1.5% lower, with most of the damage occurring in the first three.
But the issue is that GDP can hold quite well even though everyone seems to acknowledge that higher energy prices, and inflation more broadly, act as a tax on households.
ELECTION YEAR PRESSURES
If the impact of a sustained oil shock were to have the minimal GDP impacts some suggest, then all that's really happening is that ‚Äčmoney is being redistributed to domestic energy ‚Äčcompanies from already stretched households.
Government could step ‚Ā†in to subsidize and cushion households, but that simply shifts the burden onto an already stretched Treasury - and ultimately back onto the public.
The political toxicity of high U.S. headline inflation - even if the Fed focuses on core rates that strip out energy and food - has been well documented since the COVID-19 pandemic.
In ‚Äča midterm election year , sensitivities in Washington will be even higher, which helps explain why Wall Street seems convinced the conflict will be limited and that ‚Äčit should buy the ‚Ā†dip as GDP sails on regardless.
But a raid on household wallets that just ends up in domestic oil and gas firms' coffers might not go down well at the polls, especially if voters believe the oil surge was triggered by U.S. military action in the first place.
(The opinions expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source ‚Ā†for global ‚Äčfinancial commentary.

item.title: Carlyle to sell Colombian oil firm SierraCol to Philippine's Prime Infrastructure
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - Private equity group Carlyle (CG.O) , opens new tab has agreed to sell its Colombian oil producer SierraCol to Prime Infrastructure Capital, the infrastructure unit ‚Äčof Filipino businessman Enrique K. Razon Jr., for an undisclosed ‚ÄĆsum, the U.S. company said on Wednesday.
Carlyle, which set up SierraCol in 2020 after buying assets from Occidental Petroleum (OXY.N) , opens new tab , had sought around $1.5 billion for the Colombian ‚Äčfirm, sources had told Reuters in 2025.
Elsewhere in the oil ‚Äčand gas sector, Carlyle in January reached a non-binding, initial ‚Ā†agreement to buy most international assets from sanctioned Russian firm Lukoil (LKOH.MM) , opens new tab ‚Äčand merge its European refining vehicle Moeve with Portuguese energy firm Galp's (GALP.LS) , opens new tab downstream ‚Äčbusiness .
"This is where our track record is strong and I expect to continue that. We have a clear playbook for executing complex carve-outs and strengthening these businesses," ‚Äčsaid co-head of Carlyle International Energy Partners (CIEP), Bob Maguire.
He said CIEP ‚Äčhad no fixed views on how much investment to allocate to downstream or upstream ‚ÄĆacquisitions.
CIEP ‚Ā†managing director Parminder Singh told Reuters that it has been tough to extract assets from the bigger players in the current market as majors are keen to boost their own oil and gas reserves ‚Äčwhile retrenching on ‚Äčlow-carbon projects.
Carlyle said ‚Ā†it has invested around $1 billion in SierraCol since 2020, mainly spending on the firm's existing assets to ‚Äčstabilise its net production at around 45,000 barrels of ‚Äčoil equivalent ‚Ā†per day and reduce operational emissions.
SierraCol's gross output of 77,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day makes up around 10% of Colombia's overall production.
SierraCol ‚Ā†had $205 ‚Äčmillion in free cashflow for the 12 ‚Äčmonths to October 2025 and net debt of $618 million, according to its website.
Prime Infrastructure runs ‚Äčenergy, waste and water infrastructure.

item.title: Israeli strike hits Beirut apartment block, Lebanese media reports
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 11 (Reuters) - An Israeli strike hit an apartment block in central Beirut on Wednesday, Lebanon's state news ‚Äčagency reported, further widening Israeli attacks in the ‚ÄĆcapital beyond the Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs where heavy strikes continued.
Footage showed extensive damage to two floors of the apartment ‚Äčblock in the Aicha Bakkar neighbourhood, and smoke ‚Äčrising from the building.
There was no immediate comment ‚Ā†from the Israeli military and no immediate reports ‚Äčof casualties.
It would mark the second Israeli strike in ‚Äčthe heart of Beirut in four days. On Sunday, an Israeli strike hit a hotel in the seafront Raouche neighbourhood. The Israeli ‚Äčmilitary said that strike targeted five senior members ‚Äčof Iran's Revolutionary Guards Quds Force.
Lebanese authorities say nearly 570 people ‚ÄĆhave ‚Ā†been killed by Israeli strikes since Iran-backed Hezbollah opened fire on March 2 to avenge the killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei at the start ‚Äčof the U.S.-Israeli war ‚Äčon Iran.
Israel ‚Ā†kept up heavy strikes on the southern suburbs, known as Dahiyeh, overnight. The ‚ÄčIsraeli military has ordered residents of the ‚Äčpredominantly Shi'ite ‚Ā†Muslim Dahiyeh to leave, along with residents of a swathe of southern Lebanon and parts of the east.
Some ‚Ā†700,000 ‚Äčpeople have been displaced in ‚ÄčLebanon, or more than a tenth of the population, the United Nations ‚Äčhas reported.

item.title: UK police ban pro-Iranian London march due to 'extreme tensions'
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - British police said they had banned a pro-Iranian march due to take place in London on Sunday, citing the possibility of "extreme tensions" with ‚Äčcounter-protesters and the risk posed by Tehran during the conflict in the ‚ÄĆMiddle East.
The Al Quds march in central London is organised annually by the Islamic Human Rights Commission, which the police said was supportive of the Iranian regime.
The threshold to ban a protest ‚Äčis high in Britain, with the police saying this was the first ‚Äčtime the power had been invoked in 14 years, but the ‚Ā†risks of public disorder were "so severe" it was right to block it. The ban ‚Äčalso applies to any counter-protest marches.
The Islamic Human Rights Commission has previously said on ‚ÄčX that the march was being held in support of Palestinian liberation.
Police said that previous Al Quds marches have resulted in arrests for supporting terrorist organisations and anti-Semitic hate crimes, and said ‚Äčthat even with the ban in place they were facing a "challenging, potentially violent weekend".
"We ‚Äčhave taken into consideration the likely impact on protests of the volatile situation in the Middle ‚ÄĆEast, ‚Ā†with the Iranian regime attacking British allies and military bases overseas," the police statement published late on Tuesday said.
"We must also consider that the security services have been publicly clear about the threats we are facing on UK soil from the Iranian ‚Äčregime."
British police have faced ‚Äčheavy scrutiny of ‚Ā†their handling of regular, large pro-Palestinian protests in London since the October 7, 2023 attacks by Hamas on Israel, with supporters ‚Äčand opponents at odds over the right of free speech, the ‚Äčsupport shown ‚Ā†for a proscribed group and the impact on the Jewish community.
Last week, British police arrested four men on suspicion of helping Iran's intelligence services carry out surveillance of people and ‚Ā†locations ‚Äčlinked to the Jewish community in London.
Should the Islamic ‚ÄčHuman Rights Commission go ahead with a static assembly, which there is no law to ban, police said ‚Äčit would be placed under strict conditions.

item.title: Two drones fall in vicinity of Dubai airport as Iran crisis shows no sign of easing
item.description:
DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - Two drones came down in the vicinity of Dubai ‚ÄčInternational Airport (DXB) on Wednesday, injuring four people, Dubai's media ‚ÄĆoffice said, as attacks on infrastructure across the Gulf continued on the 12th day of the Iran crisis , which has caused widespread ‚Äčdisruption to global air traffic.
"Authorities confirm that two ‚Äčdrones fell in the vicinity of Dubai International ‚Ā†Airport (DXB) a short while ago," the media office posted ‚Äčon X, adding that air traffic is operating as ‚Äčnormal.
The attack resulted in minor injuries to two Ghanaian nationals and one Bangladeshi national, as well as moderate injuries to one Indian ‚Äčnational, it added.
The outbreak of the U.S.-Israel war against ‚ÄčIran has led to flight cancellations, rescheduling and re-routing for airlines ‚ÄĆacross ‚Ā†the globe, as most airspaces in the Middle East, including Qatar, remain shut over missile and drone concerns. It has also led to an energy crisis that has ‚Äčled to ‚Äča spike in fuel ‚Ā†prices.
UAE airlines like Dubai's Emirates and Abu Dhabi's Etihad have resumed some flights since ‚Äčthe beginning of the crisis on February ‚Äč28 ‚Ā†but they are still operating below their capacity, with the attack on Wednesday marking a renewed hit to DXB, ‚Ā†the world's ‚Äčbusiest airport for international passengers, ‚Äčwhich last year handled nearly 100 million passengers.

item.title: Opposition candidates in Congo Republic's presidential election
item.description:
DAKAR, March 11 (Reuters) - Denis Sassou Nguesso , the 82-year-old president of Republic of Congo, is seeking another five-year term in an election on March 15, aiming to extend his more than four decades in office in ‚Äčthe oil-rich Central African nation.
A former paratrooper, Sassou seized power in 1979 and has ‚ÄĆgoverned almost continuously since, except for a five-year hiatus in the 1990s.
He faces six opposition candidates in a race he is widely expected to win. The opposition remains fragmented, with established parties boycotting the vote and some prominent potential ‚Äčcandidates either in prison or in exile.
Following are details on his challengers: JOSEPH KIGNOUMBI KIA MBOUNGOU
Joseph ‚ÄčKignoumbi Kia Mboungou, 73, a veteran lawmaker from the southwestern Lekoumou department and leader of the political ‚Ā†party La Cha√ģne, is running for president for the fifth time since 2002. He earned 0.62% ‚Äčin the 2021 election.
His campaign has focused on what he describes as the need for political change ‚Äčand economic diversification, highlighting his goals of reducing Congo's heavy reliance on oil and tackling poverty.
Uphrem Dave Mafoula, 43, an economist, is making his second consecutive presidential bid after running in 2021, when he was the youngest candidate and ‚Äčsecured 0.52% of the vote.
His platform centres on governance reforms, job creation and reducing territorial inequalities.
Melaine Destin Gavet Elengo, 35, is the youngest contender in the race and a first-time presidential candidate.
An engineer ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†the oil sector, he presents himself as part of a new generation seeking political renewal. His campaign emphasizes transparency, an independent justice system and inclusive development.
Mabio Mavoungou Zinga, 69, a retired customs inspector and former member of parliament, is running under the banner of the Alliance party, a coalition of opposition ‚Äčgroups.
He has pledged to ‚Äčtackle corruption and free ‚Ā†jailed opposition leaders Jean-Marie Michel Mokoko and Andr√© Okombi Salissa, who were arrested after the disputed 2016 election and later sentenced to 20 years in ‚Äčprison on charges of undermining state security. This is his first presidential race.
Vivien ‚Ā†Romain Manangou, 43, an independent candidate and public law university lecturer, is also making his first presidential bid.
His campaign has centered on institutional reforms, improving public finances and promoting national unity.
Anguios Nganguia Engambe, around 60, president ‚Ā†of the ‚ÄčParty for Action of the Republic, is running for president ‚Äčfor the fourth consecutive time, having stood in 2009, 2016 and 2021, when he won just 0.18% of the vote.
His platform ‚Äčfocuses on bridging political divisions and increasing political participation.

item.title: South Korea Presidential aide says AI cooperation with UAE could accelerate after conflict
item.description: SEOUL, March 11 (Reuters) - South Korea's senior presidential secretary for AI, Ha Jung-woo, said on Wednesday that artificial intelligence cooperation with the ‚ÄčUnited Arab Emirates could accelerate after the Middle East ‚ÄĆconflict ends, given the UAE's need to strengthen its defence capabilities.

item.title: Israel believes Iran's new leader was lightly wounded in attacks, senior official says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 11 (Reuters) - Israel's ‚Äčintelligence assessment is that Iran's ‚ÄĆnew supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei , was lightly wounded in the Israeli-U.S. joint air war ‚Äčagainst Iran and that is ‚Äčwhy he has not been ‚Ā†seen in public, a senior ‚ÄčIsraeli official told Reuters.
Iran's Revolutionary ‚ÄčGuards forced through the choice of Mojtaba Khamenei as the new supreme leader, ‚Äčseeing him as a more ‚Äčpliant version of his father who would back ‚ÄĆtheir ‚Ā†hardline policies.
Khamenei's selection may add up to a more aggressive stance abroad and sterner internal ‚Äčrepression, said ‚Äčthree ‚Ā†senior Iranian sources, a reformist former official and ‚Äčanother insider.
Israeli Defense Minister ‚ÄčIsrael ‚Ā†Katz said last week that any leader appointed by the current Iranian leadership ‚Ā†would "be ‚Äčan unequivocal target ‚Äčfor elimination."

item.title: Romanian to review US request to use local air base for Iran operations
item.description:
BUCHAREST, March 11 (Reuters) - Romanian President Nicusor Dan has convened the EU ‚ÄĆand NATO nation's top defence council on Wednesday to discuss whether to allow U.S. aircraft access to its military bases for support linked ‚Äčto its Tehran operations , political sources said.
The council will meet ‚Äčfor the first time this year to discuss the ‚Ā†security fallout from the conflict in the Middle East, ‚Äčits impact on Romania's energy market and "the temporary deployment of military ‚Äčcapability on Romanian territory."
That deployment, political sources said without elaborating, referred to a U.S. request to use the Mihail Kogalniceanu air base.
While some EU ‚Äčcountries--such as France, Greece and Italy-- have sent warships to Cyprus ‚Äčafter Iranian-made drones struck a British base on the island, others allow use ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†their military bases.
Most EU top officials have condemned Iranian strikes in the region and urged an end and diplomatic solution to the conflict.
The U.S. withdrew about ‚Äč1,000 troops from ‚ÄčRomania's Mihail ‚Ā†Kogalniceanu air base last year, as the U.S. focused on its own borders and the Indo-Pacific ‚Äčregion. Another 1,000 U.S. troops remain in ‚ÄčRomania.
The permanent ‚Ā†allied presence in Romania stands at around 3,500 NATO troops, including U.S. soldiers.
Romania shares a 650 km (400 mile) land border with ‚Ā†Ukraine, ‚Äčover which Russian drones have flown ‚Äčtowards Kyiv, while mines in the Black Sea from the conflict impact key ‚Äčtrade and energy routes.

item.title: Russia says it shot down two British-supplied Storm Shadow missiles
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 11 (Reuters) - Russian Defence Ministry said on ‚ÄčWednesday that its forces ‚ÄĆshot down two British-supplied Storm Shadow missiles over ‚Äčthe past 24 ‚Äčhours, according to the ‚Ā†RIA news agency.
Earlier ‚ÄčUkraine said it had ‚Äčused Storm Shadow missiles to strike a key plant ‚Äčproducing missile components ‚Äčin the Russian city of Bryansk ‚ÄĆon ‚Ā†Tuesday.
The governor of Bryansk region, Alexander Bogomaz, said that six ‚Äčcivilians were ‚Äčkilled ‚Ā†and 42 injured in the ‚Äčattack.
Separately, the Russian ‚Äčdefence ‚Ā†ministry said its forces had captured the ‚Ā†settlement ‚Äčof Chervona Zoria ‚Äčin Ukraine's Sumy region.

item.title: Gasunie urges Dutch government to build strategic gas reserve
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 11 (Reuters) - Dutch natural gas grid operator Gasunie on Wednesday urged ‚Äčthe government to build a strategic gas ‚ÄĆreserve to ensure the Netherlands can withstand a prolonged disruption of supplies.
Gasunie said the Netherlands was protected against short-term ‚Äčdisruptions due to cold weather, but not for ‚Äčlong-term problems caused by, for instance, geopolitical tensions ‚Ā†or sabotage.
"The current geopolitical climate demands realism and ‚Äčpreparations for the unexpected to happen on the ‚Äčlonger term," Gasunie director Hans Coenen said.
The gas company called for research on the use of the so-called cushion gas that ‚Äčis available in the country's large gas storages ‚Äčas a reserve.
Cushion gas is permanently stored in natural gas ‚ÄĆstorages ‚Ā†to maintain adequate pressure.
The Netherlands was once one of Europe's major gas suppliers, but has turned into an importer after it shut down the large Groningen ‚Äčfield due to ‚Äčthe earthquakes ‚Ā†that gas extraction had caused there.
Most of the gas used in the Netherlands ‚Äčnow comes from Norway, but an ‚Äčincreasing part ‚Ā†is supplied by the U.S. in the form of liquefied natural gas.
The country still has large underground ‚Ā†gas ‚Äčstorages, of which about 85% of capacity ‚Äčwould be needed to meet yearly gas demand under regular circumstances, ‚ÄčGasunie said.

item.title: Dutch court scraps cap on number of flights at Amsterdam airport
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 11 (Reuters) - The Dutch Supreme ‚ÄčCourt on ‚ÄĆWednesday scrapped a government decision ‚Äčto limit ‚Äčthe number of flights ‚Ā†at Amsterdam's ‚ÄčSchiphol airport, ‚Äčone of Europe busiest hubs.
The court ‚Äčsaid the ‚Äč2024 decision to limit ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†number of flights to 478,000 per ‚Äčyear, ‚Äčmade ‚Ā†in an effort ‚Äčto cut ‚Äčnoise ‚Ā†pollution, had not been ‚Ā†properly ‚Äčmotivated.

item.title: South Korea and Ghana to expand cooperation on climate, tech and maritime security
item.description: SEOUL, March 11 (Reuters) - South Korean President Lee Jae Myung held summit talks on ‚ÄčWednesday with Ghanaian President John ‚ÄĆMahama, agreeing to deepen cooperation in climate, digital innovation and maritime security.

item.title: Exclusive: Foreign hacker in 2023 compromised Epstein files held by FBI
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - A foreign hacker compromised files relating to the FBI‚Äôs investigation of the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein during a break-in at the bureau‚Äôs New York Field Office three years ago, according to ‚Äča source familiar with the matter and recently published Justice Department documents reviewed by Reuters.
The details of who accessed a server at the FBI‚Äôs New York Field Office, ‚ÄĆincluding the allegation that a foreign hacker was involved, are being reported here for the first time.
In a statement, the FBI said what it described as a "cyber incident" was "an isolated one."
"The FBI restricted access to the malicious actor and rectified the network. The investigation remains ongoing, so we do not have further comments to provide at this time."
Although the source said the intrusion appeared to have been carried out by a cybercriminal rather than a foreign government, ‚Äčthe incident underscores the files' potential intelligence value, one academic said. The legally mandated publication of U.S. Justice Department documents has exposed the dead financier's ties to prominent people in politics, finance, academia and business, ‚Äčtriggering investigations in numerous countries around the world.
‚ÄúWho wouldn‚Äôt be going after the Epstein files if you‚Äôre the Russians or somebody interested in kompromat?‚ÄĚ said ‚Ā†Jon Lindsay, who researches the role of emerging technology in global security at the Georgia Institute of Technology. ‚ÄúIf foreign intelligence agencies are not thinking seriously about the Epstein files as a target, then ‚ÄčI would be shocked.‚ÄĚ
The breach was reported contemporaneously by CNN and Reuters on February 17; the connection to Epstein materials was made by the French magazine Marianne.
Epstein, a longtime associate of President Donald Trump, ‚Äčpleaded guilty in 2008 to prostitution charges, including soliciting an underage girl. He was found hanged in his jail cell in 2019, in what was ruled a suicide, after being arrested again on federal charges of sex trafficking of minors.
The hack occurred after a server at the Child Exploitation Forensic Lab in the FBI‚Äôs New York Field Office was inadvertently left vulnerable by Special Agent Aaron Spivack, who was trying to navigate the bureau‚Äôs complex ‚Äčprocedures for handling digital evidence, according to the source and the documents.
A timeline written by Spivack and included in the large cache of Epstein documents released earlier this year said the break-in happened ‚Äčon February 12, 2023. It was discovered the following day when Spivack turned on his computer and discovered a text file warning him that his network had been compromised, according to that document.
Further investigation turned up traces ‚ÄĆof unusual activity ‚Ā†on the server, the document said, adding that the activity "included combing through certain files pertaining to the Epstein investigation.‚ÄĚ
The timeline does not say which specific files were accessed, whether the hacker downloaded the data, or who the hacker was. Reuters could not establish what, if any, overlap the affected data had with the Epstein documents published earlier this year or the files that remain under wraps.
Spivack, whose name appears elsewhere , opens new tab in the documents , opens new tab in connection with the Epstein investigation, did not return repeated messages seeking comment. Reuters was unable to reach the man identified in the documents as Spivack‚Äôs lawyer, Richard J. Roberson, Jr. Seven FBI ‚Äčagents identified in the documents as being involved ‚Äčin the investigation into the incident did ‚Ā†not return messages.
In his statement to FBI investigators examining whether he was responsible for the breach, Spivack said he was being made "a scapegoat for the intrusion" and that conflicting bureau policies and faulty guidance around information technology were to blame. Reuters could not establish the result ‚Äčof the bureau's internal investigation.
The person familiar with the breach said the intrusion was carried out by a foreign hacker who did not appear ‚Äčto realize they had penetrated ‚Ā†a law enforcement server. The hacker expressed disgust at the presence of child abuse images on the device and left a message threatening to turn its owner over to the FBI, the person said.
The source said bureau officials defused the situation by convincing the hacker that they actually were the FBI, in part by having the hacker join a video chat where they flashed their law enforcement credentials in front ‚Ā†of a web ‚Äčcamera.
Reuters could not determine - and the source said they did not know - who the hacker was, what country they ‚Äčwere operating from, what they did with the material accessed, or whether any effort was made to identify or punish them for breaking into the FBI‚Äôs server.
Many of the Justice Department's documents have been heavily redacted and others have been kept ‚Äčsecret altogether despite a law mandating their full release last year . The Trump administration says it is withholding material that could compromise victims‚Äô identities or jeopardize ongoing investigations.

item.title: As Iran war rages, Trump pivots to cost of living in Kentucky trip
item.description:
HEBRON, Kentucky, March 11 (Reuters) - Twelve days into the Iran war, U.S. President Donald Trump is refocusing on domestic issues, traveling ‚Äčto Kentucky on Wednesday to promote his economic plans as Republicans urge him to address rising concern over the ‚ÄĆcost of living.
The president's campaign trip is his first since the start of the U.S.-Israeli military operation in Iran . It offers Trump a chance to sharpen his economic message ahead of the November midterm elections, when Republicans will be defending narrow majorities in both chambers of the U.S. Congress.
Trump is due to ‚Äčdeliver remarks at two events in northern Kentucky and nearby Cincinnati, Ohio. White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt could not ‚Äčsay why Trump had chosen to go to those places in particular, but the Kentucky district ‚Ā†is also home to Republican U.S. Representative Thomas Massie, a Trump critic who has frequently broken with his party.
Massie led the high-profile ‚Äčpush for more transparency from Trump's Justice Department's handling of files connected to the late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.
Trump has endorsed the ‚ÄčRepublican candidate who will face off against Massie in the party's primary in May.
In his public remarks, Trump is expected to focus on his efforts to lower some prescription drug costs, a key selling point for Republicans trying to address voters' economic concerns.
It is the latest in a series of speeches Trump ‚Äčhas delivered around the country to sell the American public on his economic policies, but they have often been overshadowed by his ‚Äčpolicies abroad, most recently the war in Iran, which has caused gas prices to soar.
Gas prices are up 61 cents on average in both Kentucky ‚ÄĆand nationwide ‚Ā†compared to last month, according to travel organization AAA.
"Short term oil prices, which will drop rapidly when the destruction of the Iran nuclear threat is over, is a very small price to pay," Trump posted earlier this week, "ONLY FOOLS WOULD THINK DIFFERENTLY!"
Bill Kunkel, a 67-year-old retired welder in Union, Kentucky, told Reuters he is concerned about how the Iran war and Trump's tariff program will continue to affect an ‚Äčarea known for logistics and ‚Äčmanufacturing.
"I voted for Trump because ‚Ā†he was going to get us out of all of these wars. That is one of the reasons why I agree with Massie - it can't just be a forever war," Kunkel added, referring to ‚ÄčMassie's criticism of Trump's decision to go to war against Iran.
Massie said in an interview that ‚Äčhe welcomes Trump ‚Ā†to his district as they share a lot of the same supporters.
‚ÄúI‚Äôve taken care to never insult the president, to never call him names. If I have a policy disagreement with him, I articulate it and keep it to that," he said.
Trump has endorsed Ed Gallrein, a ‚Ā†former Navy ‚ÄčSEAL officer and farmer Trump calls "high-quality," to take on Massie, a man Trump ‚Äčcalls "unreliable." Gallrein‚Äôs campaign said he will attend the president‚Äôs event.
"There is growing dissatisfaction with where Massie is, and part of it is not supporting the president," said ‚ÄčLarry Mazzuckelli, a retired federal government employee and political commentator from Union, Kentucky.

item.title: Kremlin says Britain was involved in Ukraine's missile strike on Russia
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 11 (Reuters) - The Kremlin on Wednesday accused British specialists of involvement in a deadly ‚ÄčUkrainian strike on the Russian city of Bryansk that ‚ÄĆused British Storm Shadow missiles and said it would take Britain's role "into account".
The Bryansk regional governor said Tuesday's strike killed at least six civilians and injured ‚Äč37 in what he called a "terrorist missile attack", without saying ‚Äčwhat was hit. Ukraine said it had struck a ‚Ā†key plant producing missile components. Moscow accused it of deliberately targeting ‚Äčcivilians.
Asked by Reuters if there would be a military response to ‚Äčthe use of British missiles against sovereign Russian territory, Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said Russia would take into account the British involvement.
"It is obvious that the launch ‚Äčof these missiles was impossible without British specialists," Peskov told ‚Äčreporters. "We are aware of this, we know it well, and we naturally take it ‚ÄĆinto ‚Ā†account."
"In order to prevent such barbaric actions by the Kyiv regime from continuing, the special military operation is being conducted," he said, adding that one of the goals of the operation was to demilitarise ‚ÄčUkraine.
There was no ‚Äčimmediate comment ‚Ā†from London, though Russia has repeatedly said that Ukraine needs Western expertise and Western-supplied targeting data and ‚Äčsatellite imagery to fire advanced missiles deep into Russia.
Ukrainian ‚ÄčPresident ‚Ā†Volodymyr Zelenskiy said that Kyiv had struck one of the most important military factories in Bryansk that produced electronics for Russian missiles.
Russian Foreign Ministry ‚Ā†spokeswoman ‚ÄčMaria Zakharova said the strike was premeditated ‚Äčand directed against civilians, and challenged the U.N. to assess what had occurred.

item.title: China 'does not agree' with attacks on Gulf countries, says foreign ministry
item.description: BEIJING, March 11 (Reuters) - China "does ‚Äčnot agree" ‚ÄĆwith attacks on ‚ÄčGulf states ‚Äčand condemns all ‚Ā†indiscriminate ‚Äčattacks on ‚Äčcivilians and non-military targets, ‚Äčsaid ‚ÄčGuo Jiakun, spokesperson ‚ÄĆat ‚Ā†the Chinese foreign ministry, at ‚Äča ‚Äčregular ‚Ā†press conference ‚Äčon ‚ÄčWednesday.

item.title: Thailand says 3 crew missing after ship hit by projectiles in Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
BANGKOK, March 11 (Reuters) - Twenty crew have been rescued ‚Äčfrom a Thai ‚ÄĆship hit by unknown projectiles in the ‚ÄčStrait of Hormuz ‚Äčbut three crew remain ‚Ā†missing, Thailand's Transport ‚ÄčMinistry said on Wednesday.
The ‚Äčministry said the crew had abandoned the vessel ‚Äčin a lifeboat ‚Äčand were rescued by the ‚ÄĆOmani ‚Ā†navy. It said an explosion occurred in the stern of ‚Äčthe ‚Äčship ‚Ā†and caused a fire in the ‚Äčengine compartment, ‚Äčwhere ‚Ā†the remaining three crew members had been ‚Ā†working.

item.title: Pope Leo laments death of civilians, children in Iran war
item.description:
VATICAN CITY, March 11 (Reuters) - Pope Leo on Wednesday ‚Äčlamented the death ‚ÄĆof numerous civilians in the ongoing Iran war ‚Äčand also expressed ‚Äčcloseness to people in ‚Ā†Lebanon, saying the ‚Äčcountry, targeted by Israeli ‚Äčstrikes, was going through a "great trial."
Leo, the first ‚ÄčU.S. pope, called ‚Äčon pilgrims in his weekly ‚ÄĆaudience ‚Ā†in St. Peter's Square to pray for peace.
"Let us continue ‚Äčto pray ‚Äčfor ‚Ā†peace in Iran, and throughout ‚Äčthe Middle East, ‚Äčespecially ‚Ā†for the many civilian victims, including many ‚Ā†innocent ‚Äčchildren," said ‚Äčthe pontiff.

item.title: Thailand's Phuket airport closes runway after Air India Express plane malfunction
item.description:
BANGKOK, March 11 (Reuters) - Thailand's Phuket airport said on Wednesday it had temporarily closed its runway to remove a disabled Air India Express plane ‚Äčafter it experienced a malfunction upon landing.
Air India Express Flight AXB938 ‚ÄĆarriving from Hyderabad suffered damage to its landing gear as it hit the runway, Phuket airport said in a statement.
The plane "experienced an issue with the nose wheel at ‚ÄčPhuket Airport," a spokesperson for Air India Express said, adding: "The ‚Äčcrew followed all standard protocols, and guests were deplaned.
There were no ‚Ā†casualties and the runway was expected to reopen at 6 p.m. ‚Äčlocal time (1100 GMT), the airport said.
An image posted on X by Thai state ‚Äčbroadcaster MCOT showed part of the aircraft landing gear, with a wheel missing, in direct contact with the runway, with a long, swerving skid mark on the tarmac.
Air India Express, the ‚Äčbudget arm of Air India, has a fleet of over 100 Boeing ‚Äčand Airbus aircraft.
India's aviation watchdog reprimanded Air India Express in March last year for ‚ÄĆnot ‚Ā†changing the engine parts of an Airbus A320, as directed by the European Union's aviation safety agency, Reuters has reported . It was also criticised for falsifying records to show compliance.
Air India Express reported 95 technical defects from January to November last ‚Äčyear, India's civil ‚Äčaviation ministry said ‚Ā†in December, the second highest among India's airlines over the period. The ministry did not provide details on the ‚Äčnature of the faults.
Parent Air India, a full-service carrier, ‚Äčhas come ‚Ā†under intense scrutiny in the last few months after a series of safety lapses and a deadly crash in in Ahmedabad last June that killed ‚Ā†260 ‚Äčpeople.
Phuket is one of Thailand's busiest gateways, with ‚Äčhundreds of flights daily, including from the Middle East, Europe and Asia, as well as ‚Äčdomestic routes.

item.title: Kremlin says it is in constant touch with Iranian leaders
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 11 (Reuters) - Russia is constantly in touch ‚Äčwith the Iranian leadership and ‚ÄĆwilling to contribute to efforts to stabilise the region, ‚Äčthe Kremlin said on ‚ÄčWednesday.
"Here I can only say ‚Ā†that we are in ‚Äčconstant contact with the Iranian ‚Äčside and with the Iranian leadership," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov ‚Äčtold reporters.
"As President (Vladimir) Putin ‚Äčhas said, Russia is always ready ‚ÄĆto ‚Ā†do what it can to restore peace and stability in the region."
Putin and ‚ÄčForeign Minister ‚ÄčSergei ‚Ā†Lavrov have spoken with their Iranian counterparts ‚Äčin recent days, ‚Äčwhile ‚Ā†Putin also held a phone call with U.S. President ‚Ā†Donald ‚ÄčTrump on Monday.

item.title: Hungary's opposition Tisza party maintains lead as election nears, poll shows
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 11 (Reuters) - The main Hungarian opposition party Tisza's lead over long-time Prime Minister Viktor Orban's Fidesz shrank slightly in a poll published on ‚ÄčWednesday, as campaigning enters its final month before an April ‚ÄĆ12 election.
Nationalist Orban faces the biggest challenge to his rule in 16 years, although the outcome of the vote - coming against the backdrop of the Ukraine-Russia war ‚Äčand building economic challenges - remains highly uncertain, with opinion polls showing ‚Äčthat many voters are still undecided.
A poll by 21 Research ‚Ā†Centre (21 Kutatokozpont), conducted between March 2 and 6 and released on ‚ÄčWednesday, showed Tisza leads by 14 percentage points ahead of Fidesz among ‚Äčdecided voters, down from a 16-point lead in the agency's last poll in January.
Centre-right Tisza, led by former government insider Peter Magyar , had the support of 53% of decided ‚Äčvoters, unchanged from January, according to 21 Research Centre, while 39% backed ‚ÄčFidesz, up from 37% in the previous poll.
The poll, published by news site 24.hu, ‚ÄĆshowed ‚Ā†38% support for Tisza among all voters, with Fidesz backed by 30%.
Based on the poll, Tisza could win 115 seats in Hungary's 199-seat parliament, and Fidesz could expect 78 seats. The far-right Our Homeland (Mi Hazank) party ‚Äčwould be the ‚Äčonly other party ‚Ā†crossing the 5% threshold to win seats.
Magyar has said his party would curb corruption, unlock billions of euros ‚Äčin frozen European Union funds to boost the economy, ‚Äčand firmly ‚Ā†anchor Hungary in the EU and NATO.
Fidesz has pointed to other surveys that still show it on course to victory, though opponents - who criticise Orban ‚Ā†for ‚Äčharming the rule of law and keeping ‚Äčwarm ties with Russia - say these have mainly been conducted by institutes with financial or personal ‚Äčties to the ruling party.

item.title: Sweden's Stegra seeks to raise over $2.3 billion, daily DI reports
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 11 (Reuters) - Swedish startup Stegra must raise more than 2 billion euros ($2.3 billion) to ‚Äčcomplete construction of its green steel plant, ‚ÄĆmore than double its previous estimate, business daily Dagens Industri reported on Wednesday, citing unnamed sources.
The ‚Äčcompany has appointed a new CFO and ‚Äčshaken up its board , as it scrambles to ‚Ā†raise more cash to finish its hydrogen-based ‚Äčsteel plant in northern Sweden.
($1 = 0.8602 euros)

item.title: US ignites Iran war, but Gulf Arab states pay the price, Gulf sources say
item.description:
DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - The U.S. may have pulled the trigger on the Iran war , but it is the oil-producing Gulf that will pay the price, Gulf sources and analysts say, signalling unease in ties between a region under Iranian attack and the superpower it relies on for protection.
Behind the scenes, resentment is mounting ‚Äčin Gulf Arab capitals at being drawn into a war they neither initiated nor endorsed but are now paying for economically and militarily, with airports, hotels, ports and military and oil installations hit by Iranian strikes, said three ‚ÄĆregional sources, who declined to be identified as they were not authorised to speak publicly.
"It is not our war. We did not want this conflict, yet we are paying the price in our security and our economy," Ebtesam Al-Ketbi, President of the Emirates Policy Center, told Reuters.
That doesn't mean Iran is "innocent", she said. Gulf governments had assured Tehran they would not allow their territories or airspace to be used by Washington in the war. Yet Iran has unleashed waves of drone and missile strikes across the region despite those assurances, denting business confidence in the process.
While ‚Äčdisquiet about U.S. President Donald Trump is growing over a conflict many believe he launched without consultation, some regional sources argue that having started the war, Washington should now see it through to eliminate what they see as a persistent ‚ÄčIranian threat on their doorstep.
‚ÄúIf America leaves the war now without achieving victory, it will be like abandoning an injured lion,‚ÄĚ Ketbi said. ‚ÄúIran will remain a threat to the region, capable of ‚Ā†striking again. And if the regime collapses, leaving a power vacuum, neighbouring states will suffer the consequences.‚ÄĚ
Asked for comment, the White House said U.S. and Israeli strikes had reduced Iran‚Äôs retaliatory missile attacks by 90 percent, "crushing their ability to shoot these weapons or ‚Äčproduce more".
White House spokeswoman Anna Kelly added that Trump was in close contact with Middle East partners and that Iran‚Äôs attacks on its neighbors underscore why the threat had to be eliminated.
There was no immediate response from Gulf states to requests for comment.
Iran's Supreme ‚ÄčLeader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, was killed on the first day of the war. Tehran responded by hitting Israel and Gulf states hosting U.S. military installations, effectively halting oil and gas shipments through the Strait of Hormuz -- a conduit for roughly a fifth of the world‚Äôs petroleum and LNG.
Across the region, airspace closures have resulted in airlines cancelling some 40,000 flights, the largest disruption to global air travel since the COVID-19 pandemic. Gulf tourism meanwhile is also taking a hit , putting at risk the region's carefully crafted image as a safe and high-end vacation hotspot.
Amid the turmoil, Gulf states have ‚Äčsought to project calm and resolve. The president of the United Arab Emirates, Sheikh Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan, said on Friday his nation was in a time of war but was well and told his enemies it was no easy prey , in his ‚Äčfirst public comments since Iran fired missiles at the UAE.
At the same time, analysts say the war has left Gulf states reassessing both their security dependence on Washington and the prospect of eventually engaging Tehran on new regional security arrangements -- even as trust in Iran has collapsed.
For decades, ‚ÄĆrelations between Washington ‚Ā†and the Gulf states rested on an implicit trade-off: Gulf energy and capital -- including hundreds of billions of dollars spent on U.S. arms, advanced technology and goods and services -- in return for U.S. protection, said Fawaz Gerges of the London School of Economics.
Gerges said the war had shaken those assumptions. Now, he said, Gulf states would accelerate efforts to diversify their foreign and security partnerships, realising "they cannot really rely on the United States to protect their energy, oil, gas, their people and their sovereignty.‚ÄĚ
The Gulf's latest misgivings about Washington echo disquiet felt in Saudi Arabia at the U.S. failure to retaliate against Iran after drone and missile attack in 2019 on the kingdom's Abqaiq and Khurais oil facilities. Washington and Riyadh blamed the attacks on Tehran, which denied responsibility.
Abdulaziz Sager, Chairman of the Saudi-based ‚ÄčGulf Research Center, said Washington had failed to prepare safeguards ‚Äčfor its regional allies or ensure oil and gas flows ‚Ā†during wartime, saying the cost on the Gulf states' economies is "horrendous".
Sager said the war had demonstrated the limits of relying solely on external security guarantees, particularly from the United States, adding that Gulf countries need to strengthen their own defense capabilities and prepare for future crises.
"External powers also tend to make decisions based on their own strategic interests rather than ‚Äčthose of the Gulf. As a result, Gulf states will likely pursue a more cautious and balanced approach in their relations with both Iran and major international partners," added Sager.
In a ‚Äčrare public rebuke, prominent Emirati businessman ‚Ā†Khalaf Al Habtoor questioned the objectives of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
‚ÄúIf the strikes were aimed at containing Iran, did they take into account the regional consequences -- or was the cost of dragging the Gulf into a conflict it was not party to simply ignored?‚ÄĚ said Al Habtoor, founder of the Al Habtoor Group.
Saudi Aramco CEO Amin Nasser warned on Tuesday that continued disruption to shipping through Hormuz would have ‚Äúcatastrophic consequences‚ÄĚ for oil markets .
Iran‚Äôs Revolutionary Guards, however, said they would not allow ‚Äúone litre of oil‚ÄĚ to be shipped from the Middle East if U.S. ‚Ā†and Israeli attacks ‚Äčcontinued. Trump has warned Washington would strike Iran harder if it blocked oil exports.
Sources close to Gulf Arab government circles say frustration with Trump privately runs ‚Äčdeep across the region. Many believe he dragged the Gulf into a war shaped heavily by Israel, without sharing a plan and acting hastily and without fully weighing the political and economic fallout for allies.
One Gulf source familiar with U.S. policymaking said key decisions were crafted by a small inner circle around Trump operating ‚Äčlargely outside traditional U.S. policy channels.
‚ÄúThey are businessmen and dealmakers, not career policymakers,‚ÄĚ the source said, adding this approach left Gulf partners exposed to the consequences of this circle's decisions.

item.title: Meloni bets on a lottery to curb Italy's judicial clans
item.description:
ROME, March 11 (Reuters) - Italy has tried random selection before.
Ancient Romans used to cast lots to decide who governed in distant provinces while Renaissance Florence pulled names from bags to avoid powerful families gaining a stranglehold on power.
Now Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni is looking to revive the centuries-old ‚Äčtradition of "sortition" as a way to stop what she says are political factions lording it over Italy's long-troubled judiciary.
The plan to select magistrate members of judicial governing councils by lottery ‚ÄĆlies at the heart of a reform being put to referendum on March 22-23, pitting Meloni's conservative government against most of the nation's judges and prosecutors.
Recent opinion polls show the "no" camp, backed by the main opposition centre-left parties, has nudged ahead, threatening one of Meloni's signature initiatives which was championed by her predecessor Silvio Berlusconi before his death in 2023.
