Berlusconi, who faced dozens of trials largely tied to his media empire after entering politics, accused the judiciary of being in the ‚Äčhands of the left. Magistrates denied this, but Meloni vowed to take up his crusade after she won power in 2022.
"I think change is needed because justice is one of the ‚Äčthree fundamental powers that keep Italy afloat, but it's also the one that we haven't managed to substantially reform since the 1980s," Meloni told RTL ‚Ā†radio last week.
"If we don't succeed this time, we won't have another chance," she added, saying future governments would be unlikely to take on this sort of constitutional reform that almost always requires ‚Äča referendum that is hard to win in a divided country.
The referendum campaign is unfolding amid growing distrust between the judiciary and the ruling coalition, with "no" supporters arguing that Meloni's plan would undermine the justice ‚Äčsystem and boost political interference.
They seized on remarks by Giusi Bartolozzi, the justice ministry's chief of staff, who urged voters in a TV interview this week to back the reform, saying it would allow Italians to "get rid of a judiciary that acts like a firing squad."
The government's reform has three main pillars.
First, it would separate the career paths of judges and public prosecutors, who currently enter through the same exam, can swap ‚Äčjob roles and are governed by a single council (the CSM), which oversees appointments and promotions.
Second, it would split the CSM into two councils - one for judges and one for prosecutors. Third, it would create ‚Äča new disciplinary court to handle misconduct cases.
Under the proposal for the new-look CSMs, two thirds of seats would be reserved for working judges or prosecutors, and one third for lay members, such as law professors or ‚ÄĆlawyers, whose ‚Ā†names would be put forward by parliament.
All CSM members will ultimately be drawn by lot.
Meloni says this will prevent the sort of wheeler-dealing between various internal factions that recent scandals have revealed, and create a system in which council members do not have to "say thank you to anyone" after an election.
Critics counter that in institutions designed to safeguard independence, legitimacy comes from having leaders chosen on purpose, not picked at random.
"It is one thing to punish individuals who make mistakes; it is another to humiliate the entire judiciary by saying that you are not capable of electing your own representatives," said David Ermini, a ‚Äčformer vice president of the CSM and an ‚Äčex-lawmaker with the centre-left Democratic Party.
No one looms over this debate quite like Luca Palamara, a former CSM member whose spectacular downfall spurred the reform.
Wiretaps in 2019 exposed late-night meetings involving Palamara, senior judicial figures and politicians, with discussions focused on steering appointments ‚ÄĒ especially to coveted and sensitive prosecutor posts.
Careers were clearly being fixed by cliques ‚Äčand Palamara was expelled from the magistracy.
Now, in a twist that infuriates many of his former colleagues, Palamara is campaigning for a "Yes" vote.
"The introduction ‚Äčof random selection would dismantle ‚Ā†the possibility of pre-selecting who sits on the CSM, thereby eliminating factional ties," he said, calling the reform "the one most feared" by those within the factions.
Some opponents say the proposed new selection rules risk promoting people who are ill-prepared or under-qualified, sweeping away any notion of merit and leaving everything up to chance. But Palamara said these concerns were overblown.
"We are talking about people who have passed a highly competitive public examination ‚Ā†to enter the ‚Äčprofession and who make extremely serious decisions every day - life sentences, delicate family situations," he said.
But even if the reform ‚Äčpasses the referendum hurdle, history suggests that sortition may not achieve the desired result.
In both ancient Rome and Renaissance Florence, lotteries promised fair rotation in theory, but in practice elites learned to game the system, steering outcomes so that the biggest ‚Äčprizes still tended to land in familiar hands.
"The mechanism of factionism would not end with drawing lots. Everyone selected might all come from the same faction," said Ermini.

item.title: Ukrainian trainers will help German army get ready to defend against Russia by 2029, chief says
item.description:
BERLIN, March 11 (Reuters) - Ukrainian military trainers will help ‚ÄčGermany's military meet a target of being ready to defend against any Russian attack on ‚ÄĆNATO by 2029, the German army chief said, in a role reversal after years of Western troops training Ukrainian forces.
Berlin and Kyiv agreed last month that Ukraine would send military instructors to German army schools to teach lessons they ‚Äčhave learned from fighting against Russia's invasion.
"We have high expectations," Lieutenant General Christian Freuding, the head ‚Äčof the German army, told Reuters in an interview. "The Ukrainian military is currently ‚Ā†the only one in the world with frontline experience against Russia."
Germany is not the only Western country ‚Äčdrawing on Kyiv's experience: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy on Thursday pledged to provide assistance to the U.S. in ‚Äčdealing with Iranian drones in the Middle East.
Freuding said Germany was the first country to reach an arrangement with Ukraine to provide military instructors but predicted others would follow suit.
Asked what the Ukrainian trainers would contribute, he referred to assessments ‚Äčby German and other Western intelligence agencies that Russia could be ready for a large-scale attack on ‚Äčthe NATO military alliance by 2029.
"That's almost the day after tomorrow. We have no time - the enemy doesn't wait for ‚ÄĆus ‚Ā†to declare we're ready. So we have to use every possibility to prepare," Freuding said.
Russia has insisted it does not intend to attack NATO, but Western officials say such assurances cannot be taken at face value as Moscow made similar statements before invading Ukraine.
Freuding, who became army chief ‚Äčlast October after previously coordinating ‚ÄčGermany's arms supplies to ‚Ā†Kyiv , said the initial contingent of Ukrainian trainers would likely number in the "middle double-digits" and stay for a few weeks at a time.
The Ukrainian instructors are ‚Äčexpected to contribute expertise in artillery, engineering, armoured operations, drone usage and command ‚Äčand control.
Since ‚Ā†Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, Germany has trained Ukrainian forces on weapons such as Marder infantry fighting vehicles, Leopard tanks, howitzers and air defence systems.
Freuding said Ukraine had built on these skills in combat, developing ‚Ā†tactics ‚Äčand capabilities in data-centric warfare - the military term for highly ‚Äčdigitalised and interconnected operations - that cannot be found in textbooks.
"The fact that they are now coming to us as instructors reflects a ‚Äčsecurity partnership on an equal footing," Freuding said.

item.title: Italy‚Äôs Meloni criticises US war on Iran as part of dangerous trend
item.description:
ROME, March 11 (Reuters) - Italy's Prime Minister ‚ÄĆGiorgia Meloni on Wednesday delivered her strongest criticism yet of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, describing it as part of a growing and dangerous trend of interventions "outside the scope of international law".
Her ‚Äčremarks to parliament came after repeated accusations from the opposition that her ‚Äčright-wing government had been too soft toward its allies. Most other ‚Ā†European nations, with the notable exception of Spain , have withheld direct critique of the U.S. ‚Äčand Israeli attacks, largely calling for restraint.
Meloni - who has close ties with U.S. President ‚ÄčDonald Trump - also said Iran must not be allowed to develop nuclear weapons, as that would end the international non-proliferation framework with "dramatic repercussions for global security", leaving Italy and Europe exposed to a ‚Äčpotential nuclear threat from Tehran.
The U.S. and Israel traded air strikes with Iran ‚Äčacross the Middle East as the war stretched into its 12th day, a conflict that has halted ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†flow of one-fifth of the world's oil and gas supplies.
Addressing parliament on the crisis, Meloni drew parallels between the Middle East conflict and Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, which she said triggered a broader global destabilisation.
"It is in this context of ‚Äčstructural crisis in the ‚Äčinternational system, in ‚Ā†which threats are becoming increasingly frightening and unilateral interventions outside the scope of international law are multiplying, that we must also ‚Äčplace the American and Israeli intervention against the Iranian regime," she ‚Äčtold the ‚Ā†Senate.
Meloni said Rome was providing air-defense assets to Gulf countries hit by strikes from Tehran.
"This is not only because these are friendly nations and strategic partners of Italy, but also ‚Ā†because ‚Äčthere are tens of thousands of Italian citizens in ‚Äčthat area whom we must protect, not to mention the fact that there are about 2,000 Italian ‚Äčsoldiers stationed in the Gulf."

item.title: US navy aircraft transits Taiwan Strait ahead of Trump's planned China visit
item.description:
TAIPEI, March 11 (Reuters) - A U.S. Navy P-8A Poseidon aircraft transited the Taiwan Strait in international ‚Äčairspace on Wednesday, in a move the ‚ÄĆU.S. 7th Fleet said "demonstrates the United States' commitment to a free and open Indo-Pacific."
The transit comes ahead of ‚ÄčU.S. President Donald Trump's planned visit to China ‚Äčthis month as Washington and Beijing look ‚Ā†to stabilise ties marred by issues including Taiwan, ‚Äčtrade disputes and technology curbs.
"By operating within the ‚ÄčTaiwan Strait in accordance with international law, the United States upholds the navigational rights and freedoms of all nations," the ‚Äč7th Fleet said in a statement.
U.S. military ‚Äčships or aircraft routinely pass through or above the waterway ‚ÄĆthat ‚Ā†separates democratically governed Taiwan from China - missions that always anger Beijing.
China claims democratically governed Taiwan as its own territory and says it has jurisdiction over ‚Äčthe strait. ‚ÄčTaiwan and ‚Ā†the U.S. dispute that, saying the strait is an international waterway.
China has repeatedly ‚Äčsaid the Taiwan issue is its ‚Äčinternal affair ‚Ā†and at the core of Beijing's interests - a red line that must not be crossed or ‚Ā†trampled ‚Äčon.
Taiwan's government rejects China's sovereignty claims, ‚Äčsaying that only Taiwan's people can decide their future.

item.title: A 'corporate welfare' claim vs Walmart and Amazon over worker benefits
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - (The opinions expressed here are those of the author, a correspondent for Reuters. This column is part of the Reuters Sustainable Finance Newsletter, which you can sign up for here )
Providing low-paid workers with government benefits like health insurance and food assistance is usually considered to be socially minded policy. But another way to look at the payments is as subsidies giving big businesses access to a large pool of labor able to work for less-than-living wages.
That's the critique of Sarah Anderson, Global Economy ‚ÄčProject Director at the Institute for Policy Studies, a progressive-leaning think tank in Washington, D.C. In a new report , opens new tab she reviewed pay practices of companies she dubbed "The Low-Wage 20," or the 20 largest employers of low-wage U.S. ‚ÄĆworkers.
Among her top findings: more than half of these firms reported 2024 median pay that would make a family of three eligible for food benefits under the federal Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, also known as SNAP, and for government-subsidized Medicaid health insurance benefits in most states. All the while, most of their CEOs earned hundreds of times as much per year.
The following transcript of our conversation has been edited for length and clarity. At the end I've published responses from the two top U.S. employers, Walmart and Amazon. (AMZN.O) , opens new tab
Question: Sarah, could you talk about how you were able to develop the calculations?
Answer: I have worked on executive compensation issues for a long, ‚Äčlong time. And this is a report that really connects those high CEO pay levels to low-wage worker issues. These are all companies that are in the S&P 500 and they're the ones that have the lowest median pay levels ‚Äčthat they've reported to the SEC (U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission) and that also have a majority of their workers in the United States.
Q: You found a lot of these employees were eligible for things ‚Ā†like Medicaid and SNAP food assistance, which are social benefits to lower-wage Americans.
A: We think that median pay number that the companies report themselves is a really good indicator of how many of their workers might be on Medicaid and SNAP. We don't have ‚Äčthe exact figures because they're not required to report that, but there were a handful of state governments that have revealed specific data company by company, and Walmart and Amazon were at the top of all of those lists with really clear numbers in terms ‚Äčof how many of their employees are.
These benefits are at risk right now. So it's a good time to be having this conversation because these workers who are already having to stretch to pay their bills are going to be facing even more hardship.
Q: If I were an executive for one of these companies, I'm sure I would respond by saying, well, a lot of these are part-time workers who don't want to be working full time. And, the calculations for public assistance are based on household size. Maybe it's not realistic to expect a head of household, a three-person household, to survive ‚Äčon just one income in this day and age.
A: I would say that having a business model that is heavily reliant on part-time workers is a choice. It's a way to get around having to pay full benefits at a lot of these companies under ‚Äčthe Affordable Care Act. Large companies have to provide affordable health insurance, or at least offer it to people working at least 30 hours a week. So if you can keep your workers, you know, at a lower hourly level, then you can get around that. I (also) question ‚ÄĆwhether people are ‚Ā†working part time because they want to work part time. A lot of people are having to work several part-time jobs to try to make ends meet.
You know, a huge share of the low-wage workforce are single parents. And for these companies to just abdicate their obligation to provide a living wage says a lot about their priorities.
Question: Surely mom-and-pop businesses face the same challenge. A lot of them can't pay their workers enough to get them above the thresholds for public assistance as well. What's the difference?
A: These should be the leading among the leading corporations of America. They're in the S&P 500. They set the standards for wages and other things. The other thing that just makes it so outrageous to so many people is to see the CEO pay levels at these companies. There's ‚Äčno owner of a small mom and pop that is making ‚Äčthe $18 million a year, which is the average (for CEOs) ‚Ā†in our low-wage 20. It's a business model that is based on really extracting from the people often who are the frontline workers.
Q: Something that jumped out at me, among the large employers in Nevada (a state cited in the study) is that a couple of public sector organizations, a school district and a county that have a lot of their employees eligible for public assistance.
A: That's true. I have to ‚Äčsay I was dismayed to see the U.S. Postal Service was on some of these lists as well. It just gets to my broader view on this that having these extreme ‚Äčgaps between CEO pay and worker ‚Ā†pay, where people don't feel like they're part of a team, it just doesn't bring out the best in people and that does have real costs in terms of lower employee morale and higher turnover rates.
Here is what a Walmart spokesman said about the study - "We offer a ladder of opportunity so people can build a career at Walmart, which includes a small percentage of our workforce that comes to us on public assistance. We hire them, train them and give them the chance to build a better life."
Here is what Amazon spokesperson Eileen Hards told me: ‚ÄúAmazon pay ‚Ā†is among the ‚Äčbest in the industry ‚Äď well over double the federal minimum wage and significantly more than other retailers. Pointing fingers at Amazon over SNAP and/or Medicaid is a ‚Äčred herring when eligibility is based on total household income and size ‚Äď and not individual wages."
"For example, two employees who work at the same site and earn identical pay in the same state can have completely different SNAP or Medicaid eligibility depending on whether they support children, elderly parents, or are the sole earner in ‚Äčtheir household. As we‚Äôve said for years, what really needs to happen is a significant and large increase in the federal minimum wage‚ÄĒthat would be a big boost for American families.‚ÄĚ

item.title: Morning Bid: From panic to patience
item.description:
March 11 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
Both energy and stock markets appear to be in a holding pattern after the wild swings earlier this week as they look for any evidence that oil and gas supplies might ‚Äčbe freed up in the Middle East.
For now, there are few signs on the ground to back up President Trump‚Äôs view that the war will ‚ÄĆbe over very soon, with Tuesday seeing intense bombardments , shipping still blocked in the Strait of Hormuz, and Saudi Aramco‚Äôs CEO warning of ‚Äúcatastrophic consequences‚ÄĚ for the oil market if conflict drags on.
I‚Äôll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why an Iran oil shock could prove politically toxic in the U.S. even if GDP escapes largely unscathed.
And listen to today's episode of the Morning Bid podcast , where I discuss ‚Äčoil-shock inflation fears and look ahead to today's big macro release - inflation data for the leadup to the crisis.
Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news ‚Äčin markets and finance seven days a week.
Oil prices initially dropped below $90 per barrel on Wednesday on reports that the ‚Ā†International Energy Agency had proposed the largest release of oil reserves in its history - more than double the size of the 182 million barrels released after the Ukraine invasion in 2022.
Oil then staged ‚Äča rebound , however, as traders digested the proposal. Goldman Sachs estimated a release the size of that in 2022 would offset 12 days of an estimated 15.4 million barrel per day ‚Äčexport disruption in the Gulf.
As prices seesaw, though, it will be hoped that Monday's panic surge in crude markets to nearly $120 per barrel has set the parameters for prices - at least until the duration of the Iran conflict becomes clearer.
Wall Street was mixed on Tuesday as the intense bombardments in the Middle East - and further Iranian threats of a comprehensive oil blockade - dampened earlier hopes for a short-lived conflict. All major ‚Äčindexes closed largely flat.
Global shares steadied somewhat on Wednesday as Japan‚Äôs Nikkei gained 1.7% and South Korea‚Äôs KOSPI rose by 1.75%. European stocks opened lower, however, while U.S. stock futures held ‚Äčsteady ahead of the bell.
Meantime, the dollar rebounded after slipping early on Wednesday, holding onto some of the early-week gains it recently shed as traders looked for signs of a quick conclusion to the war.
Later ‚ÄĆtoday, traders ‚Ā†will get a read on where U.S. CPI was in February, just before the Iran attacks started late in the month. More important, though, will be the release on Friday of February PCE data, the Fed‚Äôs favoured measure of inflation. Core inflation measured by the latter was likely already running above 3% before the oil spike.
Elsewhere, Oracle‚Äôs shares surged 8% in extended trading after its latest results, which saw it predict boosted revenue estimates well into 2027 on the AI data center boom, helping to reassure investors about the massive scale of its capex. ‚ÄčThe results also serve as a reminder of ‚Äčthe enduring AI theme in equities ‚Ā†beyond the day-to-day energy market ructions.
Ongoing angst about private credit funds and the quality of the loans contained within them is also rising, however. JPMorgan Chase has marked down the value of certain loans held by private credit groups and is tightening its lending to the sector, ‚Äčthe FT reported on Wednesday
Chart of the day
U.S. consumer prices likely picked up in February as the cost of gasoline increased in ‚Äčanticipation of an escalating ‚Ā†war in the Middle East, and with the conflict driving up oil prices, a further rise in inflation is expected in March.
The anticipated increase in the Consumer Price Index last month would also reflect the continued but staggered pass-through from President Donald Trump's sweeping tariffs, which he pursued under a law meant for use in national emergencies, that have since been struck down by the ‚Ā†U.S. Supreme ‚ÄčCourt.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. February CPI (8:30 AM EDT)
* U.S. 10-year note auction
* Fed's Michelle Bowman speaks
* OPEC ‚ÄčMonthly Oil Market Report
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of ‚Äčthe author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Exclusive: Shell, TotalEnergies and others declare FM to their clients who take LNG from Qatar - sources say
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 - Several companies that buy liquefied natural ‚ÄĆgas (LNG) from QatarEnergy as portfolio players or offtakers ‚ÄĒ including Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab , TotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab and some firms in Asia ‚ÄĒ have declared force majeure ‚Äčto customers they supply, three sources told Reuters ‚Äčon Wednesday.
Qatar, the world's second-largest LNG exporter, announced ‚Ā†a production halt at its 77 million tons ‚Äčper annum (mtpa) facility last week and declared force majeure ‚Äčon LNG shipments.
Shell , the world's largest LNG trader, declined to comment. TotalEnergies did not immediately respond to Reuters request for comment.
Both ‚Äčcompanies have long-term partnerships with QatarEnergy and are partners ‚Äčin the company's massive North Field expansion project which aims to ‚ÄĆboost ‚Ā†capacity by 2027.
Analysts estimate Shell takes 6.8 million tons per annum (mtpa) of Qatari LNG, while TotalEnergies takes 5.2 mtpa and sells them to their clients worldwide.
Qatari ‚ÄčEnergy Minister Saad ‚Äčal-Kaabi told ‚Ā†the Financial Times last week that it would take "weeks to months" to return to ‚Äčnormal deliveries, even if the war ended ‚Äčtoday. ‚Ā†The company declared force majeure on LNG shipments on Wednesday.
Sources told Reuters last week that the FM notices ‚Ā†sent ‚Äčto clients stated that LNG deliveries ‚Äčfor March will not be affected, with the impact being felt as ‚Äčof April.

item.title: Return to Russian fuels would be a strategic blunder, von der Leyen says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 11 (Reuters) - Europe would be making a strategic ‚Äčblunder if it sought to ‚ÄĆblunt soaring energy prices with a return to Russian ‚Äčfossil fuels, European ‚ÄčCommission president Ursula von der ‚Ā†Leyen said on Wednesday.
"In ‚Äčthe current crisis, some argue ‚Äčthat we should abandon our long-term strategy and even go ‚Äčback to Russian fossil ‚Äčfuels. This would be a strategic ‚ÄĆblunder," ‚Ā†von der Leyen said in a speech in European Parliament.
Von der Leyen ‚Äčsaid the ‚ÄčEU ‚Ā†was preparing options to lower energy prices, ‚Äčincluding the better ‚Äčuse ‚Ā†of power purchasing agreements, state aid measures and ‚Ā†gas ‚Äčprice subsidies or ‚Äčcaps.

item.title: Israel sees no certainty Iran‚Äôs government will fall despite war
item.description:
JERUSALEM/DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - Israeli officials in closed discussions have acknowledged there is no certainty the war against Iran will lead to a collapse of its clerical government, a senior Israeli official told Reuters, with no sign ‚Äčof an Iranian uprising amid the bombardment.
However, despite commentary by U.S. President Donald Trump that the war may finish soon , Israel's assessment is that Washington is not close to instructing an end ‚ÄĆto the conflict , two Israeli officials said.
The intense U.S. and Israeli bombing campaign killed the supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, as well as a host of senior military commanders, but it has also killed civilians and smashed homes and public buildings, angering many Iranians.
With missiles striking across Tehran and other cities, and with the Iranian authorities threatening deadly force against any who dares protest, Iranians who might otherwise come to the streets may also fear to do so until the war ends.
Still, Iran's long-term challenges ‚Äčlook worse than ever, with ever harsher sanctions strangling the economy and little prospect of better times ahead for a population whose protests in January were crushed with thousands dead.
The senior Israeli official did not say what had led the country to assess that a collapse of Iran's ruling system was not ‚Ā†a certainty.
On the day Israel launched its joint air war with the U.S., its Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said: "Our joint action will create the conditions for the brave Iranian people to take their destiny ‚Äčinto their own hands."
He particularly spoke of Iran's main ethnic and linguistic minorities, the Kurds , Baluchis and Arabs, adding to reports that the U.S. or Israel may back uprisings by those groups.
However, in a statement on Tuesday, ‚ÄčNetanyahu repeated that though Israel's aspiration was to help Iranians "cast off the yoke of tyranny," that ultimately "it is up to them" - an apparent acknowledgement that a rising does not look imminent.
Israel and the U.S. have not issued a joint public statement setting out clear unified war aims, or articulating the conditions under which they might decide to end the campaign.
On Monday, Trump described the war as "very complete, pretty much", but on Tuesday the White House said it would only end when Trump determined its ‚Äčobjectives were met and that Iran was in a state of unconditional surrender.
In a closed‚ÄĎdoor briefing with foreign diplomats on Tuesday, Israeli Foreign Minister Gideon Saar declined to set a timeframe for the military campaign - ‚Äčchiming with the government's assessment that Trump was not close to ending it.
Saar acknowledged in the briefing that Iran‚Äôs government could survive the war but expressed confidence it would collapse later, sources said.
Speaking to reporters on Tuesday, Saar said ‚ÄĆthe war ‚Ā†would proceed until Israel and the U.S. determined the time had come to end hostilities, but that Israel did not seek "endless war".
Assaf Orion, a former head of strategy with the Israeli military and a fellow at the Washington Institute for Near East Policy, said weakening Iranian military capabilities appeared a more direct, tangible and measurable war aim.
"Creating conditions for regime change is indirect and hence more difficult to fathom," he said, adding that while the military campaign appeared to be planned for weeks, any uprising against Iran's ruling system might take months or years.
On Wednesday Iran's police chief Ahmadreza Radan warned ‚Äčthat "anyone taking to the streets at the enemy's ‚Äčrequest will be confronted as an enemy, not ‚Ā†a protester. All our security forces have their fingers on the trigger".
While many Iranians want change and some openly celebrated the death of Khamenei, whose security forces killed thousands of anti-government protesters just weeks ago, there has been no sign of protest since the war began.
"I hate this regime. I want it to ‚Äčgo, but under bombardment there are no streets left to protest in," said Ali, 26, from Tehran.
Although Tehran's streets are far quieter than usual, banks, petrol ‚Äčstations and shops are all ‚Ā†operating as usual, though with reduced hours and tighter rationing of fuel. All government offices are open.
"Every day and every night there is bombing. My apartment building and its windows shake," said a Tehran resident who asked to retain anonymity for fear of reprisals.
"Despite discontent with the regime, the sense of Iranian pride and patriotism is growing and people are expressing hatred for Reza Pahlavi, Trump and Netanyahu," the resident added, naming the son of ‚Ā†Iran's former shah, ‚Äčwho had urged military intervention.
However, the bombing has destroyed airports and ports as well as other civilian infrastructure, adding to the ‚Äčproblems Iran's government may face in reviving a stagnant economy that has angered much of the population.
"Iran prefers the war to continue because if it ends, protests will start due to economic problems and shortcomings that the war has also worsened," said a university ‚Äčteacher in Tehran who also requested anonymity.

item.title: Spain permanently withdraws ambassador as rift with Israel deepens
item.description:
MADRID, March 11 (Reuters) - Spain permanently withdrew its ambassador to Israel on Tuesday as a ‚Äčdiplomatic standoff worsened between the two countries ‚ÄĆover Spain's opposition to the U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran .
The ambassador was summoned back to Spain last September amid a diplomatic ‚Äčrow over Spanish measures banning aircraft and ships ‚Äčcarrying weapons to Israel from its ports ‚Ā†or airspace due to Israel's military offensive in ‚ÄčGaza, which Israeli Foreign Minister Gideon Sa'ar denounced ‚Äčas antisemitic.
On Tuesday, Spain published an announcement in its official gazette that the ambassador's position had been terminated. Spain's Foreign ‚ÄčMinistry said its embassy in Tel Aviv ‚Äčwill be led by a charge d'affaires for the foreseeable ‚ÄĆfuture.
The ‚Ā†move marks the latest escalation in diplomatic relations between the two countries, which have been heavily strained since Israel launched its assault on the Gaza ‚ÄčStrip in ‚ÄčOctober of ‚Ā†2023.
Israel's embassy in Spain is also run by a charge d'affaires after the ‚Äčcountry summoned its ambassador last May ‚Äčin protest ‚Ā†at Spain's decision to recognise a Palestinian state.
Tensions have heightened since the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran, ‚Ā†with ‚ÄčSa'ar accusing Spain in early March ‚Äčof "standing with tyrants" for opposing the war.

item.title: Iran's new supreme leader 'lightly injured' but still active, Iranian official tells Reuters
item.description:
DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - Newly-appointed Supreme ‚ÄčLeader Mojtaba Khamenei ‚ÄĆwas lightly injured but ‚Äčis continuing ‚Äčto operate, an Iranian ‚Ā†official ‚Äčtold Reuters on ‚ÄčWednesday.
The official did not give details ‚Äčabout ‚Äčwhen he was injured ‚ÄĆor ‚Ā†why he had not made any ‚Äčstatement ‚Äčto ‚Ā†the public since ‚Äčbeing appointed ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†new leader.

item.title: Drones hit east Congo's biggest city, killing French aid worker, rebels and UN say
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - Drone strikes hit Goma in east Congo ‚Äčon Wednesday, killing at least three people including a French aid worker, the ‚ÄĆfirst such attack in the city since AFC/M23 rebels seized it last year, according to the group and the U.N.
The AFC/M23 blamed the attack on the Congolese army, saying in a post on X that Kinshasa ‚Äčhad launched drones against a densely populated urban area in the lakeside city.
A spokesperson ‚Äčfor the rebels said in a separate post that three people had died, ‚Ā†including a foreign humanitarian worker.
A senior official for U.N. children's agency UNICEF told Reuters ‚Äčthat one of its employees, a French national, was killed.
French President Emmanuel Macron and Hadja Lahbib, ‚ÄčEuropean Commissioner for equality, preparedness and crisis management, condemned the attack in posts on X, calling for respect for international humanitarian law and saying aid workers must never be targeted.
A spokesperson for Congo's army declined to ‚Äčcomment.
A spokesperson for Congo's government did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
A Reuters ‚Äčjournalist in Goma reported hearing two loud explosions at around 4 a.m. (0200 GMT). The blasts shook windows and ‚ÄĆdoors ‚Ā†and were followed by the sound of ambulance sirens.
A senior AFC/M23 official told Reuters the house struck by one of the drones had been rented by UNICEF employees and is close to a residence used by former Congolese President Joseph Kabila in a neighbourhood that houses several ‚Äčprominent political and business ‚Äčfigures.
The official said a ‚Ā†second drone had targeted the residence of AFC/M23 political coordinator Corneille Nangaa, but fell into Lake Kivu.
The strikes come after weeks of intensifying ‚Äčdrone operations on both sides of the conflict.
AFC/M23 military spokesperson Willy Ngoma ‚Äčwas killed in ‚Ā†a drone attack by the Congolese army near the strategic mining town of Rubaya, some 60 km from Goma, on February 24.
The rebels have also claimed a series of drone attacks on the airport ‚Ā†serving ‚Äčthe northeastern city of Kisangani in recent weeks.
Fighting has ‚Äčcontinued in eastern Congo on several fronts, despite mediation efforts by parties including Qatar and the U.S.

item.title: Israeli settler violence rises in West Bank under Iran war curbs
item.description:
ABU FALAH, West Bank, March 11 (Reuters) - Israeli settlers in the occupied West Bank are taking advantage of curbs on movement imposed during the war on Iran to attack Palestinians, with military roadblocks ‚Äčpreventing ambulances reaching victims quickly, rights groups and medics say.
Settlers have killed at least five Palestinians in the West Bank since the United States and Israel began airstrikes against Iran ‚ÄĆon February 28, according to the Palestinian health ministry. A sixth man died after inhaling teargas fired during an attack, according to Israeli rights group B'Tselem.
Israel's military blocked many West Bank roads with iron gates and mounds of earth on the first day of the war, and has largely shut crossings with Israel.
The Israeli military says the curbs are preemptive measures while it is carrying out airstrikes on Iran and against Lebanese group Hezbollah, which has fired missiles at Israel in solidarity with ‚ÄčTehran.
Palestinians in remote West Bank villages say the roadblocks have left them increasingly exposed to settler violence.
The Israeli military has also continued to carry out the raids it frequently conducts in Palestinian ‚Äčcities and towns during peacetime to arrest Palestinians, often without charge, they say.
A spokesperson for the Yesha Council, which represents Jewish settlements, did not immediately respond ‚Ā†to a Reuters request for comment on the attacks.
Malak Beirat's husband, Thaer, was one of two Palestinians who residents and the Palestinian health ministry say were shot dead by settlers before dawn on Sunday ‚Äčin Abu Falah, north of the city of Ramallah.
"Thaer loved life. I never expected he would die," said Beirat, fighting back tears as she sat with her two children.
Witnesses told Reuters that when over 100 settlers ‚Äčgathered on the outskirts of Abu Falah, a local WhatsApp group rallied men to protect the small village. The initial confrontation involved stone throwing, but armed settlers arrived later and began shooting, they said.
Beirat's husband was shot dead while trying to protect a house from attack, a man who helped defend the village said.
Blood could still be seen on Monday in olive groves at the scene of the attack, where villagers have erected two Palestinian flags at the spots where the ‚Äčtwo men were killed - one for each victim.
A third Palestinian died after the attack. B'Tselem said his death was probably caused by the effect of teargas fired by Israeli troops deployed to the village during the ‚Äčattack.
The Israeli military says an investigation has been launched into the incident and that it condemns "violence of any kind".
Medics say the new roadblocks have led to delays in reaching injured Palestinians.
"There are obstacles - and even ‚ÄĆattacks by settlers ‚Ā†and the military on the (medical) crews," said Ahmed Jibril, spokesman for the Palestinian Red Crescent ambulance service.
There have been over 109 reports of settler violence against Palestinians in the West Bank since the start of the war with Iran including shootings, physical assaults, property damage, and threats, said Israeli monitoring group Yesh Din.
All the reported killings of Palestinians by settlers this year were in the last week, B'Tselem said.
Settlers shot dead Amir Muhammad Shanaran in a village near the city of Hebron on Saturday, and brothers Muhammad and Fahim ‚ÄėAzem were shot dead in Qaryut southeast of the city of Nablus last Monday, the Palestinian health ministry said.
"Taking advantage ‚Äčof the war, armed settler militias, often operating with ‚Äčsupport from the army, continue to attack and ‚Ā†harass Palestinian communities across the West Bank in an effort to force them out," B'Tselem said.
In three of the settler shootings, the settlers were wearing Israeli army uniform, Yesh Din said. The Israeli military did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Palestinians accuse the military of protecting settlers rather than villagers. Israel's ‚Äčmilitary denies this.
Israeli indictments of settler violence are rare. At the end of 2025, Yesh Din said that of the hundreds of cases of settler ‚Äčviolence it had documented since ‚Ā†the Hamas-led attacks on Israel in October 2023 that led to the Gaza war , only 2% resulted in indictments.
The United Nations says nearly 700 Palestinians were displaced by settler violence from the start of 2025 through early February 2026.
Israel's government has expanded settlements in a construction push that Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich says is aimed at burying the idea of a Palestinian state.
Right-wing Israeli minister Yossi Dagan announced on Wednesday the establishment of a new settlement ‚Ā†in a strategic ‚Äčposition in the mountain overlooking Nablus, one of 22 new settlements announced by the Israeli government last May.
Palestinians have long sought an ‚Äčindependent state in the West Bank, Gaza and East Jerusalem, territories Israel captured and occupied in the 1967 Middle East war.
Over 700,000 settlers live in East Jerusalem and the West Bank among more than 3 million Palestinians, according to a European Union report in 2024.
Most ‚Äčof the world considers Israel's settlement activity in the West Bank illegal under international law relating to military occupations. Israel disputes this view.

item.title: EU envoys approve sanctions on 19 Iranian officials, entities over rights violations
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 11 (Reuters) - European Union envoys have approved new ‚Äčsanctions targeting 19 ‚ÄĆIranian officials and entities responsible for serious human rights ‚Äčviolations, the bloc's ‚Äčforeign policy chief, Kaja ‚Ā†Kallas, said on Wednesday.
"As ‚Äčthe Iran war continues, ‚Äčthe EU will protect its interests and pursue those ‚Äčresponsible for domestic repression," ‚ÄčKallas said on X, adding ‚ÄĆthat "it ‚Ā†also sends a message to Tehran that Iran‚Äôs future cannot be built ‚Äčon ‚Äčrepression".
The ‚Ā†sanctions, which have been in the ‚Äčmaking since before ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†beginning of the war, will require final approval ‚Ā†from ‚Äčthe Council ‚Äčof the EU.

item.title: Israeli dentist returns to identifying war victims in Iran conflict
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 11 (Reuters) - After helping to identify the remains of the last Israeli hostage in Gaza, dentist Esi Sharon-Sagie hoped her work naming the dead killed ‚Äčin more than two years of war was finally over. Weeks later she was called back to duty.
On February 28, a ‚ÄĆU.S.-Israel air assault in Iran ignited a war now engulfing the Middle East and Sharon-Sagie is again seeking to give a name to those whose bodies are too badly mutilated for visual identification techniques.
On March 1, she was called to the site of an Iranian missile attack on the town of Beit Shemesh in central Israel.
"We were informed that a missile ‚Äčkilled nine people that weren't able to get into a shelter in time. And the minute we heard that the disaster happened, ‚Äčwe had to go to do the identification," said Sharon-Sagie.
Among the dead were a brother and two sisters, 13, 15 ‚Ā†and 16 years old.
Although she has been volunteering with police since 2010, Sharon-Sagie says that everything changed on ‚ÄčOctober 7, 2023, when Palestinian militant group Hamas launched a surprise attack on Israel.
Health authorities struggled to cope with the hundreds killed in Israel's single deadliest ‚Äčday. Identification of about 1,200 fatalities, some badly burned, was not completed for many months.
Forensic odontology is used to identify the remains of people whose bodies are in a condition that makes other methods, like visual recognition or fingerprinting, impossible. For instance, a charred or decomposed body can be identified by matching X-rays with past dental records.
Sharon-Sagie, who ‚Äčis on duty day and night, often had to summon the strength to overcome exhaustion. On one occasion, at the end of an hours-long shift, ‚Äčshe was asked to help with one more victim. She looked at the woman's slim hand, and rings.
"I spoke with her in my heart with no sound. I ‚ÄĆlooked at ‚Ā†her and I said: 'now you're going to help me because I have no strength anymore. You'll help me and I'll help you. I'll bring you back to your family'," said Sharon-Sagie.
A year later she helped to identify one of the masterminds of the October 7 attack.
Yahya Sinwar , then the leader of Hamas, was killed by Israeli fighters in southern Gaza in October 2024.
Sinwar's body was transferred to Israel, which still held his ‚Äčmedical records from his time in ‚Äčan Israeli prison, where he was ‚Ā†jailed for killing Israeli soldiers and Palestinians whom Hamas suspected were spying for Israel.
"He had brain surgery in Israel many, many years ago. So we had all his information," said Sharon-Sagie.
For many in Israel, the pain of the ‚ÄčOctober 7 attack could only be alleviated with the release of the last remaining hostages from Hamas captivity ‚Äčin Gaza.
That relief finally ‚Ā†came on January 26 when the remains of the last of the 251 hostages were found in the Palestinian enclave.
Sharon-Sagie was on the team that checked scores of bodies buried in a cemetery in northern Gaza in the search for police officer, Ran Gvili, who fought Palestinian militants at an Israeli kibbutz.
As a volunteer with the police ‚Ā†force, it ‚Äčwas a complex moment for Sharon-Sagie.
"I was surprised it was actually happening. I felt my ‚Äčhands trembling, I was very moved. When we did the identification and finished signing the report that this is him, at first I was happy but seconds after that I understood ‚Äčthat it is over, he is not coming back to Israel alive," she said.

item.title: OPEC confirms big Saudi oil production hike ahead of Iran war, holds forecasts steady
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - OPEC said on ‚ÄčWednesday that Saudi Arabia sharply increased ‚ÄĆoil production in February ahead of U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran and ‚Äčkept its forecasts for relatively ‚Äčstrong global oil demand growth this ‚Ā†year.
Saudi Arabia increased its oil ‚Äčoutput and exports as part of ‚Äča contingency plan in case any U.S. strike on Iran disrupts supplies from the Middle ‚ÄčEast, sources familiar with the ‚Äčplan had said in February.
The attack came on ‚ÄĆFebruary ‚Ā†28 and the resulting conflict has disrupted oil exports, forced production stoppages and sent prices soaring.
OPEC, in a ‚Äčmonthly report ‚Äčon its ‚Ā†website, said that Saudi Arabia told OPEC its ‚Äčoil supply to the market ‚Äčin ‚Ā†February was 10.111 million barrels per day while production was 10.882 million ‚Ā†bpd. ‚ÄčSaudi Arabia reported its ‚Äčproduction at 10.10 million bpd for January.

item.title: EU countries give final approval to 2040 target
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - European ‚ÄĆUnion countries gave the final approval on Thursday to a new climate target to slash greenhouse gas emissions 90% by 2040, pressing ahead with the bloc's ambitious climate agenda despite political resistance.
The new climate target ‚Äčis a hard-fought political compromise, struck by governments and EU lawmakers last year. ‚ÄčIt is more ambitious than most major economies' emissions-cutting commitments, including China's.
In ‚Ā†practice, the target will require an 85% emissions reduction from European industries against 1990 ‚Äčlevels. The EU will pay developing countries via carbon credits, so they cut emissions on ‚ÄčEurope's behalf to make up the rest, to reach 90%.
The EU agreed the target last year following months of wrangling between countries, such as Spain, which say worsening droughts and wildfires justified more ambitious ‚Äčgoals, and those like Poland and Italy, which sought to soften the emissions cuts, ‚Äčarguing that struggling industries cannot afford the upfront investments.
A reinforced majority of EU countries' ministers gave the ‚ÄĆfinal ‚Ā†formal sign-off to the legally binding goal at a meeting in Brussels. The Czech Republic, Slovakia, Poland and Hungary opposed it. The climate target will now pass into EU law.
As part of the agreement, the EU will also consider the option in future to ‚Äčuse international carbon credits ‚Äčto meet a ‚Ā†further 5% of its 2040 emissions reductions - potentially further softening the domestic efforts required.
The deal also delays the launch of a politically ‚Äčsensitive new EU carbon market by one year, to 2028 - a ‚Äčmove designed ‚Ā†to win over countries sceptical of the climate goal.
The target, which is designed to keep Europe on track to meet its pledge to reach net-zero emissions by 2050, falls short of the ‚Ā†90% domestic ‚Äčemissions cut recommended by the EU's climate science ‚Äčadvisers.
It is also weaker than Brussels' original plan for the goal, reflecting disagreement between EU governments over the speed and ‚Äčcost of their green agenda.

item.title: U.S. military tells civilians to avoid port facilities where Iranian navy operating
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - The U.S. military on ‚ÄčWednesday warned civilians ‚ÄĆto leave port facilities where Iranian ‚Äčnaval forces are ‚Äčoperating, adding that U.S. ‚Ā†forces could ‚Äčnot guarantee their ‚Äčsafety.
"Iranian dockworkers, administrative personnel, and commercial vessel crews ‚Äčshould avoid ‚ÄčIranian naval vessels and military ‚ÄĆequipment," ‚Ā†U.S. Central Command said in a statement. The ‚ÄčUnited States ‚Äčhas ‚Ā†been carrying out strikes ‚Äčagainst Iranian naval ‚Äčvessels-- ‚Ā†sinking or destroying 60 so ‚Ā†far.

item.title: Turkey opposition leader says Imamoglu trial is purely political
item.description:
ISTANBUL, March 11 (Reuters) - The trial of Istanbul's jailed mayor, Ekrem Imamoglu, is a purely political affair ‚Äčaimed at preventing him from challenging President Tayyip Erdogan in the next election, Turkey's main opposition leader told ‚ÄĆReuters in an interview on Wednesday.
Imamoglu, 55, appeared in court on Monday in the opening hearing of a sweeping corruption case involving more than 400 defendants linked to the Istanbul municipality.
Prosecutors accuse him of leading a criminal organisation for profit through tender-rigging and bribery, charges he and his Republican People's Party (CHP) deny.
"Let ‚Äčme put it plainly. In Turkey, what is happening is not a trial in the normal sense of the word. ‚ÄčIt is not a legal process but a political one," CHP leader Ozgur Ozel said.
The judges handling ‚Ā†the case have been tasked "not with conducting a fair trial but with delivering the decision that Erdogan wants", he said.
Erdogan's government denies ‚Äčinfluencing the judiciary and says Turkey's courts are independent. The investigation into the Istanbul municipality was led by Akin Gurlek, who last month was ‚Äčappointed justice minister.
Imamoglu has been held in pre-trial detention for nearly a year amid an unprecedented judicial crackdown on the CHP that rights groups and opposition figures say has damaged the democratic credentials of NATO member Turkey.
Ozel said he did not expect Imamoglu to be released from detention during ‚Äčhis trial, adding that such an outcome would require greater social pressure within Turkey, international pressure, or a further drop in Erdogan's ‚Äčpoll ratings.
Ozel said Erdogan wanted to keep Imamoglu in prison because, if released, he could campaign across the country ahead of elections that the opposition ‚ÄĆleader ‚Ā†believes will take place next year, ahead of schedule.
"If Imamoglu gets out, he could travel all over Turkey and launch a major campaign. That is why Erdogan wants to keep him inside," he said.
Opinion polls show Imamoglu performing strongly against Erdogan in any presidential race, while party polling suggests a tight race between the secularist CHP and Erdogan‚Äôs Islamist-rooted AKP, with the CHP holding a narrow lead.
Ozel said ‚ÄčTurkish democracy had sharply deteriorated ‚Äčunder Erdogan, who has led ‚Ā†Turkey as prime minister or president since 2003.
"Turkey's democracy has probably gone back 50 or 60 years," he said. "We have fallen into what I would call the third division in the world."
Ozel also said ‚Äčhe expected pressure on the opposition to intensify as elections approach.
"Things will get worse, and [Erdogan] will ‚Äčbecome more aggressive. ‚Ā†We are open to every kind of attack, both through legal channels and through other means," he said, adding that the opposition was not afraid and believed it would defeat Erdogan and his ruling AK Party at the ballot box.
Ozel said he believed Erdogan was preparing for ‚Ā†an early ‚Äčelection in September or October 2027 rather than waiting until the scheduled 2028 ‚Äčvote because under the constitution an earlier ballot could allow the president to run again.
"We want an election as soon as possible," Ozel added. "If the election is held ‚Äčin June 2028, Erdogan cannot run. If it is held early, he can."

item.title: Chile's Kast to take office in biggest right-wing shift in decades
item.description: Chile's Kast to take office in biggest right-wing shift in decades

item.title: Iran cannot participate in World Cup, sports minister says
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - Iran cannot participate in the 2026 World Cup after co-host ‚Äčthe U.S. launched airstrikes alongside Israel, Sports Minister Ahmad Donyamali said on Wednesday.
The attacks killed the Islamic Republic's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali ‚ÄĆKhamenei and triggered a region-wide conflict .
"Considering that this corrupt regime has assassinated our leader, under no circumstances can we participate in the World Cup," the minister told state television.
The 48-team World Cup will be held in the U.S., Mexico and Canada from June 11 to July 19.
"Our children are not safe and, fundamentally, such conditions for participation do not exist," Donyamali said.
"Given ‚Äčthe malicious actions they have carried out against Iran, they have forced two wars on us over eight or nine months and have ‚Äčkilled and martyred thousands of our people. Therefore, we certainly cannot have such a presence."
More than 1,300 Iranian civilians have ‚Ā†been killed since the U.S. and Israeli airstrikes began on February 28, according to Iran's U.N. ambassador Amir Saeid Iravani.
Iran are grouped with Belgium, Egypt and New Zealand.
All three of their Group G matches are scheduled to take place in the U.S., two in Los ‚ÄčAngeles and one in Seattle.
Iran, who dominated the Asian qualifying rounds to qualify for the tournament in March last year, was the only nation missing from a FIFA planning summit for World Cup participants held last week in Atlanta.
There was no immediate comment from the Iranian Football Federation or world soccer's governing body FIFA.
FIFA regulations state that any team that ‚Äčwithdraws from the tournament "no later than 30 days before the first match" will be fined at least 250,000 Swiss francs ($320,800).
"Disciplinary sanctions may include the expulsion ‚Äčof the participating member association concerned from subsequent FIFA competitions and/or the replacement of the participating member association with another member association," FIFA's regulations say.
"The FIFA Council or the ‚ÄĆrelevant committee ‚Ā†may decide, in particular, to replace the member association in question with another association."
Iran had selected Tucson's sprawling Kino Sports Complex as the team's base camp and 18 months of preparation hang in the balance with a potential economic hit in Arizona.
There is also the question of tickets to World Cup games involving Iran. Should Iran boycott the tournament, Iranian fans who bought tickets for eye-watering prices may be tempted to sell them on the huge resale market .
Earlier, FIFA President Gianni Infantino said ‚Äčhe had met U.S. President Donald ‚Ā†Trump, who told him he welcomed Iran's participation in the World Cup.
Trump had previously said " I really don't care " if Iran participated or not, but Infantino said he had a productive discussion with the president.
"During the discussions, President Trump reiterated that ‚Äčthe Iranian team is, of course, welcome to compete in the tournament in the United States," Infantino said.
A ‚Äčsource in Tehran familiar ‚Ā†with the matter said that as well as Iran's decision not to attend the World Cup, warm-up games were not possible because of the war.
Earlier this week, Australia granted humanitarian visas to five Iranian women soccer players after they sought asylum, fearing persecution on their return home for their refusal to sing the national anthem at ‚Ā†a Women's ‚ÄčAsian Cup match.
Trump had called on Australia to give asylum to members of the Iran ‚Äčwomen's soccer team.
On Wednesday, Australian police helped two more members of the Iranian women's soccer delegation slip their minders to claim asylum, but one has changed her mind and decided to go back to ‚ÄčIran, the country's interior minister said.
($1 = 0.7793 Swiss francs)

item.title: Turkey's Erdogan says Iran war must stop before whole region dragged in
item.description:
ANKARA, March 11 (Reuters) - Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan ‚ÄĆsaid on Wednesday that the war in Iran must be stopped before the whole Middle East region is "thrown into the fire", adding that diplomacy must be given a chance.
NATO air defences have in the last week intercepted two ‚ÄčIranian missiles headed toward southern Turkey, where there are air and radar bases used ‚Äčby NATO and the United States, and the alliance has since beefed up ‚Ā†defences there.
"This war must be stopped before it grows and the region as a whole ‚Äčis thrown into the fire. If diplomacy is given a chance, it is very possible to ‚Äčachieve this," Erdogan told lawmakers in parliament.
"In light of the sensitivity of the period we are in, we are speaking very carefully. We are acting with caution to protect Turkey from the fire that is surrounding ‚Äčit," he said, adding that Ankara was engaged with all sides in seeking an end to ‚Äčthe war.
Turkey had offered to mediate between the sides before the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran began 12 days ‚ÄĆago. ‚Ā†Since then, Erdogan has repeatedly called for diplomacy to end the conflict, while also warning that Turkey would respond to any threats to its national security.
Ozgur Ozel, chairman of Turkey's main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP) told Reuters in an interview on Wednesday that Erdogan had avoided criticising U.S. President ‚ÄčDonald Trump by name ‚Äčin order to ‚Ā†maintain their good personal rapport, adding that this had only emboldened the U.S. leader.
Ankara's "shy silence regarding Trump's actions, and its failure to take a ‚Äčstance against that disproportionate force, is not neutrality," Ozel said, adding that ‚Äčthe U.S. ‚Ā†was not taking Turkish sensitivities into account during the conflict.
Erdogan has criticised U.S., Israeli and Iranian actions and has called the U.S.-Israeli air campaign a "clear violation" of international law.
But in his comments, like many other world ‚Ā†leaders, he ‚Äčhas refrained from mentioning Trump by name. Erdogan's good ‚Äčpersonal ties with Trump have helped ease bilateral U.S.-Turkish tensions over the last year.

item.title: More than 330 arrested in Europol-led operation against illicit waste trade
item.description:
MADRID, March 11 (Reuters) - More than 330 people have been arrested across 70 countries in a ‚Äčcrackdown on organised crime networks accused ‚ÄĆof trafficking illicit waste within Europe and exporting it to Africa, Asia and Latin America, Europol said ‚Äčon Wednesday.
Authorities seized 127,149 metric tons ‚Äčof waste, 602 tons of polluting agents ‚Ā†along with 75 tons of plant ‚Äčprotection chemicals, 2.3 tons of mercury and nearly ‚Äč10 million euros ($11.6 million) in cash and bank accounts.
The seized waste could have generated illicit profits of ‚Äčat least 31 million euros, officials said.
Illegal ‚Äčwaste trafficking has become a global problem, operating through ‚ÄĆparallel ‚Ā†criminal circuits, Spain's Guardia Civil, which co‚ÄĎled the operation, said.
"These criminal networks not only fraudulently manage urban and industrial waste, but ‚Äčalso systematically resort ‚Äčto ‚Ā†document forgery and fraud to move hazardous materials, causing negative environmental ‚Äčimpacts and risks to public health," ‚Äčit ‚Ā†said.
Last year, Spanish police said they had arrested a gang suspected of smuggling more than 40,000 ‚Ā†tons ‚Äča year of untreated urban ‚Äčwaste from Italy to Spain.
($1 = 0.8627 euros)

item.title: How many ships have been attacked in the Gulf since start of Iran war?
item.description:
DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - The U.S.-Israeli war on Iran has threatened Gulf ports and disrupted global trade through the Strait of Hormuz through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas normally passes.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards have warned that ‚Äčany ship passing through the narrow Strait will be targeted.
Below is a list of reported attacks since the ‚ÄĆwar started on February 28:
A crew member was killed on board Marshall Islands‚Äďflagged crude tanker MKD VYOM after it was hit by a projectile as the vessel sailed off the Omani coast, 50 nautical miles north of the capital Muscat, manager V.Ships said.
Gibraltar-flagged oil bunkering tanker ‚ÄčHercules Star, which supplies fuel to ships, was struck by a projectile 17 nautical miles northwest of UAE's ‚ÄčMina Saqr in Ras Al Khaimah. A fire caused by the attack was extinguished, UKMTO said.
A ‚Ā†Palau-flagged tanker was attacked in the Strait of Hormuz, two nautical miles north of Oman's Kumzar. The crew of ‚Äčthe U.S.-sanctioned Skylight was evacuated, Oman's maritime security centre said.
U.S.-flagged products tanker Stena Imperative was struck by two projectiles in ‚Äčthe port of Bahrain, which resulted in a fire and the crew evacuating, UKMTO said.
Marshall Islands crude oil tanker Libra Trader and Panama-flagged bulker Gold Oak also sustained minor damage about 7-10 nautical miles off the UAE's port of Fujairah, UKMTO said.
Maltese-flagged container ship Safeen ‚ÄčPrestige was damaged by a projectile as it sailed towards the top of the strait, two nautical miles north of ‚ÄčOman. The attack caused a fire in the engine room and prompted its crew to abandon ship, according to shipping sources.
Crude oil ‚ÄĆtanker ‚Ā†Sonangol Namibe was hit by a blast while anchored near Iraq's Khor al Zubair port, U.S. representative company Sonangol Marine Services said.
An Iranian remote-controlled boat laden with explosives was used to target and damage the Bahamas-flagged ship, according to initial assessments from two Iraqi port security sources.
A tugboat was hit by projectiles in the Strait of Hormuz, six nautical miles north ‚Äčof Oman, while conducting operations ‚Äčwith vessel Safeen Prestige, which ‚Ā†was struck on March 4, UKMTO said.
UKMTO reported - citing a third party it did not name - a possible drone attack 10 nautical miles north of Saudi Arabia's Jubail. It ‚Äčsaid the majority of the crew was evacuated.
Thailand-flagged bulk carrier Mayuree Naree was attacked ‚Äčby a projectile ‚Ā†in the Strait, 11 nautical miles north of Oman. A fire broke out on board and prompted the crew to evacuate, the vessel's owner Bangkok-headquartered Precious Shipping (PSL.BK) , opens new tab said in a statement.
Japan-flagged container ship One Majesty sustained minor damage after it was hit by ‚Ā†a projectile ‚Äč25 nautical miles northwest of UAE's Ras Al Khaimah.
The hull of Marshall ‚ÄčIslands-flagged bulk carrier Star Gwyneth, was damaged after being hit by a projectile 50 nautical miles northwest of Dubai, maritime risk management company Vanguard said. ‚ÄčThere was no environmental impact reported and the crew was reported safe, UKMTO said.

item.title: Trump tells Axios war in Iran will end soon, 'practically nothing left' to target
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump told Axios on Wednesday that ‚Äčthere's "practically nothing ‚Äčleft" to target in Iran ‚Ā†and that the war ‚Äčthere will end "soon."
"Little this ‚Äčand that... Any time I want it to ‚Äčend, it will ‚Äčend," Trump said during a ‚ÄĆbrief ‚Ā†phone interview.
The conflict began nearly two weeks ago with ‚Äčstrikes ‚Äčon ‚Ā†Iran by the U.S. and ‚ÄčIsrael aimed at ‚Äčdismantling ‚Ā†Iran's military capabilities and nuclear program.

item.title: UK releases first files on Mandelson's appointment as US ambassador
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - The British government published the first documents covering the appointment of ‚ÄčPeter Mandelson as ambassador to Washington on Wednesday, reviving questions about Prime Minister Keir Starmer's judgment in employing a man ‚ÄĆclose to Jeffrey Epstein.
The publication of the first tranche of documents covering the vetting of Mandelson, under police investigation for allegedly leaking government documents to the late sex offender, does little to reduce the pressure on Starmer, who is under fire over the appointment and a series of policy U-turns.
The more than 100 pages of documents ‚Äčsuggested concerns were raised about the "reputational risks" in appointing Mandelson, particularly over his friendship with Epstein, but also his previous resignations ‚Äčfrom government and his support for closer ties with China.
Starmer sacked Mandelson from what is considered the most prestigious ‚Ā†posting in Britain's diplomatic service in September, when the depth of his friendship with the convicted sex offender started to become clear.
A document labelled "Advice to the prime minister, checks conducted on 4 December, 2024", said: "After Epstein was first convicted of procuring an underage girl in ‚Äč2008, their relationship continued across 2009-2011, beginning when Lord Mandelson was business minister and continuing after the end of the Labour government. Mandelson reportedly stayed in Epstein's House while he was in jail in June 2009."
In a summary of a fact-finding call between the prime minister's general counsel and the national security adviser, Jonathan Powell, in ‚ÄčSeptember, a document said that Powell found the appointment process "unusual" and "weirdly rushed".
Powell said he had concerns about Mandelson's "reputation‚ÄĚ in conversations with Morgan McSweeney, ‚Äčthen the prime minister's chief of staff. According to the note, Philip Barton, then the most senior government official at the foreign ministry, "also had reservations around the ‚ÄĆappointment".
Starmer's allies have sought to play down the importance of the first set of documents, saying an ongoing police investigation meant some of the more revealing exchanges were being withheld.
Further documents will be released at a later date, papers which the British prime minister's team say will prove Mandelson lied to Starmer about the extent of his relationship with Epstein before his appointment as ambassador in December 2024.
Darren Jones, Starmer's chief secretary, ‚Äčtold lawmakers last month the first ‚Äčtranche of documents would not ‚Ā†include any correspondence between Starmer's Downing Street office and Mandelson, when follow-up questions were asked, due to the probe.
Mandelson, a government minister when Labour was previously in power more than 15 years ago, quit the House ‚Äčof Lords, the upper house of parliament, in February over his links to Epstein. He was arrested ‚Äčlast month on suspicion of ‚Ā†misconduct in a public office, and later released on bail.
Files released by the U.S. Justice Department in January included emails suggesting Mandelson had leaked government documents to Epstein, and that the convicted offender had recorded payments to Mandelson or his then-partner, now husband.
Mandelson has said he does not recall having ‚Ā†received payments. ‚ÄčHe has not commented publicly on allegations he leaked documents, and did not respond to ‚Äčmessages seeking comment.
Starmer has expressed regret over the appointment, saying the Labour veteran had created a "litany of deceit" about his ties to Epstein, an apology that did little to quieten ‚Äčopposition voices saying the prime minister should step down.

item.title: UNESCO fears for fate of historical sites during Iran war
item.description:
PARIS, March 11 (Reuters) - UNESCO said it is deeply concerned about the fate of world heritage sites in Iran and across the region, after Tehran's Golestan palace, often compared to ‚ÄčVersailles, and a historic mosque and palace in Isfahan were damaged in the ‚ÄĆwar.
The United Nations' cultural agency on Wednesday urged all parties to protect the region's outstanding cultural sites, saying four of Iran's 29 world heritage sites had been damaged since the start of the U.S. and ‚ÄčIsraeli war with Iran.
"UNESCO is deeply concerned by the first impact that the hostilities ‚Äčare already having on many world heritage sites," Lazare Eloundou Assomo, director ‚Ā†of the World Heritage Centre, told Reuters, adding he was also concerned for sites in ‚ÄčIsrael, Lebanon and across the Middle East.
Tehran's Golestan palace, damaged in U.S.‚ÄďIsraeli strikes, is testimony ‚Äčto the grandeur of Iran's civilization in the 19th century, he said.
The palace was chosen as the Persian royal residence and seat of power by the Qajar family and shows the introduction of European styles in ‚ÄčPersian arts, according to the UNESCO website. The last Shah of Iran, Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, ‚Äčheld a coronation ceremony there in 1969.
"We sometimes even compare it with the Versailles Palace in France, ‚ÄĆfor instance, ‚Ā†and it has suffered, unfortunately, some damage. We don't know the extent for the moment. But clearly, with the images that we have been able to receive, we can confirm ... it has been affected," Eloundou Assomo said.
Photos of the interior of the palace have shown piles ‚Äčof smashed glass and ‚Äčshards of wood ‚Ā†on the floor, and shattered woodwork.
Isfahan was one of Central Asia's most important cities and a key point on the Silk Road trading ‚Äčroute. Its Masjed-e Jame (Jameh Mosque) is more than 1,000 years old and ‚Äčshows the ‚Ā†development of Islamic art through 12 centuries.
Buildings close to the buffer zone of the prehistoric sites of the Khorramabad Valley have also been damaged, UNESCO said.
UNESCO has shared coordinates of key cultural ‚Ā†sites to ‚Äčall parties, Eloundou Assomo said, and was monitoring damage.
"We ‚Äčare calling for the protection of all sites of cultural significance ... everything that tells the history of all the ‚Äčcivilisations of the 18 countries in the region," he said.

item.title: Carney on verge of majority in Canada parliament after opposition MP defects
item.description:
OTTAWA, March 11 (Reuters) - Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney on Wednesday moved closer to a parliamentary majority that could help ‚Äčhim counter U.S. tariffs when an opposition legislator joined the ‚ÄĆruling Liberals.
The centrist Liberals, governing with a minority after an election last April, need opposition support to pass key legislation such as budgets. That can be a ‚Äčslow process, and Carney has said he needs a majority ‚Äčto react more effectively to U.S. President Donald Trump's trade ‚Ā†measures.
Carney said in a statement that Lori Idlout of the ‚Äčsmall left-leaning New Democratic Party, who represents the Arctic territory of Nunavut, ‚Äčwould sit with the Liberals in the House of Commons, the elected chamber.
Idlout is the fourth opposition legislator to defect to the Liberals since last November and ‚Äčher decision means the party now has 170 seats in the ‚Äč343-seat House, two short of a majority.
Special elections will be held on April ‚ÄĆ13 ‚Ā†in three vacant constituencies previously held by Liberals, two of which are safe seats. If the Liberals win two of the three, Carney would have control of the chamber.
A majority would open the way to ‚ÄčCarney serving until ‚ÄčOctober 2029. Canadian ‚Ā†elections are held no later than every four years.
"At this important moment in our history, Canadians are ‚Äčcoming together to build a strong future," said Carney, ‚Äčwho ‚Ā†has promised to increase defense spending to better protect the vast and almost totally uninhabited Canadian Arctic.
‚ÄúWith new threats against our sovereignty and pressures ‚Ā†on ‚Äčthe wellbeing of people throughout the North, ‚Äčwe need a strong and ambitious government that makes decisions with Nunavut ‚ÄĒ not only about ‚ÄčNunavut," Idlout said in a statement.
(This story has been refiled to fix the hyperlink in paragraph 2)

item.title: Poland to seek help from two other countries in Epstein investigation
item.description:
WARSAW, March 11 (Reuters) - Poland will ask two other European countries for information and evidence needed for its investigation into human trafficking related to late U.S. sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, ‚Äčprosecutors said on Wednesday.
In a statement, they said documents from the Epstein ‚ÄĆfiles indicated a reasonable suspicion that human trafficking had taken place in Poland. They did not name the European countries they would contact but a source familiar with the matter told Reuters ‚Äčthe prosecutors would ask France and Sweden for help.
The U.S. Justice Department's release of ‚Äčmillions of internal documents related to Epstein has revealed the late financier ‚Ā†and sex offender's ties to many prominent people in politics, finance, academia and ‚Äčbusiness - both before and after he pleaded guilty in 2008 to prostitution charges.
In February, Polish ‚ÄčPrime Minister Donald Tusk announced that Poland would launch an investigation into possible links between Epstein and Russian intelligence, as well as any offences affecting Polish citizens.
The Polish National Prosecutor's Office confirmed in its ‚Äčstatement that it had initiated an investigation into human trafficking committed in the period ‚Äčfrom 2009 to August 2019 on the territory of Poland and other countries.
Prosecutors suspect the trafficking consisted ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†recruiting women and girls for work abroad under false pretences and of then transporting them outside Poland and handing them over to other people for sexual exploitation, the statement said.
Files reviewed by Reuters show that a man called Daniel Siad had informed Epstein ‚Äčabout his travels through ‚ÄčPoland, Slovakia, the Czech ‚Ā†Republic, among other countries, scouting for models.
He also mentioned his cooperation with Jean-Luc Brunel, a key suspect and longtime Epstein associate, ‚Äčwho died in a French prison in 2022.
According to Polish media ‚Äčreports, Siad ‚Ā†was born in Algeria and moved to Sweden at the age of 23.
Reuters reached out to him on two phone numbers and an e-mail address found in the files, but ‚Ā†has ‚Äčnot yet received answers to questions sent.
In February, Swedish ‚Äčnewspaper Expressen quoted Siad as saying he had never committed a crime and was open to talking to ‚Äčinvestigators in any interested country.

item.title: Higher oil clouds Wall Street's sunny earnings outlook
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 11 (Reuters) - U.S. companies face structurally higher oil prices this year even if the Iran war ends soon, meaning investors may need to rethink those sunny 2026 corporate earnings forecasts.
Heading into the year, the consensus 2026 outlook for oil was fairly bearish, while the earnings forecasts for Wall Street were pretty optimistic.
The latter ‚Äčhasn't changed. As of Friday, full-year 2026 earnings growth estimates, according to LSEG data, were nearly 16%, up from 14% last year and 12% the year before.
However, those ‚ÄĆrosy outlooks assume an average oil price this year close to $60 per barrel ‚Äď an expectation that disappeared into thin air with the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran on February 28 and subsequent supply disruption.
Oil market volatility has been wild. Crude recorded its biggest weekly rise on record last week, and rose further to almost $120 a barrel this week before plunging on hopes for a swift end to the conflict.
While oil will likely drift lower when the war eventually subsides, the damage is done. The finely tuned ‚Äčglobal energy system has been upended, infrastructure has been hit and the expected supply glut has evaporated.
Average oil prices this year will almost certainly be much higher than businesses were budgeting ‚Äčfor on January 1. Companies will absorb part of that increase, and consumers will certainly feel the pinch. Either way, corporate earnings will be squeezed.
In a Reuters poll in December, the consensus average 2026 forecast for Brent crude was $61.27 a barrel, with oversupply expected to offset possible supply disruption from the brewing U.S.-Iran tensions. That would have ‚Äčrepresented a 7% decline from the 2025 average of $68.20.
These forecasts have since been torn up.
Analysts at HSBC on Tuesday raised their average 2026 Brent forecast to $80 from $65 and their U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude outlook to $76 from $61. These ‚Äčare increases of 23% and 25%, respectively.
Also on Tuesday, the U.S. Energy Information Administration upped its average 2026 Brent crude forecast to $79 from $58 last month. That's a 36% increase.
Few sectors of the economy will escape the impact of energy price hikes like that. Gasoline, jet fuel, fertilizer, petrochemicals and plastics will all be more expensive, with pain felt in industries as diverse as transportation, manufacturing, metals, retail and food.
"As prices rise, consumption is affected, and, ultimately, corporate earnings erode," ‚Äčsays Joe Brusuelas, chief economist at RSM US LLP.
Equity strategists at Goldman Sachs reckon the direct impact of "modestly" higher oil prices on S&P 500 earnings should be fairly muted, ‚Äčbut an extended period of supply disruption or uncertainty poses much greater risk to economic activity.
For every one percentage point decline in real U.S. GDP growth, S&P 500 earnings per share could fall 3-4%, they posit.
Other estimates suggest ‚ÄĆa 30% ‚Ā†rise in oil prices could knock as much as 4% off S&P 500 earnings, with the pain felt most acutely in transportation, industrial, and consumer discretionary.
Consumer spending accounts for around 70% of U.S. economic activity. Sharply higher energy costs will squeeze household budgets, and spending elsewhere will suffer.
Alarm bells are already ringing. Average U.S. gasoline prices are now over $3.50 a gallon, according to American Automobile Association data. That's up 17% from before the war started.
There's a flip side, of course. Sustained higher oil prices boost energy sector profits, and double-digit earnings growth for the sector is not out of the question. But energy only accounts ‚Äčfor 4‚Äď5% of total S&P 500 earnings and ‚Äčthus is unlikely to offset the hit to ‚Ā†margins elsewhere.
Within current 2026 earnings growth forecasts, there is incredibly wide dispersion among the 11 sectors.
As of Friday, the energy sector EPS had sunk to minus 1.2%, by far the gloomiest forecast and the only one pointing to declining profits.
At the other end of the scale, tech's ‚Äč2026 EPS growth estimate was 35.9%, comfortably the highest among all sectors and up from 30.8% on January 1. Tech has contributed the lion's ‚Äčshare of the S&P 500's ‚Ā†earnings growth in recent years.
But higher energy prices are a blow for the mega-cap firms leading the artificial intelligence arms race. UBS analysts estimate capex spending by the "hyperscalers" this year will hit $770 billion. The construction and running of those data centers could now get a lot more expensive.
Investors were already nervous about the future returns on these outlays. Significantly more expensive energy will only deepen their fears at a time when risks to ‚Ā†many other sectors ‚Äčare growing too.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for Reuters)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab ‚Äčyour essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: UN warns global aid at risk as Middle East war spreads
item.description:
GENEVA, March 11 (Reuters) - The United Nations aid chief warned on Wednesday that the conflict in the Middle East is straining humanitarian operations worldwide, ‚Äčdisrupting supply chains and slowing the delivery of life‚ÄĎsaving assistance ‚ÄĆto numerous crisis zones.
‚ÄúWe are in a moment of grave peril for the Middle East and, actually I believe, for the wider world,‚ÄĚ Tom Fletcher, the U.N. aid ‚Äčchief, told Reuters.
The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran, which has expanded to ‚ÄčLebanon and dragged in Gulf countries, has convulsed global markets ‚Ā†and disrupted supply chains , with airspace closures and the halt of shipping ‚Äčthrough the critical Strait of Hormuz .
Fletcher said aid supplies to Gaza and sub‚ÄĎSaharan Africa are ‚Äčbeing affected by the conflict, as humanitarian relief that needs to travel through the Strait of Hormuz or through airspace in the Gulf has been largely blocked or constrained.
Conflict-ridden ‚ÄčSomalia, which is facing a major drought, and Sudan are among ‚Äča number of countries facing dire humanitarian crises.
"These (constraints) will damage our humanitarian supply chains, reduce ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†humanitarian supplies we can get to people who need them, but they'll also drive up energy costs and food costs across the region," Fletcher said.
"This really is a perfect storm of factors right now, and I'm seriously ‚Äčworried," he stated.
There ‚Äčis particular concern ‚Ā†for aid supplies to sub‚ÄĎSaharan Africa, Flether said, which are being impacted by restricted movement through the Strait ‚Äčof Hormuz, with alternative routes slapped with rising freight costs.
