item.title: RWE targets gas-fired power plants in $20 billion US expansion drive
item.description:
ESSEN, Germany, March 12 (Reuters) - Germany's largest power producer, RWE (RWEG.DE) , opens new tab , said on Thursday ‚ÄĆit would expand more aggressively in the United States, a market where data centres have significantly fuelled power demand, by investing in new gas-fired power plants.
The move marks a major push by ‚ÄčGermany's biggest utility into a market where the rapid construction of ‚Äčdata centres, as well as the need to modernise ageing power ‚Ā†infrastructure, has fuelled a boom in generation assets and network equipment.
This is ‚Äčmainly driven by big tech firms, so-called hyperscalers, which are planning to spend $600 ‚Äčbillion this year on artificial intelligence, a technology that needs substantial power supply and has also benefited German industrial peers .
"The United States remains our most important growth market, not least due to ‚Äčthe rapidly growing demand for electricity," RWE said in its annual report, ‚Äčadding that more gas-fired power plants would ensure round-the-clock supply.
"A decisive factor, particularly for industrial companies ‚ÄĆand ‚Ā†data centres."
Overall, the United States, where RWE already has 13 gigawatts (GW) of installed solar, wind and battery storage capacity, will account for 17 billion euros ($20 billion), or nearly half, its planned spending by 2031.
Installed U.S. capacity is expected to ‚Äčincrease to 22 ‚ÄčGW as a ‚Ā†result, the company said, while releasing full-year results that showed core profit fell 10% to 5.1 billion euros, still beating ‚Äčthe 4.9 billion poll.
RWE said it was developing a 5 ‚ÄčGW pipeline ‚Ā†of gas-fired power plant projects in the United States, adding that more than 3 GW would be realised by 2035.
Shares in the company, which have risen nearly ‚Ā†a ‚Äčfifth year-to-date, were set to open 1.1% lower ‚Äčin pre-market trade, with traders citing a slight cut in the group's outlook for 2027 core profit.
($1=0.8643 ‚Äčeuros)

item.title: TotalEnergies caps oil prices for consumers amidst 'exceptional volatility'
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - TotalEnergies ‚Äčsaid on Thursday it ‚ÄĆwould cap the price of petrol at its service stations ‚Äčin mainland France ‚Äčas a result of "exceptional market ‚Ā†volatility".
Oil prices have ‚Äčbeen on a rollercoaster ride ‚Äčever since Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz following American ‚Äčand Israeli aerial strikes, ‚Äčespecially straining economies and consumers ‚ÄĆin ‚Ā†energy-import reliant regions such as Europe.
($1 = ‚Äč0.8659 euros)

item.title: China orders immediate ban on March fuel exports, sources say
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 12 (Reuters) - China has ordered an immediate ban on refined ‚Äčfuel exports in March in a further step to pre-empt ‚ÄĆa potential domestic fuel shortage caused by the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran , four sources with knowledge of the matter said on Thursday.
The ban, issued by the National ‚ÄčDevelopment and Reform Commission (NDRC), includes shipments of gasoline, diesel ‚Äčand aviation fuel, the sources said.
The halt applies to cargoes ‚Ā†that had yet to clear customs as of March 11. ‚ÄčThe restriction on outbound flows goes beyond last week's move by Beijing urging ‚Äčrefiners not to agree to new exports and to try to cancel shipments they had already committed to.
Jet fuel for aviation bunkering is not included under ‚Äčthe ban, two of the four sources said.
The NDRC did not ‚Äčimmediately respond to a request for comment.
China is the world's biggest oil importer ‚ÄĆand ‚Ā†a major fuel exporter.
Chinese oil majors had planned to ramp up fuel exports in February and March to profit from stronger margins amid a seasonal lull in domestic demand during the Lunar New Year ‚Äčholiday period, traders ‚Äčhave said.
Traders had ‚Ā†previously forecast March fuel exports at 2.2 million to 2.3 million tons for gasoline, diesel and ‚Äčjet fuel, excluding volumes for aviation bunkering, up by ‚Äč300,000 to 400,000 tons ‚Ā†from February estimates.
For March so far, however, China has only shipped up to 50,000 metric tons of gasoline (422,500 barrels), 300,000 tons of diesel (2.24 ‚Ā†million ‚Äčbarrels) and 300,000 tons of seaborne jet ‚Äčfuel (2.36 million barrels), data from ship trackers and trade sources showed.

item.title: Asia LNG scramble pulls cargoes from Europe; one US shipment switches destination to China
item.description:
SINGAPORE/BEIJING, March 12 (Reuters) - Asia's scramble for LNG to replace output cut off by war in the Middle East is drawing more cargoes away from Europe, with ‚Äčat least one U.S. shipment switching its destination to China, which would end ‚ÄĆa year-long halt in such imports due to tariffs.
The conflict that began with U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran on February 28 has halted traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, disrupting supply from Qatar - the second-biggest LNG ‚Äčexporter after the U.S. - causing buyers to compete for spare cargoes.
Shiptracking data from analytics ‚Äčfirm Kpler showed the UMM Ghuwailina, Elisa Ardea and Pan Americas tankers signalling ‚Ā†for European ports before changing destination to Asia.
The UMM Ghuwailina picked up a cargo ‚Äčfrom Plaquemines LNG in the U.S. state of Louisiana on March 1 and signalled its destination ‚Äčas Zeebrugge in Belgium before changing to Tianjin in China, Kpler data showed.
The cargo would be the first from the U.S. to arrive in China since Mu Lan discharged at Zhangshou in February last year before ‚Äčtit-for-tat tariffs between Beijing and Washington made such imports uncompetitive.
China suspended a 24% top-up tariff ‚Äčon U.S. goods for one year but retained a base 10% imposed in November. Tariffs on U.S. energy goods, ‚ÄĆincluding ‚Ā†15% on LNG, also remain, leading Chinese importers to divert U.S.-sourced cargoes elsewhere.
"The vessel is still quite far from China and near the Brazil coast at the moment... It could still change its route along the way," said ICIS senior analyst Yuanda Wang.
U.S. LNG does not have ‚Äča cost advantage under ‚Äčcurrent tariff levels, Wang ‚Ā†said.
"Even if tariffs were removed, the delivered cost would still remain above $10 per million British thermal units, higher than the price of imported ‚Äčpipeline gas into China."
Asian buyers of liquefied natural gas have been paying ‚Äčaround $20 to $25 ‚Ā†per mmBtu for March and April delivery.
Apart from the UMM Ghuwailina, the Elisa Ardea which loaded at Freeport LNG in the U.S. state of Texas was set for the Netherlands before diverting to ‚Ā†Taiwan.
The ‚ÄčPan Americas loaded at Bonny LNG in Nigeria and ‚Äčwas on course for Croatia before turning toward Asia via the Cape of Good Hope.

item.title: China's new ethnic unity law could target Taiwanese, Taipei officials warn
item.description:
TAIPEI, March 12 (Reuters) - A new Chinese law on ethnic unity could ‚Äčgive Beijing another legal basis to go after Taiwanese it views as separatists given language it ‚ÄĆcontains on protecting China's sovereignty and security, officials in Taipei say.
China, which views democratically governed Taiwan as its own territory, already has laws and regulations against Taiwanese independence supporters, among them guidelines published in 2024 to punish "diehard" activists, including with the death penalty, even though Chinese courts ‚Äčhave no jurisdiction on the island.
The new law , passed by China's largely rubber-stamp parliament on Thursday, aims to create ‚Äča "shared" national identity among the country's 55 ethnic minority groups, which include Tibetans and Uyghurs. ‚Ā†It does not directly mention Taiwan, whose people Beijing considers Chinese citizens, apart from a brief mention of a ‚Äčneed to encourage people on both sides of the Taiwan Strait to identify as being Chinese.
But it does say Chinese ‚Äčcitizens "shall protect the country's sovereignty" and cites President Xi Jinping as saying everyone has to "consciously uphold national unity, national security, and social stability."
Shen Yu-chung, a deputy minister at Taiwan's China-policy-making Mainland Affairs Council, told reporters in Taipei shortly before the law was passed in Beijing ‚Äčthat such language could "spill over into becoming a legal basis for handling cross-Strait issues."
Asked whether it could be used as a ‚Äčbasis for targeting those China views as supporters of Taiwan independence, he said: "It is highly possible."
"How exactly one is supposed to promote ‚ÄĆunification ‚Ā†or promote unity is left vague and hollow, but the punishments are concrete," Shen added.
China's Taiwan Affairs Office did not respond to a request for comment.
Taiwan has long complained about what the government calls Chinese "lawfare" against the island: passing laws to give a legal basis for targeting those it believes are separatists, including overseas.
The new law includes another ‚Äčclause saying people and groups beyond ‚Äčthe borders of the People's ‚Ā†Republic of China can be held legally accountable for undermining "ethnic unity and progress or inciting ethnic separatism."
"Many of the Chinese communists' actions are nominally presented as measures for maintaining domestic ‚Äčstability, but in reality they could also be transformed into long-arm jurisdiction," Shen said, ‚Äčreferring to Chinese ‚Ā†efforts to enforce its laws overseas.
Taiwanese officials say the new law represents a shift in tactics from Beijing: whereas previously Chinese legislation was about opposing certain beliefs, like Taiwan independence, now it threatens punishment if people do not actively promote China's unity.
"In ‚Ā†the past, ‚Äčyou'd be punished for supporting Taiwan independence. Now, you also have ‚Äčto actively support unification, or you'll get into trouble as well," one senior Taiwanese official told Reuters, speaking on condition of anonymity citing the sensitivity ‚Äčof the matter.

item.title: France's far right taps into security fears in Marseille mayoral race
item.description:
MARSEILLE, March 12 (Reuters) - Security-focused campaign videos by the far-right National Rally candidate shaking up Marseille's mayoral race have helped die-hard supporter Marie-Helene Martin convince previously reticent ‚Äčrelatives to vote for him, she said.
To rousing music, Franck Allisio promises to triple the number of municipal cops, double the number of security cameras ‚ÄĆand put a police post in every district to "bring happiness back" to the people of Marseille.
Allisio is tied in first-round polls ahead of Sunday's first round with incumbent Socialist Mayor Benoit Payan, providing the RN party with a once-unthinkable shot at power in France's second-largest city.
Famous for its historic port and striking Mediterranean views, Marseille has become the epicentre of authorities' battle with a nationwide surge in cocaine use, according to a ‚Äč2024 senate report.
Security is the top voter concern ahead of France's two-round municipal elections on March 15 and March 22, polls show, which could benefit the RN and ‚Äčits tough-on-crime rhetoric.
Allisio's strong showing in Marseille tracks national trends, with polls suggesting the RN, an anti-immigrant party once known for racism and ‚Ā†antisemitism, could clinch the presidency in the 2027 election.
"We need to win the municipal elections, and then our goal is the presidential," said RN supporter Martin, a schoolteacher.
A March Ifop poll found an Allisio victory hinges on how many rivals reach the second round. If the left rallies behind a single candidate, Payan is seen ‚Äčwinning by a 10-point margin. But if the field is split between four contenders, the race tightens.
French mayors have limited powers over local security issues, controlling municipal police forces that lack the powers and resources of national police.
Yet both Allisio and Payan have made crimefighting the centrepiece of their campaigns.
"Obviously we are the narco-city," Allisio told Reuters. "We are witnessing an explosion in drug trafficking, and in the face of this, for ‚Äčyears ... nothing has been done."
Payan dismissed allegations of inaction, pointing to a bolstered local police force.
"The far right is playing on fears," he told Reuters. "Ultimately, it's not addressing the issue ‚Äčof security, since what it proposes is practically nothing or completely unrealistic."
Official data showed a 4.1% decrease in overall crime in Marseille last year compared to 2024 and police data show drug-related killings have ‚ÄĆfallen after a ‚Ā†peak in 2023, but murders related to drug dealing have shocked residents.
Claire Duport, a sociologist with the French Monitoring Centre for Drugs and Addictive Trends, said that although drug-related violence was not rising in Marseille compared with previous decades, killings had shifted from score-settlings to more indiscriminate attacks.
"The feeling of insecurity is legitimate ‚Äď it's just not representative of wider trends," she said.
In a bid to bolster his security bona fides, Payan has drafted into his campaign Amine Kessaci, a well-known 22-year-old anti-drugs activist who has lost two brothers in drug-related murders.
During an interview at ‚Äčthe left-wing coalition headquarters, Kessaci told Reuters ‚Äčthe RN's plans would fail to combat ‚Ā†the social root causes of drug-related violence that the Socialists planned to address.
"We're going to respond to drug trafficking with healthcare, with education, with transport, with housing," he said.
In La Busserine, one of the northern neighbourhoods most affected by drug violence, some residents also chafed at the ‚ÄčRN's rhetoric. Fadella Ouidef, who volunteers in the neighbourhood social centre, said she was frustrated to see security dominate the campaign.
"The far ‚Äčright is always the ‚Ā†one setting the terms of the debate ... All they know how to say is security, security, security," said the mother of four, who feared the underlying message was that Arab and Black people were "the bad ones".
A 15-minute walk away, four youngsters were selling drugs to the occasional client outside a tall, decrepit housing block dubbed "Le Mail". Ouidef said she avoids Le Mail, but that she feels ‚Ā†good in ‚Äčthe diverse district of La Busserine, where she has lived for the past 17 years.
"If the National Rally ‚Äčcomes to power ... it will be disastrous," Ouidef said, referring to feared RN cuts to social services and saying drug use was a pressing public health issue.
"They think that by creating more social hardship, they'll create more security, but ‚Äčthey'll soon realize it will create more hardship and more delinquency," she said. "People like them aren't interested in people like us."

item.title: Iran oil shock prompts ECB hawks to seek 2021/22 rematch
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - European Central Bank hawks are itching to rewrite the record. This month's energy price shock may not match the fallout from Russia's invasion of Ukraine , but officials are clearly wary of repeating the slow post-pandemic policy tightening that left them scrambling in 2022.
The debate within the ECB Governing Council will surely be intense over whether, how or when to respond to this potentially inflationary oil and gas price spike . Uncertainty ‚Äčabout the course of the Iran conflict and the length of the related energy hiatus means few will want to decide much as soon as next week's policy meeting .
But financial markets ‚ÄĆaren't hanging about to find out who's going to win the argument. Money markets shifted on Tuesday to fully price an ECB interest rate rise by July - a seismic shift from the flat policy horizon seen only a few weeks ago, when markets were even flirting with the chance of one more cut earlier this year.
And hawks have been out in force - perhaps listened to by markets more closely now after recent speculation that ECB President Christine Lagarde may stand down early, as soon as this year.
One name ‚Äčtouted as a possible successor, Bundesbank boss and well-known policy hawk Joachim Nagel , made his view clear on Wednesday.
"We must be very vigilant," Nagel told Reuters, adding that the debate about a possible ‚Äčinflation undershoot and further easing had ended.
"If it becomes apparent that the current energy price increases will translate into broad consumer price inflation in the medium ‚Ā†term, the Governing Council of the ECB will act decisively in a timely manner."
"Timely manner" is doing a lot of heavy lifting in that comment.
That's likely because there's still a bruise at the ECB - and at ‚Äčmany other major central banks - from the attempt to see through what was considered a "transitory" post-pandemic inflation surge, stoked mainly by economies rebooting from COVID-19 lockdowns and huge fiscal rescue spending.
Leaving ECB policy rates in negative territory ‚Äčeven as headline euro zone inflation soared from 2% to almost 6% in the eight months through February 2022 probably hurts in hindsight.
ECB board member Isabel Schnabel went one further by saying on Wednesday there are "scars from this high-inflation episode."
NOT RELIANT ON LUCK
It may even have worked out okay back then too, but it left the whole picture very vulnerable to another left-field event.
Once Russia invaded Ukraine in February 2022 and sent natural gas and oil prices soaring - an event the central bank couldn't ‚Äčreasonably have predicted - euro zone inflation shot above 10% later that year.
The ECB eventually scrambled into action by July, lifting rates from below zero to 4% in just 14 months and returning inflation to target a year later. The price ‚Äčwas steep: higher energy bills, general inflation and the sharp rate shock for businesses, households and the wider economy.
For many, that sequence of events may have been just unlucky - but it also displayed the risks of not moving quickly to head off above-target inflation, rising ‚ÄĆinflation expectations and ‚Ā†second-round effects including business margin-padding and wage bargaining pressure.
The delay, they argue, meant the ECB eventually had to stamp on the brakes harder than would otherwise have been necessary.
Former Dutch central banker Klaas Knot, another name widely touted as a possible successor to Lagarde, made that very point late last week in a podcast with Brussels think tank Bruegel , opens new tab .
"The lessons (from 2021/22) are that while standard theory prescribes that you should look through a negative supply shock like an energy shock, you have to be very, very wary of non-linearities and second-round effects," Knot said.
"If necessary, if a similar response was warranted, then the ECB would look back to ‚Äčthe playbook of 2022/23," he said.
There's plenty of argument ‚Äčagainst that still, of course - the most ‚Ā†obvious one being that we don't know the extent and length of this Gulf energy hit yet, and overreaction may prove unnecessarily damaging.
ECB Vice President Luis de Guindos spoke on Wednesday of a need for cool heads and warned that stoked financial market volatility could cause its own problems.
For others the comparison with 2021/2022 is ‚Äčunfair because both fiscal and monetary policy were much looser back then than they are now - so the starting points are different.
Even Schnabel argued that ‚Äčthe energy supply shocks are ‚Ā†different now than four years ago - the earlier one being a more permanent cut of Russian gas and oil supplies and this month's likely a somewhat more temporary halt to Gulf exports.
For now, however, markets seem to favor the idea that the ECB will move more quickly to limit the need for subsequent harsher action down the road.
If oil and gas prices don't fall back very soon, an early shot across the bow to price-setters may well ‚Ā†be in store.
(The ‚Äčopinions expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new ‚Äčsource for global financial commentary.

And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: It‚Äôs time for short sellers to make a comeback
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 12 (Reuters) - The last three years have been a wonderful time to own U.S. stocks. The S&P 500 returned about 22% a year between 2022 and 2025. For short sellers - those betting on falling prices - the environment has been far more punishing.
Some of the best-known short-focused firms have either retreated or ‚Äčshut down. Jim Chanos' Kynikos Associates closed its main hedge funds in 2023, citing pressure on the long/short equity model. By then, Pershing Square's Bill Ackman had already sworn off ‚ÄĆactivist short campaigns. And last year Hindenburg Research founder Nathan Anderson announced that he was disbanding his firm.
Short sellers have historically been cast as the ‚Äúbad guys‚ÄĚ, those rooting for companies to fail so that the ‚Äúshorts‚ÄĚ can benefit at the expense of long-only investors - the vast majority of market participants.
All the way back in 1929, short sellers were widely blamed for deepening the stock market crash. President Herbert Hoover excoriated them for supposedly helping to plunge the country into distress by pummelling security prices.
More ‚Äčrecently, in March 2025, Kyndryl Holdings (KD.N) , opens new tab blasted a bearish report on its business by short‚ÄĎfocused Gotham City Research. The IT services provider rejected Gotham's claim that it was using deceptive accounting to ‚Äčcreate an illusion of profitability, calling the allegations ‚Äúdeliberately misleading‚ÄĚ and ‚Äúdesigned to manipulate the company's stock.‚ÄĚ
However, it turned out that investors would have benefited from heeding the ‚Ā†short-oriented research shop‚Äôs warning that Kyndryl appeared to be grossly overvalued. On February 9 , 2026, Kyndryl's chief financial officer and general counsel both resigned amid news of a Securities and Exchange Commission inquiry into its ‚Äčaccounting practices. That day, the share price plummeted from $23.49 to $10.59.
NORMAL SELLING ALONE CAN'T PREVENT BUBBLES
The instinctive dislike of short sellers is understandable. But their disappearance would not necessarily make life better for long-only investors. Going ‚Äčlong a stock when it‚Äôs severely overvalued can prove extremely costly. Short sellers can reduce investors‚Äô risk by helping push prices down to a more reasonable level.
Critics might argue that short sellers are unnecessary because markets already have a natural check on overvaluation. After all, for every buyer there is a seller, and the seller, by definition, must think the stock is no longer worth holding at the current price. Doesn‚Äôt that prevent a stock from becoming objectively overvalued?
The flaw in ‚Äčthis argument is that value isn‚Äôt always the primary consideration behind a sale. Some investors take profits for tax reasons or to raise cash for personal needs. Others follow short-term trading strategies designed to capitalize ‚Äčon tiny price moves, with little or no regard to intrinsic value.
Such investors won‚Äôt drive down a stock that‚Äôs unreasonably inflated by the latest investment fad, especially if buyers are ignoring telltale signs that reported profits have been ‚ÄĆexaggerated through accounting ‚Ā†gimmickry.
Correcting an overvaluation under those conditions requires proactive selling, which isn‚Äôt feasible if short sales are prohibited. Under such a ban, a trader aiming to push the stock lower by selling would first have to buy it, which would defeat the purpose.
The popular image of short sellers as wealth destroyers is therefore mistaken. In reality, they discourage the bandwagon effect, whereby masses of enthusiastic buyers jump into a stock at a wildly excessive price and subsequently suffer a massive wipeout.
Of course, short sellers are not selfless heroes working for the common good. They are seeking to make a profit, just like any investor, and heavy shorting can ‚Äčpotentially worsen market stress. That may warrant reasonable regulation, ‚Äčbut not the driving out of informed ‚Ā†skeptics who help keep prices tied to reality.
A COMEBACK FOR SKEPTICS?
Could we soon see a reversal of the recent contraction in short sellers‚Äô ranks?
There are certainly signs that investor optimism about frothy valuations is being tested.
As of March 9, the previously high-flying Magnificent Seven tech stocks were down by an average of 11% from ‚Äčtheir 2026 highs.
Meanwhile, shares of leading lenders in the private-credit market have also lost their luster, as investors worry about these firms‚Äô substantial exposure to software producers ‚Äčthreatened by advances in artificial ‚Ā†intelligence.
At the iConnections Global Alts conference late last month, Dan Loeb, the CEO of hedge fund Third Point, proclaimed, ‚ÄúThe lost art of short selling has come back and it‚Äôs absolutely critical this year.‚ÄĚ
If he is right, long-only investors may experience a bit more volatility in the coming months. But they may also get help avoiding devastating losses in stocks that temporarily detach from their fundamentals before nose-diving back to fair value.
(The views expressed here ‚Ā†are those of Marty ‚ÄčFridson , the publisher of Income Securities Advisor. He is a past governor of the CFA Institute, consultant to the Federal ‚ÄčReserve Board of Governors, and Special Assistant to the Director for Deferred Compensation, Office of Management and the Budget, The City of New York.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen ‚Äčto the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: Energean to acquire Chevron's Angola oil stakes for $260 million
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - Energean (ENOG.L) , opens new tab on Thursday said it will acquire Chevron's (CVX.N) , opens new tab interests in two offshore ‚ÄčAngola oil blocks for a base consideration ‚ÄĆof $260 million, as the Mediterranean-focused gas producer follows through on its plan to build out a ‚Äčhub in West Africa.
Energean has been ‚Äčramping up spending to boost output amid ‚Ā†geopolitical disruptions and assessing new merger and ‚Äčacquisition opportunities, particularly in West Africa, as ‚Äčit aims to expand its business.
Here are some key details about the deal:

item.title: Russia's Tatneft says 2025 net profit falls 48% y/y to $2 bln
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 12 (Reuters) - Russian ‚Äčmid-sized oil ‚ÄĆproducer Tatneft on ‚ÄčThursday posted ‚Äča 48.1% fall ‚Ā†in ‚Äč2025 full-year ‚Äčnet profit to 158.62 ‚Äčbillion ‚Äčroubles ($2 billion).
Its 2025 ‚ÄĆrevenue ‚Ā†fell 10% to 1.8 trillion ‚Äčroubles, ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†company said.
($1 = ‚Äč79.1500 ‚Äčroubles)

item.title: Tesla's energy business wins approval to supply electricity in UK
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - Britain's energy regulator Ofgem ‚Äčsaid on Thursday ‚ÄĆit had approved an electricity supply ‚Äčlicence for ‚ÄčTesla Energy Ventures Limited, ‚Ā†a subsidiary ‚Äčof Tesla (TSLA.O) , opens new tab , allowing ‚Äčthe company to provide power to households and ‚Äčbusinesses across ‚ÄčBritain.
The authorisation took effect ‚ÄĆon ‚Ā†Wednesday after a months-long assessment process.
Ofgem said the ‚Äčlicence covers ‚Äčelectricity ‚Ā†supply activities only and ‚Äčis separate ‚Äčfrom ‚Ā†an existing generation licence held by ‚Ā†Tesla ‚ÄčMotors ‚ÄčLimited.

item.title: Explainer: How persistently high oil prices could impact India's vulnerable economy
item.description:
MUMBAI, March 12 (Reuters) - India's external balance and government finances could be hit if oil prices stay high for an extended period, economists have said, as the Iran war pushes up oil import costs and the subsidies needed to keep key commodities affordable.
India is considered to be ‚Äčamong the most vulnerable to a global oil shock as it imports nearly 90% of its crude requirements and about ‚ÄĆ50% of its gas requirements. Over half of its crude is from the Middle East, where export flows have been disrupted by the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran , and India's current oil stocks are only enough to cover 20 to 25 days.
Gas supply shortages have already begun hitting industries and consumers , and Iran has threatened a protracted conflict and $200 ‚Äčper barrel for oil .
If oil prices average $100 a barrel for close to 12 months, the South Asian nation could also see ‚Äčgrowth fall sharply and inflation rise.
A prolonged crisis could widen the country's current account deficit, weaken the rupee ‚Ā†and stoke inflation, the government said in its monthly report last week.
The most immediate impact would be on India's current account deficit. ‚ÄčThis concern has pushed the rupee to a record low and forced the central bank to sell dollars from its reserves.
An average price ‚Äčof $100 a barrel would widen the current account deficit to 1.9%-2.2% of GDP for the 2026/27 financial year, from a projected 0.7%-0.8% of GDP, rating agency ICRA said in a note.
India's current account deficit was last at 2% in 2022. Its financial year runs from April 1 to March 31.
The federal government's annual expenditure ‚Äčcould also rise by 3.6 trillion rupees ($39 billion) in the next financial year if oil prices hold at an average of $100 per barrel, ‚Äčaccording to Mumbai-based Elara Securities.
The government's total estimated expenditure for the next financial year is 53.5 trillion rupees, according to the annual budget presented in February.
A ‚ÄĆkey expense ‚Ā†would be higher subsidies for the fertiliser sector to ensure farmers have the key input at affordable costs.
At an average price of $100 a barrel, fertiliser subsidies could rise by 200 billion rupees, Elara Securities said, and the government might also need to compensate oil marketing companies if they are asked to keep retail petrol and diesel prices low.
While retail fuel costs are technically deregulated in India, oil companies tend to delay ‚Äčprice adjustments in times of economic ‚Äčstrain.
The government is targeting a ‚Ā†fiscal deficit of 4.3% of GDP for the 2026/27 year.
If it chooses to maintain that deficit, it may have to cut long-term infrastructure spending being used to boost growth and jobs, Elara Securities said.
The Indian economy is expected to grow at more than 7% in the next financial year, on ‚Äčtop of growth ‚Ā†of 7.6% forecast for the current year.
If oil prices hold near $100 per barrel through the next financial year, GDP growth could fall to 6.6% and inflation could rise to 4.1%, the research department at the State Bank of India said in a report on March 7. If oil prices average $130 ‚Ā†per barrel, ‚ÄčGDP growth could plummet to 6%, it said.
India's economy has been in a "Goldilocks" phase, ‚ÄčReserve Bank of India Governor Sanjay Malhotra said in December.
While growth has been strong, inflation is low - coming in at 2.75% in January, close to the lower end of ‚Äčthe central bank's comfort band of 2%-6%.
($1 = 92.0000 Indian rupees)

item.title: Taiwan parties agree government can sign stalled agreements on U.S. arms deals
item.description:
TAIPEI, March 12 (Reuters) - Taiwan's three main political parties agreed on Thursday to authorise its government to sign U.S. agreements for four arms sales packages, after ‚Äčofficials warned that Taipei would go to the back of the ‚ÄĆline if it missed the deadline.
The back and forth on Taiwan's defence spending has provoked concern in the United States, as it is the most important international backer and ‚Äčarms supplier of the Chinese-claimed island, despite a lack of formal ‚Äčdiplomatic ties.
President Lai Ching-te's government has tried to get parliament ‚Ā†to pass $40 billion in extra defence spending but the opposition, which controls ‚Äčthe most seats, says the proposals are unclear, and it cannot be expected ‚Äčto pass "blank cheques" despite supporting defence.
Both opposition parties have come up with their own, less expensive proposals, but the defence ministry has said the letters of offer and acceptance ‚Äčfor the weapons with the United States have to be signed or ‚ÄčTaiwan would lose its place in the production and delivery queue.
Lawmakers from both sides agreed ‚ÄĆduring ‚Ā†a meeting of parliament's foreign affairs and defence committee that the government can still sign the agreements in advance, even if the reviews of the spending proposals are not approved in time.
The weapons to be signed for include ‚ÄčTOW anti-tank missiles, M109A7 ‚Äčself-propelled howitzers, ‚Ā†Lockheed Martin-made (LMT.N) , opens new tab Javelin missiles and the HIMARS multiple launch rocket system.
On Tuesday, Defence Minister Wellington Koo told reporters the ‚ÄčHIMARS letter expired on March 26, for 82 systems the ‚ÄčU.S. announced ‚Ā†as part of an $11-billion arms sale package for Taiwan.
Sunday is the deadline to sign for the other weapons systems, the ministry says.
Last month, a bipartisan group ‚Ā†of ‚Äč37 U.S. lawmakers voiced concern to senior Taiwan lawmakers ‚Äčabout the stalled plans.
The Trump administration has pressed allies to increase defence spending, a plank Lai ‚Äčand his government have enthusiastically embraced.

item.title: China teapots maintain refinery runs to cash in on lucrative fuel sales
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 12 (Reuters) - China's smaller independent refiners are maintaining output to cash in on a surge in domestic fuel prices, but may be forced to trim runs ‚Äčfrom late April as cheap feedstocks from inventories run thin and new deliveries price ‚ÄĆin the war-induced rally, industry and trade sources said.
Plants in Shandong province, China's hub for smaller refineries known as teapots that make up roughly a quarter of national output, are keeping run rates steady to maximise fuel sales at ‚Äčprices 20%-30% higher since Israel and the U.S. attacked Iran on February 28.
"Runs are ‚Äčstable. We're focusing on lifting the refined fuel prices and maximising sales at higher prices, ‚Ā†aiming to reap profits in the month of March for the whole of 2026," said a ‚Äčteapot official.
Many refiners in Asia have been cutting runs to preserve feedstock.
Teapots are capitalising on deeply discounted Iranian ‚Äčand Russian crude bought before the war, when benchmark Brent was around $73 a barrel. Brent is now around $100 after touching nearly $120 on Monday.
"We built some inventories earlier, so the pressure is not that big for the near ‚Äčterm," said another Shandong teapot executive.
Ex-factory wholesale prices for 0#diesel stood at 7,840 yuan ($1,142.19) per metric ‚Äčton on Wednesday, up 37% from February 28, while that of 92-octane gasoline rose 26% during the same period, ‚ÄĆaccording ‚Ā†to postings by Shandong Dongming Petrochemical, a large independent plant.
Hengli Petrochemical, a large independent in Dalian in the northeast, is maintaining processing rates at around 105% of its nameplate capacity of 400,000 barrels per day plant, a company representative told Reuters on Wednesday.
Consultancy Oilchem last assessed that Shandong teapots ‚Äčwere operating at 54.58% ‚Äčof capacity in the ‚Ā†week ended March 5, up 2.89 percentage points on the week, adding that runs may inch up further this week on improving margins.
Crude oil bought and ‚Äčdelivered before February 28 should last one and half months for many ‚ÄčShandong-based plants, according ‚Ā†to traders dealing with teapots, but operators may soon feel the pinch of soaring crude costs as the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively shut.
Meanwhile, Chinese demand for refined gasoline and diesel has been declining ‚Ā†as ‚Äčits cars, buses, trucks and motorcycles are rapidly becoming electricity-powered, ‚Äčand Beijing's move to ban fuel exports to pre-empt a war-induced shortage will keep a lid on margins, traders said.
($1 = 6.8640 ‚ÄčChinese yuan renminbi)

item.title: Shell CEO Wael Sawan's pay jumps 60% to $19 mln for 2025 -annual report
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - The pay package ‚Äčfor Shell < SHEL.L , opens new tab Chief ‚ÄĆExecutive Wael Sawan was 13.8 million ‚Äčpounds ($18.47 million) ‚Äčfor 2025 compared with ‚Ā†8.6 million ‚Äčpounds in the ‚Äčprevious year, Shell's annual report showed on Thursday.
Shell's ‚Äčannual profit of ‚Äčaround $18.5 billion last year ‚ÄĆwas ‚Ā†more than a fifth below 2024's amid weaker ‚Äčoil ‚Äčprices ‚Ā†and chemicals results, but ‚Äčit kept the ‚Äčpace ‚Ā†of its bumper share buyback ‚Ā†programme.
($1 = ‚Äč0.7473 ‚Äčpounds)

item.title: Shell's emissions broadly stable at around 1.1 bln tons CO2 equivalent
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - Shell's (SHEL.L) , opens new tab emissions were largely stable in 2025 at ‚Äčaround 1.1 billion metric tons ‚ÄĆof CO2 equivalent, according to its annual report published on Thursday and Reuters ‚Äčcalculations.
The bulk of the energy ‚Äčmajor's emissions come from so-called Scope ‚Ā†3 emissions based mainly on the ‚Äčcombustion of the fuel a company ‚Äčsells. In comparison, Britain's emissions stood at around 480 million tons of CO2 equivalent ‚Äčin 2024.
Net carbon intensity (NCI), the main ‚Äčmeasure the group uses for its energy transition ‚ÄĆstrategy, ‚Ā†stood at 71 grams of CO2 equivalent per megajoule, unchanged from 2024, said Shell, which wants to ‚Äčreduce this ‚Äčmeasure to ‚Ā†zero by 2050.
Measuring emissions performance by intensity means a ‚Äčcompany can technically increase its ‚Äčfossil ‚Ā†fuel output and overall emissions while using offsets or adding renewable energy ‚Ā†or ‚Äčbiofuels to its ‚Äčproduct mix to bring the headline figure down.

item.title: Generali posts record 2025 profit, unveils 500-million-euro share buyback
item.description:
MILAN, March 12 (Reuters) - Italy's top insurer Generali on Thursday posted a record profit for 2025, driven by all business segments, ‚Äčand said it was preparing a new 500-million-euro ($577.1 million) share ‚ÄĆbuyback, highlighting CEO Philippe Donnet's focus on shareholder returns.
In its first full-year results since Donnet's critics gained influence, Generali posted record operating profit of 8 billion euros, up ‚Äč9.7%, while adjusted net profit rose 14.5% to an all-time high ‚Äčof 4.3 billion euros.
Both figures were broadly in line with ‚Ā†a company-provided analyst consensus.
Lower natural catastrophe claims helped lift non-life ‚Äčearnings, while life business held up on broad-based growth and fewer surrenders, Generali ‚Äčsaid in a statement.
State-backed Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena (BMPS.MI) , opens new tab , with the backing of Italian tycoon Francesco Gaetano Caltagirone and Delfin, the holding company of late billionaire Leonardo Del ‚ÄčVecchio, gained control of Mediobanca, Generali's largest investor, last year.
Caltagirone and ‚ÄčDelfin, two major Generali investors, tried unsuccessfully to oust Donnet, who was backed by ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†former Mediobanca CEO Alberto Nagel, in 2022, blaming him for failing to grow the insurer fast enough and have since criticized several of his decisions.
MPS have not yet outlined its plans for Generali or the future of Donnet.
Generali's board ‚Äčapproved a dividend ‚Äčof 1.64 euros ‚Ā†per share, above the highest analyst estimate in the company's consensus, for a total payout of up to 2.5 ‚Äčbillion euros.
Under its strategy 2025-2027, Generali pledged at least ‚Äč500 million ‚Ā†euros a year in share buybacks and more than 7 billion euros in cumulative dividends.
Despite warning that recent U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran would ‚Ā†weigh ‚Äčon the global economy, Generali said it ‚Äčremained committed to the targets of its 2025-2027 plan, including 8%-10% annual growth in earnings per ‚Äčshare.
($1 = 0.8664 euros)

item.title: First train to Pyongyang in six years leaves Beijing
item.description:
BEIJING, March 12 (Reuters) - The first passenger train service between the Chinese and North Korean capitals left Beijing ‚ÄčRailway Station on Thursday, ending a six-year gap, as China moves to ‚ÄĆshore up cross-border infrastructure and rebuild ties with its neighbour.
Train K27 will arrive in the North Korean capital at 6:07 p.m. (0907 GMT) on Friday, after a journey of ‚Äč24 hours and 41 minutes skirting north of the Bohai Sea ‚Äčwith a stopover in the border city of Dandong , China's railway ‚Ā†authority said.
China and North Korea are "friendly neighbours" and a cross-border passenger ‚Äčtrain service facilitates people-to-people exchanges, a foreign ministry spokesperson told reporters on Thursday.
China ‚Äčalso backs stronger communication between both sides to ease such exchanges, the spokesperson added.
The service was suspended when the COVID-19 pandemic broke out in 2020.
North Korea is largely ‚Äčclosed to foreign tourism, with few exceptions, largely for Russian tour ‚Äčgroups under restricted arrangements, say travel agencies organising trips to the country.
The service linking ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†capitals will operate four days a week in both directions, running on Mondays, Wednesdays, Thursdays and Saturdays, China's railway authority said in a notice.
Tickets, restricted to business visa holders, were sold out for Thursday's trip, but those for ‚ÄčMarch 18 were ‚Äčstill available, a ‚Ā†Beijing travel agency said.
The shorter Dandong-Pyongyang link will operate daily in both directions, with the first service leaving ‚ÄčChina's northeastern city of Dandong at 10 a.m. on ‚ÄčThursday to ‚Ā†arrive in Pyongyang at 6:07 p.m., the official news agency Xinhua said.
Cross-border flights were also halted during the pandemic.
North Korea's state carrier Air Koryo resumed flights ‚Ā†to China ‚Äčin 2023 and now offers services between ‚Äčthe capitals twice weekly on Tuesdays and Saturdays, its website booking service showed.

item.title: Kenya arrests man trying to smuggle over 2,000 live ants in his luggage
item.description:
NAIROBI, March 12 (Reuters) - A man was arrested with more than 2,200 live garden ants in his luggage at Nairobi's main airport ‚Äčthis week amid a rise in cases of smuggling ‚ÄĆof the insects in Kenya.
Chinese national Zhang Kequn, 27, was arrested at Jomo Kenyatta International Airport on Tuesday while he was trying to ‚Äčleave the country, court filings seen by Reuters on ‚ÄčThursday showed. Immigration officials flagged a "stop order" on ‚Ā†Zhang's passport after he evaded arrest in Kenya last year.
Ant ‚Äčaficionados pay large sums to maintain colonies in large transparent vessels known ‚Äčas formicariums, which offer a literal window into the species' complex social structures and behaviours.
Last year four men were fined $7,700 each for trying to traffic ‚Äčthousands of ants valuable to Kenya's ecosystem in a ‚Äčcase that experts said signalled a shift in biopiracy from trophies like elephant ‚ÄĆivory ‚Ā†to lesser-known species.
Investigators said a search of Zhang's luggage recovered 2,238 ants, including 1,948 packed in test tubes and the rest in three rolls of "soft tissue papers".
They said Zhang had ‚Äčbeen in Kenya ‚Äčfor two weeks ‚Ā†and had mentioned three accomplices who supplied him with the ants.
The Kenya Wildlife Service told ‚Äčthe court that it needed more time to ‚Äčcomplete ‚Ā†investigations, including examining an iPhone and a MacBook recovered from Zhang.
The wildlife service said a similar consignment of ants had been seized ‚Ā†in ‚ÄčBangkok on Tuesday that originated from ‚ÄčKenya, indicating the existence of a widespread and organised ant-smuggling network.

item.title: Japan PM Takaichi cancels official duties over suspected cold symptoms, Jiji reports
item.description: TOKYO, March 12 (Reuters) - Japan's ‚ÄčPrime Minister ‚ÄĆSanae Takaichi ‚Äčhas ‚Äčcut short her ‚Ā†official ‚Äčduties and ‚Äčreturned to her official ‚Äčresidence ‚Äčafter suffering suspected ‚ÄĆcold ‚Ā†symptoms, the Jiji ‚Äčnews ‚Äčagency ‚Ā†reported on ‚ÄčThursday.

item.title: World faces largest-ever oil supply disruption on Middle East war,¬†IEA says
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - The war in the Middle East ‚Äčis creating the biggest ‚ÄĆoil supply disruption in the history of the global ‚Äčmarket, the International ‚ÄčEnergy Agency said on Thursday, ‚Ā†a day after the ‚Äčagency agreed to release ‚Äča record volume of oil from strategic stockpiles.
Middle East Gulf ‚Äčcountries have cut total ‚Äčoil production by at least 10 ‚ÄĆmillion ‚Ā†bpd - a volume equal to almost 10% of world demand - as a ‚Äčresult ‚Äčof ‚Ā†the conflict, the agency said in ‚Äčits latest monthly oil ‚Äčmarket ‚Ā†report. Without a rapid restart of shipping flows, ‚Ā†these ‚Äčlosses are set ‚Äčto increase, IEA said.

item.title: Russia jails for life Islamic State-linked Tajiks over concert hall attack that killed 149
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 12 (Reuters) - A Russian military court handed down life sentences on Thursday to four ‚ÄčTajik men and 11 accomplices over ‚ÄĆtheir involvement in a mass shooting that killed 149 people at a concert hall near Moscow two ‚Äčyears ago, Russian state news agency ‚ÄčTASS reported.
Prosecutors charged 19 people in connection ‚Ā†with the shooting spree at the Crocus ‚ÄčCity Hall on March 22, 2024 - the deadliest ‚Äčsuch attack in Russia in two decades. The Islamic State-Khorasan, or ISIS-K, claimed responsibility.
The four other defendants ‚Äčwere given sentences ranging from 19 to ‚Äč22 years in prison, Russia's RIA state news agency ‚ÄĆreported.
It ‚Ā†was not immediately clear how the defendants pleaded or whether they would appeal. Their trial was held behind closed doors, as ‚Äčis customary with ‚Äčsuch ‚Ā†cases in Russia.
ISIS-K is the Afghan offshoot of the militant group ‚Äčthat once sought control over swathes ‚Äčof ‚Ā†Iraq and Syria. It has been linked to several other plotted attacks in southern Russia and Azerbaijan ‚Ā†in ‚Äčrecent years, a trend that ‚Äčhas alarmed intelligence services.

item.title: Oil jumps 6% as Iran escalates attacks on Gulf shipping
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - Oil prices rose sharply on Thursday as Iran stepped up attacks ‚ÄĆon oil and transport facilities across the Middle East, fuelling concerns of a prolonged conflict and potential disruptions to oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz.
Brent futures climbed $5.95, or 6.47%, to $97.93 a barrel at 0915 GMT, having hit $100 per barrel in earlier trading, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate ‚Äčcrude was up $5.25, or 6%, to $92.50.
Brent hit $119.50 a barrel on Monday, its highest since mid-2022, then dropped after ‚ÄčU.S. President Donald Trump said the Iran war could be over soon.
The war in the Middle ‚Ā†East is causing the biggest oil-supply disruption in the history of global markets, the International Energy Agency said ‚Äčon Thursday, a day after approving the release of a record volume of 400 million barrels of oil from strategic stockpiles.
Middle ‚ÄčEast Gulf countries have cut total oil production by at least 10 million barrels per day - a volume equalling almost 10% of world demand - as a result of the conflict, the agency said in its latest monthly oil market report.
The market pricing is taking ‚Äčthe IEA release with a pinch of salt, as its timeline is not established, PVM analyst John Evans said, ‚Äčadding that it is presumed to span 90 days, equating to approximately 4.5 million barrels per day.
Goldman Sachs forecasts Brent crude to ‚ÄĆaverage $98 ‚Ā†per barrel in March and April before declining to $71 by the fourth quarter, but warns that in an upside-risk scenario, where flows through the strait are disrupted for a month, the March and April average could surge to $110.
"The only way to see oil prices trade lower on a sustained basis is by getting oil flowing through the Strait of ‚ÄčHormuz," ING analysts said. "Failing to ‚Äčdo so means that ‚Ā†the market highs are still ahead of us."
Explosives-laden Iranian boats appear to have attacked two fuel tankers in Iraqi waters, setting them ablaze and killing one crew member on Wednesday ‚Äčafter projectiles struck four vessels in Gulf waters, port, maritime security and risk firms ‚Äčsaid.
Lebanon's Hezbollah launched its ‚Ā†biggest rocket salvo of the current war on Wednesday night, prompting Israeli strikes that shook Beirut. Hezbollah's attack also raised fears about Yemen's Houthis joining the war alongside Iran, to further disrupt shipping in the Middle East.
Also on the supply side, ‚Ā†China has ordered ‚Äčan immediate ban on refined fuel exports in March, a further ‚Äčstep to preempt a potential domestic fuel shortage caused by the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, sources said on Thursday.

item.title: Landslide in southern Ethiopia kills 52, communications office says
item.description: NAIROBI, March 12 (Reuters) - A landslide ‚ÄĆin the ‚ÄčGamo ‚ÄčZone of ‚Ā†southern ‚ÄčEthiopia ‚Äčearlier this week ‚Äčhas ‚Äčkilled at least ‚ÄĆ52 ‚Ā†people, the ‚ÄčGamo ‚ÄčZone ‚Ā†communications office ‚Äčsaid ‚Äčon ‚Ā†Thursday.

item.title: ASEAN ministers to hold meetings to address Middle East crisis
item.description:
MANILA, March 12 (Reuters) - The widening conflict in the Middle East is expected to dominate discussions at Friday's ASEAN economic ministers' retreat, with foreign ministers also holding a virtual meeting on the same day to tackle a deepening ‚Äčcrisis that has upended global markets.
The Philippines, this year's ASEAN chair, is ‚ÄĆhosting the meetings as officials weigh impacts and responses to surging oil prices as well as disruptions to shipping, logistics, and trade flows across the region's export-reliant economies.
"The concern is a given," Philippine ‚Äčtrade undersecretary Allan Gepty told reporters, saying the region could not ignore the ‚Äčeffects of the crisis on inflation and jobs.
Joint U.S. and Israeli strikes ‚Ā†on Iran launched almost two weeks ago have so far killed around 2,000 people and have ‚Äčthrown global energy markets and transport into chaos.
The conflict has effectively shut the Strait of ‚ÄčHormuz, the chokepoint that funnels about a fifth of the world's oil and LNG to Asia, sending crude surging to above $100 a barrel.
The crisis has forced ASEAN foreign ministers to call a special virtual meeting to assess ‚Äčthe implications for Southeast Asia, where several economies rely heavily on crude and LNG ‚Äčshipments from the Gulf.
The Philippines sources much of its oil needs from the Middle East and Qatar's ‚ÄĆLNG ‚Ā†halt has also tightened supplies.
Several ASEAN countries have already taken concrete steps to cope with the impact of the Middle East crisis. The Philippines has shortened the government work week to conserve fuel, and its president has asked Congress for authority to suspend excise taxes ‚Äčon fuel to help ‚Äčstabilise costs.
Vietnam on Thursday cut its ‚Ā†retail fuel prices overnight following the easing in global crude oil prices from earlier highs, but the government warned prices will ‚Äčremain volatile, with more supply disruptions anticipated.
Earlier this month, Thailand also halted energy ‚Äčexports to ‚Ā†all countries other than Laos and Myanmar.
"It is important that our actions and responses to the ongoing conflicts must be synchronised," Gepty said.
ASEAN foreign ministers have said that the escalation in ‚Ā†conflict ‚Äčwas " particularly regrettable , opens new tab ," and urged an immediate cessation of hostilities, ‚Äčcalling on all sides to exercise maximum restraint, protect civilians, and resolve differences through dialogue in line with ‚Äčinternational law.

item.title: Israeli military says it targeted site used in Iran's nuclear programme
item.description:
DUBAI, March 12 (Reuters) - The Israeli ‚Äčmilitary said ‚ÄĆon Thursday it ‚Äčhad in ‚Äčrecent days targeted ‚Ā†a ‚Äčsite used ‚Äčin the Iranian nuclear programme ‚Äčwhich ‚Äčis identified as "Taleghan".
Taleghan is ‚ÄĆa ‚Ā†facility at the Parchin ‚Äčmilitary ‚Äčcomplex ‚Ā†which Western officials suspect ‚Äčis ‚Äčlinked ‚Ā†to Tehran's nuclear programme.

item.title: Up to 3.2 million people displaced inside Iran by conflict, UN says
item.description:
GENEVA, March 12 (Reuters) - The U.N. refugee ‚Äčagency said ‚ÄĆon Thursday that up to ‚Äč3.2 million ‚Äčpeople have been displaced ‚Ā†inside Iran ‚Äčsince the ‚Äčconflict began on February 28.
"This figure ‚Äčis likely ‚Äčto continue rising as ‚ÄĆhostilities ‚Ā†persist, marking a worrying escalation in humanitarian ‚Äčneeds," ‚ÄčUNHCR ‚Ā†said in a statement, ‚Äčciting ‚Äčpreliminary ‚Ā†assessments based on the number ‚Ā†of ‚Äčuprooted ‚Äčhouseholds.

item.title: Poland says foiled cyberattack on nuclear centre may have come from Iran
item.description:
WARSAW, March 12 (Reuters) - Poland has foiled a cyberattack on its nuclear research centre and is examining signs that Iran may be behind it, ‚Äčthe government said on Thursday, cautioning the indicators might ‚ÄĆbe a deliberate misdirection to hide the attackers‚Äô true location.
Poland says it has been the target of numerous cyberattacks since Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in ‚Äč2022. Moscow has repeatedly denied involvement.
Minister for Digital Affairs ‚ÄčKrzysztof Gawkowski told private broadcaster TVN24+ that the attack on ‚Ā†Poland's National Centre for Nuclear Research had taken place "in the ‚Äčpast few days".
"The attack may not have been on a huge ‚Äčscale, but there was an attempt to break through the security that was stopped. Appropriate services are already working", Gawkowski said, adding that the centre ‚Äčwas safe.
"The first identifications of the entry vectors, i.e. those places ‚Äčfrom which (the centre) was attacked, are related to Iran," he said. "When there is ‚ÄĆfinal ‚Ā†information and the services will check it, we will verify it, but there are many indications that it took place on the territory of Iran."
The Iranian embassy in Warsaw did not immediately ‚Äčreply to an ‚Äčemailed request for ‚Ā†comment.
The centre conducts research into nuclear energy, subatomic physics and related fields. Poland has no nuclear ‚Äčweapons and is building its first nuclear power plant.
The ‚ÄčU.S. ‚Ā†and Israel unleashed coordinated airstrikes on Iran on February 28, killing its Supreme Leader , Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
Tehran responded by hitting Israel and Gulf states hosting ‚Ā†U.S. ‚Äčmilitary installations, effectively halting oil and gas ‚Äčshipments through the Strait of Hormuz -- a conduit for roughly a fifth of the ‚Äčworld‚Äôs LNG and petroleum.

item.title: Explainer: Who might the US be talking to in Cuba?
item.description:
HAVANA, March 12 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump has said repeatedly the United States is in talks with high-level ‚ÄčCuban representatives, and that Cuba is eager to reach a deal to defuse tensions between the two neighbors that have deepened the island's economic crisis .
The Cuban government ‚ÄĆhas denied any official talks are under way, but has yet to explicitly deny press reports that U.S. officials were in talks with Raul Guillermo Rodriguez Castro, the grandson of former Cuban President Raul Castro, who is 94 and still wields great influence.
If talks are taking place, they come at a time when Washington has tightened its economic sanctions on Cuba by imposing a near-total oil blockade, and after the U.S. ‚Äčcaptured Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro on January 3, removing a crucial Cuban ally from power.
Raul Castro fought alongside his older brother Fidel in ‚Äčthe revolution that toppled a U.S. ally in 1959 and served as his brother's loyal defense minister for decades. He ascended to the ‚Ā†presidency, first on an interim basis when Fidel got sick in 2006, then took over definitively when Fidel retired in 2008. With Fidel's death in 2016, Raul assumed the mantle ‚Äčas the single unifying leader among those loyal to the Cuban revolution.
He still wields influence even after stepping down as president in 2018 and as leader of the Communist Party in 2021, ‚Äčsince then carrying the honorific title army general.
At his 2018 swearing-in ceremony, current President Miguel Diaz-Canel said Raul Castro "will lead the most important decisions for the present and future of the nation."
That was on public display as recently as December 2025. As Cuba's Communist Party prepared to choose Diaz-Canel's successor at an all-important party congress this year, Raul Castro proposed postponing the congress indefinitely, given Cuba's economic crisis. The party's ‚ÄčCentral Committee approved the proposal unanimously.
Known as "El Cangrejo," or "The Crab," because of his hand, Rodriguez Castro, 41, is widely seen as one of his grandfather's closest ‚Äčconfidants. As former bodyguard to Raul Castro, he was at his grandfather's side, sometimes dressed in the military uniform of the Interior Ministry, throughout Raul Castro's presidency. He has been reported to hold the ‚ÄĆrank of ‚Ā†lieutenant colonel.
The Cuban government did not immediately respond to a query about Rodriguez Castro's biography or possible role in negotiations.
Axios, citing three unnamed sources, reported on February 18 that Rodriguez Castro was in secret talks with U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio. The Miami Herald, citing unnamed sources, reported on February 26 that officials close to Rubio met with Rodriguez Castro on the sidelines of a regional Caribbean Community conference in St. Kitts and Nevis.
The Trump administration has been having secret, high-level conversations with several people in Ra√ļl Castro‚Äôs inner circle, similar to the ‚Äčdiscussions held in Venezuela before the U.S. captured ‚ÄčMaduro in a military raid earlier this ‚Ā†year, U.S. Representative Mario D√≠az-Balart told the Miami Herald.
Rodriguez Castro is the son of Raul's daughter Deborah Castro Espin and
That lineage places Rodriguez Castro at the intersection of Cuba's political leadership and its most powerful economic institution, making him a potentially significant go-between for Washington.
Rodriguez Castro has never spoken publicly nor given interviews.
If "El Cangrejo" is acting as a back-channel connection between Havana and Washington, he would be playing a similar role to ‚Ā†that of his ‚Äčuncle and Raul Castro's son, Alejandro Castro Espin, who conducted then-secret negotiations marking a major shift by Washington ‚Äčtoward Cuba during Barack Obama's presidency.
Family ties appear to matter. Neither of the younger Castros held a diplomatic job. Raul Castro in turn had been a confidant and adviser to older brother Fidel.
Alejandro Castro Espin, 60, is believed to ‚Äčhave been sidelined after his father left office, though the Interior Ministry colonel may have ascended to one-star general.

item.title: Russia's Rosatom says it will stay in Iran despite war
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 12 (Reuters) - Russia‚Äôs state nuclear corporation Rosatom will stay in Iran despite the conflict in the Middle East and ‚Äčis committed to its agreement to build two ‚ÄĆmore units at the Bushehr nuclear plant, its head Alexei Likhachev said on Thursday.
Rosatom, which built the first 1‚ÄĎgigawatt unit of ‚ÄčIran‚Äôs sole nuclear power plant at Bushehr, evacuated ‚Äčsome of its staff and suspended construction work on ‚Ā†the new units after the United States and Israel launched ‚Äčstrikes against Iran on February 28.
Around 450 Rosatom employees remain ‚Äčat the site, Likhachev said, after 150 returned to Russia via Armenia this week.
"The construction of the second and third units ‚Äčremains among the corporation‚Äôs priorities. It is definitely not ‚Äčthe time to leave. What is happening in the Middle East is ‚ÄĆonly ‚Ā†part of a global mosaic," Rosatom quoted Likhachev as saying.
On Monday, Likhachev said that the situation around Bushehr remained tense but that there had been no strikes on ‚Äčeither the plant ‚Äčor the ‚Ā†construction site.
A bilateral agreement between Russia and Iran allows for the construction of up ‚Äčto eight nuclear units, four of them at ‚ÄčBushehr.
Last ‚Ā†autumn, Iran said it had signed a $25 billion agreement with Rosatom to build four nuclear power units with a capacity ‚Ā†of ‚Äč5 gigawatts at another site in the country‚Äôs ‚Äčsoutheast. The parties also signed a memorandum on developing small nuclear power ‚Äčplants in Iran.

item.title: Morning Bid: Triple digit crude
item.description:
March 12 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
Crude oil is testing $100 per barrel once again, briefly topping that important threshold overnight after rising nearly 5% on Wednesday, as more ‚Äčattacks on ships in the Gulf outweighed the impact of the International Energy Agency‚Äôs plan for a record release of ‚ÄĆcrude reserves. Oil market volatility by one gauge recently hit levels not seen since 2020.
Iran, clearly aware that an oil shock is one of its biggest defensive weapons, warned yesterday that the conflict could send oil to $200 per barrel - and the Islamic Republic seems in no hurry to free Gulf shipping as long as the war rages.
I‚Äôll ‚Äčget into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why the lessons of the Ukraine war may now embolden ‚Äčhawks on the ECB.
And listen to today's episode of the Morning Bid podcast , where I discuss the longer-term implications of ‚Ā†this oil shock. Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.
Stock markets ‚Äčare down across the world again on the latest jump on oil, with major U.S. indexes finishing flat to lower on Wednesday and Asian indexes giving up ‚Äčsome recent gains on Thursday. European and U.S. stock futures were down before the bell.
The oil market‚Äôs shrug at the record 400-million-barrel reserve release is worrying and an indication of the timeline traders are now contemplating.
The 400 million barrels - more than twice the release seen after the Ukraine invasion in 2022 - would cover about 2-4 weeks of global ‚Äčdemand. If the war is still shutting down the Gulf after that, markets could tighten further.
With that timeline in mind, financial markets are already ‚Äčturning attention to potential inflation impacts and central bank responses.
A second U.S. interest rate cut has all but disappeared from the Fed futures curve, with markets now ‚ÄĆpricing in ‚Ā†barely one for 2026. February CPI inflation numbers came in as expected , but that data is from before this oil shock.
Looking ahead, there‚Äôs a sweep of central bank decisions next week. The Reserve Bank of Australia is expected to raise interest rates again. Markets are already fully priced for a European Central Bank rate rise by July, and mortgage rates are rising again in Britain. No central bank is likely to be brave enough to ‚Äčcut in this environment.
Meantime, U.S. Treasury ‚Äčyields hit their highest in almost ‚Ā†six months heading into Thursday. Soft debt auctions haven‚Äôt helped restore confidence this week.
And the dollar continued to firm on the dwindling rate cut expectations, holding near its strongest levels of the year so far, weighing down gold ‚Äčin turn.
Chart of the day
The IEA has coordinated emergency stock releases only four times since it was created ‚Äčby OECD members ‚Ā†in the mid‚ÄĎ1970s in response to the 1973 OPEC oil embargo. The infrequency underscores the gravity of the current situation, writes ROI Energy Columnist Ron Bousso .
Today's events to watch
* U.S. January trade balance (8:30 AM EDT), January housing starts (8:30 AM EDT), weekly jobless claims (8:30 AM EDT)
* U.S. 30-year note auction
* Fed's Michelle Bowman speaks
* ‚Ā†International Energy ‚ÄčAgency releases its March 2026 Oil Market Report
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your ‚Äčinbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They ‚Äčdo not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Brazil's dreams for industrial-scale cocoa farms fading after price crash
item.description:
SAO PAULO, March 12 (Reuters) - Cocoa farmers in Brazil have slammed the brakes on new planting projects following a 70% plunge in cocoa prices from their 2024 record high, stalling growth that investors had expected would make the country a ‚Äčmajor supplier of the main ingredient in chocolate.
At current prices of around $3,000 per metric ton , farmers and analysts told Reuters they expected around half the projects in Brazil to ‚ÄĆgrow cocoa on an industrial scale could be canceled.
The projects, centered in Northeastern Brazil, would have added at least some 75,000 hectares of growing area, according to an estimate by supply chain services provider Czarnikow, enough to supply nearly 5% of the global demand for cocoa.
"I think Brazil expansion plans have had a massive cold shower," said Paulo Torres, a London-based cocoa industry advisor and cocoa farmer in Brazil's Bahia state. Torres himself canceled a plan for an additional 30 hectares of cocoa at his farm ‚Äčin Bahia.
Current prices do not cover the investment or production costs of new fields, making the Brazilian projects unfeasible, he said.
The farmers and investors that planned the giant cocoa farms in Brazil , backed ‚Äčby big players in the industry such as Cargill (CARG.UL) and Barry Callebaut (BARN.S) , opens new tab , saw them as a solution to years of supply shortages that led to skyrocketing prices.
The planned farms would ‚Ā†have provided an alternative to the main growing region in West Africa. Ghana and Ivory Coast produce nearly 50% of global cocoa, which has left the market vulnerable to production problems there.
Unfavorable weather, illegal mining and diseases ‚Äčreduced West Africa production in 2023 and 2024, causing prices to spike from an average $2,500 per ton to more than $11,000 per ton and generating panic in the industry.
Cocoa traders scrambled to source beans. Smuggling rose sharply in Africa ‚Äčwith people bypassing official government buying to sell across the border at higher prices. The chocolate industry raised prices to offset spiraling production costs.
African production has since recovered, and other geographies such as Ecuador have increased production. At the same time, consumers have cut back on buying expensive chocolate, and small cocoa farmers in Brazil have not been making money. A group of them last month blocked a road leading to the Ilheus port in Bahia, setting old tires on fire, to protest ‚Äčthe arrival of imported cocoa from Africa.
After that, Brazil's food supply agency Conab suspended any imports of cocoa from Ivory Coast.
Weight-loss drugs have further curbed demand, and the industry has reduced package sizes and turned to non-cocoa ingredients, ‚Äčsuch as artificially flavored palm oil butter, hitting demand for the beans and exacerbating the cocoa price crash.
Moises Schmidt, one of the biggest cocoa farmers in Northeast Brazil, said the current price of cocoa would not cover the investments in irrigation ‚ÄĆand machines for ‚Ā†crop care and post-harvest processing typical of the region's farms.
"If the market remains below $5,000 per ton, more than 50% of the projects are gone," he said.
Schmidt had expected to oversee as much as 10,000 hectares (24,710 acres) of cocoa plantations, roughly the size of Manhattan. He declined to comment on what the price crash means for his own plans.
Two sources, one a large farmer in the region and the other an executive at an agricultural equipment supplier, told Reuters that a large project by Switzerland-based investment company NewAg Partners , opens new tab , for up to 22,000 acres (8,900 hectares) of cocoa fields, had been suspended.
"At this stage we prefer not to comment," said NewAg chief executive Detlef ‚ÄčSchoen.
There are other initiatives in the area whose future ‚Äčis uncertain.
Copa Investimentos, a Sao Paulo-based asset manager ‚Ā†with investments in agriculture and forestry, was also planning an industrial-scale cocoa farm, but one of its partners, Apolonio Sales, said it is now "evaluating" the investment.
Sales said he visited some of the new cocoa farms and also held talks with cocoa traders and chocolate companies, but the company has not yet reached a conclusion ‚Äčabout whether to go forward.
Brazil cocoa production could still grow, experts said, but at a much slower rate, mostly as a diversification strategy in farms ‚Äčwhere the trees would grow ‚Ā†alongside other crops.
The big Brazilian farms, however, had intended to deliver quick and massive production growth. An additional area of 75,000 hectares of irrigated fields could deliver 225,000 tons of beans in four years, or 4.5% of global production.
"If some big farm had plans for 400, 500 hectares, now it will do 80 or 100, only to start learning about the crop," said Emerson Silva, a sales executive at irrigation equipment maker Netafim.
Brazilian co-op Cooabriel, which announced a cocoa initiative ‚Ā†with Cargill last ‚Äčyear , said it would continue developing its small-scale project.
Projects from Sao Paulo's state government, which include using cocoa trees to reforest ‚Äčdegraded parts of farms, will also go ahead, said the coordinator Ricardo Pereira.
"In some areas, we already have seedlings almost ready to go into the field," he said, adding that as with any commodity market, cocoa prices would eventually rebound.

item.title: Iran war unsettles India's packaged water makers as bottles, caps get pricey
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 12 (Reuters) - The Iran war is rattling India's $5 billion packaged water market just ahead of the sweltering ‚ÄĆsummer season.
One of the world's fastest growing bottled water markets is seeing some manufacturers hike prices for distributors, as supply disruptions linked to the war fuel higher costs in everything from plastic bottles to caps, labels and cardboard boxes.
Though retail prices are yet to feel the heat and bigger ‚Äčcompanies are absorbing the pain, about 2,000 smaller bottled water makers have increased rates for their resellers by around ‚Äč1 rupee per bottle, a 5% hike, which will rise by a further 10% in coming ‚Ā†days, according to the Federation of All India Packaged Drinking Water Manufacturers' Association.
Consumers usually pay less than 20 rupees, or ‚Äčaround 20 U.S. cents, for a one-litre bottle.
"There is chaos and within the next 4-5 days, this will start impacting customer prices," ‚Äčsaid Apurva Doshi, the federation's secretary general.
Rising oil prices have increased the cost of polymer, which is made from crude oil and is a key material for the industry's plastic bottles.
The cost of material used in making plastic bottles has risen by 50% to 170 rupees per kilogram, ‚Äčwhile the price of the caps has more than doubled to 0.45 rupees apiece. Even corrugated boxes, labels and adhesive tape ‚Äčare costing much more, industry letters showed.
Clean water is a privilege in the country of 1.4 billion people where researchers say 70% of the ‚ÄĆgroundwater ‚Ā†is contaminated, leaving people reliant on bottled water. Companies including Bisleri, Coca-Cola's (KO.N) , opens new tab Kinley, Pepsi's (PEP.O) , opens new tab Aquafina, billionaire Mukesh Ambani's Reliance (RELI.NS) , opens new tab and Tata all compete for a share of the $5 billion market.
The companies did not respond to Reuters request for comment.
Within the broad bottled water market, natural mineral water is a $400 million business in India and a new, fast-growing wellness product for India's wealthy.
The ‚Äčpremium water segment accounted for ‚Äč8% of the bottled water ‚Ā†market last year in India, compared to just 1% in 2021, Euromonitor says.
Aava, which sells mineral water sourced from the foothills of the Aravalli mountains, has increased prices of its water bottles ‚Äčby 18% for resellers, Shiroy Mehta, CEO of the company, told Reuters.
"Most manufacturers are absorbing ‚Äč40-50% of the ‚Ā†cost to ensure that they don't lose clients. It's a poor situation for the beverage industry ahead of the summer season," he said.
The mass market, however, is dominated by companies that produce "drinking water" to be sold in 1-litre bottles to customers.
Clear Premium Water, a ‚Ā†brand of ‚ÄčIndia's Energy Beverages, said in a notice to its distributors there had been ‚Äčan "unprecedented and continuous surge" in prices of key raw materials used in packaging and production.
"It is no longer possible for us to absorb the escalating costs ‚Äčwhile maintaining existing product prices," the notice said.
(This story has been refiled to fix the date in the dateline to March 12)

item.title: China's mediation eases fighting between Pakistan, Afghanistan, sources say
item.description:
ISLAMABAD/BEIJING, March 12 (Reuters) - Chinese mediation efforts, including ‚Äča message from President Xi Jinping, have helped ease the worst fighting between Pakistan and Afghanistan since ‚ÄĆthe Taliban returned to power in 2021, three Pakistani government officials said.
The officials said a meeting between the Chinese ambassador to Pakistan, Jiang Zaidong, and Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif late last month included a message from Xi to cease hostilities.
Neither side has reported any Pakistani air strikes on ‚ÄčAfghanistan in recent days and ground fighting along the 2,600-km (1,600-mile) border has tapered off, although daily clashes continue ‚Äčto be reported.
China has said it is in contact with both countries about ending hostilities but ‚Ā†Mosharraf Zaidi, a spokesman for Sharif who has previously said there would not be any talks with the Taliban, did ‚Äčnot respond to questions about Beijing's efforts.
Pakistani security officials have said the military campaign will continue until desired goals were achieved, ‚Äčwhich was to prevent militant attacks in Pakistan launched from Afghan soil.
Pakistan's foreign ministry and military did not respond to Reuters requests for comment.
Islamabad launched air strikes on Afghanistan on February 26, saying the Taliban were providing a safe haven to militants carrying out attacks in Pakistan. ‚ÄčKabul denies the charge and says militancy in Pakistan is an internal problem.
The Chinese efforts came as Qatar, Saudi Arabia ‚Äčand Turkey, who hosted talks between Pakistan and Afghanistan during previous clashes in October, have been embroiled in the war in the Middle ‚ÄĆEast following ‚Ā†the U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran .
"China's Special Envoy for Afghanistan Affairs is currently shuttling between the two countries to mediate, while Chinese embassies in both nations maintain close communication with the respective parties," the Chinese foreign ministry told Reuters in an e-mail.
"The most urgent task is to prevent the fighting from expanding and for the two countries to return to the negotiating ‚Äčtable as soon as possible."
The ‚Äčforeign ministry added that Foreign ‚Ā†Minister Wang Yi held telephone talks with Pakistan's Deputy Prime Minister and Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar on Tuesday to discuss the conflict.
China's ambassador to Kabul, Zhao Xing, and the special envoy ‚ÄčYue Xiaoyong met Afghanistan's acting Foreign Minister Amir Khan Muttaqi this week, the Afghan foreign ‚Äčministry said in ‚Ā†a statement.
Afghanistan and Pakistan have said they inflicted heavy damage on the other in the conflict and killed hundreds of opposition troops, without providing evidence. Reuters has not been able to verify the reports.
Beijing, a longtime Pakistani ally, has invested heavily in mines ‚Ā†and minerals ‚Äčin both nations.
The investments include over $65 billion in road, rail and other ‚Äčdevelopment projects in Pakistan, part of Beijing's Belt and Road Initiative to expand land and sea trade routes to Europe and Africa.

item.title: Thailand seeks apology from Iran after vessel hit in Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
BANGKOK, March 12 (Reuters) - Thailand's Foreign Ministry has demanded ‚Äčan apology from Iran ‚ÄĆover damage caused to a Thai vessel that ‚Äčwas hit by ‚Äča projectile in the ‚Ā†Strait of Hormuz ‚Äčon Wednesday, which led ‚Äčto a fire and forced the crew to abandon ‚Äčship.
The ministry said ‚Äčit was seeking clarity from Iran's ‚ÄĆambassador ‚Ā†on Thursday on the facts surrounding the incident.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards ‚Äčsaid in ‚Äča ‚Ā†statement carried by the Tasnim ‚Äčnews agency that ‚Äčthe ‚Ā†ship was "fired upon by Iranian fighters".

item.title: Russia calls on Israel and US to end the Iran war
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 12 (Reuters) - Russia on Thursday called ‚Äčon Israel and the ‚ÄĆUnited States to end their attacks on Iran ‚Äčand come to ‚Äčthe negotiating table.
"Russia will continue ‚Ā†to take steps ‚Äčto end the escalation ‚Äčin the Middle East as soon as possible and ‚Äčresolve any contradictions ‚Äčby peaceful means," Foreign Ministry spokeswoman ‚ÄĆMaria ‚Ā†Zakharova told reporters.
"The number of victims of the illegal military ‚Äčaction of ‚ÄčWashington ‚Ā†and Tel Aviv among the civilian ‚Äčpopulation of Iran, ‚Äčaccording ‚Ā†to the authorities, is in the thousands," ‚Ā†she ‚Äčsaid.

item.title: Russia‚Äôs Iran setback comes with consolation prize
item.description:
BERLIN, March 12 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Russia is reaping a hefty reward from the loss of its influence in the Middle East. The bombing of Iran by the U.S. ‚Äčand Israel has boosted oil prices just as Moscow‚Äôs finances are crumbling. That will give President Vladimir Putin some ‚ÄĆfinancial firepower to fund his war in Ukraine and postpone painful fiscal decisions.
The Russian government built its 2026 budget based on Urals crude trading at $59 a barrel. That was a realistic forecast ‚ÄĒ and just about the price its oil was trading at the end of February. But in the ‚Äč10 days since the war in the Middle East erupted it has shot up to more than $80. That ‚Äčhands Russian President Vladimir Putin a helpful windfall given taxes and levies on oil and gas account ‚Ā†for about 20% , opens new tab of the central government‚Äôs revenue.
The boost is also timely. Oil and gas receipts were down 47% , opens new tab in the first ‚Äčtwo months of 2026 compared to the same period in 2025. The Kremlin had already exhausted its planned annual deficit by ‚Äčthe end of February.
If the Russian government abides by its own fiscal rules, the windfall won't flow into the budget. Anything above the $59 threshold must be saved in the country‚Äôs National Wealth Fund. But the fund acts as a rainy-day facility that can cover budget shortfalls ‚ÄĒ the situation Russia ‚Äčis in now. The liquid assets of the fund (held in foreign currency or gold) have already been depleted from $150 billion to $50 ‚Äčbillion since the war started.
If the prices of oil and gas remain at their current level for a few months, Russia can expect ‚ÄĆa significant ‚Ā†financial boost. According to an estimate , opens new tab from investment banker Yevgeniy Kogan, a $1 change in the Urals price triggers a variation of up to $1.9 billion in government revenue. Maintained over a whole year, higher oil prices could then deliver some $40 billion in extra revenue to Moscow.
And Putin may also sell more oil and gas. Donald Trump last week granted India a 30-day waiver from Russian oil sanctions, ‚Äčand the U.S. president hinted ‚Äčhe could go further to ‚Ā†make up for the impact of the Strait of Hormuz blockade on world oil markets.
