Moscow refrained from condemning the U.S. when it launched its recent Iran bombing campaign. It already lost a ‚Äčkey ally in 2024 in the region with the end of Syria‚Äôs Assad regime. Many ‚Äčgovernments in the ‚Ā†Middle East and beyond may soon wonder what is the point of having Russia as a supposed ally. But Putin has his consolation prize ‚Äď an unexpected windfall to fund his war.
Follow Pierre Briancon on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Indian source says Iran to allow India-flagged tankers through Hormuz as first tanker arrives
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 12 (Reuters) - Iran will allow Indian-flagged tankers ‚Äčto transit the Strait of Hormuz, a conduit for 40% of the South Asian nation's crude imports, an Indian government source said ‚ÄĆon Thursday, but an Iranian source outside the country denied any such deal was reached.
India, the world's No. 3 oil consumer, said on Thursday the foreign ministers of the two countries have had three conversations in recent days and the latest one this week focused on "issues pertaining to the safety of shipping and India's energy security".
"Beyond that, it would be premature ‚Äčfor me to say anything," foreign ministry spokesperson Randhir Jaiswal told a weekly media briefing.
The Indian source, who was briefed on the matter, ‚Äčdeclined to be identified, citing a lack of authorisation to speak to media, while the Iranian source said the matter was ‚Ā†a sensitive one.
On Thursday, the Suezmax tanker Shenlong, carrying Saudi crude, arrived at a port in Mumbai after transiting the strait. The Liberia-flagged vessel was ‚Äčthe first crude carrier to reach India from the Middle East since the war between Iran and the United States and Israel broke out in late February, ‚Äčaccording to LSEG data. The customer is state-run Bharat Petroleum Corp (BPCL.NS) , opens new tab , said a source with direct knowledge of the matter.
The company did not immediately respond to a request seeking comment.
The Indian source said two other foreign-flagged tankers believed to be bound for India had recently transited the Strait, and that Iran had given assurances of safe passage for Indian-flagged vessels after ‚Äčthe foreign ministers of both countries spoke by telephone late on Tuesday.
The source said the situation remained fluid, with limited clarity on how instructions were being ‚Äčrelayed across different layers of Iran‚Äôs administration.
In a statement after the talks between India's S. Jaishankar and his counterpart, Seyed Abbas Araghchi, Iran's foreign ministry said the United States ‚ÄĆshould be ‚Ā†held accountable for the "insecure situation and problems arising for shipping in the Persian Gulf".
Neither side mentioned any agreement on allowing safe passage for Indian vessels.
On Wednesday, India said 28 Indian-flagged vessels were operating west and east of the Strait with 778 Indian sailors aboard.
"Authorities, ship managers and recruitment agencies are coordinating closely with Indian embassies and local authorities to ensure safety and provide assistance to Indian seafarers," the Indian petroleum ministry said in a statement.
India has given ‚Äčsafe harbour to 183 Iranian sailors ‚Äčfrom a vessel that docked after ‚Ā†the war broke out between Iran and the United States and Israel.
New Delhi had allowed three Iranian ships that departed following a naval exercise in the Bay of Bengal to dock, but one was later sunk by a U.S. submarine ‚Äčin international waters, and another sought assistance from Sri Lanka.
Iran has attacked at least 16 ships in the ‚ÄčStrait since the war ‚Ā†began in late February. Tehran has warned that oil prices could nearly double to $200 a barrel as it retaliates.
A Thai vessel bound for India's western port of Kandla was attacked in the Strait on Wednesday, prompting criticism from New Delhi.
"India deplores the fact that commercial shipping is being made a target of military attacks in the ongoing ‚Ā†conflict," the ‚Äčforeign ministry said on Wednesday, adding that its citizens had also died.
The near-closure of the Strait ‚Äčof Hormuz has compelled India to scramble for alternatives, such as buying more from Russia.

item.title: Bombing won't overthrow ruling clerics, Iran dissidents say
item.description:
PARIS, March 11 (Reuters) - A senior official from ‚Äča Paris-based Iranian opposition group said on Thursday that the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran ‚ÄĆwould not topple the clerical leadership, arguing that only a popular uprising backed by internal resistance could do so.
Almost two weeks of bombing have killed around 2,000 people in Iran including supreme leader Ayatollah ‚ÄčAli Khamenei, and damaged much of its military and security apparatus.
Iran has responded in ‚Äčkind, throwing global energy markets and transport into chaos and spreading the ‚Ā†conflict across the Middle East, while the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps has tightened its grip ‚Äčon power and threatened to crush any unrest.
"The 12-day war in June, and the current ‚Äčwar, now in its 12th day, proved that bombings cannot overthrow the regime," Mohammad Mohaddesin, head of foreign policy at the National Council of Resistance of Iran (NCRI), told a news conference.
"Even if you have 50,000 ‚Äčarmed soldiers on the ground, you need the support of Iranian people. You need a ‚Äčpopular uprising. The combination of this 50,000 or 20,000 or any other number with a popular uprising, ‚ÄĆthen ‚Ā†you have this power to overthrow the regime."
Mohaddesin said he did not consider a deployment of U.S. ground troops realistic.
The NCRI, also known by its Farsi name Mujahideen-e-Khalq, was listed as a terrorist organisation by the United States until 2012.
It is banned in Iran, and it ‚Äčis unclear how much ‚Äčsupport it has there. ‚Ā†However, along with its bitter rival , the monarchists backing Reza Pahlavi, exiled son of the toppled shah, it is one of the few ‚Äčopposition groups able to rally supporters.
Mohaddesin acknowledged that his group alone could ‚Äčnot bring ‚Ā†down the system. But he said mass protests, like those that raged in January until they were bloodily quashed, would resume once bombing stopped, and could eventually shift the balance.
"I cannot say ‚Ā†how ‚Äčmany months or a year, but ... this is the ‚Äčtrack of overthrowing the regime," he said.
Israeli officials have said that one of their objectives is to weaken the ‚Äčsecurity apparatus so that Iran's people can take control of their own destiny.

item.title: In Norwegian Arctic, civilian doctors, volunteer 'casualties' rehearse for war
item.description:
NARVIK, Norway, March 12 (Reuters) - In Narvik harbour in the Norwegian Arctic, the city's chief medical officer watches as dozens of "casualties" are unloaded from ‚Äča train and get triaged before being transported in ambulances to nearby hospitals.
Thomas Hultstedt is here to observe and learn ‚ÄĆduring an exercise that is part of NATO's biennial drills in the European Arctic, Cold Response, which started on Monday and which this year places more focus on the role of civilians, businesses and public institutions in supporting the military in case of war.
He and other health personnel are rehearsing a scenario in which war has broken out ‚Äčin Finland and U.S. and Norwegian soldiers fighting on front lines, as well as civilians, have to be transported to Norway for ‚Äčmedical treatment.
Under the simulation, some 1,200 people are meant to be shipped over the next 10 days to Narvik, ‚Ā†though the actual exercise lasts just one day - Thursday - and involves about 100 volunteers, including students, playing the "casualties".
"I have never done this type of ‚Äčexercise before," Hultstedt said minutes before the train arrived at the quay.
"It is good in the sense that you prepare yourself for things that are ‚Äčout of the ordinary. This is very different from normal life. This is a war situation."
The train has come from Finland via Sweden to Narvik, where the volunteers will be "treated" in civilian hospitals in the region. In a real-life situation, they would then be sent on to southern Norway or abroad.
Hultstedt says this type of exercise was unthinkable five years ago.
"Then Sweden and Finland were not part of NATO... Now Norway has to step ‚Äčup. We must get equipment in and the injured out," he told Reuters.
Finland and Sweden joined the Atlantic military alliance in 2023 and 2024, respectively, following Russia's full-scale ‚ÄĆinvasion of ‚Ā†Ukraine on February 24, 2022. Norway joined NATO in 1949 as a founding member.
While NATO military planners never refer to Russia by name, the scenario they train for involves an attack from an enemy in the east. Russia lies roughly 600 km (372 miles) to the east, as the crow flies.
Moscow has repeatedly dismissed as baseless fear-mongering suggestions that it might attack a NATO member in the future.
Narvik, an industrial city nestled between a 1,006-metre (3,300 ft) ‚Äčsnow-capped mountain and the pristine Ofot ‚Äčfjord, is one of the ‚Ā†key places along Norway's coast where NATO allies plan to land if Finland, Sweden or Norway comes under attack.
The train line from Narvik to Kiruna in Sweden, and further east to Finland, is the main way to ‚Äčship equipment and troops between east and west in Arctic Scandinavia.
Narvik was the site in 1940 of an ‚Äčearly battle of ‚Ā†World War Two, when Norwegian, British, French and Polish forces pushed back Nazi German troops, before withdrawing when Germany invaded France.
Norway has declared 2026 the year of "total defence", the latest move by a Nordic nation to boost civilian preparedness.
"It is very important to narrow the gap between military forces and civil society ‚Ā†because in ‚Äča really critical situation - war - there is a need for a lot of resources," ‚Äčsaid Elisabeth Aarsaether, outgoing director of the Norwegian Directorate for Civil Protection.
While Norwegian planners are confident most households are better prepared to cope with war, she said, they are concerned that ‚Äčlocal authorities are not ready and so are preparing new guidelines.
"We would really like to help them," she said.

item.title: First message from Iran's new supreme leader to be released soon
item.description: DUBAI, March 12 (Reuters) - First ‚ÄĆmessage from ‚ÄčIran's ‚Äčnew ‚Ā†supreme ‚Äčleader, Mojtaba ‚ÄčKhamenei, will ‚Äčbe ‚Äčreleased within ‚ÄĆminutes, ‚Ā†Iranian state ‚Äčmedia ‚Äčreported ‚Ā†on ‚ÄčThursday.

item.title: Ghana to submit UN resolution on slavery reparations; eyes broad support
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - Ghana ‚Äčintends to propose a United Nations resolution recognising transatlantic slavery as ‚ÄĆthe "gravest crime in the history of humankind" and calling for reparations, and expects broad support despite resistance in Europe.
The West African nation, a prominent advocate of reparations , opens new tab on the continent, plans to ‚Äčtable the proposal at the U.N. General Assembly, possibly as early ‚Äčas this month, its Foreign Ministry said in a statement to ‚Ā†Reuters.
The plan marks a new step in Africa's efforts to seek accountability for ‚Äčhistorical injustices by former colonial powers, and the country hopes it will "enjoy the support ‚Äčof many member states".
"The proposed resolution seeks to recognise transatlantic slave trade as the gravest crime in the history of humankind, taking into account its scale, duration, legalisation and enduring consequences," ‚Äčit said.
While calls for reparations have gained momentum in recent years, there is also a growing backlash .
Several ‚ÄčEuropean leaders have opposed even discussing the subject, with critics arguing today's states and institutions ‚ÄĆshould not ‚Ā†be held responsible for historical wrongs.
Asked if it expected opposition, the ministry said: "A backlash against truth is one that we hope would not occur. Ghana is not seeking to reopen old wounds but to heal those wounds with truth."
African and Caribbean nations ‚Äčhave been seeking to ‚Ā†establish a special U.N. reparations tribunal, with lawyers noting previous tribunals had been created by resolution or by the Security ‚ÄčCouncil.
The African Union (AU) last year set out to create a "unified vision" ‚Äčamong its ‚Ā†55 member states on what reparations may look like, from financial compensation and formal apologies to policy reforms. AU leaders endorsed the proposed resolution at a summit last ‚Ā†month.
Member ‚Äčstates of the Caribbean Community, which has outlined its ‚Äčown reparations plan , are also expected to support it.
"We remain optimistic that others would equally be able ‚Äčto do so," the ministry said.

item.title: Strait of Hormuz issue must be resolved through diplomacy, says German foreign minister
item.description:
BERLIN, March 12 (Reuters) - A solution to security issues in the strategically important Strait ‚Äčof Hormuz can only be achieved ‚ÄĆdiplomatically, German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul said on Thursday.
"A reliable and sustainable solution can ‚Äčonly be achieved through diplomatic ‚Äčchannels, and that is why I ‚Ā†believe ... that we should pool our ‚Äčcommon interests from the Gulf region, but ‚Äčalso here in the neighbourhood," he said during a visit to Turkey.
Iran's retaliation to U.S.-Israeli ‚Äčhas included strikes on ships ‚Äčin the Strait of Hormuz, bringing non-Iranian vessel ‚ÄĆtransits ‚Ā†through the main gateway for much of Middle Eastern oil exports to a near standstill and forcing producers in ‚Äčthe region ‚Äčto ‚Ā†cut output.
"Together, we must find a way out of this ‚Äčwar and, at the same ‚Äčtime, ‚Ā†develop an initial idea of what a future security architecture for the region ‚Ā†might ‚Äčlook like," Wadephul said ‚Äčduring a news conference alongside his Turkish counterpart.

item.title: Taiwan rattled by 5.7 magnitude quake, no immediate reports of damage
item.description: TAIPEI, March 12 (Reuters) - A ‚Äč5.7 ‚ÄĆmagnitude earthquake struck ‚Äčjust ‚Äčoff the ‚Ā†coast ‚Äčof ‚ÄčTaiwan's eastern county of ‚ÄčHualien ‚Äčon Thursday, ‚ÄĆthe ‚Ā†island's weather administration said, ‚Äčwith ‚Äčno ‚Ā†immediate ‚Äčreports ‚Äčof ‚Ā†damage.

item.title: Zefyros tanker hit by unknown projectile at Iraqi anchorage - ship manager
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - The Zefyros tanker was ‚Äčhit by an unknown ‚ÄĆprojectile during a ship-to-ship transfer with ‚Äčoil tanker Safesea ‚ÄčVishnu at Iraq's Umm ‚Ā†Qasar anchorage, the ‚Äčvessel's Greece based ‚Äčmanager Benetech Shipping SA said on Thursday.
The incident ‚Äčtook place at ‚Äč2341 local time on March ‚ÄĆ11.
"All ‚Ā†23 crew members are safe and accounted for. They ‚Äčhave ‚Äčbeen ‚Ā†evacuated and were brought to ‚Äčshore," Benetech ‚ÄčShipping ‚Ā†said in a statement, adding the vessel's ‚Ā†condition ‚Äčwas being ‚Äčassessed.

item.title: Oil unlikely to hit $200 a barrel, US energy chief says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright said ‚Äčon Thursday global oil prices are unlikely to hit $200 a ‚ÄĆbarrel even as crude tankers remained stalled in the Strait of Hormuz and the U.S. and Israeli war with Iran widened.
"I would say unlikely, but we ‚Äčare focused on the military operation and solving a problem," Wright ‚Äčtold CNN when asked if prices would reach $200 a ‚Ā†barrel - a level prices could hit if the war continues to escalate, an ‚ÄčIranian official said on Wednesday.
"Get ready for the oil barrel to be ‚Äčat $200 because the oil price depends on the regional security which you have destabilized," Ebrahim Zolfaqari, the spokesperson for Tehran's Khatam al-Anbiya military command headquarters, said on ‚ÄčWednesday.
Oil prices jumped 6% to nearly $100 on Thursday as two ‚Äčtankers blazed in an Iraqi port after a hit by suspected Iranian explosive-laden boats.
The ‚ÄĆrise ‚Ā†came despite more than 30 countries in the International Energy Agency announcing a day earlier the biggest-ever coordinated drawdown of global oil reserves of 400 million barrels, about 40% of which will come from the U.S., the ‚Äčworld's largest oil ‚Äčproducer.
The war ‚Ā†has forced Middle East Gulf countries to cut total oil production by at least 10 million barrels ‚Äčper day, about 10% of world demand. The IEA ‚Äčsaid on ‚Ā†Thursday that is the biggest oil supply disruption in the history of the global market.
Wright also told CNBC on Thursday that the U.S. Navy cannot ‚Ā†escort ships ‚Äčthrough the Strait of Hormuz now but ‚Äčit was "quite likely" that could happen by the end of the month.

item.title: EU sees no immediate oil supply concerns despite Iran disruption
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 12 (Reuters) - The European Union does not see any ‚Äčimmediate concerns regarding the ‚ÄĆsecurity of its oil supplies as a result of the ‚ÄčIran war, an ‚ÄčEU Commission spokesperson said on ‚Ā†Thursday.
The EU's top oil ‚Äčsuppliers are Norway and the ‚ÄčUnited States. The spokesperson added that EU countries would notify the ‚ÄčInternational Energy Agency (IEA) of ‚Äčtheir planned oil reserve releases by ‚ÄĆ1700 ‚Ā†GMT on Thursday.
The IEA had on Wednesday agreed to release a record 400 million ‚Äčbarrels of ‚Äčoil ‚Ā†from strategic stockpiles to combat a spike ‚Äčin global crude ‚Äčprices ‚Ā†since the start of the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran, ‚Ā†with ‚Äčthe U.S. contributing ‚Äčthe bulk of the supply.

item.title: Police say drone found at mine in western Poland
item.description:
WARSAW, March 12 (Reuters) - Police in ‚Äčwestern Poland were investigating on Thursday the origin of ‚ÄĆa drone discovered by a worker at a lignite mine and it did not appear to be a civilian model, a ‚Äčpolice spokesperson said.
NATO member Poland has been on ‚Äčhigh alert for airspace incursions since more than ‚Ā†20 Russian drones entered its airspace on the night ‚Äčof September 9-10, 2025, and some of them were found ‚Äčin the east of the country.
"It doesn't look like a civilian drone, but there's no information about the model," police spokesperson ‚ÄčMaciej Swiecichowski told Reuters by phone from the western city ‚Äčof Poznan.
State broadcaster TVP Info cited sources as saying it ‚ÄĆwas ‚Ā†a Gerbera military drone . Gerbera drones are produced in Russia and are used as decoys.
Local police said on X earlier they had been alerted to an "Unmanned ‚ÄčAerial Vehicle ‚Äčof the ‚Ā†drone type" found by an employee in Galczyce, Konin County, on the premises ‚Äčof the lignite mine.
Lignite, also known as 'brown ‚Äčcoal', ‚Ā†is a combustible sedimentary rock.
"At the scene, police officers from Konin and Poznan are securing the area," ‚Ā†they said, ‚Äčadding that nobody had been ‚Äčinjured in the incident.

item.title: ICC opens probe into alleged crimes against humanity by Belarus
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 12 (Reuters) - Prosecutors at the ‚ÄčInternational Criminal Court ‚ÄĆon Thursday said they had ‚Äčopened an ‚Äčinvestigation into acts ‚Ā†of deportation ‚Äčallegedly committed by ‚ÄčBelarus.
Belarus is not a member of ‚Äčthe ICC, ‚Äčbut the case was ‚ÄĆbrought ‚Ā†by Lithuania, which is.
Prosecutors determined that at ‚Äčleast ‚Äčpart ‚Ā†of the alleged crimes ‚Äčby Belarusian ‚Äčauthorities ‚Ā†were committed on Lithuania's territory, ‚Ā†giving ‚Äčthem ‚Äčjurisdiction.

item.title: Nepal to ration cooking gas due to panic over shortage
item.description:
KATHMANDU, March 12 (Reuters) - Nepal will begin rationing cooking gas amid fears that the Middle ‚ÄčEast conflict could create a nationwide shortage, ‚ÄĆan official said on Thursday.
Chandika Prasad Bhatta, executive director of state-run Nepal Oil Corporation (NOC) said authorities would refill only ‚Äčhalf of consumers' empty cylinders starting from ‚ÄčFriday to make its liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) stocks ‚Ā†last longer.
The supply squeeze comes as the ‚ÄčU.S.-Israeli war on Iran and Tehran's retaliation across the ‚Äčregion effectively halts shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, raising energy and transport costs and reducing the output of Middle ‚ÄčEast oil and gas producers .
Bhatta said consumers were ‚Äčpanicking despite repeated assurances that Nepal had enough supplies ‚ÄĆof ‚Ā†LPG.
"The rationing is expected to end the panic and rush for hoarding," Bhatta told Reuters.
Consumers carrying empty cylinders have formed long queues outside refilling ‚Äčplants throughout ‚Äčthe country, ‚Ā†which is completely dependent on India for its supplies of fuel, ‚Äčincluding cooking gas.
Officials said Nepal requires about ‚Äč45,000 ‚Ā†cylinders each containing 14.2 kg of cooking gas every month and there was no disruption to ‚Ā†supplies.
‚ÄúWe ‚Äčare getting regular supply of ‚Äčfuel including the cooking gas according to our demands," Bhatta ‚Äčsaid.

item.title: Israeli military expands orders for people in southern Lebanon to leave
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 12 (Reuters) - The Israeli military ‚Äčissued new evacuation ‚ÄĆorders for southern Lebanon on ‚ÄčThursday which ‚Äčnearly doubled the zone ‚Ā†it said ‚Äčresidents should leave, ‚Äčsaying they should move north beyond the ‚ÄčZahrani river.
The ‚Äčnew zones, marked in ‚ÄĆred ‚Ā†in a map published on X by ‚Äča military ‚Äčspokesperson, ‚Ā†mean that the Israeli ‚Äčmilitary has ‚Äčnow ‚Ā†ordered people out of 10% ‚Ā†of ‚ÄčLebanese territory.

item.title: Iran's Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei says Strait of Hormuz should remain shut
item.description:
DUBAI, March 12 (Reuters) - Iranian Supreme Leader Mojtaba ‚ÄčKhamenei said on ‚ÄĆThursday the Strait of Hormuz should ‚Äčremain closed as ‚Äča tool of pressure.
In ‚Ā†a message ‚Äčread out by ‚Äča state TV broadcaster, the first the new ‚Äčsupreme leader ‚Äčhas issued since he was ‚ÄĆappointed ‚Ā†to succeed his slain father, Mojtaba said all ‚ÄčU.S. bases ‚Äčin ‚Ā†the region should be ‚Äčclosed as they ‚Äčwould ‚Ā†be attacked.

item.title: Iran war is one 'TACO' too far
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 12 (Reuters) - The "Trump always chickens out" investment strategy - buying beaten-down stocks on the assumption that the U.S. president will ultimately back down from his more extreme policies - has, for the most part, been a profitable one. But the Iran war may change that.
The joint U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran on February ‚Äč28 has sparked war across the Middle East, triggering the biggest energy shock since the 1970s and record volatility in oil and gas.
So far, U.S. equities have been ‚ÄĆremarkably calm, likely, in part, on the assumption that Trump will pull back if necessary. Since February 28, European, Asian and emerging stocks have lost 4-7%. The S&P 500 (.SPX) , opens new tab is down 1% and the Nasdaq is up 0.5%.
But this conflict may be a "TACO" too far.
Even if Trump were to declare an end to the war tomorrow, damage has been done that cannot be quickly reversed, least of all by him.
Shuttered oil production and refining capacity, bombed-out infrastructure, ‚Äčand the upending of global energy flows cannot be made right at the stroke of a pen ‚Äď or a Truth Social post. It will take months, possibly years, ‚Äčto get everything back to its pre-war state.
Similarly, the unprecedented surge in the cost of shipping out of the Middle East and insuring ‚Ā†vessels in the region is unlikely to return to "normal" levels any time soon, even after hostilities officially end.
"While global financial markets have been able to shake off the volatility and uncertainty caused ‚Äčby Trump's mercurial policies, the economic and geopolitical ramifications of the Iran war are likely to prove far more consequential," says Eswar Prasad, professor of economics at Cornell University.
This is the first time the ‚ÄčStrait of Hormuz ‚Äď the vital waterway through which 20% of the world's daily energy use passes ‚Äď has effectively been closed. No previous conflict in the region, including the Iran-Iraq War of the 1980s, the first Gulf War in the early 1990s or the Iraq War of the 2000s, has brought oil tankers to a virtual standstill.
Even in the market's optimistic scenario, where the U.S. wraps up its attacks quickly, instability that is almost entirely ‚Äčbeyond Trump's control would likely remain. And who knows how long that could persist, or how bad it could get.
Trump's economic and geopolitical playbook during his second term has been ‚Äčto push the boundaries of accepted norms, rules and protocols to breaking point ‚Äď or sometimes beyond ‚Äď then wait and assess the market fallout. If investors barely blink, it's a green light to carry on, even ‚ÄĆaccelerate. If ‚Ā†markets crater, Trump steps back from the brink.
Since Trump returned to the White House 14 months ago, he has unraveled America's 80-year alliance with Europe, imperiled the existence of NATO, threatened to annex Greenland and Canada, deposed Venezuela's president, and threatened to fire Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell.
To varying degrees, markets wobbled in response to each of these episodes, forcing Trump to tone down the rhetoric. That, in turn, gave rise to the so-called "TACO trade."
Despite the geopolitical tumult of the last year, U.S. equities have continued to appreciate, volatility has mostly remained subdued, and risk premia have stayed relatively ‚Äčwell-contained.
The biggest "TACO" of them all ‚Äď at least until now ‚Äď ‚Äčrevolved around the "Liberation Day" tariffs last April. After ‚Ā†trillions of dollars were wiped off U.S. stock markets in a couple of days, Trump backed down on the most punitive duties.
But today's conflict in the Middle East is different, and potentially riskier than anything seen in recent decades. If investors are looking for a historical precedent, ‚Äčthe oil shock of 1973-74 may be a useful guide in terms of its lasting economic impact.
Oil prices are unlikely to quadruple, as they ‚Äčdid then, especially now that ‚Ā†the U.S. is the world's biggest energy producer and exporter. But bear in mind, although the OPEC oil embargo lasted only six months, the inflation it spawned haunted the U.S. and other industrialized economies for the rest of the decade and into the 1980s.
A persistent surge in energy prices and disrupted global supply chains could easily light the stagflationary fuse. Investors may now start asking whether this ‚Ā†crisis is one ‚ÄčTrump can't relieve, even if he does "chicken out."
(The opinions expressed here are those of Jamie McGeever , a columnist for ‚ÄčReuters)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets ‚Äčand finance seven days a week.

item.title: Helium prices soar as Qatar LNG halt exposes fragile supply chain
item.description:
March 12 (Reuters) - Disruptions to Qatar's natural gas processing from the Iran war have driven helium prices sharply higher, exposing the fragility of a small but critical market that supports industries from semiconductors to medical imaging.
Helium spot prices have doubled since the Middle East crisis began, according to Phil Kornbluth, ‚Äčpresident of Kornbluth Helium Consulting, as buyers scramble to secure supply.
State energy giant QatarEnergy, the world's second-largest LNG exporter, announced a production halt at its 77 ‚ÄĆmillion tons per annum (mtpa) facility last week and declared force majeure on LNG shipments , amid the conflict.
Because helium is extracted as a byproduct of natural gas processing, any disruption to LNG output directly cuts helium supply.
Qatari Energy Minister Saad al-Kaabi told the Financial Times last week that it would take "weeks to months" for deliveries to return to normal even if the conflict ended immediately.
Qatar is a pivotal ‚Äčsupplier rather than a marginal one.
Data from the U.S. Geological Survey shows the country produced about 63 million cubic meters of helium in 2025, out of ‚Äčroughly 190 million cubic meters globally, accounting for close to one-third of world supply.
"If those conditions (supply disruption) persist, the market is ‚Ā†effectively missing about 5.2 million cubic meters of helium per month," said Aleksandr Romanenko, CEO of market research firm IndexBox.
The disruption is reverberating through a market with little ‚Äčspare production capacity and limited storage, leaving buyers with few short-term alternatives.
Japan's top helium supplier Iwatani (8088.T) , opens new tab said it had so far maintained stable supply to customers including semiconductor ‚Äčmanufacturers, partly because it also sources helium from the United States and maintains stockpiles in both Japan and the U.S.
Helium markets operate very differently from most commodities.
Most supply is sold through long-term contracts rather than a transparent spot market, meaning price signals often emerge slowly even as supply tightens.
That opacity makes price discovery difficult, but signs of tightening supply have ‚Äčalready begun to emerge.
"Early indications show about 50% spot price increases already," said Anish Kapadia, CEO of market research firm AKAP Energy.
"In a sustained disruption, prices could ‚Äčrise sharply and potentially retest past shortage peaks of more than $2,000 per thousand cubic feet."
Romanenko said a 30-day disruption could lift delivered helium prices by 10% to 20%, while a 60-to-90-day outage ‚ÄĆcould push ‚Ā†prices higher by 25% to 50%, particularly for buyers without long-term supply contracts.
Helium's physical properties add another constraint. The gas is typically shipped in liquid form and gradually evaporates during transport.
"It's a commodity, but it also has a shelf life," said Chris Bakker, CEO of helium developer Avanti (AVN.V) , opens new tab .
"So when you liquefy it, and that's how they tend to ship it worldwide, you've got notionally 45 days to get it to the end-user."
If supply tightens further, suppliers typically prioritise key sectors when allocating volumes ‚Äčduring force majeure events.
Kornbluth said industries such ‚Äčas medical MRI systems and rocket ships would ‚Ā†probably get 100% of their needs, while semiconductor manufacturers might receive 95%.
Lower-priority uses, including welding, diving equipment and party balloons, would likely face deeper cuts.
Last week, South Korea's ruling party lawmaker Kim Young-bae warned the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran could disrupt supplies of ‚Äčkey semiconductor manufacturing materials, giving helium as one example.
The hierarchy reflects helium's role in technologies where substitutes are limited.
Industrial gas ‚Äčcompanies sourcing helium from Qatar, ‚Ā†including Air Liquide, Linde (LIN.DE) , opens new tab and Air Products and Chemicals (APD.N) , opens new tab , are among those most exposed to the supply shock, said Kapadia.
Air Products said it was taking steps to ensure continuity of supply but did not give further details.
Linde declined to comment, while Air Liquide said it relied on multiple sources in different continents and on its storage cavern in Europe.
Iwatani Japan is ‚Ā†also exposed, ‚ÄčKornbluth and Bakker said.
Producers outside the region could benefit if disruptions persist.
Exxon Mobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab is the largest helium ‚Äčproducer outside Qatar, while Canada-based North American Helium and smaller developers such as Helix Exploration (HEXH.L) , opens new tab and Blue Star Helium (BNL.AX) , opens new tab could see stronger demand, Kapadia added.

item.title: Trump: US benefits from high oil prices, but priority is stopping Iran
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - U.S. ‚ÄčPresident Donald ‚ÄĆTrump said ‚Äčon ‚ÄčThursday that rising ‚Ā†oil ‚Äčprices ‚Äčmean greater profits for ‚Äčthe ‚ÄčUnited States, but ‚ÄĆhis ‚Ā†priority is stopping ‚ÄčIran ‚Äčfrom ‚Ā†having nuclear ‚Äčweapons.

item.title: EU looks to soften energy bill pressures for industry
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 7 (Reuters) - The European Union is examining energy ‚ÄĆtaxes, network charges and carbon costs as possible areas for short-term measures to ease pressure on industries hit by high energy prices, a document seen by Reuters showed.
Brussels is looking ‚Äčfor quick fixes after companies warned they cannot compete with rivals in ‚ÄčChina and the U.S. - even before this week's surge in oil ‚Ā†and gas prices sparked by the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
European Commission President ‚ÄčUrsula von der Leyen has pledged to present options for EU leaders to consider at ‚Äča summit on 19 March.
A Commission paper prepared for a meeting of EU Commissioners on Friday showed the bloc is exploring short-term measures to help the hardest-hit regions and sectors, without ‚Äčundermining longer-term climate laws meant to shift Europe to a cheaper, low-carbon energy ‚Äčsystem.
"Any proposal for legislative change will not deliver immediately and a bridge solution may be ‚ÄĆneeded ‚Ā†to reduce energy prices in the next 2-5 years until the clean transition eases pressure on power prices as already seen in some regions," said the document, seen by Reuters.
The paper said the Commission would look at network charges - which make ‚Äčup about 18% ‚Äčof industrial power ‚Ā†bills - and national taxes and levies, as well as carbon costs, which account for around 11% of bills.
It noted that ‚Äčgovernments are underusing existing tools to cut companies' energy bills, ‚Äčincluding state ‚Ā†aid to offset carbon costs and contracts for difference that guarantee industrial consumers a stable power price.
The document said that if energy supplies are disrupted further, Brussels must ‚Ā†be ‚Äčready to introduce measures to encourage consumers to ‚Äčuse less energy, as it did in 2022 when Russia slashed gas deliveries.
A Commission spokesperson did not ‚Äčimmediately respond to a request for comment.

item.title: EU to soften gas authorisation rules to help secure LNG supplies, diplomats say
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 12 (Reuters) - The European Commission will instruct governments to be flexible in enforcing EU rules on gas imports, diplomats told Reuters ‚Äčon Thursday, amid fears they could hold up liquefied natural gas ‚ÄĆdeliveries needed to stabilise supplies during the Iran crisis .
The Commission plans to issue guidance before March 18 on how to apply rules within the European Union's law to ‚Äčphase out Russian gas, the diplomats said.
The guidance marks an attempt ‚Äčby Brussels to prevent its Russia phaseout rules from inadvertently ‚Ā†choking Europe's gas supply at a moment of vulnerability. It would not ‚Äčaffect the EU's phase-out of Russian gas.
With the Iran conflict rattling global LNG ‚Äčflows and raising the risk of cargo diversions, EU officials fear rigid enforcement could delay or strand shipments, undermining the bloc's ability to keep storage adequately stocked and ‚Äčsafeguard energy security heading into next winter.
The rules in question require ‚Äčthat shipments of LNG from certain non-Russian countries receive "prior authorisation" - meaning companies must provide European ‚ÄĆcustoms ‚Ā†authorities with proof of the gas's origin five days before it arrives in the EU.
The main country affected by a softening of the "prior authorisation" rules would be Azerbaijan, which last year supplied 4% of EU gas ‚Äčimports.
The EU has ‚Äčalready said its ‚Ā†main gas suppliers, including Norway and the U.S., will not face the prior authorisation rules since the EU deems ‚Äčthe risk of Russian gas entering their exports to ‚Äčbe low.
Europe's ‚Ā†gas industry had already asked Brussels to temporarily suspend the rules on prior authorisation.
"We simply cannot afford that a single flexible LNG cargo is delayed ‚Ā†in port, ‚Äčstranded at sea, or rerouted to Asia ‚Äčbecause prior authorisation is unclear or pending," industry group Eurogas said in a statement on ‚ÄčThursday.

item.title: Russia says eight medics killed in Ukrainian drone attack in Donetsk region
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 12 (Reuters) - The Russian Defence Ministry said on ‚ÄčThursday that eight medics ‚ÄĆwere killed and 10 people were wounded in a ‚ÄčUkrainian drone attack ‚Äčthis week on a medical ‚Ā†facility in Ukraine's ‚ÄčDonetsk region.
There was no immediate ‚Äčcomment from Ukraine.
Reuters could not independently verify the incident. The Russian ‚Äčministry said it ‚Äčtook place on Tuesday in Donetsk ‚ÄĆregion, ‚Ā†which is mostly controlled by Russian forces, but it did not state ‚Äčthe ‚Äčprecise ‚Ā†location.
"This facility has never been used for ‚Äčmilitary purposes, and ‚Äčits ‚Ā†deliberate destruction by the Kyiv regime constitutes a ‚Ā†gross ‚Äčviolation of international ‚Äčhumanitarian law and human morality," ‚Äčthe ministry said.

item.title: Morocco plans repatriation of IS-linked citizens from Iraq, official says
item.description:
RABAT, March 12 (Reuters) - Morocco is developing plans to repatriate its ‚ÄĆnationals who fought for Islamic State in Syria and were transferred by the United States into Iraqi detention, a senior security official said on Thursday.
The U.S. began moving detained IS members out of Syria in ‚ÄčJanuary after the collapse of the Kurdish-led Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF), which had been guarding around ‚Äča dozen facilities holding IS fighters and affiliated civilians, including foreigners.
Last month, ‚Ā†the U.S. Central Command said the U.S. military had completed its mission in Syria after transferring ‚Äč5,700 adult male Islamic State detainees to Iraq.
Iraq has since then urged Muslim and Western ‚Äčnations to repatriate their nationals.
Morocco "is currently developing an action plan on this matter ... taking into consideration the diversity of the population targeted by the procedure, namely the fighters, as well as the women who lived ‚Äčin the SDF camps in Syria, in addition to their children," a security official close ‚Äčto the matter told Reuters.
There were 1,667 Moroccan foreign fighters in Syria and Iraq, of whom 244 ‚ÄĆwere ‚Ā†detained in prisons controlled by the SDF in northeastern Syria, while 279 former fighters have returned to the Kingdom, the official said.
The official added that 269 Moroccan women remain in the conflict zone along with 627 minors, while 134 women and 354 children are being held ‚Äčin SDF-run camps in ‚Äčnortheastern Syria. So far, ‚Ā†125 women have returned to Morocco since the beginning of the Syrian crisis.
In March 2019, Morocco repatriated eight fighters detained by the ‚ÄčSDF, who stood trial in Morocco and now are serving sentences ranging ‚Äčfrom 13 ‚Ā†to 18 years on terrorism charges.
Joining jihadist groups abroad is punishable by up to 10 years in jail under Moroccan law. In recent years, IS branches in Africa have recruited more than 130 ‚Ā†Moroccan ‚Äčfighters, the head of Morocco's counter-terrorism agency, the Central ‚ÄčBureau of Judicial Investigations (BCIJ), Haboub Cherkaoui said last year.
Since its establishment in 2015, the BCIJ has dismantled dozens of militant ‚Äčcells and arrested more than 1,000 suspected jihadists.

item.title: UK exploring additional Gulf deployments after minehunter withdrawal
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - Britain is exploring additional options for deployments to the Gulf after Iran stepped up attacks on ‚Äčvessels, defence minister John Healey said on Thursday, ‚ÄĆadding that the UK already has autonomous mine hunting systems in the region.
The Royal Navy (RN) said last week its last minehunter ‚ÄčHMS Middleton, which was based in Bahrain, had ‚Äčarrived back in Britain on March 1. The vessel, ‚Ā†which was more than 40 years old, was ‚Äčno longer certified to sail, it said.
"We do have some ‚Äčautonomous mine hunting systems already in the region," Healey said. "There are additional options we're starting to consider that we could deploy alongside ‚Äčallies."
Iran has deployed about a dozen mines , opens new tab in the Strait ‚Äčof Hormuz, two sources familiar with the matter told Reuters on ‚ÄĆWednesday, ‚Ā†in a move likely to complicate any reopening of the narrow waterway, an important route for shipping oil and LNG.
The U.S. military says it has targeted Iranian mine-laying ‚Äčvessels, eliminating 16 ‚Äčof them ‚Ā†on Tuesday.
Two tankers were ablaze in an Iraqi port on Thursday after a hit by ‚Äčsuspected Iranian explosive-laden boats. Hours earlier, three ‚Äčother ships ‚Ā†had been struck in the Gulf. Healey described the situation as a "major escalation from Iran".
"It is a breach of ‚Ā†international ‚Äčlaw," Healey said. "It's a very serious ‚Äčsituation that is having an impact on oil prices and the cost ‚Äčof living for everyone."

item.title: Caribbean islands seek $200 million for regional biodiversity push
item.description:
LONDON, March 12 (Reuters) - A group of Caribbean countries plan to raise $200 ‚Äčmillion for a joint bid to boost biodiversity, in a push for more influence ‚ÄĆon internationally financed environment projects to maximise their impact, Grenada's climate ambassador has told Reuters.
The 30X30 initiative by the Organisation of Eastern Caribbean States (OECS) and its 12 members is part of a broader global drive towards targets, agreed in ‚Äč2022, that include protecting 30% of the world's land and sea by 2030.
The hope is ‚Äčthat such cross-border initiatives, with government oversight from the start, will be more ‚Ā†effective as they harness the power of multilateral, bilateral, philanthropic and private sector funding along with ‚Äčfinancial tools such as debt-for-nature swaps, Safiya Sawney said.
While around $650 ‚Äčmillion was raised to fund conservation in member states over the 20 years to 2024, governments often had to consider the priorities of multilateral donors before their own. For lack of coordination, some donors duplicated efforts.
"From the outside looking ‚Äčin, you say, 'Well, that's a lot of money. Why do you need more?'. The issue ... is ‚Äčthat we're not seeing that in the impact," she added, in an interview last week.
A report , opens new tab from the Back ‚ÄĆto Blue ‚Ā†initiative, which focuses on ocean sustainability policy, suggested regional approaches such as "OECS 30X30" could be transformative in closing a huge funding gap.
Small Island Developing States (SIDS) worldwide - which will bear the brunt of rising sea levels - need around $12 billion , opens new tab a year to adapt to the effects of climate change, and get just $2 ‚Äčbillion, according to the ‚ÄčGlobal Center on Adaptation.
"There's ‚Ā†a huge disconnect between what the governments have invested time and effort into planning for themselves, based on their own indigenous circumstances, and what the ‚Äčdonor is funding," Sawney said.
"We're challenging them to say 'No, you have to ‚Äčdo a better ‚Ā†job if you really want to ensure that your money stretches. You have to trust us, trust that we know what would we need to do for ourselves'."
Sawney said that, as development aid budgets are ‚Ā†cut in ‚Äčricher countries, it was important to scale projects regionally and ‚Äčattract more private sector investors.
"For us, the programme is really, really important because it's really on us to be able to ‚Äčshow investors, particularly non-traditional investors, that we're a good bet."

item.title: Ukraine opens battlefield data access to allies' AI models
item.description:
KYIV, March 12 (Reuters) - Ukraine is opening up access to its battlefield data for its allies to train drone AI software, the defence minister said ‚Äčon Thursday, as Kyiv seeks to harness the experience it has garnered ‚ÄĆfending off Russia's four-year, full-scale invasion.
The move comes as militaries across the globe start to use automated systems which can guide drones to their targets without a pilot, or ‚Äčquickly analyse vast pools of data.
Foreign allies and companies have sought ‚Äčaccess to Ukraine's datasets, as these are crucial for training models to ‚Ā†recognise patterns, shapes, and the behaviour of people and machines on the ‚Äčbattlefield.
Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov said a platform had been created to safely train ‚ÄčAI models without giving away sensitive data, but which nevertheless provides constantly updating datasets and large quantities of photos and video footage.
"Today, Ukraine has a unique array of battlefield data ‚Äčthat is unmatched anywhere else in the world," he wrote on Telegram.
"This ‚Äčincludes millions of annotated images collected during tens of thousands of combat flights."
Fedorov, a tech-savvy ‚ÄĆally ‚Ā†of President Volodymyr Zelenskiy, said Ukraine would benefit from speeding up the development of AI models which it can then use in its war against Russia.
"We are ready to work with partners on joint analytics, model training, and the creation ‚Äčof new technological solutions," ‚Äčhe said, adding ‚Ā†that Ukraine wants to increase the role played by autonomous systems in the war.
When he was appointed in January, ‚ÄčFedorov laid out his plans to conduct a broader data-driven overhaul ‚Äčof ‚Ā†Ukraine's vast defence ministry.
Ukraine is keen to maximise its advantage from the experience gained from Europe's largest conflict since 1945, as it strives to retain its allies' ‚Ā†interest and ‚Äčfunding in the fifth year of full-scale ‚Äčwar.
It has sent anti-drone specialists to four Middle Eastern nations this week after they requested Kyiv's help in ‚Äčdowning Iran's vast barrages of Shahed UAVs.

item.title: European rights body says abortion refusal complaints from Poland have ceased
item.description:
WARSAW, March 12 (Reuters) - The number of legal abortions in Poland doubled in 2024, while complaints to the European Court of Human Rights over conscience clause refusals in ‚Äčthe country have ceased, the Council of Europe said on Thursday.
Poland, a ‚ÄĆpredominantly Catholic country, introduced a near-total abortion ban in 2021 under the previous nationalist government after pregnancy termination due to foetal abnormalities was ruled unconstitutional.
Prime Minister Donald Tusk's pro-European coalition won the ‚Äč2023 election partly on the promise it would ease the restrictions, but it ‚Äčhas so far failed to agree on a bill. Nationalist President ‚Ā†Karol Nawrocki has signalled he will block any attempt at liberalisation.
However, Tusk's government ‚Äčhas taken steps to enforce the existing law. Ministers issued recommendations to hospitals and ‚Äčprosecutors clarifying that abortion on the grounds of a woman's mental health is legal, and that all hospitals are obliged to provide the procedure.
The Council of Europe's Committee of Ministers, which oversees ‚Äčthe implementation of European Court of Human Rights rulings, said on Thursday the ‚Äčgovernment's actions "appear to bring results in practice."
"The number of lawful abortions has doubled year to year ‚ÄĆand ‚Ā†no complaints have recently been received about the refusal of abortion based on the conscience clause", the committee said in its decision on Poland's compliance with judgments concerning access to legal abortion.
It also noted "that since the introduction of the unconditional obligation of ‚Äčhospitals to provide abortion ‚Äčas a contracted ‚Ā†medical service, no complaint has been reported about a refusal of lawful abortion based on the conscience clause."
The committee, however, expressed ‚Äčregret over the lack of progress in adopting legislation on ‚Äčthe safe ‚Ā†termination of pregnancy.
Health ministry data shows that nearly 900 legal abortions were performed in Polish hospitals in 2024, compared with 425 a year earlier. There were 411 legal terminations ‚Ā†in ‚Äčthe first half of 2025, with full-year data ‚Äčnot yet available.
Before the 2021 ruling that tightened the law, more than 1,000 abortions were performed annually in ‚ÄčPoland, nearly all attributed to foetal abnormalities.

item.title: Eight countries warn EU not to weaken carbon market, document shows
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 12 (Reuters) - Spain, the Netherlands and six other governments have urged the European Union not to dismantle or suspend the bloc's emissions ‚Äčtrading system, its main climate change policy, as Brussels hunts for ‚ÄĆways to curb energy prices.
With energy prices surging on disruption to Middle Eastern oil and gas supplies, Brussels is facing calls from governments including Italy to suspend its ETS, which ‚Äčrequires power plants to buy permits to cover their CO2 emissions.
"Making ‚Äčfundamental changes to the ETS, calling into question the ETS ‚Ā†instrument itself, or suspending it, would constitute a very worrying step backwards," ‚Äča group of eight EU countries said in a joint paper, seen by ‚ÄčReuters.
Weakening the scheme would "dramatically penalise early movers who have already invested and innovated in decarbonisation", said the paper, which was also signed by Denmark, Finland, Luxembourg, Portugal, Slovenia and Sweden.
By putting ‚Äča price on pollution, the ETS aims to provide a financial incentive ‚Äčto companies to invest in lower-carbon production.
The Commission has so far held off calls from ‚ÄĆother ‚Ā†governments, including Slovakia and the Czech Republic , to weaken the ETS.
But officials are hunting for quick fixes to avoid the Iran conflict triggering a repeat of 2022, when Europe faced record-high energy prices after Russia invaded Ukraine and slashed ‚Äčgas deliveries to ‚ÄčEurope.
The European Commission ‚Ā†has said it will provide options for EU leaders to consider at a summit on 19 March. European Commission ‚ÄčPresident Ursula von der Leyen said on Wednesday Brussels was ‚Äčexploring ‚Ā†a gas price cap , but did not mention changes to the ETS.
The ETS cost comprises around 11%, on average, of electricity bills in Europe, according to EU data. ‚Ā†It ‚Äčresults in higher costs for countries like Poland, ‚Äčwhere the ETS's contribution to power bills is 24% due to the dominant role of coal ‚Äčpower plants in the power mix.

item.title: Foreign investors threaten legal action against Vietnam over renewables, document says
item.description:
HANOI, March 12 (Reuters) - Foreign investors may take legal action if Vietnam does not fully pay electricity tariffs agreed with multiple wind and solar projects, according to ‚Äča document signed by five chambers of commerce seen by Reuters.
The dispute ‚ÄĆbegan last year when Vietnam cut previously agreed subsidised prices for electricity from some solar and wind farms, citing irregularities. The move comes as Vietnam's energy sector struggles with higher prices and risks of shortages ‚Äčlinked to the conflict in Iran.
Lengthy negotiations with affected investors, who run ‚Äčprojects with a combined capacity of 12 gigawatts, failed to produce a ‚Ā†compromise, prompting the chambers of commerce in Vietnam from the European Union, Britain, Japan, ‚ÄčSouth Korea and Thailand to send a joint letter to the government on Thursday.
If ‚Äčpayment obligations are not met, electricity producers "may seek to enforce remedies, including pursuing dispute resolution in Vietnam or other jurisdictions," said the document, dated March 12.
The letter urges authorities to find an amicable ‚Äčsolution to a dispute that the chambers said could lead to defaults and significant ‚Äčlosses on multibillion-dollar investments in Vietnam's renewable energy sector.
Vietnam's industry ministry, which received the letter, did ‚ÄĆnot ‚Ā†immediately respond to a request for comment.
In recent years, the Southeast Asian country saw a boom in renewable energy investments driven by generous subsidised, or feed-in, tariffs under which the state committed to buying electricity for 20 years at above-market prices.
However, the high ‚Äčtariffs increased losses for ‚Äčstate-owned power utility ‚Ā†EVN, the sole buyer of the generated electricity, and pushed up power prices for households and factories.
The retroactive change to tariffs, which ‚Äčtook effect in January 2025, followed investigations into alleged abuses ‚Äčin accessing ‚Ā†the preferential rates.
EVN had no immediate comment.
The letter follows several coordinated actions aimed at persuading Vietnam to change course. These included a document in May signed by 16 affected firms, ‚Ā†among them ‚Äčprivate equity fund Dragon Capital, the Vietnamese subsidiary ‚Äčof Philippines' ACEN ACEN.PS energy group, and investors from Thailand, Portugal, the Netherlands, South Korea, Singapore and China.

item.title: US sues California over zero emission vehicle, greenhouse gas rules
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 12 (Reuters) - The Trump administration on Thursday sued the state ‚Äčof California, claiming the state's zero-emission vehicle and tailpipe greenhouse gas emissions ‚ÄĆrules are illegal and preempted by federal law.
The U.S. Transportation Department sued the California Air Resources Board in U.S. District Court in California over vehicle rules that remain in place after President ‚ÄčDonald Trump signed legislation last year to overturn California‚Äôs Advanced Clean Cars ‚ÄčII rules that aim to phase out new gasoline-powered cars by 2035.
The ‚Ā†lawsuit wants a ruling declaring that all zero-emission vehicle mandates by California are ‚Äčunlawful and unenforceable.
"This litigation will help automakers design and produce cars and trucks to ‚Äčmeet one federal fuel economy regulation," said Jonathan Morrison, who heads the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration.
California won approval in 2022 from the Environmental Protection Agency for its current vehicle ‚Äčrules known as Advanced Clean Cars I, which the state says are still ‚Äčin effect. The California Air Resources Board did not immediately comment.
This is the latest in a ‚ÄĆseries ‚Ā†of efforts by the Trump administration to bar California from setting vehicle rules. In August, it sued California to stop the state from enforcing stringent emissions standards for heavy-duty trucks.
California's rules require automakers to sell a rising number of electric ‚Äčvehicles and meet ‚Äčincreasingly stringent limits ‚Ā†on tailpipe emissions.
Those rules are much stricter than those imposed by the Trump administration, which plans to roll back federal fuel economy rules.
California contends ‚Äčthe fuel savings for consumers from the rules far exceed ‚Äčthe higher ‚Ā†upfront costs of EVs.
Congress rescinded authority for California to outlaw traditional gas-powered vehicles after 2035 after Toyota and the Detroit Three lobbied Congress and the White House for significant relief ‚Ā†from ‚ÄčCalifornia's emissions regulations.
The White House also significantly weakened ‚Äčfederal tailpipe rules and Congress passed legislation to stop collecting penalties for not meeting vehicle tailpipe standards.
A group ‚Äčrepresenting major automakers did not immediately comment.
